Intent Statements
An analysis of formal complaints can give the 
organization valuable information to facilitate 
change that results in better customer service 
and results for the persons served.
K. Accessibility
Description
CARF-accredited organizations promote accessi-
bility and the removal of barriers for the persons 
served and other stakeholders.
1.K. 1. The organization’s leadership:
1.K. Accessibilty
a. Assesses the accessibility needs of 
the:
1.a.K. Accessibilty
(1) Persons served.
1.a.(1)K. Accessibilty
(2) Personnel.
1.a.(2)K. Accessibilty
(3) Other stakeholders.
1.a.(3)K. Accessibilty
b. Implements an ongoing process 
to consider barriers in each of the 
following areas:
1.b.K. Accessibilty
(1) Architecture.
1.b.(1)K. Accessibilty
(2) Environment.
1.b.(2)K. Accessibilty
(3) Attitudes.
1.b.(3)K. Accessibilty
(4) Finances.
1.b.(4)K. Accessibilty
(5) Employment.
1.b.(5)K. Accessibilty
(6) Communication.
1.b.(6)K. Accessibilty
(7) Technology.
1.b.(7)K. Accessibilty
(8) Transportation.
1.b.(8)K. Accessibilty
(9) Community integration, when 
appropriate.
1.b.(9)K. Accessibilty
(10) Any other barrier identified by 
the:
1.b.(10)K. Accessibilty
(a) Persons served.
1.b.(10)(a)K. Accessibilty
(b) Personnel.
1.b.(10)(b)K. Accessibilty
(c) Other stakeholders.
1.b.(10)(c)K. Accessibilty
Intent Statements
The leadership demonstrates a commitment to 
accessibility and the removal of barriers. Organi-
zations address barriers to accessibility in order 
to:
n Foster a person-centered approach to service 
delivery, facilitate inclusion, and enhance the 
quality of life for those served in their pro-
grams and services.
n Implement nondiscriminatory employment 
practices.
n Meet legal and regulatory requirements.
n Meet the expectations of stakeholders in the 
area of accessibility.

SECTION 1.K. ACCESSIBILITY
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 93
The leadership should address how input was 
solicited from the persons served, personnel, and 
other stakeholders to assist in the identification 
of barriers and take into consideration any 
accessibility needs—physical, cognitive, sensory, 
emotional, or developmental—that may hinder 
full and effective participation on an equal basis 
with others.
Examples
Examples of accessibility planning may be found 
in minutes of meetings where analysis, action 
planning, and goals are established; conversa-
tions with stakeholders; focus groups and council 
meetings; community events; surveys; etc.
1.b.(1) Architectural or physical barriers are gen-
erally easy to identify and may include steps that 
prevent access to a building for an individual who 
uses a wheelchair, narrow doorways that need to 
be widened, bathrooms that need to be made 
accessible, high ceilings with poor acoustics or 
the absence of light alarms for individuals who 
are deaf or hard of hearing, the absence of signs 
in Braille for individuals who are blind or have 
low vision.
1.b.(2) Environmental barriers can be interpreted 
as any location or characteristic of the setting that 
compromises, hinders, or impedes service deliv-
ery and the benefits to be gained. Some clinics 
may be located in areas where the persons served 
and/or personnel do not feel safe or feel that con-
fidentiality may be risked.
In addition to such external environmental 
barriers, internal barriers may include noise level, 
lack of soundproof counseling rooms, highly 
trafficked areas used for service delivery, or 
type or lack of furnishing and décor that impacts 
the comfort level of the persons served and 
personnel.
1.b.(3) Attitudinal barriers may include, but are 
not limited to:
n The terminology and language that the 
organization uses in its literature or when it 
communicates with persons with disabilities, 
other stakeholders, and the public (e.g., 
whether the organization uses person-first 
language).
n How persons with disabilities are viewed and 
treated by the organization, their families, and 
the community (e.g., dependent versus inde-
pendent or interdependent and not valuable 
versus valuable.).
n Whether or not input from persons served is 
solicited and used.
n Whether or not the eligibility criteria of the 
organization screen out individuals with 
specific types of disabilities.
1.b.(6) Communication barriers may include the 
absence of a telecommunication device for the 
deaf (TDD) and the absence of material in a 
language or format that is understood by the 
persons served.
1.b.(8) Transportation barriers may include per-
sons being unable to reach service locations at all 
or to participate in the full range of services and 
other activities.
1.b.(9) Barriers to community integration 
include any barrier that would keep the persons 
served from returning to full participation in 
their community of choice. For example, partici-
pation in sports may be limited by the lack of a 
lift at the public swimming pool for access by 
persons served with limited mobility or the lack 
of scheduling availability of the local gym for an 
adaptive sports program, or accommodations 
may be needed for the persons served to return to 
previous volunteer activities with the community 
food bank. Another barrier to community inte-
gration might be involvement of the persons 
served in the criminal justice system, which 
could impact employment, housing, etc.
1.b.(10) Stakeholder experience surveys may 
help identify other barriers. Other barriers may 
include those raised by evolving technology, 
upkeep of previous repairs or changes, or issues 
more specific to the populations to whom the 
organization provides services.
1.K. 2. The organization implements an 
accessibility plan that:
2.K. Accessibilty
a. Reflects the diversity of:
2.a.K. Accessibilty
(1) Persons served.
2.a.(1)K. Accessibilty
(2) Personnel.
2.a.(2)K. Accessibilty
(3) Other stakeholders.
2.a.(3)K. Accessibilty

SECTION 1.K. ACCESSIBILITY
94 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
b. Includes, for identified barriers:
2.b.K. Accessibilty
(1) Actions to be taken.
2.b.(1)K. Accessibilty
(2) Timelines.
2.b.(2)K. Accessibilty
c. Is reviewed at least annually for rele -
vance, including:
2.c.K. Accessibilty
(1) Progress made in the removal of 
identified barriers.
2.c.(1)K. Accessibilty
(2) Areas needing improvement.
2.c.(2)K. Accessibilty
d. Is updated as needed.
2.d.K. Accessibilty
Intent Statements
There may be barriers identified that the organi-
zation does not have the authority or resources 
to remove; effective accommodations may be 
the appropriate action to be taken in those 
circumstances.
2.a. Through an understanding of the diversity 
of its stakeholders, the organization may reduce 
barriers to accessibility and enhance outcomes 
for the persons served.
2.b. “Identified barriers” refers to the barriers 
identified in Standard 1.K.1.
Examples
Written documentation of potential barriers to 
services exists. When identifying potential barri-
ers to services, the organization looks at barriers 
within the organization itself and in the commu-
nity, including the attitudes that its personnel and 
other stakeholders have toward persons with dis-
abilities, which may greatly impact initial and 
ongoing access to services.
2.a. Diversity may be considered in terms of cul-
ture, age, gender, sexual orientation, spiritual 
beliefs, socioeconomic status, language, race, and 
other factors that are relevant to persons served, 
personnel, and other stakeholders and that affect 
the work of the organization.
For example, an organization that serves a cul-
turally diverse population may not only address 
communication barriers, e.g., through the use of 
translators and provision of information and 
materials in different languages, but also consider 
how those cultures view health and disability to 
understand and address potential attitudinal 
barriers to accessing services. Similarly, an 
organization’s understanding of the socioeco-
nomic diversity of the community it serves may 
lead it to explore resources for transportation, 
childcare, or supplemental assistance programs 
that would support persons served in attending 
the programs/services to which they are referred.
An organization that serves diverse groups such 
as veterans or active duty military, persons who 
are involved in the criminal justice system, or 
persons who are part of the deaf community may 
incorporate information on these groups into 
personnel training on cultural competency and 
diversity.
1.K. 3. Requests for reasonable accommoda-
tions are:
3.K. Accessibilty
a. Identified.
3.a.K. Accessibilty
b. Reviewed.
3.b.K. Accessibilty
c. Decided upon.
3.c.K. Accessibilty
d. Documented.
3.d.K. Accessibilty
Intent Statements
The organization evaluates and carefully consid-
ers the merits of all requests for accommodation 
to determine whether any remedial actions are 
appropriate. 
Please see the Glossary for a definition of reason-
able accommodations.
Examples
A request for a reasonable accommodation does 
not automatically require that the organization 
meet the request. There should be a review of 
the request. How is the organization alerted to 
the need for the reasonable accommodation? 
What is the review process? Who is identified as 
responsible for approving or denying the accom-
modation request? What are the decision-making 
criteria?
When an accommodation cannot be made, the 
organization demonstrates a referral system that 
assists the persons served in the use of other 
resources that are accessible.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 95
Review Results
L. Performance 
Measurement and 
Management
Description
CARF-accredited organizations demonstrate a 
culture of accountability by developing and 
implementing performance measurement and 
management plans that produce information an 
organization can act on to improve results for 
the persons served, other stakeholders, and the 
organization itself. 
The foundation for successful performance mea-
surement and management includes:
n Leadership accountability and support.
n Mission-driven measurement.
n A focus on results achieved for the persons 
served.
n Meaningful engagement of stakeholders.
n An understanding of extenuating and influ-
encing factors that may impact performance.
n A workforce that is knowledgeable about and 
engaged in performance measurement and 
management.
n An investment in resources to implement 
performance measurement and management.
n Measurement and management of business 
functions to sustain and enhance 
the organization.
NOTE: Please refer to the Glossary for definitions of 
the following terms used throughout this section: 
person served, family/support system, stakeholders, 
performance indicator, performance target, effec
-
tiveness, efficiency, service access, validity, 
reliability, and value.
1.L. 1. The leadership demonstrates account-
ability for performance measurement 
and management in:
1.L. Performance Measurement
a. Service delivery.
1.a.L. Performance Measurement
b. Business functions.
1.b.L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
To survive and thrive in today’s environment, 
health and human services providers must 
produce value. Through service delivery and 
business practices that are ethical, state of the art, 
and durable, organizations strive to meet the 
needs of their stakeholders, sustain their pro-
grams/services, and support growth. Leadership 
accountability is demonstrated through engage-
ment in and support of the organization’s 
performance measurement and management 
activities and effective utilization of the infor-
mation produced to improve the quality of 
programs/services; make decisions; uphold the 
organization’s mission; and objectively dem-
onstrate value to persons served and their 
families/support systems, other stakeholders, 
and the organization itself.
Examples
The leadership may review established objectives, 
performance indicators, and measures to confirm 
that the information being gathered can be used 
to answer questions from a variety of stakehold-
ers, guide the organization, and identify areas for 
improvement.
There may be leadership representation on 
quality committees, performance improvement 
teams, and in meetings with stakeholders to 
gather input and suggestions about the organiza-
tion’s performance management activities and 
plans.
The leadership may hold forums to seek input 
and insight into topics that could be incorporated 
into and addressed in the performance measure-
ment and management plan.

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
96 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
There may be an ongoing, open exchange with 
internal stakeholders via an intranet to ensure 
that items measured are addressing areas that are 
critical to the organization and meeting stake-
holder needs.
There may be a variety of mechanisms used to 
exchange information with stakeholders, such as 
town hall meetings; on-line surveys; members of 
the leadership team meeting with small groups of 
persons served, personnel, or other stakeholders; 
etc.
The leadership supports and may participate with 
personnel in training on assessment tools being 
used and methods to ensure data integrity.
The governance board may establish a quality 
committee that reviews performance reports on a 
regular basis.
1.L. 2. The organization identifies gaps and 
opportunities in preparation for the 
development or review of a performance 
measurement and management plan, 
including consideration of:
2.L. Performance Measurement
a. Input from:
2.a.L. Performance Measurement
(1) Persons served.
2.a.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) Personnel.
2.a.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(3) Other stakeholders.
2.a.(3)L. Performance Measurement
b. The characteristics of the persons 
served.
2.b.L. Performance Measurement
c. Expected results.
2.c.L. Performance Measurement
d. Extenuating and influencing factors 
that may impact results.
2.d.L. Performance Measurement
e. The comparative data available.
2.e.L. Performance Measurement
f. Communication of performance 
information.
2.f.L. Performance Measurement
g. Technology to support implemen -
tation of the performance and 
measurement and management plan.
2.g.L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
An analysis of gaps and opportunities provides 
an organization with essential information in 
preparation for developing a performance mea-
surement and management plan for the first time 
or reviewing it for continued relevance and/or 
necessary changes. Input from persons served, 
personnel, and other stakeholders about what 
they want to know about the organization and its 
programs/services guides the selection of objec-
tives, performance indicators, and measures and 
positions the organization to later share perfor-
mance information that meets stakeholder 
needs. A meaningful performance measurement 
and management plan elevates an organization’s 
accountability for and knowledge and under-
standing of the results of its service delivery and 
business practices.
Through the analysis of gaps and opportunities it 
may be determined that changes are indicated 
not only in the performance measurement and 
management plan but also in areas such as 
strategic planning, person-centered practices, 
advocacy for persons served, corporate respon-
sibilities, etc.
Examples
2.b. Characteristics include a wide variety of data 
that reflect relevant information about the per-
sons served, such as age, gender, ethnicity, race, 
education levels, living arrangements, primary 
language, impairment, and activity limitation. 
In the context of performance management this 
information can be used to analyze performance 
relative to specific groups of persons served who 
share similar characteristics. 
Similarly, there are characteristics or conditions 
in the environment that may be shared by a 
group of persons or a population served. These 
are referred to as social determinants of health 
(SDOH)—conditions in the environments in 
which people are born, live, learn, work, play, and 
age that affect a wide range of health, function-
ing, and quality-of-life outcomes and risks. 
SDOH such as income and social status, employ-
ment and working conditions, education and 
literacy, physical environments, social supports 
and coping skills, health behaviors, and access 
to health services may also impact the results 
that can be achieved for the persons served by 
a program. For example, in an area where the 
unemployment rate is high, job placement for 
persons served may be more challenging or 
return-to-work rates may be lower; in an area 
where there is limited public transportation, 
persons served may have difficulty attending the 
program or following through with transition/
discharge recommendations; in an area where 
there is limited affordable housing, the discharge 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 97
or placement options for persons served may 
be limited. All of these SDOH may impact the 
results the program can achieve for the persons 
served and may also be considered in establishing 
the program’s performance targets.
2.d. Examples of extenuating or influencing fac-
tors that could impact performance include a 
change in leadership, relocation of an organiza-
tion or program, reductions in budget, personnel 
shortages, new regulations, changes in the geo-
graphic area where services are offered such as 
a housing shortage, financial challenges in the 
community such as high unemployment, poor 
public transportation, a natural disaster such as 
a hurricane or earthquake, and individuals 
entering the programs/services with more 
co-morbidities or co-occurring conditions.
1.L. 3. The organization implements a perfor-
mance measurement and management 
plan that:
3.L. Performance Measurement
a. Addresses:
3.a.L. Performance Measurement
(1) Collection of relevant data on the 
characteristics of the 
persons
 served.
3.a.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) For each program/service seek -
ing accreditation, identification 
of measures for service delivery 
objectives, including, at a 
minimum:
3.a.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(a) Results achieved for the per -
sons served (effectiveness).
3.a.(2)(a)L. Performance Measurement
(b) Experience of services 
received and other feedback 
from the persons served.
3.a.(2)(b)L. Performance Measurement
(c) Experience of services and 
other feedback from other 
stakeholders.
3.a.(2)(c)L. Performance Measurement
(d) Resources used to achieve 
results for the persons 
served (efficiency).
3.a.(2)(d)L. Performance Measurement
(e) Service access.
3.a.(2)(e)L. Performance Measurement
(3) The collection of data about the 
persons served at:
3.a.(3)L. Performance Measurement
(a) The beginning of services.
3.a.(3)(a)L. Performance Measurement
(b) Appropriate intervals during 
services.
3.a.(3)(b)L. Performance Measurement
(c) The end of services.
3.a.(3)(c)L. Performance Measurement
(d) Point(s) in time following 
services.
3.a.(3)(d)L. Performance Measurement
(4) Identification of priority 
measures determined by the 
organization for business func-
tion objectives.
3.a.(4)L. Performance Measurement
(5) The extent to which the data 
collected measure what they are 
intended to measure (validity).
3.a.(5)L. Performance Measurement
(6) The process for obtaining data:
3.a.(6)L. Performance Measurement
(a) In a consistent manner 
(reliability).
3.a.(6)(a)L. Performance Measurement
(b) That will be complete.
3.a.(6)(b)L. Performance Measurement
(c) That will be accurate.
3.a.(6)(c)L. Performance Measurement
(7) Extenuating and influencing 
factors that may impact results.
3.a.(7)L. Performance Measurement
(8) Timeframes for the:
3.a.(8)L. Performance Measurement
(a) Analysis of data.
3.a.(8)(a)L. Performance Measurement
(b) Communication of results.
3.a.(8)(b)L. Performance Measurement
(9) How:
3.a.(9)L. Performance Measurement
(a) Data are collected.
3.a.(9)(a)L. Performance Measurement
(b) Data are analyzed.
3.a.(9)(b)L. Performance Measurement
(c) Performance improvement 
plans are developed.
3.a.(9)(c)L. Performance Measurement
(d) Performance improvement 
plans are implemented.
3.a.(9)(d)L. Performance Measurement
(e) Performance information is 
communicated.
3.a.(9)(e)L. Performance Measurement
b. Is reviewed at least annually for 
relevance.
3.b.L. Performance Measurement
c. Is updated as needed.
3.c.L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
3.a.(1) Characteristics include a wide variety 
of data that reflect relevant information about 
the persons served. As data are collected and 
aggregated at the level of each program/service 
seeking accreditation, the identification and 
analysis of any significant performance differ-
ences of the program/service in serving relevant 
groups ties into later being able to target specific 
program improvements.
3.a.(4) Measurement of business functions is 
addressed in related standards throughout Sec-
tion 1, including, but not limited to, 1.F.3. and 
1.F.4. on the analysis of financial performance, 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
98 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
1.H.7. on the testing of emergency procedures, 
1.H.11. on the analysis of critical incidents, and 
1.J.4. on the analysis of formal complaints. The 
organization might identify these and/or other 
priority areas for business function measurement.
3.a.(5)–(6) It is not required that the organization 
subscribe to a proprietary data vendor in order to 
achieve data integrity.
Examples
3.a.(3)(d) Data may be collected at point(s) in 
time following services through use of a survey 
that is mailed or emailed to persons served, 
phone calls to persons served, or in-person at a 
follow-up visit to the program/service or to 
another provider to which the person was 
referred at transition/discharge.
When the reason for transition/discharge from 
the program/service is that the person served has 
died, or this is discovered after the person has left 
the program/service, follow-up efforts may focus 
on the family/support system; e.g., whether they 
were satisfied with the services the person served 
received, whether they received the support they 
desired from the program, resources the program 
can offer to support the family/support system in 
their grieving process, etc.
3.a.(4) An organization may identify priority 
business areas based on input from stakeholders 
or circumstances such as implementation of new 
program/service, opening of a new location, 
turnover in key leadership or management posi-
tions, or recent history of a natural disaster.
3.a.(5) The organization chooses indicators, 
measures, and data elements that measure what it 
intends to measure. 
The analysis of gaps and opportunities addressed 
in Standard 1.L.2. may identify changes in mea-
sures for service delivery objectives that may 
need to occur because of changes in the environ-
ment, legislative or regulatory changes, societal 
or financial changes, or development or revision 
of programs/services.
3.a.(5)–(6) There are a variety of ways an organi-
zation can demonstrate that it addresses the 
integrity of the data it uses for performance mea-
surement, management, and improvement and 
management decision making. These approaches 
can range from simple to sophisticated.
3.a.(6)(a) The organization takes steps to ensure 
that data are collected consistently in a way that 
could be reproduced at another time or by other 
data gatherers. For example:
n New and existing personnel are trained on 
recording each data element they are respon-
sible for collecting, and measures or codes are 
explained and periodically reviewed.
n Inter-rater reliability assessments can be con-
ducted in which different personnel record 
measures for the same persons served and 
data are compared statistically to assess 
whether different personnel arrive at the same 
ratings for a given individual.
n A potential training opportunity might be for 
a multi-site organization to have personnel 
from one location conduct screenings/assess
-
ments at other locations to help to increase 
the reliability of the screening/assessment 
process.
3.a.(6)(b) The organization takes steps to ensure 
that the data used for decision making are as 
complete as possible, no accredited programs are 
omitted from the information and performance 
improvement effort, no groups of persons served 
are omitted from the data gathering or analysis, 
no data elements or indicators are systematically 
missing, and any database is checked for com-
pleteness of records before final analyses are run 
and decisions made. For example:
n The quality council and data managers collab-
orate on designing an information system 
regarding the persons served that includes 
necessary data elements for all programs of 
the organization. They decide to design an 
organization-wide system, but identify each 
record with the particular program in which 
the person participates so analysis can be 
done separately for all the programs to be 
surveyed.
n Personnel training for data-recording activi-
ties includes attention to the importance of 
recording each data field for every person 
served.
n Complete information on the characteristics 
of the persons served, for example, is critical 
to being able to analyze performance related 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 99
to specific groups or populations served by a 
program.
n The data manager routinely cross checks the 
number of records of persons served in the 
database with the operations officer’s report 
of
 the number of persons served during a 
reporting period to ensure that data are avail-
able on all persons served before analysis is 
conducted and reports are generated. Missing 
records are located and entered into the data-
base before analysis is conducted.
3.a.(6)(c) The organization takes steps to ensure 
that data are recorded properly and that errors 
are caught and corrected. For example:
n Spot checks of the records of the persons 
served are made to ensure that data abstracted 
from the record are correctly entered into the 
database.
n The data manager routinely reviews the dis-
tribution of values in test data runs and asks 
the direct care personnel to double check the 
accuracy of cases that seem to be outside of 
expectations in terms of maximum or mini
-
mum values. (For example, did someone 
really stay in the program 205 days or was it 
20 days?)
1.L. 4. To measure its results achieved for the 
persons served (effectiveness), each 
program/service seeking accreditation 
documents:
4.L. Performance Measurement
a. An objective(s).
4.a.L. Performance Measurement
b. A performance indicator(s), 
including:
4.b.L. Performance Measurement
(1) To whom the indicator(s) will be 
applied.
4.b.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) The person(s)/position(s) respon -
sible for collecting the data.
4.b.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(3) The source(s) from which data 
will be collected.
4.b.(3)L. Performance Measurement
(4) Identification of relevant time -
frames for collection of data.
4.b.(4)L. Performance Measurement
(5) A performance target that is 
based on the organization’s 
performance history or estab-
lished by the organization or a 
stakeholder or is based on an 
industry benchmark.
4.b.(5)L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
In accordance with the performance measure-
ment and management plan, each program/
service seeking accreditation measures results 
achieved for the persons served (effectiveness).
4.b.(5) The establishment of a performance 
target of a level to be achieved is critical. Some 
organizations develop their own targets by 
reviewing their historical performance in an 
identified indicator while other organizations 
may use standardized tools that already have an 
established benchmark. A review of the literature 
and published practices is helpful in this process. 
The development of a performance target 
ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.
When an organization first implements a perfor-
mance indicator, it may not have a performance 
history upon which to base a performance target. 
It may also lack an industry benchmark or a per-
formance target established by a stakeholder. In 
this case, the organization establishes its own 
performance target and measures over time to 
establish its history. Typically, the performance 
target established is maintained for a period of 
time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-
mance in critical areas. If it is meeting all of its 
performance targets over a period of time the 
organization will want to consider whether the 
targets should be adjusted as well as whether 
there are other indicators that would be mean-
ingful to measure.
Examples
4.b.(4) Measuring change over time is inherent in 
the measurement of results achieved for the per-
sons served. Data collected at the beginning of 
services, at specific intervals during services, at 
the end of services, and/or at a point(s) in time 
following services allow the program/service to 
measure change over time. Examples of effective-
ness measures include:

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
100 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n Maintenance of abstinence.
n Community integration.
n Reduction or elimination of incidence of 
relapse.
n Reduction or elimination of negative involve-
ment with the criminal justice system.
n Improvement of physical health.
n Improvement in school functioning.
n Reduction of hospitalization.
n Reduction of symptoms.
n Increase in level of psychological functioning.
n Increase in self-esteem.
n Home visitation completion rates.
n Reduction of reported interventions by the 
program.
n Decreased episodes of anger.
n Reduction or elimination of the prevalence of 
a prevention target.
n Number, duration, and frequency of symp-
tomatic and/or asymptomatic behaviors.
n Involvement in activities of daily living.
n Employment status.
n Community tenure.
n Housing status.
n Receipt of entitlement benefits.
n Quality of relationships.
n Health status.
n Subjective psychological well-being.
n If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT), participation in virtual support groups.

received and other feedback from the 
persons served, each program/service 
seeking accreditation documents:

5.a.L. Performance Measurement
b. A performance indicator(s), 
including:
5.b.L. Performance Measurement
(1) To whom the indicator(s) will be 
applied.
5.b.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) The person(s)/position(s) respon -
sible for collecting the data.
5.b.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(3) The source(s) from which data 
will be collected.
5.b.(3)L. Performance Measurement
(4) Identification of relevant time -
frames for collection of data.
5.b.(4)L. Performance Measurement
(5) A performance target that is 
based on the organization’s per-
formance history or established 
by the organization or a stake-
holder or is based on an industry 
benchmark.

Intent Statements
In accordance with the performance measure-
ment and management plan, each program/
service seeking accreditation measures the

feedback from the persons served.
5.b.(5) The establishment of a performance

may use standardized tools that already have an

and published practices is helpful in this process.

ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.

mance indicator, it may not have a performance

formance target established by a stakeholder. In 
this case, the organization establishes its own

time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-
mance in critical areas. If it is meeting all of its

there are other indicators that would be mean-

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 101
Examples
The perception of the persons served regarding 
their experience with the program/service 
provides valuable feedback on how well the pro-
gram/service is meeting or met their expectations 
in areas such as service responsiveness, respect, 
informed choice, participation, and overall value. 
For example:
n W as the person served treated with dignity 
and respect?
n Did the program focus on recovery for the 
person served?
n Were grievances or concerns addressed?
n Overall feelings of satisfaction.
n Use of informed choices about modes of treat-
ment, medications, etc.
n Satisfaction with physical facilities, fees, 
access, and results achieved.
n W as the person served given hope?
n The program demonstrated respect for and 
understanding of my culture.
n If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT):
– Satisfaction with connectivity/quality of 
platform.
– Convenience of days and times that ser -
vices/appointments were available. 
– The services I received through the use of 
ICT met my needs.
– When services were provided using ICT 
did you find them equal to or better than 
services provided face to face?
The tools used to measure the experience of ser-
vices received and other feedback are appropriate 
for the population served in terms of age, devel-
opmental level, language, and culture. 
Programs/services that serve children often 
measure experience of services and other feed-
back from the perspective of members of the 
family/support system. When analyzing the data 
gathered, it is important for the program to dis-
tinguish whether the data reflect the feedback of 
the family/support system or feedback that was 
provided on behalf of the child/youth.

each program/service seeking accredita
-
tion documents:

other stakeholders.

organizations develop their own targets by

identified indicator while other organizations 
may use standardized tools that already have an 
established benchmark. A review of the literature 
and published practices is helpful in this process. 
The development of a performance target 
ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.

mance indicator, it may not have a performance

It may also lack an industry benchmark or a per-

this case, the organization establishes its own

establish its history. Typically, the performance 
target established is maintained for a period of 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
102 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-

Examples
The perception of stakeholders regarding their 
experience with the program/service provides 
valuable feedback on how well the program/
service is meeting or met their expectations. 
For example:
n The information I received from the program 
met my needs.
n The information I received from the program 
was useful in my decision making.
n I received information from the program in a 
timeframe that met my needs.
n I am satisfied with the opportunities available 
to interact with the team serving my family 
member.
n I am satisfied with the services/care my family 
member received from the program.
n I would recommend this program to others.
n I would refer again to this program.
n I am satisfied with the timeframe for admis-
sion to services for persons referred.
n I am satisfied with the communication mech-
anisms to coordinate care for persons served.
n If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT):
– I am satisfied with the options offered by 
the organization for service delivery 
through the use of ICT.
– The services delivered by the organization 
through the use of ICT will positively 
impact the lives of the persons served.
1.L. 7. To measure the resources used to 
achieve results for the persons served 
(efficiency), each program/service seek
-
ing accreditation documents:
7.L. Performance Measurement
a. An objective(s).
7.a.L. Performance Measurement
b. A performance indicator(s), 
including:
7.b.L. Performance Measurement
(1) To whom or what the indicator(s) 
will be applied.
7.b.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) The person(s)/position(s) respon -
sible for collecting the data.
7.b.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(3) The source(s) from which data 
will be collected.

(4) Identification of relevant time -
frames for collection of data.
7.b.(4)L. Performance Measurement
(5) A performance target that is 
based on the organization’s per-
formance history or established 
by the organization or a stake
-
holder or is based on an industry 
benchmark.
7.b.(5)L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
In accordance with the performance measure-
ment and management plan, each program/
service seeking accreditation measures the 
resources used to achieve results for the persons 
served (efficiency).
7.b.(5) The establishment of a performance 
target of a level to be achieved is critical. Some 
organizations develop their own targets by 
reviewing their historical performance in an 
identified indicator while other organizations 
may use standardized tools that already have an 
established benchmark. A review of the literature 
and published practices is helpful in this process. 
The development of a performance target 
ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.
When an organization first implements a perfor-
mance indicator, it may not have a performance 
history upon which to base a performance target. 
It may also lack an industry benchmark or a per-
formance target established by a stakeholder. In 
this case, the organization establishes its own 
performance target and measures over time to 
establish its history. Typically, the performance 
target established is maintained for a period of 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 103
time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-
mance in critical areas. If it is meeting all of its 
performance targets over a period of time the 
organization will want to consider whether the 
targets should be adjusted as well as whether 
there are other indicators that would be mean-
ingful to measure.
Examples
7.b. Efficiency measures address the relationship 
between resources used and results achieved. 
Resources may include time, money, personnel, 
etc. For example:
n Service delivery cost per service unit.
n Occupancy rates.
n Direct service hours of clinical staff.
n Personnel turnover.
n Length of stay.
n Service utilization.
n Retention rates.
n Percent of staff time spent on direct clinical 
care.
n Average caseload size.
n Number/types of services to achieve func-
tional improvement.
n Intake to engagement in care.
n If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT):
– Number or percentage of sessions that 
started on time, were completed as sched-
uled, were delayed/cancelled/rescheduled 
due to technical issues.
– Retention in services was the same or 
better than in-person services.
– The cost of service delivery compared to 
in-person services.
– The cost of service delivery versus reim -
bursement compared to in-person 
services.

documents:

sible for collecting the data.

will be collected.

frames for collection of data.

(5) A performance target that is 
based on the organization’s per-

by the organization or a stake-

benchmark.
8.b.(5)L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
In accordance with the performance measure-
ment and management plan, each program/
service seeking accreditation measures service 
access.
8.b.(5) The establishment of a performance 
target of a level to be achieved is critical. Some 
organizations develop their own targets by 
reviewing their historical performance in an 
identified indicator while other organizations 
may use standardized tools that already have an 
established benchmark. A review of the literature 
and published practices is helpful in this process. 
The development of a performance target 
ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.
When an organization first implements a perfor-
mance indicator, it may not have a performance 
history upon which to base a performance target. 
It may also lack an industry benchmark or a per-
formance target established by a stakeholder. In 
this case, the organization establishes its own 
performance target and measures over time to 
establish its history. Typically, the performance 
target established is maintained for a period of 
time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
104 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-
mance in critical areas. If it is meeting all of its 
performance targets over a period of time the 
organization will want to consider whether the 
targets should be adjusted as well as whether 
there are other indicators that would be mean-
ingful to measure.
Examples
8.b. Service access addresses the organization’s 
capacity to provide services to those who desire 
or are in need of receiving services. For example:
n Convenience of service hours and locations.
n Acceptance rate of participants into the 
program.
n Meaningful engagement in care.
n Waiting time for routine or emergency care.
n Time taken to set a first or subsequent 
appointment.
n Waiting list information on persons found 
ineligible for services.
n Telephone response time or abandonment 
rates.
n The success of formal referral mechanisms.
n Information and materials available to per-
sons served in their preferred language and 
through their preferred mode.
n If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT):
– Increased engagement of families/support 
systems in services/decision making/
education.
– Increased availability/convenience of 
appointments for the persons served.
– Service delivery via the use of ICT resulted 
in additional convenience to the persons 
served, e.g., fewer missed appointments, 
missed episodes of service provision, less 
time away from work.
1.L. 9. To measure its business function, the 
organization documents:
9.L. Performance Measurement
a. Objectives in priority areas deter -
mined by the organization.
9.a.L. Performance Measurement
b. For each objective, a performance 
indicator(s), including:
9.b.L. Performance Measurement
(1) To what the indicator(s) will be 
applied.
9.b.(1)L. Performance Measurement
(2) The person(s)/position(s) respon -
sible for collecting the data.
9.b.(2)L. Performance Measurement
(3) The source(s) from which data 
will be collected.
9.b.(3)L. Performance Measurement
(4) Identification of relevant time -
frames for collection of data.
9.b.(4)L. Performance Measurement
(5) A performance target that is 
based on the organization’s 
performance history or estab
-
lished by the organization or a 
stakeholder or that is based on 
an industry benchmark.
9.b.(5)L. Performance Measurement
Intent Statements
Measurement of business functions is addressed 
in related standards throughout Section 1, includ-
ing but not limited to, 1.F.4. on the analysis of 
financial performance, 1.H.7. on the testing of 
emergency procedures, 1.H.11. on the analysis 
of critical incidents, and 1.J.4. on the analysis of 
formal complaints. The organization might iden-
tify these and/or other priority areas for business 
function measurement.
9.b.(5) The establishment of a performance 
target of a level to be achieved is critical. Some 
organizations develop their own targets by 
reviewing their historical performance in an 
identified indicator while other organizations 
may use standardized tools that already have an 
established benchmark. A review of the literature 
and published practices is helpful in this process. 
The development of a performance target 
ensures that there will be action for improvement 
if the target is not met.
When an organization first implements a perfor-
mance indicator, it may not have a performance 
history upon which to base a performance target. 
It may also lack an industry benchmark or a per-
formance target established by a stakeholder. In 
this case, the organization establishes its own 
performance target and measures over time to 

SECTION 1.L. PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 105
establish its history. Typically, the performance 
target established is maintained for a period of 
time in order to establish a foundation rather 
than changing it each cycle of analysis.
Through its performance measurement and 
management activities, an organization gains 
an accurate and complete picture of its perfor-
mance in critical areas. If it is meeting all of its 
performance targets over a period of time the 
organization will want to consider whether the 
targets should be adjusted as well as whether 
there are other indicators that would be mean-
ingful to measure.
Examples
In the area of financial planning and manage-
ment, the organization might identify indicators 
related to expenses, revenues, or other budgetary 
information; financial reliance on related entities; 
its review of billing against records of the persons 
served; collections on bills for services rendered; 
or denial rates for billed services.
In the area of accessibility status reports, the 
organization might identify an indicator related 
to implementation of its accessibility plan and 
progress made in the removal of barriers.
In the area of risk management, an indicator 
might be identified related to the organization’s 
actions to reduce risks, success of a fall preven-
tion program, remediation plans addressing 
financial stability, complaints received, 
or medication errors.
Human resource indicators might be identified 
for recruitment, retention, or personnel turnover 
or the provision of training to personnel.
Health and safety indicators might relate to the 
analysis of unannounced tests of emergency pro-
cedures, critical incidents, infection control, or 
timeliness of response to maintenance requests 
for repairs found during external or internal 
inspections.
If services were provided through the use of 
information and communications technology 
(ICT), indicators might address additional hours 
of technical support needed, expenses related 
to the purchase or upgrade of software and/or 
hardware, or changes in staffing to support ser-
vice delivery related to the revenue generated.
1.L. 10. Personnel are provided with docu-
mented education and training in 
accordance with their roles and respon-
sibilities for performance measurement 
and management.
10.L. Performance Measurement
Examples
Personnel may have a variety of roles and respon-
sibilities in implementing the organization’s 
performance measurement and management 
plan, including:
n Completing assessment tools from which data 
are gathered.
n Collecting data.
n Analyzing data.
n Participating on performance improvement 
teams or committees.
n Identifying or revising objectives, perfor-
mance indicators, and performance targets.
n Establishing methods to communicate 
performance information to persons served, 
personnel, and other stakeholders.
n Training and/or mentoring peers on the 
importance of using performance 
information.
n Working in a quality department that has 
overall responsibility for performance man
-
agement and quality.



2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 107
Effect Change
M. Performance 
Improvement
Description
CARF-accredited organizations demonstrate a 
culture of performance improvement through 
their commitment to proactive and ongoing 
review, analysis, reflection on their results in both 
service delivery and business functions, and 
transparency. The results of performance analysis 
are used to identify and implement data-driven 
actions to improve the quality of programs and 
services and to inform decision making. Per-
formance information that is accurate and 
understandable to the target audience is shared 
with persons served, personnel, and other stake-
holders in accordance with their interests and 
needs.
1.M. 1. The analysis of service delivery 
performance:
1.M. Performance Improvement
a. Is documented.
1.a.M. Performance Improvement
b. Is completed at least annually and 
in accordance with the timeframes 
outlined in the performance mea-
surement and management plan.
1.b.M. Performance Improvement
c. Addresses service delivery indicators 
for each program/service seeking 
accreditation, including, at a 
minimum:
1.c.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Results achieved for the persons 
served (effectiveness).
1.c.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Experience of services received 
and other feedback from the 
persons
 served.
1.c.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Experience of services and 
other feedback from other 
stakeholders.
1.c.(3)M. Performance Improvement
(4) Resources used to achieve results 
for the persons served 
(efficiency).
1.c.(4)M. Performance Improvement
(5) Service access.
1.c.(5)M. Performance Improvement
d. If applicable, addresses service deliv -
ery indicators at the level of each 
specialty program, specialty designa-
tion, or special population for which 
the organization is seeking accredita
-
tion, including at a minimum:
1.d.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Results achieved for the persons 
served (effectiveness).
1.d.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Experience of services received 
and other feedback from the 
persons served.
1.d.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Experience of services and other 
feedback from other 
stakeholders.
1.d.(3)M. Performance Improvement
(4) Resources used to achieve results 
for the persons served 
(efficiency).
1.d.(4)M. Performance Improvement
(5) Service access.
1.d.(5)M. Performance Improvement
e. Incorporates the:
1.e.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Characteristics of the persons 
served.
1.e.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Impact of extenuating or influ -
encing factors.
1.e.(2)M. Performance Improvement
f. Includes:
1.f.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Comparative analysis.
1.f.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Identification of trends.
1.f.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Identification of causes.
1.f.(3)M. Performance Improvement
g. Is used to:
1.g.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Identify areas needing perfor -
mance improvement.
1.g.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Develop an action plan(s) to 
address the improvements 
needed.
1.g.(2)M. Performance Improvement

SECTION 1.M. PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT
108 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
(3) Implement the action plan(s).
1.g.(3)M. Performance Improvement
(4) Determine whether the actions 
taken accomplished the intended 
results.
1.g.(4)M. Performance Improvement
Intent Statements
Through implementation of the standards in 
Section 1.L. Performance Measurement and Man-
agement, the organization establishes its 
framework for performance measurement and 
management, including the identification of 
objectives, performance indicators, and perfor-
mance targets related to service delivery. 
Analyzing each performance indicator in relation 
to its target, including consideration of the char-
acteristics of the persons served and extenuating 
or influencing factors that may impact perform-
ance, provides the organization with information 
on areas meeting or exceeding targets and areas 
in need of improvement. An action plan for 
improvement can then be developed in accord-
ance with the organization’s priorities, resources, 
and other considerations. 
The analysis of performance at least annually 
provides information on value and satisfaction 
to the persons served and other stakeholders.
The performance analysis reviews data aggre-
gated at a program/service level for each 
program/service seeking accreditation in order 
that the action plan(s) can target improvements 
at the individual program/service level.
1.d. When a program/service seeks accreditation 
in combination with any of the specialty pro-
grams in Section 4 of the Aging Services, 
Continuing Care Retirement Community, or 
Medical Rehabilitation Standards Manuals; 
specialty designations in Section 5 of the Behav-
ioral Health, Child and Youth Services, or Opioid 
Treatment Program Standards Manual; or specific 
population designations/enhancements in 
Section 5 of the Employment and Community 
Services Standards Manual, it is expected that 
the analysis of service delivery indicators be 
conducted at the level of those designations.
1.M. 2. The analysis of business function 
performance:
2.M. Performance Improvement
a. Is documented.
2.a.M. Performance Improvement
b. Is completed at least annually and 
in accordance with the timeframes 
outlined in the performance mea-
surement and management plan.
2.b.M. Performance Improvement
c. Addresses priority business function 
indicators determined by the 
organization.
2.c.M. Performance Improvement
d. Incorporates the:
2.d.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Characteristics of the persons 
served, if applicable.
2.d.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Impact of extenuating or influ -
encing factors.
2.d.(2)M. Performance Improvement
e. Includes:
2.e.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Comparative analysis.
2.e.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Identification of trends.
2.e.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Identification of causes.
2.e.(3)M. Performance Improvement
f. Is used to:
2.f.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Identify areas needing perfor -
mance improvement.
2.f.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Develop an action plan(s) to 
address the improvements 
needed.
2.f.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Implement the action plan(s).
2.f.(3)M. Performance Improvement
(4) Determine whether the actions 
taken accomplished the intended 
results.
2.f.(4)M. Performance Improvement
Intent Statements
Through implementation of the standards in 
Section 1.L. the organization establishes its 
framework for performance measurement and 
management, including the identification of 
objectives, performance indicators, and perfor-
mance targets related to business functions. 
Analyzing each performance indicator in relation 
to its target, including consideration of extenuat-
ing or influencing factors that may impact 
performance and, if applicable, the characteristics 
of the persons served, provides the organization 
with information on areas meeting or exceeding 
targets and areas in need of improvement. An 
action plan for improvement can then be devel-
oped in accordance with the organization’s 
priorities, resources, and other considerations.

SECTION 1.M. PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 109
The analysis of performance at least annually 
provides information to aid in the strategic posi-
tioning of the organization and builds a stronger 
business foundation and engaged workforce.
1.M. 3. The results of performance analysis are 
used to:
3.M. Performance Improvement
a. Improve the quality of programs and 
services.
3.a.M. Performance Improvement
b. Facilitate organizational decision 
making regarding:
3.b.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Service delivery.
3.b.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Business functions.
3.b.(2)M. Performance Improvement
c. Guide changes to the performance 
measurement and management plan.
3.c.M. Performance Improvement
Intent Statements
CARF-accredited organizations use a fact-based 
decision-making process to identify and respond 
to organizational and stakeholder needs. 
Although not every performance indicator that 
is measured and analyzed may be acted on, the 
information gleaned from the analysis allows for 
a fact-based approach to decision making, plan-
ning, and performance improvement.
Examples
The organization demonstrates:
n Knowledge of the operational status of the 
organization, the business strategies it 
employs to be successful, and how perfor
-
mance improvement is utilized at all levels 
of
 the organization.
n How it makes decisions to expand, remodel, 
open new sites, develop new services, modify 
a treatment or service approach, or change 
staffing patterns.
n How it responds to or prepares for changes 
in
 regulations, reimbursement, or corporate 
structure.
n Methods for reaching these decisions, which 
may include reviews of information, perform
-
ance management reports, budgets, strategic 
plans, input forums, gap analyses, and satis-
faction surveys.
1.M. 4. In accordance with the performance 
measurement and management plan, 
the organization communicates accu
-
rate performance information:
4.M. Performance Improvement
a. To:
4.a.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Persons served.
4.a.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Personnel.
4.a.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Other stakeholders.
4.a.(3)M. Performance Improvement
b. According to the needs of the specific 
group, including:
4.b.M. Performance Improvement
(1) Content.
4.b.(1)M. Performance Improvement
(2) Format.
4.b.(2)M. Performance Improvement
(3) Timing.
4.b.(3)M. Performance Improvement
Intent Statements
Sharing performance information with internal 
and external stakeholders is a vital aspect of 
continuously improving the programs/services of 
the organization. CARF-accredited organizations 
realize the importance of transparency and shar-
ing performance information that is relevant and 
accurate with the persons served, personnel, and 
other stakeholders. Stakeholder input guides the 
content, format, and timing of the information 
shared so that it meets the needs of stakeholders 
both internal and external to the organization.
Examples
The content of the information shared is tailored 
to the needs of the audience and what the specific 
stakeholder group would like to know about the 
programs/services. The information is presented 
in a format and language that is understandable 
to those with whom it is shared. Format may vary 
for different stakeholder groups. For example, 
performance information could be communi-
cated through:
n Annual reports.
n Press releases.
n Newsletters.
n Narrative reports.
n Bulleted summaries or fact sheets.
n Pie charts.
n Bar graphs.
n Balanced scorecards.
Information could be mailed, emailed, posted 
on the organization’s website, displayed in a 

SECTION 1.M. PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT
110 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
reception area, audio or video recorded, or pre-
sented in person. To further meet the needs of 
stakeholders, information may be provided in a 
large font size, using more graphics than text, in 
various languages, in Braille, or utilizing caption-
ing or interpreters.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 111
SECTION 2
General Program Standards
Description
For an organization to achieve quality services, 
the persons served are active participants in the 
planning, prioritization, implementation, and 
ongoing evaluation of the services offered. A 
commitment to quality and the involvement of 
the persons served span the entire time that 
the persons served are involved with the org-
anization. The service planning process is 
individualized, establishing goals and objectives 
that incorporate the unique strengths, needs, 
abilities, and preferences of the persons served. 
The persons served have the opportunity to 
transition easily through a system of care.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
116 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
A. Program/Service 
Structure 
Description
A fundamental responsibility of the organization 
is to provide a comprehensive program structure. 
The staffing is designed to maximize opportuni-
ties for the persons served to obtain and 
participate in the services provided.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable 
Standards on page 112 to determine how these 
standards will be applied to the core program or ser-
vice areas for which you are seeking accreditation.
2.A. 1. Each program/service:
1.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Documents the following parameters 
regarding its scope of services:
1.a.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Population(s) served.
1.a.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Settings.
1.a.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Hours of services.
1.a.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
(4) Days of services.
1.a.(4)A. Structure and Staffing
(5) Frequency of services.
1.a.(5)A. Structure and Staffing
(6) Payers and funding sources.
1.a.(6)A. Structure and Staffing
(7) Fees.
1.a.(7)A. Structure and Staffing
(8) Referral sources.
1.a.(8)A. Structure and Staffing
(9) The specific services offered, 
including whether the services 
are provided directly, by contract, 
or by referral.
1.a.(9)A. Structure and Staffing
(10) If the program delivers services 
via information and communica-
tion technologies:
1.a.(10)A. Structure and Staffing
(a) Geographic areas served.
1.a.(10)(a)A. Structure and Staffing
(b) The communication technol -
ogy used to deliver services.
1.a.(10)(b)A. Structure and Staffing
b. Shares relevant information about 
the scope of services with:
1.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) The persons served.
1.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Families/support systems, in 
accordance with the choices of 
the persons served.
1.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Referral sources.
1.b.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
(4) Payers and funding sources.
1.b.(4)A. Structure and Staffing
(5) Other relevant stakeholders.
1.b.(5)A. Structure and Staffing
(6) The general public.
1.b.(6)A. Structure and Staffing
c. Reviews the scope of services at least 
annually.
1.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Updates the scope as necessary.
1.d.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The scope is defined at the level of the program/
service. It is not expected that the program/
service shares all information listed in 1.a. with 
every stakeholder, but that it provides the per-
sons served, families/support systems, referral 
sources, payers and funding sources, and other 
stakeholders with relevant information that helps 
them understand what the program/service has 
to offer and determine whether it will meet the 
needs of the persons served. If the program is 
part of a continuum of services, the scope is 
defined for each program or specialty program 
within the continuum.
1.a.(10) Service delivery using information and 
communication technologies is addressed in 
Section 2.I. of this manual.
Examples
Many organizations may incorporate this infor-
mation in the program description required in 
Standard 2.A.11.
1.a.(10) A program/service that uses information 
and communication technologies (ICT) to 
deliver services, whether for selected services 
or exclusively, includes this information in the 
documentation of its scope.
1.a.(10)(a) The program documents and shares 
information on the geographic areas it serves at 
the level of detail needed by stakeholders to con-
sider whether the program can meet their needs. 
This may be locale or states/provinces or other 
jurisdictions related to access/convenience by 
persons served, credentialing of providers, or 
billing for services.
1.a.(10)(b) The communication technology used 
to deliver services may be described in terms 
such as phone call, live chat, text, Zoom, video 
conference, and platforms.
1.b. Training programs often use websites to post 
information regarding fees as well as hours and 
days of classes.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 117
2.A. 2. The organization provides the resources 
needed to support the overall scope of 
each program/service.
2.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The ability to provide the program/services 
defined in the scope statement is evidenced by 
adequate materials, equipment, supplies, space, 
finances, training, and human resources.
Examples
The program has the facilities, space, materials, 
and staffing to provide the proper amount of care 
for the proper length of time based on the needs 
of the persons served.
Resources may include confidential interview 
rooms if face-to-face counseling is provided or 
large space if the program includes the use of 
group activities. Training programs often provide 
or give access to study space, texts, and web-
based resources to support the curriculum.
2.A. 3. Based on the scope of each program/
service provided, the organization 
documents its:
3.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Entry criteria.
3.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Transition criteria, if applicable.
3.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Exit criteria.
3.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The organization determines which persons it is 
qualified and able to serve and identifies condi-
tions/time/events for transition and/or exit. This 
includes transitions to other levels of care/ser-
vices as well as transitions within a program/
service. Transition criteria may also address con-
tinuing stay criteria. Transition may not always 
occur based on the nature of the program/
service.
Examples
Although a program/service may use terms that 
are different than those above, the concepts are 
the same. The program may develop their own 
criteria or base their criteria on best practices 
within the field including: diagnoses, ASAM 
Level of Care Criteria, medical necessity, or 
Children’s Global Assessment of Functioning.
3.a. Entry criteria may also be called admission 
criteria, enrollment criteria, or move-in criteria.
Entry criteria regarding admission and readmis-
sion should be clearly written, should be adhered 
to, and should consist of how to prioritize admis-
sions, decision-making responsibilities, and what 
would cause a person seeking services to be 
excluded or found ineligible.
When this determination is formalized and in 
writing, it significantly minimizes subjectivity 
during the screening or admission process.
Clearly written and defined admission criteria 
reduce the need to exercise subjective judgment 
in making a decision regarding whether a partic-
ular program is applicable to a person’s needs.
Training programs often outline the specific 
admission criteria such as prerequisite course-
work, minimum grades, and preferred 
background.
The criteria address both the initial admission of 
a person served and subsequent readmissions.
3.b.–c. Transition criteria may also be called 
referral, aftercare, or continuing care criteria or 
guidelines. Exit criteria may also be called agree-
ment, contract termination, criteria graduation, 
or discharge criteria.
Written transition and discharge criteria are 
established and are used in such documents as 
program descriptions, admission/readmission 
criteria, or other documents.
2.A. 4. When a person is found ineligible for 
services:
4.A. Structure and Staffing
a. The person is informed as to the 
reasons.
4.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. In accordance with the choice of the 
person:
4.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) The family/support system is 
informed as to the reasons.
4.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) The referral source is informed 
as to the reasons.
4.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
c. Recommendations are made for 
alternative services.
4.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Examples
Persons who are found to be ineligible for 
services are given the reasons and directed 
to alternative or more appropriate services. 

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
118 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
4.a. Informing persons as to why they are ineligi-
ble helps them to locate and access appropriate 
services to meet their needs.
4.b. In some situations, the referral source is pro-
viding the information for the screening and will 
be informed as to reasons for ineligibility without 
specific consent.
4.c. When an individual is not accepted into a 
training program, suggestions are offered to 
improve the person’s future successful admission.
2.A. 5. Service delivery models and strategies 
are based on accepted practice in the 
field and incorporate current research, 
evidence-based practice, peer-reviewed 
scientific and health-related publica
-
tions, clinical practice guidelines, and/or 
expert professional consensus.
5.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The service delivery model and the strategies 
used are based on accepted practice, including 
consideration of areas such as information on 
the efficacy of specific techniques, pertinent 
research findings, protocols published by various 
professional groups, or approaches receiving 
professional recognition for achieving successful 
outcomes.
Examples
The organization uses field-recognized practices 
and, ideally, adopts evidence-based or research-
supported practices where the evidence and 
research are sound.
Some interventions may be more commonly 
accepted by a particular culture or supported 
by evidence as more effective when used with 
specific populations or to treat certain disabilities 
or disorders. For example, alcohol and other 
substance use disorder programs will not prevent 
access to medications for the treatment of opioid 
use disorder (MOUD) based solely on their treat-
ment philosophy. If persons served would benefit 
from MOUD and a program does not offer such, 
then a referral will be made.
Evidence of conformance to this standard may 
be demonstrated through minutes of meetings 
in which these topics were discussed, literature 
available to the personnel in a program library, 
development of treatment guidelines, etc. 
Resources used in this process might include 
journal subscriptions, online access to learning 
opportunities and reference materials or journals, 
guest speakers, sponsoring educational events at 
the organization, in-service programs, journal 
clubs, collaborative resources, or education 
efforts with other area providers of services.
2.A. 6. To facilitate integrated service delivery, 
each program/service implements com
-
munication mechanisms regarding the 
person served that:
6.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Address:
6.a.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Emergent issues.
6.a.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Ongoing issues.
6.a.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Continuity of services, including:
6.a.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
(a) Contingency planning.
6.a.(3)(a)A. Structure and Staffing
(b) Future planning.
6.a.(3)(b)A. Structure and Staffing
(4) Decisions concerning the person 
served.
6.a.(4)A. Structure and Staffing
b. Ensure the exchange of information 
regarding the person-centered plan.
6.b.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
This standard addresses the need for timely com-
munication to ensure that services and programs 
are consistently provided, whether provided 
24 hours a day, 7 days a week or on a part-time, 
scheduled basis.
Examples
Communication mechanisms may include 
written communication; face-to-face or virtual 
meetings; electronic medical records, or other 
electronic means.
2.A. 7. The program/service demonstrates:
7.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Knowledge of the legal decision-
making authority of the persons 
served.
7.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. When applicable, the provision of 
information to the persons served 
regarding resources related to legal 
decision-making authority.
7.b.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The persons served may not have the capacity or 
be of the age to make decisions in their own best 

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 119
interests. An individual may need to be assigned 
to make decisions regarding healthcare choices, 
financial decisions, or life care planning. Legal 
terminology may vary from jurisdiction to juris-
diction; e.g., healthcare power of attorney, power 
of attorney, and guardianship. The program/
service should be able to discuss how it addresses 
the issue of the legal decision-making authority 
of the persons served.
7.b. Any limitation on a person’s legal decision-
making authority should be continued only as 
long as is appropriate and necessary. The pro-
gram/service assists the person served and 
family/support system to access resources, such 
as attorneys with expertise in this area, who can 
assist with facilitating changes, if appropriate, in 
legal autonomy status.
2.A. 8. If the program holds funds for the per-
sons served, it implements written 
procedures that address:
8.A. Structure and Staffing
a. How the persons served can access 
their funds.
8.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Safeguards in place to:
8.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Segregate funds for individual 
persons served.
8.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Ensure that funds are used for the 
designated purposes.
8.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
c. The provision of information to 
persons served on the status of their 
funds:
8.c.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) At a frequency determined by 
the program.
8.c.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Including:
8.c.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(a) Funds spent.
8.c.(2)(a)A. Structure and Staffing
(b) Funds remaining.
8.c.(2)(b)A. Structure and Staffing
d. How funds will be returned to the 
persons served upon transition/exit 
from the program.
8.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. Communication of these procedures 
to the persons served.
8.e.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
If a program holds funds such as cash; checks; or 
credit, debit, or gift cards as a courtesy to persons 
served for their personal use during the program, 
it has a system in place to safeguard those funds 
and make them available to the persons served 
for the intended purposes.
2.A. 9. If the organization has related entities, it:
9.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Documents the types of 
relationships.
9.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Makes information regarding its 
relationships with related entities 
available to:
9.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Persons served.
9.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Families/support systems.
9.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Other relevant stakeholders.
9.b.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Disclosure of relationships demonstrates an 
organization’s commitment to transparency. 
Information is made available to support decision 
making on the part of persons served, families/
support systems, and other stakeholders regard-
ing entry, transition, or exit from a program.
Examples
Examples of relationships include:
n Parent-subsidiary structures.
n Affiliations.
n Alliances.
n Guarantees.
n Joint ventures.
n Limited partnerships.
n Management agreements.
n Other third-party operating support.
n Financial support from related foundations.
There are a variety of ways that information 
regarding relationships with related entities may 
be shared with different stakeholders. Websites, 
videos, informational brochures, or facility tours 
may be used to communicate this information 
with persons served and families/support sys-
tems, while marketing efforts, in-person business 
forums, or educational events may be used to 
share information with other stakeholders such 
as referral sources and business partners.
