2.A. 10. When services are provided from or 
within a mobile unit, written procedures 
are implemented that address, at a 
minimum, the unique aspects of the 
following areas related to mobile 
settings:
10.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Responsibilities of:
10.a.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Drivers.
10.a.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Service providers.
10.a.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
b. Confidentiality of:
10.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Records of persons served.
10.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Communication.
10.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
c. Privacy related to service delivery.
10.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Accessibility.
10.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. Availability of information on 
resources to address needs unable 
to be met at the mobile setting.
10.e.A. Structure and Staffing
f. Security of:
10.f.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Medications provided from or 
within the mobile unit, when 
applicable.
10.f.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Equipment and supplies used in 
service provision.
10.f.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) The mobile unit when not in use.
10.f.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
g. Safety of:
10.g.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Records of persons served.
10.g.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Personnel.
10.g.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
h. Maintenance of:
10.h.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Equipment.
10.h.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Vehicles.
10.h.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Mobile unit services are services provided from a 
vehicle such as a motor home or van that func-
tions as a site for the program/service seeking 
accreditation.
Examples
10.b. Written procedures address confidentiality 
related to the use of mobile technology for docu-
mentation and telephonic communication about 
the persons served.
10.d. The mobile unit:
n Provides adequate space for persons served to 
approach and move around inside of it.
n Is equipped with a ramp, handrails, and adap-
tive equipment for use by personnel and/or 
persons served.
n Operates from a location where there is ample 
parking.
n Operates from a location that limits exposure 
to the sun and noise in the environment such 
as traffic noise.
10.f.(3) Security of the mobile unit when it is not 
in use might address the location where the unit 
is parked overnight and/or between stops, lock-
ing the unit, protection of records, and the use 
of security personnel or surveillance systems to 
monitor the unit.
10.g. Safety considerations might include com-
munication systems available, availability of 
emergency procedures in the mobile unit, what 
to do in the event of an emergency situation, 
determination of the location where the mobile 
unit provides services, and minimum personnel 
that must be present during hours of operation.
10.h. Maintenance of mobile units might include 
keeping logs of mileage, gasoline use, oil changes, 
and tire wear.
2.A. 11. Each core program for which the organi-
zation is seeking accreditation has a 
written program description that guides 
the delivery of services and includes:
11.A. Structure and Staffing
a. A description of the program.
11.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. The philosophy of the program.
11.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Program goals.
11.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Service/treatment modalities to be 
provided to achieve the 
program objectives, including:
11.d.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Description of the service/
treatment modalities.
11.d.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) The credentials of staff qualified 
to provide the service/treatment 
modalities.
11.d.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
e. Identification or a description of 
special populations and mechanisms 
to address their needs.
11.e.A. Structure and Staffing

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 121
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to clearly define, in 
writing, how service delivery is accomplished. 
Description would include broad strategies to be 
used to achieve objectives and the rationale for 
the choice of service modality(ies). Many organi-
zations may incorporate the information required 
in a program description to meet Standard 
2.A.1.a.
Examples
The written program description can be 
described in policy and procedure manuals, the 
performance improvement plan, program hand-
books, brochures, or other documentation. It 
may vary in length, depending on the size of the 
organization and the services that are provided.
11.a. The program description includes informa-
tion such as the populations and age groups 
served, relevant characteristics of the popula-
tions, hours and days of operation, after-hours 
contact, and admission criteria.
11.b.–c. The philosophy and goals of a program 
may be the same as the philosophy and goals of 
the organization; however, they are restated in 
the program descriptions for clarity.
11.d. The program description includes modali-
ties used for prevention, intervention, and 
treatment as appropriate. The description can 
identify the type(s) of therapy(ies) used for spe-
cific needs of persons served and include when 
medications are routinely prescribed. 
11.e. Special populations may include children 
and adolescents, aging and older adults, persons 
who are pregnant, persons with intellectual or 
other developmental disabilities, persons living 
with HIV/AIDS, persons who inject illicit sub-
stances, persons with DUI offenses, persons who 
have committed sexual offenses, or persons with 
offenses related to substance use.
2.A. 12. The program implements written proce-
dures for measurement-informed care 
that:
12.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Identify the tools to be used.
12.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Prescribe intervals for administration 
of the tools.
12.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Specify how results will be shared 
with persons served.
12.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Identify personnel training required.
12.d.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Measurement-informed care is a clinical practice 
that involves the systematic use of standardized 
tools/measures to track symptoms and functional 
status of persons served over time. By regularly 
assessing progress, clinical personnel can make 
more informed treatment decisions and improve 
outcomes for the persons served.
Examples
12.a. The type of tool used should be appropriate 
to the person served and based on their needs 
and goals. Consideration should also be given to 
cultural and linguistic backgrounds, as well as 
reading level (when given to the person served to 
complete). Common examples of tools used in 
behavioral health include, but are not limited to
General Mental Health:
n Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9): 
Assesses the severity of depression symptoms.
n Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7): 
Assesses the severity of anxiety symptoms.
n Clinical Outcomes in Routine Evaluation-
Outcome Measure (CORE-OM): A compre-
hensive measure of overall mental health and 
well-being.
n Brief Symptom Inventory (BSI): A general 
measure of psychological distress.
Substance Use Disorders:
n Drug Abuse Screening Test (DAST-10): 
Screens for substance use disorders.
n Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test 
(AUDIT): Screens for alcohol use disorders.
n Brief Addiction Monitor (BAM): Tracks 
changes in substance use and recovery.
Specific Disorders:
n Y ale-Brown Obsessive-Compulsive Scale 
(YBOCS): Assesses the severity of obsessive-
compulsive disorder (OCD).
n Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II): Assesses 
the severity of depression.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
122 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n Beck Anxiety Inventory (BAI): Assesses the 
severity of anxiety.
n Borderline Personality Disorder Inventory 
(BPDI): Assesses the severity of borderline 
personality disorder.
Quality of Life:
n Short Form-36 (SF-36): Measures physical 
and mental health-related quality of life.
n Functional Assessment:
n Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS): 
Assesses work and social functioning.
n Therapeutic Alliance:
n Session Rating Scale (SRS): Measures the 
quality of the therapeutic alliance between 
the
 clinician and patient.
12.b. Effectiveness of measurement-informed 
care relies on regular administration of the 
identified tools. The optimal frequency of 
assessments can vary depending on the person’s 
condition, treatment phase, and individual needs. 
However, common intervals include:
n Baseline Assessment: Before starting treat-
ment to establish a baseline.
n Regular Monitoring: At specific intervals 
during treatment, such as weekly, bi-weekly, 
or monthly.
n Critical Transition Points: At key moments, 
like the end of a treatment phase or before 
transitioning to a new phase of care.
n As-Needed Assessments: When there are 
concerns about a person’s condition or 
when
 significant life events occur.
12.c. Sharing the results of screening/assessment 
tools with the persons served can have a signifi-
cant treatment impact. Examples include:
Enhanced Engagement:
n Shared Decision-Making: Involving persons 
served in the review of their progress fosters 
a
 collaborative partnership between the 
clinician and person.
n Increased Motivation: Seeing tangible evi-
dence of improvement can motivate persons 
served to continue with treatment, even 
during challenging times.
n Empowerment: By understanding their symp-
toms and progress, persons served feel more 
empowered to take an active role in their own 
care. 
Improved Treatment Adherence:
n Accountability: Knowing that their progress 
is being monitored can encourage persons 
served to adhere to treatment plans.
n Reduced Stigma: Openly discussing symp-
toms and progress can help reduce the stigma 
associated with mental health conditions.
Enhanced Therapeutic Alliance:
n Trust and Transparency : Sharing results 
builds trust and transparency between the 
clinician and person served, strengthening 
the
 therapeutic relationship.
n Open Communication: Regular discussions 
about progress can facilitate open communi
-
cation and address any concerns or questions.
Improved Treatment Outcomes:
n Early Identification of Issues: Persons served 
can identify early signs of worsening symp-
toms and alert their clinician promptly.
n Tailored Treatment Plans: By understanding 
their progress, persons served can work with 
their clinician to adjust treatment plans as 
needed.
n Increased Satisfaction:
n Personalized Care: Persons served feel valued 
and cared for when their clinician takes the 
time to explain their progress and answer 
their questions.
n Positive Treatment Experience: A transparent 
and collaborative approach can lead to a more 
positive overall treatment experience.
12.d. Considerations for personnel training may 
include reasons for implementation of measure-
ment-informed care, training on the scoring 
and meaning of tools selected, expectations for 
frequency of administration, IT or data require-
ments related to the administration of tools, and 
clinical training on responding to results.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 123
2.A. 13. When applicable, there are policies and 
written procedures that address positive 
approaches to the program’s use of 
behavioral interventions, including:
13.A. Structure and Staffing
a. An emphasis on building positive 
relationships with persons served.
13.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Evaluation of the environment.
13.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Appropriate interaction with staff to:
13.c.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Promote de-escalation.
13.c.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Manage behavior.
13.c.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
d. Empowering persons served to man -
age their own behavior.
13.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. When restrictions on privileges may 
be implemented, identifying how it:
13.e.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Regularly evaluates the 
restrictions.
13.e.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Reinstates privileges.
13.e.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Evaluates the effectiveness of the 
restrictions.
13.e.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The intent of the standard is that organizations 
implement policies and procedures that support 
the use of positive alternatives to behavioral 
interventions such as redirecting and de-escala-
tion in its effort to avoid negative behaviors by 
the persons served. The policies and procedures 
should reflect the use of positive approaches 
prior to the implementation of 
behavioral interventions. 
The organization demonstrates commitment to 
a system that nurtures personal growth and 
dignity, and it supports the use of positive 
approaches and supports. This standard would 
apply to any program that deals with persons 
with a history of behavioral problems (e.g., anger, 
conduct disorder) or where the goal is to help the 
persons served change their behavior. Thus, you 
could see these policies and procedures in almost 
any behavioral health program except perhaps 
prevention/intervention, diversion, 
or information call centers.
2.A. 14. The organization implements a policy 
that identifies for each program the 
scope of medication services, including 
whether or not it directly provides 
medication:
14.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Control.
14.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Administering.
14.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Prescribing.
14.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
There may be separate policies for each program 
if the scope of medication services varies, or there 
may be one policy for the organization that 
reflects variances that may exist between 
programs.
Policies specify whether personnel must admin-
ister all medications or self-administration is 
permitted.
NOTE: Please refer to the Medication Use description 
in Section 2.E. to ensure that the policy covers all 
applicable areas of medication use.
2.A. 15. The program receives medical consulta-
tion regarding medically related policies 
or procedures, when appropriate.
15.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Medical consultation is typically provided by a 
medical practitioner on policies and procedures 
within their expertise and practice area. This 
consultation may be provided by a licensed phy-
sician, a nurse practitioner, a physician’s assistant, 
or a registered nurse. The person does not have to 
be a staff member but can be connected through 
a contract for consulting or voluntary agreement.
Examples
Medical consultation may be indicated for poli-
cies and procedures involving medication use, 
seclusion or restraint, admission eligibility, 
infection control, medical emergencies, or other 
medically related issues.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
124 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
2.A. 16. The organization implements a policy for 
each program that identifies:
16.A. Structure and Staffing
a. How it will respond to unsafe behav -
iors of the persons served.
16.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Whether:
16.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Seclusion is used.
16.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Restraints are used.
16.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Each program seeking accreditation must have a 
policy that outlines its position on the use of 
seclusion and/or restraints. Personnel and the 
persons served (refer to Standard 2.B.8.) under-
stand the program’s policy and position on the 
use of seclusion and restraint, including physical, 
mechanical, or chemical restraint, and it is clearly 
understood under what circumstances, if any, 
seclusion or restraint would be used. There may 
be separate policies for each program seeking 
accreditation within an organization if variances 
exist between how programs respond to unsafe 
behaviors of the persons served and/or whether 
and under what circumstances seclusion or 
restraint is used.
Examples
Unsafe behaviors are typically those character-
ized as dangerous and causing potential harm 
to self or others. Unsafe behaviors may include 
impulsive and chronic physical aggression 
toward others including threats of violence and 
possessing and utilizing weapons, cutting or 
other forms of self-mutilation, and destructive 
behaviors toward property. 
2.A. 17. As applicable to the population served, 
all direct service personnel receive docu-
mented competency-based training:
17.A. Structure and Staffing
a. At orientation.
17.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. At least annually.
17.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. That addresses prevention of unsafe 
behaviors, including:
17.c.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Contributing factors or causes 
that may lead to unsafe 
behaviors.
17.c.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Health conditions that may con -
tribute to unsafe behaviors.
17.c.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) How interpersonal interactions 
may impact the behaviors of the 
persons served, including:
17.c.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
(a) How persons served interact 
with each other.
17.c.(3)(a)A. Structure and Staffing
(b) How personnel interact with 
persons served.
17.c.(3)(b)A. Structure and Staffing
(c) How personnel interact with 
each other.
17.c.(3)(c)A. Structure and Staffing
(4) Use of alternative interventions 
in an effort to avoid the use of 
seclusion or restraint.
17.c.(4)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
17.c.(2) The training addresses health conditions, 
both physical and behavioral, that may underlie 
or influence unsafe behaviors on the part of the 
persons served. 
17.c.(4) Done in a supportive and respectful 
manner, alternative interventions are imple-
mented to prevent or de-escalate a potential 
crisis.
NOTE: Please refer to the description of Promoting 
Nonviolent Practices in Section 2.F . for further 
description of seclusion and restraint.
Examples
17.c.(1) Although contributing factors or causes 
leading to unsafe behavior vary considerably 
between individuals and programs, consideration 
should be given to the physical environment as 
well as conditions in the milieu or the social 
environment. An individual’s history of unsafe 
behaviors and how the person has responded to 
various interventions may be the best predictor 
of future behaviors and responses.
17.c.(3) Programs may consider training in 
eCPR, a holistic empowering approach to assist-
ing persons served to cope with emotional crises. 
Information on this approach can be found at 
www.emotional-cpr.org.
17.c.(4) Examples may include engagement,  
one-to-one attention, meditation, de-escalation, 
self-protection, time out, re-direction, sensory or 
comfort rooms, mediation, conflict resolution, 
prompting, and active listening.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 125
2.A. 18. The program ensures that information 
and education that is relevant to the 
needs of the persons served is provided.
18.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Education about mental health and/or substance 
use disorders is an integral component of behav-
ioral healthcare services. These psychoeducation 
activities can be general in groups, but should 
also be customized to meet individual needs 
identified through the assessment process and 
responding to issues that come up throughout 
care.
Examples
Information may be provided on medical issues, 
sexually transmitted infections, or infectious dis-
eases; housing; mental health; alcohol and other 
substance use/addictive issues; relationships; life 
skills, harm reduction, etc. Education may be 
provided through:
n Individual and group sessions.
n Group education.
n Audio/video or written materials.
n The internet.
n Resource listings.
Education about mental health and/or substance 
use disorders is an integral component of behav-
ioral healthcare services. These psychoeducation 
activities can be general in groups, but should 
also be customized to meet individual needs 
identified through the assessment process and 
responding to issues that come up throughout 
care.
2.A. 19. Families are:
19.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Encouraged to participate in edu -
cational programs offered by the 
organization.
19.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Invited to participate in clinical pro -
grams or services with the persons 
served, with consent or legal right.
19.b.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The program seeks to engage the family of the 
person served into care. The family can include 
traditional family members such as the person’s 
spouse, parents, siblings, or other involved rela-
tives. When related persons are not involved or 
their involvement is impractical or inappropriate, 
the program seeks to involve other individuals 
in the person’s life to provide support. These 
persons may include friends, coworkers, peer 
supports, or others the person served views as 
community support. Support systems are vital to 
successful recovery, and the program encourages 
strengthening or developing community sup-
ports to improve and maintain outcomes upon 
completion of care.
2.A. 20. The program implements written proce-
dures for crisis intervention that:
20.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Avoid harm.
20.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Ensure person-centered 
interventions.
20.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Are sensitive to potential trauma of 
persons served.
20.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Are designed to ensure safety of:
20.d.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Persons served.
20.d.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Personnel.
20.d.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The program contemplates various types of crisis 
situations that may occur at the program and 
designs interventions to protect the persons 
served as well as personnel. Responding to a crisis 
requires individual interventions rather than a 
“one size fits all” approach. All programs must 
have the capability to respond to an immediate 
crisis, even if they plan to transfer the person to 
another specialized crisis program.
2.A. 21. To meet the needs of the persons served, 
the program demonstrates how it uses 
technology to:
21.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Increase access to services.
21.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Increase supports.
21.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Enhance services.
21.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Program management and leadership seek to 
find and implement technologies that assist the 
persons served in meeting their goals. The pro-
gram can describe what technologies it has 
implemented and what it is considering for the 
future.

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
126 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
21.a. The program may improve access to ser-
vices through the use of websites, patient portals, 
telehealth services, social media, text messaging, 
and other methods to remind the persons served 
of appointments.
21.b. Increased supports could include use of 
technological supports between services, such as 
recovery-based applications or encouraging per-
sons served to use online support communities 
and electronic communications with personnel, 
as appropriate.
21.c. The program may enhance services through 
technology such as patient portals for making 
appointments, requesting refills of medications 
and accessing medical records; and/or through 
the use of online tools such as outcomes mea-
sures, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) tools, 
online assessments, and other services.
2.A. 22. For personnel providing direct services, 
based on the scope of their work, the 
organization includes the following in its 
documented assessment of competency 
and competency-based training:
22.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Areas that reflect the specific needs 
of the persons served.
22.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Clinical skills that are appropriate to 
the position.
22.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Person-centered planning.
22.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Interviewing/communication skills.
22.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. Program-related research-based 
treatment approaches.
22.e.A. Structure and Staffing
f. Identification of clinical risk factors, 
including:
22.f.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Suicide.
22.f.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Violence.
22.f.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
(3) Other risky behaviors.
22.f.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
g. Safety planning.
22.g.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to ensure that the 
necessary competencies are established and 
demonstrated. These competencies are included 
in the competencies identified as a part of 
Standard 1.I.8.
Examples
Competency-based training may include training 
that is provided or recognized by a professional 
association, part of a formal training curriculum, 
or approved for continuing education units 
(CEUs) by a credentialing or licensing body. 
Competency in the areas in which training has 
occurred can be assessed by observing work and 
documenting that the skills or knowledge pre-
sented are being used on the job, through 
supervision and clinical review when assessments 
can be made regarding the retention and use of 
the training information, or through post-tests 
that are administered.
When needed, competency-based training is pro-
vided through in-service or access to external 
resources.
2.A. 23. Team members, in response to the needs 
of the persons served:
23.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Help empower each person served to 
actively participate with the team to 
promote recovery, progress, or well-
being.
23.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Provide services that are consistent 
with the needs of each person served 
through direct interaction with that 
person and/or with individuals identi

fied by that person.
23.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Are culturally and linguistically 
competent.
23.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Meet as often as necessary to carry 
out decision-making responsibilities.
23.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. Document:
23.e.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) The attendance of participants at 
team meetings.
23.e.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) The results of team meetings.
23.e.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The size and composition of the team will vary 
according to the services provided to each person 
served. Certain programs, services, or needs of 
the persons served may require that the team 
include personnel from a variety of disciplines.
Examples
23.a. This may be demonstrated through the 
active involvement of the person served in the 
development of the person-centered plan, 

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 127
participation in team meetings, or periodic 
review of identified goals.
2.A. 24. A designated individual(s) assists in coor-
dinating services for each person served 
by:
24.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Assuming responsibility for ensuring 
the implementation of person-
centered planning, if applicable.
24.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Ensuring that the person served is 
oriented to the services that will be 
provided.
24.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Promoting the participation of the 
person served on an ongoing basis 
in discussions of the person’s plans, 
goals, and status.
24.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Identifying and addressing gaps in 
service provision.
24.d.A. Structure and Staffing
e. Sharing information on how to access 
community resources relevant to the 
person’s needs.
24.e.A. Structure and Staffing
f. Advocating for the person served, 
when applicable.
24.f.A. Structure and Staffing
g. Communicating information regard -
ing progress of the person served to 
the appropriate persons.
24.g.A. Structure and Staffing
h. Facilitating the transition process, 
including arrangements for follow-up 
services.
24.h.A. Structure and Staffing
i. Involving the family or legal guard -
ian, when permitted.
24.i.A. Structure and Staffing
j. Coordinating services provided out -
side of the organization.
24.j.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
Having a person designated to coordinate ser-
vices ensures a more seamless process, thereby 
increasing the likelihood that all pertinent areas 
are effectively addressed. Additionally, the per-
sons served should know who to go to manage 
their care and request assistance. 
Examples
The individual(s) who coordinates services 
may be an employee of the organization, a peer 
advocate, on the organization’s payroll, under a 
contractual arrangement, on an internship, or a 
volunteer placement. Various designations may 
be used, such as peer advocate, case manager, 
case coordinator, program coordinator, primary 
clinician/contact, or team leader.
24.e.–f. For persons with intellectual or other 
developmental disabilities who are in long-term 
residential services, this means offering commu-
nity-based options such as independent living 
programs.
24.h. Includes the transition of the person served 
from one program to another within the same 
organization.
2.A. 25. The organization implements a policy for 
the clinical supervision of all individuals 
providing direct services.
25.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to ensure that all 
individuals providing direct services (including 
staff members, volunteers, trainees, interns, and 
contracted personnel) are provided with appro-
priate supervision or direction regarding the 
manner in which services are provided to persons 
served. This supervision is designed to develop 
and enhance the skills of direct service personnel 
in the provision of services. This is in contrast to 
managerial supervision, which is more targeted 
to accountability. Because of labor relations con-
cerns, procedures may differ when the 
organization uses contracted personnel. 
Examples
Supervision may occur through the supervisor’s 
participation in treatment/service planning 
meetings, organizational staff meetings, side-by-
side sessions with the person served, or one-to-
one meetings between the supervisor and indi-
viduals providing direct services.
2.A. 26. Ongoing supervision of clinical or direct 
service personnel:
26.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Is documented.
26.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Addresses:
26.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Accuracy of assessment and 
referral skills.
26.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) The appropriateness of the treat -
ment or service intervention 
selected relative to the specific 
needs of each person served.
26.b.(2)A. Structure and Staffing

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
128 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
(3) Treatment/service effectiveness 
as reflected by the person served 
meeting goals identified in the 
person-centered planning 
process.
26.b.(3)A. Structure and Staffing
(4) Risk factors for suicide and other 
dangerous behaviors.
26.b.(4)A. Structure and Staffing
(5) Issues of ethics, legal aspects of 
clinical practice, and professional 
standards.
26.b.(5)A. Structure and Staffing
(6) Clinical documentation issues 
identified through ongoing 
quality records review.
26.b.(6)A. Structure and Staffing
(7) Cultural competency.
26.b.(7)A. Structure and Staffing
(8) Model fidelity, when implement -
ing evidence-based practices.
26.b.(8)A. Structure and Staffing
c. Provides feedback to personnel that 
enhances skills.
26.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
This standard addresses clinical supervision and 
the provision of clinical consultation as defined 
in the policy written for Standard 2.A.25. This 
ongoing supervision should be intentional in the 
program, but is not defined as being completed 
in a prescribed interval. The interval for frequency 
should be based on the needs of individual per-
sonnel, but there should be a plan that ensures all 
areas are covered within a reasonable interval for 
all clinical or direct-service personnel.
Examples
Supervision may occur through the supervisor’s 
participation in treatment/service planning 
meetings, organizational staff meetings, side-by-
side sessions with the persons served, or one-to-
one meetings between the supervisor and 
personnel. In programs that provide services 
exclusively using information and communica-
tions technologies, these supervisory interactions 
are often handled virtually through similar 
platforms.
Clinical supervision documentation specifically 
includes assessment of professional competencies 
and clinical skills and recommendations for 
improvement, as opposed to managerial super-
vision. In programs that provide services 
exclusively using information and communica-
tions technologies, these supervisory interactions 
are often handled virtually through similar 
platforms.
When direct service staff are consultants or inde-
pendent contractors, expectations regarding 
26.b.(1)–(8) may be identified in written 
agreement.
26.c. May include information on best practices 
or identify areas for needed professional growth.
2.A. 27. The program implements policies and 
procedures that address:

a. The handling of items brought into

27.a.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) By:
27.a.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(a) Persons served.

27.a.(2)(d)A. Structure and Staffing

permitted.

b. The use of nicotine products in all:
27.b.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Locations.
27.b.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Vehicles owned or operated by 
the organization.

Intent Statements
Based on the type of program(s) the organization 
provides, the policies and procedures identify for 
each stakeholder relevant detail for each item 
identified in the standard.
Examples
27.a.(1)(c) Visitors may be given a list of items 
that are not allowed to be brought into the pro-
gram area when visiting persons served, or a list 
of items that are not permitted could be posted in 
visible location for visitors to see upon entry. Safe 
storage is provided for items on the exclusion list.
27.a.(2)(a) Illegal substances include street drugs 
and alcohol (if under the legal drinking age). 
When applicable, this includes drug 
paraphernalia.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 129
27.a.(2)(b) Legal substances may include over-
the-counter drugs, vitamins, herbs, and alcohol.
27.a.(2)(d) Weapons includes ammunition and

tine, shisha, nicotine gum, vapes, and green

2.A. 28. When applicable, programs that use 
drug screening for persons with sub
-
stance use disorders implement written 
procedures that include:
28.A. Structure and Staffing
a. An individualized approach with 
frequency based on the needs of 
the
 person served.
28.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Specific treatmen t conditions that 
would warrant drug screening.
28.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Collection of specimens in a respect -
ful manner.
28.c.A. Structure and Staffing
d. Ensuring that drug screening results 
are not used as the sole basis for:
28.d.A. Structure and Staffing
(1) Treatment decisions.
28.d.(1)A. Structure and Staffing
(2) Termination from treatment.
28.d.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
When programs treat persons with substance use 
disorders, they frequently utilize drug testing 
as a tool to assess, monitor progress, increase 
accountability, and monitor substance use, 
especially when prescribing medications. Often, 
programs have standard practices for drug test-
ing as a part of their admission and diagnosing 
procedures. After admission, the frequency of 
drug testing is based on the needs of the persons 
served and takes into consideration how it is 
perceived by the persons served. Some indiv-
iduals may find frequent testing motivating, 
while others may perceive it as indicating a lack 
of progress and trust. Because testing is less 
effective when it is predictable, programs could 
consider ways to make testing as random as 
possible.
The results of the drug testing are utilized to 
formulate clinical interventions and not used 
punitively.
Programs that do not use broad drug screening 
practices are not required to implement such 
practices to meet this standard.
28.c.  When programs use external vendors for 
collection, this standard would not be applicable.
Peer Support Services
Peer support services (inclusive of youth or fam-
ily supports) can include a wide range of planned 
activities to assist persons served in exercising 
control over their own lives and their recovery 
or resilience-building process. Peer support may 
include peer mentoring or coaching, resource 
connecting, facilitating and leading recovery, 
educational and support groups, advocating for 
the person/family served, and/or building com-
munity supports. 
Because peer supports are guided by a founda-
tion of lived experience, peer support specialists 
are persons who share with others based on that 
experience to encourage, motivate and support 
persons served and/or their families. They may 
be referred to as youth or family support special-
ists or mentors, recovery coaches, guides, peer 
resource specialists, peer service intervention-
ists, or similar titles. 
Peer and youth support services are designed to 
have persons with lived experience work directly 
with persons served. Family support services are 
designed to have persons who have lived experi-
ence through their family member’s participation 
in services directly work with the family of per-
sons served.
Applicable Standards
When an organization employs peer support 
specialists in any of the core programs seeking 
accreditation, the following standards must 
be applied in addition to other applicable 
standards in Section 1, Sections 2.A.–I., and 
the specific program standards and a specialty 
designation (if applicable).
2.A. 29. The organization implements policies 
and procedures that are inclusive of a 
peer workforce.
29.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The organization’s policies and procedures 
are written with consideration of the various 

SECTION 2.A. PROGRAM/SERVICE STRUCTURE
130 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
personnel it utilizes, ensuring that they are con-
sistent with the needs of the peer workforce.
2.A. 30. Peer support specialists assist in peer 
support services:
30.A. Structure and Staffing
a. Design.
30.a.A. Structure and Staffing
b. Development.
30.b.A. Structure and Staffing
c. Implementation.
30.c.A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The organization involves members of the peer 
support workforce in the process of designing 
and implementing these services to ensure that 
the peer support expertise is included. The 
organization should be able to demonstrate 
how it collected the input of the peer support 
workforce in design, development, and 
implementation.

tal age of the peer support 
specialist being trained.
32.d.(2)A. Structure and Staffing
Intent Statements
The organization ensures that the peer support 
workforce is adequately trained to perform the 
work assigned. When the organization provides 
its own training, it should seek curriculum from 
nationally recognized sources such as SAMHSA, 
the Psychosocial Rehabilitation Association, the 
Certification Commission for Family Support, or 
other competent source. When the organization 
hires Certified Peer Specialists (or other peer 
support specialists with an equivalent credential), 
it is accepted that the peer support specialist has 
received appropriate initial training and verifying 
the training/credential satisfies the training 
requirement in this standard.

of conduct specifically address boundar-
ies related to peer support services.
33.A. Structure and Staffing
Examples
This may include how peer support specialists’ 
boundaries with persons served differ from those 
of personnel in areas such as sharing meals, 
attending social events, sharing lived experience, 
social media connections, and communication 
(electronic and other).

or help the persons served to articulate their 
personal goals and identify means to reach those 
goals. Peer support specialists can help the per-
son served make new friends and begin to build 
alternative social networks.
34.b.(3) Community networking may include 
social, recreational, spiritual, educational, or 
vocational linkages. Peer support specialists 
encourage and support participation in self-help 
groups and provide specific information about 
various groups that may be helpful to the person 
served.
34.b.(6) Mentoring involves supporting the 
efforts of the persons served to achieve their 
individual goals through coaching, encouraging, 
providing positive guidance, sharing life experi-
ences, and offering feedback to assist with 
personal development.
34.b.(7) Navigating includes assisting the person 
served to find and access services/benefits and to 
make appeals and respond to denials if needed. 
Bridging refers to efforts made to make coopera-
tive connections between the person served and 
others and create ties to those who may be help-
ful to them in a variety of ways. It can also involve 
helping to resolve differences and reduce 
barriers.
34.c.(3) Life skills are basic skills used to handle 
problems and questions commonly encountered 
in daily life. This could include problem solving, 
accepting responsibility, money management, 
and honoring commitments. Self-care skills such 
as cooking, cleaning, laundry, and shopping are 
also essential life skills. 
2.A. 35. Peer support services are provided in 
locations that meet the needs of persons 
served.
35.A. Structure and Staffing
Examples
Peer support services may be provided in the 
community, outpatient or inpatient settings, 
recovery community organizations or centers, 
the home of the person or family served, 
churches, child welfare organizations, recovery 
homes, drug courts, pre-release jail and prison 
programs, parole and probation programs, 
behavioral health agencies, HIV/AIDS support 
centers, medical centers, and/or other social 
service centers.

SECTION 2.B. SCREENING AND ACCESS TO SERVICES
132 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
B. Screening and Access 
to Services
Description
The process of screening and assessment is 
designed to determine a person’s eligibility for 
services and the organization’s ability to provide 
those services. A person-centered assessment 
process helps to maximize opportunities for the 
persons served to gain access to the organization’s 
programs and services. Each person served is 
actively involved in, and has a significant role 
in, the assessment process. Assessments are con-
ducted in a manner that identifies the historical 
and current information of the person served as 
well as the person’s strengths, needs, abilities, and 
preferences. Assessment data may be gathered 
through various means including face-to-face 
contact, telehealth, or written material; and from 
various sources including the person served, 
family or significant others, or from external 
resources.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable Stan-
dards at the beginning of this section to determine 
how these standards will be applied to the core 
program or service areas for which you are seeking 
accreditation.
2.B. 1. Person-centered care is demonstrated 
throughout the screening and/or 
assessment process.
1.B. Screening and Access
  
2.B. 2. The program demonstrates efforts to 
minimize the times between first 
contact, screening, and admission 
or
 referral.
2.B. Screening and Access
Examples
The program is able to describe the activities 
it has implemented to decrease wait times for 
services from initial contact to engagement in 
care. If an organization has implemented an open 
access system for admissions, it can still dem-
onstrate how it reduces wait times for referral 
into care.
Screening/Eligibility
2.B. 3. The organization implements written 
procedures that define:
3.B. Screening and Access
a. If/how screening is conducted.
3.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Eligibility for services.
3.b.B. Screening and Access
c. How admissions are:
3.c.B. Screening and Access
(1) Conducted.
3.c.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Prioritized, if necessary.
3.c.(2)B. Screening and Access
d. Who is responsible for making admis -
sion decisions.
3.d.B. Screening and Access
e. Exclusionary or ineligibility criteria.
3.e.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
It is understood that some programs do not con-
duct screening as that service is provided by 
another program, such as an assessment unit 
with a directed referral to the program. In other 
cases, the persons served may be screened at 
another organization and referred directly to the 
program.
3.d. The procedures describe the position or per-
son who makes the final admission decision. This 
person should be qualified to perform this func-
tion in accordance with all legal and regulatory 
requirements, and decision making should be 
completed in a timeframe that is acceptable to 
same.
2.B. 4. When screening is conducted by the 
organization, it:
4.B. Screening and Access
a. Is documented.
4.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Includes a review of each person’s 
eligibility for admission based on:
4.b.B. Screening and Access
(1) Presenting problem(s).
4.b.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Legal eligibility criteria, when 
applicable.
4.b.(2)B. Screening and Access
(3) Availability of funding sources.
4.b.(3)B. Screening and Access
c. Identifies: 
4.c.B. Screening and Access
(1) Whether the organization can 
provide the appropriate services 
needed.
4.c.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Alternate resources when 
services cannot be provided.
4.c.(2)B. Screening and Access

SECTION 2.B. SCREENING AND ACCESS TO SERVICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 133
d. Includes:
4.d.B. Screening and Access
(1) An interview with the person to 
be served or referral source.
4.d.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) When appropriate, a pread -
mission on-site visit to the 
organization and its programs 
by
 the person to be served/legal 
guardian.
4.d.(2)B. Screening and Access
e. Ensures that, when implemented:
4.e.B. Screening and Access
(1) Screening tools used are uni -
formly administered.
4.e.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Personnel are trained on use of 
tools prior to administration.
4.e.(2)B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The screening process determines the person’s 
appropriateness and eligibility for services. The 
screening may be thought of routinely as part of 
intake or admission processes.
4.e. This element of the standard is applicable 
only when screening tools are used.
Examples
Screening may include a review of all informa-
tion available, discussions with referral sources, 
and, if necessary, face-to-face contacts. Infor-
mation may be found in admission reports or 
screening logs.
4.b.(2) May include residency, geographic area, 
status of person served, or other criteria.
4.b.(3) May include public sources, grant eligibil-
ity, private pay resources, or third-party funding.
4.d.(1) An interview can be done face-to-face, via 
telephone interview, or by other technological 
means; and may include parents, guardians, or 
others.
4.d.(2) May be more applicable prior to admis-
sion to a residential program.
2.B. 5. If the screening identifies an urgent and 
critical need, appropriate action is taken 
immediately.
5.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The organization safely and immediately 
responds to the person’s urgent or critical 
needs whether directly or through referral and 
does not wait until routine time is available.
2.B. 6. If the screening identifies unsafe sub-
stance use:
6.B. Screening and Access
a. A brief intervention is conducted 
either directly, through referral, or 
as part of the treatment program.
6.a.B. Screening and Access
b. The individual is referred for a full 
assessment, if needed.
6.b.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
This standard is directed toward programs that 
are not primarily addiction treatment programs. 
It is expected that programs that offer services 
in the integrated SUD/MH field category are 
capable of providing integrated care where 
person-centered planning integrates the sub-
stance use disorder and mental health care. 
2.B. 7. If a waiting list is maintained, the 
organization:
7.B. Screening and Access
a. Documents the person’s:
7.a.B. Screening and Access
(1) Date of placement on the list.
7.a.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Identified needs.
7.a.(2)B. Screening and Access
b. Maintains current waiting list infor -
mation through:
7.b.B. Screening and Access
(1) Ongoing review and updating of 
the list.
7.b.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Implementation of procedures 
for referral of persons in crisis to 
necessary care.
7.b.(2)B. Screening and Access
c. Documents all contacts with the per -
sons on the waiting list.
7.c.B. Screening and Access
d. Responds to long-term waiting lists 
through:
7.d.B. Screening and Access
(1) Strategic or community-based 
planning.
7.d.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Involvement of support services.
7.d.(2)B. Screening and Access
(3) Referral to available services/
community supports.
7.d.(3)B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The use of a waiting list involves an active review 
process that leads to a determination of eligibility 
based on the organization’s entrance/admission 
criteria. It also helps to ensure that needed ser-
vices are being provided in a timely manner.

SECTION 2.B. SCREENING AND ACCESS TO SERVICES
134 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
Referral lists are not the same as waiting lists. 
They are different in that referral lists include all 
persons referred for services. Such lists are not 
necessarily used to determine the sequence of 
admission.
In certain situations, such as under Centers for 
Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) waivers, 
there may be requirements that there be no wait-
ing list.
7.b.(1) Review of a waiting list may be docu-
mented in meeting minutes or by signing and 
dating the list itself to indicate review.
Monitoring a waiting list could include tracking 
the length of time on the waiting list before 
admission and the percentage of persons 
admitted. This information may assist in 
an organization’s planning process.
7.d.(3) Evidence of referrals or other actions 
taken may be included on the waiting list itself 
or documented in a referral log.
Orientation

8.d.(6)B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
This standard relates to the involvement of the 
persons served and their understanding of 
exactly what will happen as services are deliv-
ered. While the orientation can be provided in 
a written or verbal manner, documentation 
supports that it has been conducted. Orientation 
can be provided by more than one person and 
over time. The extensiveness of the orientation 
may be impacted by the type/intensity of service 
provided or the condition of the person served at 
the time of admission.
Examples
8.b. When written material is given to the per-
sons served regarding their rights, orientation, 
and other topics, it should be provided at a read-
ing level understandable to the persons served.
8.d.(1)(c) This would include an explanation of 
the organization’s practices for obtaining input 
from the persons served as well as opportunities 
offered through the organization’s outcomes 
management process, specifically regarding 
assessment of service and satisfaction by the per-
son served. Input may be given through groups 
or individual sessions, suggestion boxes; surveys, 
grievance forms, etc.
8.d.(1)(e) Information includes length of time 
benefits will be paid by payer source if this infor-
mation is available at time of orientation and how 
updates of benefits will be provided.
8.d.(1)(f)(iii) Includes alcohol and over-the-
counter medications.
8.d.(3) Includes psychiatric advance directives, 
when legally available. The following factors will 
impact the applicability of this standard: laws or 
regulations, the type of service provided, or the 
specific population served.
8.d.(5)(f) The program provides information to 
the person served about the services offered that 
family members or other members of the person’s 
support system can and should participate in.
See the Glossary for the definition of advance 
directives.
Assessment
2.B. 9. Assessments are conducted by qualified 
personnel:
9.B. Screening and Access
a. Knowledgeable to assess the specific 
needs of the persons served.
9.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Trained in the use of applicable tools, 
tests, or instruments prior to 
administration.
9.b.B. Screening and Access
c. Able to communicate with the 
persons served.
9.c.B. Screening and Access
Examples
Qualified personnel are determined by the 
organization’s leadership. The organization may 
base its determination on the skills, experience, 
and/or education of personnel and by state, fed-
eral, provincial, or other regulating guidelines. 

SECTION 2.B. SCREENING AND ACCESS TO SERVICES
136 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
2.B. 10. When assessment results in diagno-
sis(es), the diagnosis is determined by a 
practitioner legally qualified to do so in 
accordance with all applicable laws and 
regulations.
10.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The record of the person served may have diag-
nostic impressions present when offered by 
personnel who are not legally qualified to 
diagnose the person served; however, when a 
diagnosis is recorded, it must be determined by 
a practitioner legally allowed to diagnose the 
person served.
2.B. 11. The assessment process includes infor-
mation obtained from:
11.B. Screening and Access
a. The person served.
11.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Family members/legal guardian, 
when applicable and permitted.
11.b.B. Screening and Access
c. Other collateral sources, when 
applicable and permitted.
11.c.B. Screening and Access
d. External sources, when the need for 
specified assessments not able to 
be provided by the organization is 
identified.
11.d.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The program seeks information from a variety 
of sources that can ensure the assessment accu-
rately reflects the history and the impacts on the 
functioning of the person served. Speaking with 
sources other than the person served should be 
done in accordance with applicable privacy laws 
and regulations.
Examples
11.c. Collateral sources may include:
n Parents/guardians.
n Teachers.
n Social workers.
n Probation officers.
n Physicians.
n Friends.
n Peers.
2.B. 12. The assessment process:
12.B. Screening and Access
a. Focuses on the person’s specific 
needs.
12.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Identifies the goals and expectations 
of the person served.
12.b.B. Screening and Access
c. Is responsive to the changing needs 
of the person served.
12.c.B. Screening and Access
d. Includes screening for suicide risk for 
all persons served age 12 and older 
using a standardized tool normed for 
the population served.
12.d.B. Screening and Access
e. Includes provisions for communicat -
ing the results of the assessments to:
12.e.B. Screening and Access
(1) The person served/legal 
guardian.
12.e.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Applicable personnel.
12.e.(2)B. Screening and Access
(3) Others as appropriate.
12.e.(3)B. Screening and Access
f. Provides the basis for legally required 
notification, when applicable.
12.f.B. Screening and Access
g. Collects information regarding 
advance directives, when applicable.
12.g.B. Screening and Access
h. Occurs within timeframes established 
by the organization or external regu-
latory requirements.
12.h.B. Screening and Access
i. Reflects significant life or status 
changes of the person served.
12.i.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
Assessment information may be ongoing and 
collected over time or by various programs within 
an organization. The expectation is that the 
program has collected information adequate 
to result in individualized and goal-oriented, 
person-centered planning. 
12.b. This part of the assessment identifies what 
the person wants or why the person is coming for 
services.
12.d. The intent of this standard is to ensure 
universal screening of suicide risk for all persons 
served age 12 and older. Programs may choose 
to screen persons younger than 12 at their discre-
tion when clinically indicated.
12.g. The intent of this standard is to provide the 
person served with the specific opportunity to 
communicate whether or not there is an advance 
directive that may impact the course of the par-
ticular services provided. See the Glossary for the 
definition of advance directives.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 137
Examples
12.a. Specific needs may be related to:
n Age or developmental level.
n Gender.
n Sexual orientation.
n Social preferences.
n Cultural background.
n Psychological characteristics.
n Physical condition.
n Spiritual beliefs.
12.d. The most common tool for assessing sui-
cide risk is the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating 
Scale (CSSRS), and there are several versions of 
this tool for use with different populations and at 
different times. There is a significant amount of 
resource information available at the Columbia 
Lighthouse Project website at http://cssrs.colum-
bia.edu/.
Another tool to consider is the SAFE-T screener 
and information can be found here: 
www.sprc.org/resources-programs/suicide-
assessment-five-step-evaluation-and-triage-
safe-t-pocket-card.
A screener that has been normed for children 
and adolescents age 12 and older is the ASQ, 
and information about this tool can be found 
at www.sprc.org/sites/default/files/resource-
program/asQT oolkit_0.pdf and at 
www.nimh.nih.gov/labs-at-nimh/asq-toolkit-
materials/index.shtml.
12.f. Notification may include child and adult 
protective services, committing or referring 
courts, a local provider identified under the 
Indian Child Welfare Act, or probation or parole 
officers. Reports may also be required when there 
are threats of violence toward others in what is 
commonly known in the U.S. as Tarasoff warn-
ings, also known as “duty to warn.”

(2) Historical use.

q. Risk factors for:
13.q.B. Screening and Access
(1) Suicide.
13.q.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Other self-harm or risk-taking 
behaviors.
13.q.(2)B. Screening and Access
(3) Violence toward others.
13.q.(3)B. Screening and Access
r. Literacy level.
13.r.B. Screening and Access
s. Need for assistive technology 
in the provision of services.
13.s.B. Screening and Access
t. Need for, and availability of, social 
supports.
13.t.B. Screening and Access
u. Psychological and social adjustment 
to disabilities and/or disorders.
13.u.B. Screening and Access
v. Resultant diagnosis( es), if identified.
13.v.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The intent of the standard is to collect an ade-
quate amount of information to develop an 
appropriate individualized plan of care and to 
subsequently provide appropriate and safe 
services. The information may be obtained from 
a variety of sources and at different times.
In short-term programs (such as assessment and 
referral, crisis intervention, crisis stabilization, 
detoxification/withdrawal management, or 
employee assistance programs), peer-run/driven 
programs (such as community integration), or 
targeted case management such as Healthy 
Families America (HFA), the amount of infor-
mation collected may be limited by time or the 
condition of the person served or the nature of 
service being provided.
NOTE: In Canadian programs (such as Outpatient 
Treatment) where laws or regulations prohibit the 
collection of specifically identified information, an 
abbreviated assessment is allowed.
Examples
13.b. Personal strengths may include assets, 
resources, and natural positives.
13.c. Individual needs may include liabilities, 
weaknesses, and what the person needs to 
recover.
13.d. Abilities and/or interests may include skills, 
aptitudes, capabilities, talents, and competencies.
13.e. Preferences are those things the person 
served feels will enhance the treatment 
experience.
13.f. Previous behavioral health data may 
include:
n Psychiatric assessments.
n Psychological assessments.
n Medication use.
n Hospitalizations.
n Alcohol and other substance use disorder 
services.
n Pertinent medical care.
n Community programs.
13.i.(2) Includes dental health, as well as visual or 
hearing concerns, when they appear to be a con-
tributing factor to the presenting condition of the 
person served.
13.i.(3) Health issues related to pregnancy could 
include use of legal and/or illegal substances, 
whether prenatal care is being provided, or 
whether the pregnancy affects the woman’s 
participation in the program.
13.j. According to the National Institutes of 
Health National Center for Complementary 

SECTION 2.B. SCREENING AND ACCESS TO SERVICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 139
and Integrative Health (https://nccih.nih.gov/
health/integrative-health), the terms comple-
mentary and alternative refer to healthcare 
approaches developed outside of mainstream 
Western, or conventional, medicine. Comple-
mentary medicine is the use of a non-
mainstream approach together with conventional 
medicine. Alternative medicine is the use of a 
non-mainstream approach in place of conven-
tional medicine. Most use of non-mainstream 
approaches by Americans is complementary. 
Integrative health incorporates complementary 
health approaches into mainstream healthcare.
Complementary health approaches may include:
n Use of natural products, such as dietary 
supplements.
n Mind and body practices, such as acupunc-
ture, massage therapy, meditation, movement 
therapies, yoga, and relaxation techniques.
n Homeopathy, naturopathy, and traditional 
healers.
13.k. It is particularly important to identify any 
co-occurring disabilities and/or disorders, 
including primary care issues that may impact 
the therapeutic relationship with the person 
served.
13.l. Current levels of functioning may include 
cognitive, emotional, and behavioral functioning.
13.m.(2)–(6) Gender, sexual orientation, gender 
identity, cultural background, and spiritual 
beliefs may be essential components to recovery 
or treatment and therefore are not excluded as a 
factor when gathering information.
13.m.(14) There are many factors that can have 
an impact on the ability to realize the person’s 
greatest recovery potential and that need to be 
evaluated and considered when developing treat-
ment plans. These factors include, but are not 
limited to, discrimination due to race, ethnicity, 
and LGBTQ+ status. Other social factors could 
include food (in)security, housing (in)security, 
lack of transportation, and employment. 
Although some of these factors are collected as a 
part of the assessment process, considering each 
of them and how they impact the person’s func-
tioning and recovery is the purpose of collecting 
the data as an evaluation of the social determi-
nants of health.
13.n.(2)(a) Abuse may include previous trauma 
survivor concerns; spousal/partner abuse; abuse 
suffered as a child; physical, sexual, emotional, 
or psychological abuse; PTSD, including from 
military service; and information, when applica-
ble, as to whether the person served was a victim, 
perpetrator, or witness.
13.p. Behavioral health programs should identify 
and treat nicotine use in the population served 
for a variety of reasons. Its use can worsen symp-
toms of depression, anxiety, and schizophrenia, 
and nicotine withdrawal can trigger or worsen 
mental health symptoms. It can also interfere 
with treatment and the effectiveness of medica-
tions. In the treatment of substance use disorders, 
studies have shown that there is a 2–3 times 
greater likelihood of return to use after treatment 
if nicotine use continues.
13.q. Programs should look holistically at the risk 
factors of the person served for suicide, violence, 
or other risky behaviors. Personnel are encour-
aged to look across life domains in a thorough 
clinical assessment to evaluate current intent and 
plans as well as risk factors that indicate the need 
for development of a safety plan with the person 
served.
13.q.(1) Typically, suicide assessments evaluate:
n Risk factors:
– Current psychiatric illness/symptoms.
– Alcohol and/or other drug use.
– Serious physical or emotional pain.
– Previous self-harm or suicide attempts.
– Suicide attempts or completion in close 
family/support network.
– Age, gender, and social situation.
– Relationships that may be supportive/
protective or that may pose a threat 
(abuse or neglect).
– Lack of adequate coping skills/
mechanisms.
– Financial difficulties.
– Access to lethal methods.
n Current intent and plans:
–W i s h  t o  b e  d e a d .
– Feelings of hopelessness.

– Regret/remorse over current/previous 
attempt.
– Expectation about outcome of self-harm -
ing behavior or suicide attempt/threat.
–S p e c i f i c  p l a n s .
– Lethality and frequency of plans or 
attempts.
–O t h e r  s e l f - h a r m i n g  b e h a v i o r .
– Current suicide intent/wishes.
– Length of time suicidal feelings have been 
present.
– Mental state at time of self-harm or suicide 
attempt or threat (alcohol or drug intake, 
social situation, relationship changes, 
bereavements).
– Plans for others after death, including 
suicide notes, changes to will, and 
consequences.
13.q.(2) This could include, but is not limited to, 
such things as cutting, needle sharing, unpro-
tected sex, driving at excessive speeds, driving 
under the influence, and extreme sports.
13.q.(3) Programs assess the possibility that the 
person served may harm others in their living 
environment or other environments. To assist 
this assessment, programs might consider the use 
of the Historical, Clinical, Risk-Management-20 
Version 3 (HCR-20) for assistance in evaluating 
risks.
2.B. 14. The assessment process includes the 
preparation of a written interpretive 
summary that:
14.B. Screening and Access
a. Is based on the assessment data.
14.a.B. Screening and Access
b. Identifies any:
14.b.B. Screening and Access
(1) Disorders.
14.b.(1)B. Screening and Access
(2) Co-occurring disorders.
14.b.(2)B. Screening and Access
(3) Co-morbidities.
14.b.(3)B. Screening and Access
(4) Risks for suicide, violence, or

14.b.(4)B. Screening and Access

ning process.
14.c.B. Screening and Access
Intent Statements
The interpretive summary is a written clinical 
formulation designed to integrate and interpret 
from a broader perspective all history and assess-
ment information collected. It identifies needs 
and addresses how they are considered in the 
person-centered planning process.
Examples
The interpretive summary could address:
n The central theme(s) apparent in the presen-
tation of the person served.
n Histories and assessments (medical, psycho-
social, spiritual, or vocational), with special 
emphasis on potential interrelationships 
between sets of findings.
n The perception of the person served of indi-
vidual needs, strengths, limitations, and 
problems.
n Clinical judgments regarding both positive 
and negative factors likely to affect the per
-
son’s course of treatment and clinical 
outcomes after discharge (i.e., recovery).
n Recommended treatments, safety planning 
needs, any special assessments or tests, and 
routine procedures (e.g., laboratory tests).
n A general discussion of the anticipated level 
of
 care, length, and intensity of treatment 
and expected focus (goals) with 
recommendations.

SECTION 2.C. PERSON-CENTERED PLANNING
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 141
C. Person-Centered 
Planning
Description
Each person served is actively involved in and 
has a significant role in the person-centered 
planning process and determining the direction 
of the plan. The person-centered plan contains 
goals and objectives that incorporate the unique 
strengths, needs, abilities, and preferences of the 
person served, as well as identified challenges 
and potential solutions. The planning process is 
person-directed and person-centered. The per-
son-centered plan may also be referred to as an 
individual service plan, treatment plan, or plan 
of care. In a family-centered program, the plan 
may be for the family and identified as a family-
centered plan.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable 
Standards on page 112 to determine how these 
standards will be applied to the core program or ser-
vice areas for which you are seeking accreditation.
2.C. 1. The person-centered planning process is:
1.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. Conducted with:
1.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) The active participation of the 
person served.
1.a.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) The involvement of family/legal 
guardian of the person served, 
when applicable and permitted.
1.a.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
(3) The involvement of other service/
treatment providers, when 
applicable.
1.a.(3)C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Based on the information from the 
assessment process.
1.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
c. Based upon the person’s:
1.c.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) Strengths.
1.c.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) Needs.
1.c.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
(3) Abilities.
1.c.(3)C.Person-Centered Planning
(4) Preferences.
1.c.(4)C.Person-Centered Planning
d. Focused on the integration and inclu -
sion of the person served into:
1.d.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) The community.
1.d.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) The family, when appropriate.
1.d.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
(3) Natural support systems.
1.d.(3)C.Person-Centered Planning
(4) Other needed services.
1.d.(4)C.Person-Centered Planning
e. Communicated to the person served 
in a manner that is understandable.
1.e.C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to ensure a robust 
and inclusive planning process. The input of the 
person served is combined with the information 
collected during the assessment process result-
ing in an individualized person-centered 
planning process that is designed to help the 
person achieve the best possible recovery.
1.d. This standard requires both integration and 
inclusion, which is interpreted to mean that the 
person is present at and participates in integrated 
settings and situations.
Examples
1.c.(1) Personal strengths may include assets, 
resources, and natural positives.
1.c.(2) Individualized needs may include liabili-
ties, weaknesses, and what the person needs to 
recover.
1.c.(3) Abilities and/or interests may include 
skills, aptitudes, capabilities, talents, and 
competencies.
1.c.(4) Preferences are those things the person 
served feels will enhance the treatment 
experience.
1.d.(3) Natural supports may include extended 
family, friends, volunteer organizations, self-help 
or support groups, churches or other religious/
spiritual supports.

planning process:

or parent/guardian.

of:
