(a) Legal requirements.
2.a.(5)(a)C.Person-Centered Planning
(b) Legally imposed fees.
2.a.(5)(b)C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Is provided to the person served, 
when applicable.
2.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
The person-centered plan includes two main 
components, the first of which addresses the 
global needs of the person served. The organi-
zation demonstrates, through the identification 
of goals, its knowledge and awareness of the 
critical global needs of the person served. This 
component includes goals expressed in the 
words of the person served and is based on the 
person’s needs and preferences. While goals 
written in clinical terms may also be required, it 
is expected that these goals will be understand-
able to the person served.
The second component of the plan provides the 
blueprint for individual service development and 
is consistent with the outcomes expected by the 
person served and the organization. This includes 
the development of clinical service or treatment 
objectives that are measurable and time specific.
CARF does not require the person-centered plan-
ning process be documented on a single “form.” 
The components of the planning process should 
be documented in a manner that ensures consis-
tency and continuity of care and that can be 
shared with the person served.
2.a.(5)(b) This standard refers to any 
court-ordered restitution or fines.
Examples
The person-centered plan may vary in size and 
complexity based on the type of service provided. 
In a short-term crisis program, such as crisis 
intervention, crisis stabilization, or withdrawal 
management, the plan may address only the 
immediate stabilization of the person served 
and the transition to other services.
2.a.(1)(a) The words of the person served may be 
quoted, paraphrased, written by the individual, 
or described. Direct quotes are not required.

SECTION 2.C. PERSON-CENTERED PLANNING
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 143
2.C. 3. The program implements written pro-
cedures identifying timeframes for 
creating, reviewing, and modifying docu-
mentation for person-centered planning 
that ensures for each person served it:
3.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. Reflects current issues.
3.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Maintains relevance.
3.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
Person-centered planning meets the needs of 
the person served through regular review and 
updating based on ongoing assessments. Pro-
grams consider the minimum frequency for 
review of plans based on the intensity of services 
and external requirements. Goals, objectives, and 
interventions are updated to remain current and 
relevant.
2.C. 4. When assessment identifies a potential 
risk for suicide, violence, or other risky 
behaviors, a safety plan is completed:
4.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. In accordance with an established 
protocol.
4.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
b. With the person served.
4.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
c. As soon as possible.
4.c.C.Person-Centered Planning
d. That includes:
4.d.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) Warning signs.
4.d.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) Current coping skills.
4.d.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
(3) Actions to be taken to:
4.d.(3)C.Person-Centered Planning
(a) Respond to periods of 
increased emotional pain.
4.d.(3)(a)C.Person-Centered Planning
(b) Make the environment safer.
4.d.(3)(b)C.Person-Centered Planning
(4) Preferred interventions neces -
sary for: 
4.d.(4)C.Person-Centered Planning
(a) Personal safety.
4.d.(4)(a)C.Person-Centered Planning
(b) Public safety.
4.d.(4)(b)C.Person-Centered Planning
(5) Persons/supports who are avail -
able to assist, when needed.
4.d.(5)C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
The assessment should gather sufficient informa-
tion to determine when the person served has 
risk factors consistent with risky activities or 
potential for self-harm or harm to others, as noted 
in Standard 2.B.13.q. The program should estab-
lish a formal protocol that describes when 
personal safety plans are clinically indicated 
and should assist the provider and supports to 
provide methods for responding to dangerous 
behaviors exhibited by the person served in a 
safe and effective manner.
Examples
A personal safety plan may be referred to as a 
crisis intervention or behavioral management 
treatment plan, a crisis plan, or may be referred 
to in a psychiatric advance directive.
An example of a safety plan template that can be 
used is the Stanley-Brown Safety Plan found at: 
https://suicidepreventionlifeline.org/wp-con-
tent/uploads/2016/08/
Brown_StanleySafetyPlanTemplate.pdf.
Other tools that can be considered for use to 
assist persons served can be found at: 
www.moodtools.org.
4.d.(3)(b) Acute episodes of strong suicidal 
ideation are generally brief, and often people 
choose a suicide method that is readily available. 
Reducing access to the most lethal means can 
increase survival even when people make 
attempts.
2.C. 5. When assessment identifies that the 
person served currently uses nicotine 
products, the program offers one or 
more of the following:
5.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. Education on the health risks of 
nicotine, the potential impact on 
the person’s recovery, and long term 
health.
5.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Counseling and support.
5.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
c. Medications to support reduction or 
elimination of use.
5.c.C.Person-Centered Planning
d. Smoking cessation services.
5.d.C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
Nicotine has been shown to interfere with the 
treatment of many mental health conditions. The 
higher incidence of smoking in this population 
leads to poorer outcomes for persons served. 
Studies* have shown that continued use of nico-
tine after treatment for substance use disorders 
contributes to significant likelihood of return to 
use of alcohol/drugs. It is expected that programs 
address this condition concurrent with treatment 
as they are in a unique position to have an impact 

SECTION 2.C. PERSON-CENTERED PLANNING
144 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
while working on the treatment of other mental 
health or substance use disorders.
*Weinberger, A. H., Platt, J., Esan, H., Galea, S., 
Erlich, D., & Goodwin, R. D. (2017). Cigarette 
Smoking Is Associated With Increased Risk of 
Substance Use Disorder Relapse: A Nationally 
Representative, Prospective Longitudinal Investi-
gation. The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, 78(2), 
e152-e160.
2.C. 6. When the person served has concurrent 
disorders or disabilities and/or 
comorbidities:
6.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. The person-centered planning pro -
cess specifically addresses these 
conditions in an integrated manner.
6.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Services are provided by personnel, 
either within the organization or by 
referral, who are qualified to provide 
services for persons with concurrent 
disabilities and/or disorders.
6.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
Given the incidence of concurrent disabilities 
and/or disorders, effectively addressing these is 
critical to successful recovery. The intent of this 
standard is that when the assessment identifies 
concurrent needs, they are addressed either 
through provision of service by the organization 
or referral to other providers.
Examples
6.a. In addition to behavioral health issues, such 
as addictions or mental illness, and intellectual 
or other developmental disabilities, concurrent 
disabilities and disorders include all chronic 
medical conditions for which the organization 
will provide or ensure monitoring/treatment.
2.C. 7. Progress notes:
7.C.Person-Centered Planning
a. Document:
7.a.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) Progress toward achievement of 
identified:
7.a.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(a) Objectives.
7.a.(1)(a)C.Person-Centered Planning
(b) Goals.
7.a.(1)(b)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) Significant events or changes in 
the life of the person served.
7.a.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
(3) The delivery and outcomes of 
specific interventions, modali-
ties, and/or services that support 
the person-centered plan.
7.a.(3)C.Person-Centered Planning
(4) Changes in:
7.a.(4)C.Person-Centered Planning
(a) Frequency of services.
7.a.(4)(a)C.Person-Centered Planning
(b) Levels of care.
7.a.(4)(b)C.Person-Centered Planning
b. Are:
7.b.C.Person-Centered Planning
(1) Signed.
7.b.(1)C.Person-Centered Planning
(2) Dated.
7.b.(2)C.Person-Centered Planning
Intent Statements
The progress notes are signed and dated by each 
individual making an entry into the record. A 
reviewer of the progress notes is able to readily 
identify the goals and objectives that were 
achieved or revised during the reporting period, 
occurrences in the life of the person served that 
may impact the course of treatment or service, 
and the specific services and interventions that 
the organization has provided.
Examples
7.b. The use of initials would not meet the intent 
of the standard unless a signature sheet is used 
to verify the person signing. Electronic systems 
that restrict or automatically identify the person 
entering the data and the date the information 
is entered will conform to the intent of this 
standard. 
7.b.(2) Dated refers to the month, day, and year, 
but does not require the specific time of day.

SECTION 2.D. TRANSITION/DISCHARGE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 145
D. Transition/Discharge
Description
Transition, continuing care, or discharge plan-
ning assists the persons served to move from one 
level of care to another within the organization 
or to obtain services that are needed but are not 
available within the organization. The transition 
process is planned with the active participation 
of each person served. Transition may include 
planned discharge, placement on inactive status, 
movement to a different level of service or inten-
sity of contact, or a re-entry program in a 
criminal justice system. 
The transition plan is a document developed 
with and for the person served and other inter-
ested participants to guide the person served 
in activities following transition/discharge to 
support the gains made during program partici-
pation. It is prepared with the active participation 
of the person served when transitioning to 
another level of care, after-care program, or 
community-based services. The transition plan 
is meant to be a plan that the person served uses 
to identify the support that is needed to prevent 
a recurrence of symptoms or reduction in func-
tioning. It is expected that the person served 
receives a copy of the transition plan.
A discharge summary is a clinical document 
written by the program personnel who are 
involved in the services provided to the person 
served and is completed when the person leaves 
the organization (planned or unplanned). It is a 
document that is intended for the record of the 
person served and released, with appropriate 
authorization, to describe the course of services 
that the organization provided and the response 
by the person served.
Just as the assessment is critical to the success of 
treatment, the transition services are critical for 
the support of the individual’s ongoing recovery 
or well-being. The organization proactively 
attempts to connect the persons served with the 
receiving service provider and contact the per-
sons served after formal transition or discharge 
to gather needed information related to their 
post-discharge status. Discharge information is 
reviewed to determine the effectiveness of its 
services and whether additional services were 
needed.
Transition planning may be included as part of 
the person-centered plan. The transition plan 
and/or discharge summary may be a combined 
document or part of the plan for the person 
served as long as it is clear whether the infor-
mation relates to transition or pre-discharge 
planning or identifies the person’s discharge or 
departure from the program.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable Stan-
dards at the beginning of this section to determine 
how these standards will be applied to the core 
program or service areas for which you are seeking 
accreditation.
2.D. 1. The program implements written proce-
dures for:
1.D.Transition/Discharge
a. Referrals.
1.a.D.Transition/Discharge
b. Transfer to another level of care.
1.b.D.Transition/Discharge
c. Transfer to other services.
1.c.D.Transition/Discharge
d. Discharge.
1.d.D.Transition/Discharge
e. Follow-up.
1.e.D.Transition/Discharge
f. Identifying:
1.f.D.Transition/Discharge
(1) When transition planning will 
occur.
1.f.(1)D.Transition/Discharge
(2) The person responsible for 
coordinating the transfer or 
discharge.
1.f.(2)D.Transition/Discharge
(3) Actions to be taken to ensure 
coordination with external 
organizations when completing 
transfers or discharges.
1.f.(3)D.Transition/Discharge
(4) Where the following are 
documented:
1.f.(4)D.Transition/Discharge
(a) Transitional planning.
1.f.(4)(a)D.Transition/Discharge
(b) Discharge summary.
1.f.(4)(b)D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
Core programs often refer to other providers to 
more effectively meet the needs of persons 
served. Programs also may transfer persons to 
other programs to continue care once they have 
achieved the expected gains in the current pro-
gram or if it is discovered that the person needs 
additional services or care. These referrals or 
transfers can be fraught with challenges to their 
successful completion. Programs should ensure 

SECTION 2.D. TRANSITION/DISCHARGE
146 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
that they have systems and processes that effec-
tively complete the process and that there is 
adequate coordination and communication to 
meet the needs of the persons served.
1.f.(1) Transition planning should be initiated as 
soon as is clinically appropriate to meet the needs 
of persons served, but recognizes that this deci-
sion is based on a variety of factors. In programs 
that are more intensive in nature, transition plan-
ning often begins upon admission in order to find 
an appropriate and available placement. In other 
programs designed for more long-term manage-
ment of chronic illness, persons served may 
continue without transition planning for long 
periods of time, unless there is a change in status. 
The program’s written procedures should 
describe the timeframe used that considers all 
relevant factors with the earliest possible plan-
ning as its goal.
Transition
2.D. 2. Transition planning is initiated with the 
person served as soon as clinically appro-
priate in the person-centered planning 
and service delivery process.
2.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
Ideally, transition planning is done prior to or at 
the onset of treatment. The transition period is 
particularly critical in that it tends to place many 
pressures on the person served, the organization, 
and the community. Therefore, adequate prepa-
ration requires giving the person served more 
than routine notice of pending transition from or 
completion of the program. It is necessary that 
there be early and active involvement by the 
person served, the family, referral sources, and 
other community agencies that will be serving 
the person. Transition services are particularly 
critical when adolescents are reaching the age 
of majority and will require ongoing services in 
adulthood. 
In programs such as health homes, integrated 
behavioral health/primary care, or programs 
managing behavioral health conditions in which 
there is not an expectation of discharge, there 
might be only minimal transition planning for a 
person served who drops out of care.
Examples
Transition planning for persons with intellectual 
disabilities should be a part of planning from the 
beginning. Persons are given supports to explore 
other options that are available and connected 
with resources for growth, such as self-advocacy 
groups.
Clinical indication recognizes that in certain 
programs, transition planning may be delayed.
In longer-term programs, such as assertive 
community treatment, initial planning may focus 
on engagement as opposed to transition. In a 
membership-based program, transitioning may 
be a choice of the person served. In an opioid 
treatment program, the preferred goal may be 
continued engagement as opposed to transition.
2.D. 3. The written transition plan:
3.D.Transition/Discharge
a. Is prepared or updated to ensure a 
seamless transition when a person 
served:
3.a.D.Transition/Discharge
(1) Is transferred to  another level of 
care or an aftercare program.
3.a.(1)D.Transition/Discharge
(2) Prepares for a planned discharge.
3.a.(2)D.Transition/Discharge
b. Identifies the person’s current:
3.b.D.Transition/Discharge
(1) Progress in recovery or move 
toward well-being.
3.b.(1)D.Transition/Discharge
(2) Gains achieved during program 
participation.
3.b.(2)D.Transition/Discharge
c. Identifies the person’s need for 
support systems or other types of 
services that will assist in continuing 
recovery, well-being, or community 
integration.
3.c.D.Transition/Discharge
d. Includes informat ion on the continu-
ity of the person’s medication(s), 
when applicable.
3.d.D.Transition/Discharge
e. Includes referral information, such 
as contact name, telephone number, 
locations, hours, and days of services, 
when applicable.
3.e.D.Transition/Discharge
f. Includes communication of inform -
ation on options and resources 
available if symptoms recur or addi
-
tional services are needed, when 
applicable.
3.f.D.Transition/Discharge
g. Includes:
3.g.D.Transition/Discharge

SECTION 2.D. TRANSITION/DISCHARGE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 147
(1) Strengths.
3.g.(1)D.Transition/Discharge
(2) Needs.
3.g.(2)D.Transition/Discharge
(3) Abilities.
3.g.(3)D.Transition/Discharge
(4) Preferences.
3.g.(4)D.Transition/Discharge
h. Identifies the person responsible 
for coordinating the transfer or 
discharge.
3.h.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
An essential concept of this standard is to ensure 
an effective transition when a person served is 
transferred to another level of care, another 
component of care, or is discharged from the 
program.
Examples
A transition plan may be identified by another 
title such as continuing care plan, referral plan, 
discharge plan, or aftercare plan (see the Glossary 
for definition). Transition planning may be docu-
mented in progress notes, through a revision of 
the person’s plan, or in a separate document.
In some programs, a similar plan may be pre-
pared when the person served is placed on 
inactive status. There may be times when the 
transition plan is incorporated into the person-
centered service/treatment plan of the person 
served.
It is recognized that there may be times when 
the person served chooses to abruptly leave a 
program and transition planning is not possible. 
In those cases, documentation would include a 
discharge summary.
3.g.(1) Personal strengths may include assets, 
resources, and natural positives.
3.g.(2) Individualized needs may include liabili-
ties, weaknesses, and what the person needs to 
recover.
3.g.(3) Abilities and/or interests may include 
skills, aptitudes, capabilities, talents, and 
competencies.
3.g.(4) Preferences are those things the person 
served feels will enhance the treatment 
experience.
3.h. The program retains the responsibility of 
ensuring the transfer to another program is 
completed successfully and the person served 
engages in the next program, when applicable. 
The program identifies in the plan the person 
responsible for managing the transition so the 
person served knows who to contact when there 
are barriers or other challenges to a successful 
transition.
2.D. 4. The written transition plan is: 
4.D.Transition/Discharge
a. Developed with the input and 
participation of:
4.a.D.Transition/Discharge
(1) The person served.
4.a.(1)D.Transition/Discharge
(2) The family/legal guardian, when 
applicable and permitted.
4.a.(2)D.Transition/Discharge
(3) A legally authorized represen -
tative, when appropriate.
4.a.(3)D.Transition/Discharge
(4) Team members.
4.a.(4)D.Transition/Discharge
(5) The referral source, when appro -
priate and permitted.
4.a.(5)D.Transition/Discharge
(6) Other community services, when 
appropriate and permitted.
4.a.(6)D.Transition/Discharge
b. Given to individuals who participate 
in the development of the transition 
plan, when permitted.
4.b.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
4.b. A copy of the plan is provided to the person 
served and other transition planning participants 
as permitted when beneficial to the person 
served and as an assist to the referral source 
or the receiving program(s).
2.D. 5. The program implements procedures 
for referrals and transfers:
5.D.Transition/Discharge
a.  That ensure that the process is 
effectively completed.
5.a.D.Transition/Discharge
b. To monitor the program’s 
effectiveness.
5.b.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is for programs to be 
creative in finding methods of care transition 
that keep the persons served engaged in the 
process and to effectively transition into the next 
program. These efforts ensure that the gains in 
recovery made during the program are not lost 
and protect the safety of the persons served. 
When persons served are referred or transferred 
to another program upon completion, they 
frequently are unsuccessful in becoming fully 
engaged in the new program. This can have 

SECTION 2.D. TRANSITION/DISCHARGE
148 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
significant negative impacts on gains made to 
reduce symptoms and increase functioning. It is 
the responsibility of the program to ensure that 
these care transitions are effectively completed, 
and if not, to follow up and seek to re-engage the 
person into care.
5.b.  The program should engage in activities to 
monitor its overall effectiveness in the perfor-
mance of referrals and transfers. Programs should 
be able to identify their baseline effectiveness 
in these areas and seek to improve over time.
Examples
The program could follow up with persons 
served to ensure that they were able to success-
fully access the next program, and if not, help 
them to effectively navigate any barriers. The 
program might communicate with the next pro-
gram to verify that persons served have entered 
the program and provide any assistance needed 
to help with the transition. When there are gaps 
in time between programs, providing personal 
contact to the persons served to support them 
during this period can be encouraging and lead 
to success in accessing ongoing care.
Discharge
2.D. 6. For all persons leaving services, a written 
discharge summary is prepared that:
6.D.Transition/Discharge
a. Includes the date of admission.
6.a.D.Transition/Discharge
b. Describes the services provided.
6.b.D.Transition/Discharge
c. Identifies the presenting condition.
6.c.D.Transition/Discharge
d. Describes the extent to which estab -
lished goals and objectives were 
achieved.
6.d.D.Transition/Discharge
e. Describes the reasons for discharge.
6.e.D.Transition/Discharge
f. Lists recommendations for services or 
supports.
6.f.D.Transition/Discharge
g. Includes the date of discharge from 
the program.
6.g.D.Transition/Discharge
h. Includes information on medica -
tion(s) prescribed or administered, 
when applicable.
6.h.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
A discharge summary is a tool that facilitates con-
tinuity of care and serves to document a baseline 
which may be helpful for future service provision.
Examples
6.d. This could include gains achieved by the per-
sons served during program participation, strides 
made by the person served in the recovery pro-
cess, or any positive move toward well-being.
6.f. This should include referral resource infor-
mation, contact name, telephone number, and 
hours and days of operation.
2.D. 7. When an unplanned discharge occurs, 
follow-up is conducted as soon as possi
-
ble to:
7.D.Transition/Discharge
a. Provide necessary notifications.
7.a.D.Transition/Discharge
b. Clarify the reasons for the unplanned 
discharge.
7.b.D.Transition/Discharge
c. Determine with the person served 
whether further services are needed.
7.c.D.Transition/Discharge
d. Offer or refer to needed services.
7.d.D.Transition/Discharge
Intent Statements
The program makes an effort to contact persons 
who drop out of care to determine whether fur-
ther services are needed and if the person served 
needs referral to other services.
Examples
7.a. Notifications may be required when the 
person served is referred under legal process 
such as a commitment or court-ordered services, 
when the person served is under legal custody 
or guardianship, or under certain contracts for 
services.

SECTION 2.E. MEDICATION USE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 149
E. Medication Use
Description
Medication use is the practice of controlling, 
administering, and/or prescribing medications to 
persons served in response to specific symptoms, 
behaviors, or conditions for which the use of 
medications is indicated and deemed efficacious. 
The use of medication is one component of 
treatment directed toward maximizing the 
functioning of the persons served while reducing 
their specific symptoms. Prior to the use of medi-
cations other therapeutic interventions should be 
considered, except in circumstances that call for a 
more urgent intervention. 
Medication use includes all prescribed medica-
tions, whether or not the program is involved in 
prescribing, and may include over-the-counter or 
alternative medications. Alternative medications 
can include herbal or mineral supplements, vita-
mins, homeopathic remedies, hormone therapy, 
or culturally specific treatments.
Medication control is identified as the process 
of physically controlling, storing, transporting, 
and disposing of medications, including those 
self-administered by the person served.
Medication administration is the preparing and 
giving of prescription and nonprescription medi-
cations by authorized and trained personnel to 
the person served. Self-administration is the 
application of a medication (whether by oral 
ingestion, injection, inhalation, or other means) 
by the person served to the individual’s own 
body. This may include the program storing the 
medication for the person served, personnel 
handing the bottle or prepackaged medication 
dose to the person served, instructing or verbally 
prompting the person served to take the medica-
tion, coaching the person served through the 
steps to ensure proper adherence, and/or closely 
observing the person served self-administering 
the medication.
Prescribing is the result of an evaluation that 
determines if there is a need for medication and 
what medication is to be used in the treatment of 
the person served. Prior to providing a prescrip-
tion for medication, the prescriber obtains the 
informed consent of the individual authorized to 
consent to treatment and, if applicable, the assent 
of the person served. Prescription orders may 
be verbal or written and detail what medication 
should be given to whom, in what formulation 
and dose, by what route, when, how frequently, 
and for what length of time.
NOTE: CARF has determined that the use of Narcan/
Naloxone should be handled as a first-aid supply 
and not a medication. Therefore, the Medication 
Use standards are not applicable when these 
medications are used as a life-saving measure. 
CARF
 expects that the medications are secured, 
but readily accessible when needed, and at least 
some program personnel are trained on their use 
and administration.
Applicable Standards
All policies, procedures, and practices related 
to medication use are implemented consistent 
with federal, state/provincial, and other juris-
dictional laws, regulations, and licensure 
requirements. The standards in this section 
apply as follows:
n Programs that provide medication control, 
administration, or prescribing must
 apply 
Standards 1.–9. as follows:
– Programs that provide medication 
control: Standards 1.–5.
– Programs that provide medication 
administering: Standards 1.–6.
– Programs that provide medication pre -
scribing: Standards 1.–2., 4., and 6.–9.

SECTION 2.E. MEDICATION USE
150 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual

with medication use.

medication the person served is

1.b.(6)E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
As medication use is often an important compo-
nent of care and part of the management of 
symptoms, all direct service personnel should 
have training on these elements to support the 
needs of persons served as a part of the multi-
disciplinary team.
Training and education for direct service person-
nel may vary based on roles and responsibilities, 
type of program and population served, or other 
factors.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 151

2.b.(16)E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
Education about medications for persons served 
and family members of the person served is 
focused on the specific medications prescribed, 
administered, or controlled. It should be con-
ducted by personnel with the appropriate 
background within their scope of practice. When 
a program prescribes medications, this education 
is a part of the informed consent process. Docu-
mentation of education related to medications 
may include: elements deemed necessary and 
appropriate by the program to the specific needs 
of the person served; ensuring the person served, 
family members, or others identified by the 
person served understand the medication(s) 
prescribed or administered; and what to do or 
who to contact in the event of medication related 
questions or concerns.
Examples
2.a.(2) Based on the age or competency of the 
person served, training and education may need 
to be provided to others involved in the adminis-
tration or monitoring of medications used by the 
person served.
2.b.(2) Risks include medications that may be 
habit forming and adverse reactions to medica-
tions such as allergic reactions, bleeding, loss of 
consciousness, etc.
2.b.(4) Side effects might include drowsiness, 
skin irritation, headache, nausea, dry mouth, etc.
2.b.(8) Potential obstacles might include the 
cost of obtaining a medication if an individual’s 
insurance doesn’t cover it, partially covers it, or 
a generic is not available.
2.b.(13) Examples of nonprescription medica-
tions that may potentially cause a drug reaction 
include over-the-counter medications such as 
cold or flu remedies, as well as alternative medi-
cations such as herbal supplements and culturally 
specific treatments prescribed by traditional 
healers (medicine men or women, curanderas, 
and shamans). Alcohol, nicotine, caffeine, and 
illegal drugs may also cause reactions.

3.e.(7) Some programs require that nonprescrip-
tion medications may be used by persons served

family/caregiver) in their original packaging. 
Some programs limit the use of nonprescription 
medications even further to only bottles or pack-
ages that have never been opened, while other 
programs may not allow any use of nonprescrip-
tion medications unless they are supplied by the 
program.

b. Active involvement of the persons 
served, when able, or members of 
the family when appropriate, in 
making decisions related to the 
use
 of medications.
6.b.E. Medication Use
c. Availability of consultation 24  hours 
a day, 7 days a week from a physician, 
pharmacist, or qualified professional 
licensed to prescribe.
6.c.E. Medication Use

including:

 medications.

6.s.E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
6.c. Consultation can be obtained through direct 
employment, contract, consultant agreements, or 
medical facility agreements. Programs may use 
telepsychiatry or telemedicine as a method of 
obtaining consultation.
6.g. Out-of-home placements refers to children 
and youth in the foster care system who have 
been removed from their homes and put into 
a placement. When a program serves this 
population, its written procedures take into 
consideration the disproportionate data on 
medications prescribed for children and youth 
in the foster care system compared to those who 
are not.
6.k. Procedures for laboratory studies, tests, or 
other monitoring procedures are implemented 
in accordance with established practices in 
medicine.
6.l. Documentation may include formal assess-
ment or observation by appropriate medical 
personnel. Based on the setting and population 
served, the program determines the appropriate 
frequency for ongoing assessment of abnormal 
involuntary movements in persons served.
6.o. A medication is considered diverted when 
a person who legally obtains a prescription sub-
sequently transfers the medication to another 
person for use or distribution. When prescribing 
a medication that has a strong potential for 
abuse, the prescriber should consider the risk of 
diversion of the medication based on the history, 
support network, and financial circumstances of 
the person served.
6.p. Stockpiling of medication refers to inappro-
priate and sometimes excessive accumulation 
of prescribed medication for later use. There are 
a number of risks associated with this practice. 
Persons served may continue to take a medica-
tion after a change in dose or medication has 
been ordered, creating uncertainty about the 
effectiveness of the changed order. Persons 
served who stockpile medications may not 
adequately attend to expiration dates on med-
ications and take them after their effectiveness 
has diminished. Additionally, stockpiling medica-
tions may be a precursor to suicide or self-injury. 
Programs can consider methods to address these 
concerns either systemically or on a case-by-case 
basis.

SECTION 2.E. MEDICATION USE
154 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
6.q. Emergencies related to the use of medica-
tions can present in several forms; e.g., allergic 
reactions, medication errors, and overdoses, and 
having procedures to address them is vital to 
maintaining a healthy and safe environment.
Examples
6.b. Decisions related to the use of medications 
may include initial consent or assent to begin a 
medication, decisions related to dosage changes, 
withdrawal of consent or assent due to adverse 
reactions, etc.
6.e. Other drug use includes legal and illegal 
drugs.
6.m. Depending upon the primary care an indi-
vidual receives, the program may be coordinating 
with an identified physician, a physician practice 
or clinic, a nurse practitioner, etc. The procedure 
might require that unsuccessful attempts for the 
coordination of care with the primary care physi-
cian be documented.
6.n. Examples of indicators a program that does 
not prescribe (and is therefore not subject to peer 
review of its prescribing practices) might track in 
its performance measurement and management 
system include the percentage of persons served 
on prescription medications, the average number 
of medications persons served are taking, and the 
number of medication and/or dosage changes 
during time in the program. If a program con-
tracts with several prescribers, knowing which 
one(s) tend to add or remove medications may 
be valuable. If a program serves children and 
youth in the foster care system as well as children 
and youth who are not, it may look at whether 
children/youth in foster care are medicated at a 
higher rate.
6.p. Behaviors may include requesting early 
refills of a medication, alleging that a prescription 
was lost and therefore could not be filled, alleging 
that medication fell down the sink or into the 
toilet, etc. Programs may require that at each 
visit all medications are brought in for recon-
ciliation with the amount prescribed, may 
conduct random drug screens, or may conduct 
random mouth checks post administration. 
Some prescribers will only prescribe medications 
that can be administered through long-acting IM 
or IV injection or through oral ingestion to be 
given at a clinic.
6.r. Programs may assist the persons served to 
obtain medications by applying for free or 
discounted medication programs and patient 
assistance programs available through pharma-
ceutical companies or linking the persons served 
to physicians or other qualified professionals 
licensed to prescribe.
6.s. Biohazards associated with the administra-
tion of medications include items that have been 
contaminated with blood or other bodily fluids, 
such as needles, gloves, bandages, bedding, dia-
pers, etc.
2.E. 7. A program that provides prescribing of 
medications demonstrates the use of 
treatment guidelines and protocols that 
promote prescribing consistent with 
standards of care.
7.E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
There is emerging consensus in psychiatry and 
other medical disciplines on best practices in 
medication prescribing, including the use of 
guidelines, algorithms, and protocols, as well as 
the evaluation of the efficacy and safety of new 
medications. Each program regularly monitors 
and evaluates these practice trends in the field, 
including consideration of the use of formularies 
to measure cost effectiveness to the person 
served.
Examples
Standards of care might consider the diagnostic 
conditions, age, or gender of the persons served 
as well as the level or setting of care.
2.E. 8. A program that provides prescribing 
of medications implements written 
procedures that address:
8.E. Medication Use
a. Screening for common medical 
comorbidities.
8.a.E. Medication Use
b. Evaluation of coexisting medical 
conditions for potential medication 
impact.
8.b.E. Medication Use
c. Identification of potential medica -
tion interactions.
8.c.E. Medication Use

SECTION 2.E. MEDICATION USE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 155
d. Documentation of:
8.d.E. Medication Use
(1) Informed consent for each medi -
cation prescribed.
8.d.(1)E. Medication Use
(2) Assent for each medication pre -
scribed, when possible.
8.d.(2)E. Medication Use
e. The expected course of use for 
each medication, including 
discontinuation.
8.e.E. Medication Use
f. Ongoing reassessment of the current 
medication profile.
8.f.E. Medication Use
g. Use of a Prescription Drug Monitoring 
Program (PDMP), when available.
8.g.E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
8.c. Medication interactions may occur between 
any types of medications, including prescribed 
medications, over-the counter medications, and 
alternative medications. Prescribers must have a 
current, accurate account of all medications a 
person served is taking prior to them being able 
to safely prescribe.
8.d.(1) The documentation for each medication 
prescribed does not require separate documents 
for each medication.
8.d.(2) The concept of assent is agreement and is 
applicable when a person served does not have 
legal authority to give informed consent. This 
relates to Standard 2.A.7. regarding legal deci-
sion-making authority. 
8.g. Consulting a PDMP , when available in the 
United States, is particularly important when 
prescribing medications at risk of misuse, abuse, 
or diversion.
Examples
8.a. The American Psychiatric Association 
(www.psychiatry.org) and the American 
Diabetes Association (www.diabetes.org) have 
published consensus guidelines identifying the 
frequency and types of laboratory tests and 
metabolic screenings appropriate for persons 
prescribed antipsychotic medications.
8.d. Evidence of informed consent for prescribed 
medications may include signed consent forms, 
a notation by the prescriber in the record of the 
person served that the medication has been dis-
cussed and agreed upon, or medication(s) to be 
prescribed listed on an individualized plan that 
is actively developed with the person served.
The process of seeking informed consent 
includes a number of important steps and con-
siderations on the part of the prescriber such as:
n Assessing the person’s understanding of the 
condition and the need for medication.
n Discussing the risks and benefits of the medi-
cation, including both common and serious 
side effects.
n Discussing the alternatives to medication, 
such as lifestyle changes or other treatments, 
when appropriate and/or applicable.
n Answering any questions the person served 
has about the medication.
n Documenting the person’s consent/assent to 
prescribe the medication.
Additionally, it is helpful to the persons served 
when the prescriber:
n Uses clear and plain language that the person 
can understand.
n Avoids medical jargon to the extent possible.
n Demonstrates patience and takes the time to 
answer the person’s questions.
n Ensures the person has the opportunity to ask 
questions before giving consent.
8.f. Balancing the benefits and risks of the medi-
cations a person served might be taking, ongoing 
reassessment considers whether:
n The person served obtained and is taking the 
medication as prescribed.
n The medication is having the intended effect.
n There are side effects or adverse reactions that 
need to be addressed.
n The dosage is appropriate or requires 
adjustment.
n To continue/discontinue a medication.

9.b.(3)E. Medication Use
Intent Statements
The peer review is applicable whether the pro-
gram employs or contracts with prescribers and 
whether they are full- or part-time. The review is 
conducted by a qualified professional with legal 
prescribing authority or a pharmacist who is not 
immediately responsible for the prescribing 
process but is able to provide feedback to the 
prescribing practitioners. The persons conduct-
ing the peer review possess content expertise 
in working with the medication protocols used 
within the program for the population(s) served.
9.a.(1) The minimum frequency for peer review 
is annually, although the program may determine 
that a more frequent review is appropriate based 
on the risks associated with the population 
served, the addition of new prescribers, introduc-
tion of new protocols, etc. The resources required 
to conduct more frequent peer reviews may be 
offset, in part, by the reviews including fewer 
records and therefore taking less time.
9.a.(3) Refer to the Glossary for the definition of 
representative sample.
9.a.(5)(c) Medical personnel often refer to this as 
copharmacy.
Examples
Programs that have only one prescriber may 
choose to partner with another program, pre-
scriber, organization, or pharmacy to accomplish 
the peer review process. Programs that contract 
with prescribers may rotate their reviews among 
the prescribers, ensuring prescribers are not 
reviewing their own records. Some programs 
have engaged local medical and pharmacy stu-
dents/interns under the appropriate supervision 
for assistance with the peer review process. This 
provides the medical and pharmacy students/
interns with practical experience and provides 
the program a link to medical professionals with 

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 157
current knowledge on new medications and the 
latest research on existing medications. 
9.a.(5)(b) Monitoring may refer to laboratory 
studies, tests, or other monitoring procedures 
or to protocols such as frequency of visits.
F. Promoting Nonviolent 
Practices
Description
CARF-accredited programs strive to create 
learning environments for the persons served 
and to support the development of skills that 
build and strengthen resiliency and well-being. 
The establishment of quality relationships 
between personnel and the persons served 
provides the foundation for a safe and nurturing 
environment. Providers are mindful of creating 
an environment that cultivates:
n Engagement.
n Partnership.
n Holistic approaches.
n Nurturance.
n Respect.
n Hope.
n Self-direction.
It is recognized that persons served may require 
support to fully benefit from their services. This 
may include, but is not limited to, praise and 
encouragement, verbal prompts, written expec-
tations, clarity of rules and expectations, or 
environmental supports.
Even with support there are times when persons 
served may demonstrate signs of fear, anger, or 
pain that could lead to unsafe behaviors. Person-
nel are trained to recognize and respond to these 
behaviors through various interventions, such as 
changes to the physical environment, sensory-
based calming strategies, engagement in mean-
ingful activities, redirection, active listening, 
approaches that have been effective for the indi-
vidual in the past, etc. When these interventions 
are not effective in de-escalating a situation and 
there is imminent risk to the person served or 
others, seclusion or restraint may be used to 
ensure safety. Seclusion and restraint are never 
considered treatment interventions; they are 
always considered actions of last resort.
As the use of seclusion or restraint creates 
potential physical and psychological risks to the 
persons subject to the interventions, to the per-
sonnel who administer them, and to those who 

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
158 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
witness the practice, an organization that utilizes 
seclusion or restraint should have the elimination 
thereof as its goal.
Seclusion refers to restriction of the person 
served to a segregated room or space with the 
person’s freedom to leave physically restricted. 
Voluntary time out is not considered seclusion, 
even though the voluntary time out may occur 
in response to verbal direction; the person served 
is considered in seclusion only if freedom to leave 
the segregated room or space is denied.
Restraint is the use of physical force or mechani-
cal means to temporarily limit a person’s freedom 
of movement; chemical restraint is the involun-
tary emergency administration of medication as 
an immediate response to a dangerous behavior. 
The following are not considered restraints for 
the purposes of this section of standards:
n Assistive devices used for persons with physi-
cal or medical needs. 
n Briefly holding a person served, without 
undue force, for the purpose of comforting 
the individual or to prevent self-injurious 
behavior or injury to others.
n Holding a person’s hand or arm to safely guide 
the individual from one area to another or 
away from another person. 
n Security doors designed to prevent elopement 
or wandering. 
n Security measures for forensic purposes, 
such
 as the use of handcuffs instituted by law 
enforcement personnel. When permissible, 
consideration is given to removal of physical 
restraints while the person is receiving ser
-
vices in the behavioral healthcare setting.
n In a correctional setting, the use of seclusion 
or restraint for purposes of security. 
Seclusion or restraint by trained and competent 
personnel is used only when other, less restrictive 
measures have been ineffective to protect the 
person served or others from unsafe behavior. 
Peer restraint is not an acceptable alternative to 
restraint by personnel. Seclusion or restraint is 
not used as a means of coercion, discipline, con-
venience, or retaliation or in lieu of adequate 
programming or staffing.
Applicable Standards
All policies, procedures, and practices related 
to seclusion and restraint are implemented 
consistent with federal, state/provincial, and 
other jurisdictional laws, regulations, and 
licensure requirements. Programs that use 
seclusion or restraint must also apply Standards 
1.–11. in this section.
2.F. 1. Policies are implemented that specify:
1.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Seclusion or restraint is used only as 
a safety intervention of last resort to 
prevent harm to the person served or 
others.
1.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. Seclusion or restraint is not used as 
coercion, discipline, convenience, or 
retaliation by personnel.
1.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. Seclusion or restraint is not used in 
lieu of adequate programming or 
staffing.
1.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
d. Orders for all seclusion or restraint 
are administered by personnel 
who
 are competent in the proper 
techniques.
1.d.F. Nonviolent Practices
e. Standing orders authorizing the use 
of seclusion or restraint are not 
issued.
1.e.F. Nonviolent Practices
f. Persons served are released from 
seclusion or restraint immediately 
upon determination that the risk of 
harm is no longer present.
1.f.F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
1.f. As seclusion or restraint is used only to pro-
tect the person served or others from harm, the 
person is released from seclusion or restraint as 
soon as it is safe to do so. Persons served are 
never held in seclusion or restraint for fixed 
amounts of time or as discipline.

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 159
2.F. 2. All personnel involved in the direct 
administration of seclusion or restraint 
receive documented, competency-based

entities qualified to conduct such 
training:
2.F . Nonviolent Practices
a. At orientation.
2.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. At least annually.
2.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. That addresses:
2.c.F . Nonviolent Practices
(1) The circumstances under which 
seclusion or restraint is indicated.
2.c.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) Interventions to be used for 
seclusion or restraint that 
minimize harm, including:
2.c.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) Interventions done by an 
individual.
2.c.(2)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) Interventions done by a 
team.
2.c.(2)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices
(3) Signs of physical distress in a 
person who is being secluded 
or
 restrained.
2.c.(3)F. Nonviolent Practices
(4) Risks of seclusion or restraint:
2.c.(4)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) To the persons served.
2.c.(4)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) To personnel.
2.c.(4)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices
(c) Including:
2.c.(4)(c)F. Nonviolent Practices
(i) Physical risks.
2.c.(4)(c)(i)F. Nonviolent Practices
(ii) Psychological risks.
2.c.(4)(c)(ii)F. Nonviolent Practices
(5) First aid.
2.c.(5)F. Nonviolent Practices
(6) Cardiopulmonary resuscitation 
(CPR).
2.c.(6)F. Nonviolent Practices
(7) How to continually assess for the 
earliest release of the seclusion 
or restraint.
2.c.(7)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Competency-based training addresses all meth-
ods of seclusion and/or restraint used by the 
program, including physical, mechanical, or 
chemical restraint.
Examples
Programs may choose to use a formalized 
training program developed for the purpose of 
behavior management and crisis intervention. 
Many programs offer a variety of training 
options, including those that can be customized 
and provided on-site as well as multi-day certifi-
cation courses at various locations. Most training 
programs also offer a “train the trainer” option to 
allow individuals to receive specialized, advanced 
training so they are then qualified to train other 
personnel within the program. Formalized 
training programs typically offer a certification 
indicating that the individual has successfully 
passed a competency evaluation of the skills 
and coursework included in the training.
Many programs have found it beneficial to incor-
porate the viewpoint of persons served in the 
development and/or delivery of training, allow-
ing their voices of experience to add a dimension 
to the training that is often overlooked.
2.b.–c. Ongoing training is intended to refresh 
and enhance the competency of personnel. While 
the program may review important material that 
has been previously covered, it may also focus 
on specific items in response to data collected 
throughout the year. Data from critical incident 
reports and debriefings are often good sources 
to identify areas for performance improvement. 
Ongoing training is also beneficial to address any 
changing characteristics or needs of the popula-
tion served, as well as introduce new techniques 
that have been developed and changes in regula-
tory requirements.
2.F. 3. The program implements a plan to elimi-
nate the use of seclusion and/or restraint 
that:
3.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Includes:
3.a.F . Nonviolent Practices
(1) The role of leadership.

(2) Use of data to inform practice.
3.a.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(3) Development of a workforce 
culture that supports resiliency 
and well-being.
3.a.(3)F. Nonviolent Practices
(4) Input regarding the use of seclu -
sion and/or restraint from:
3.a.(4)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) Persons served.
3.a.(4)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) Families.
3.a.(4)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices
(c) Advocates.
3.a.(4)(c)F. Nonviolent Practices
(5) Consideration of the results of 
the debriefing process.
3.a.(5)F. Nonviolent Practices
(6) Identification of environmental 
factors that may contribute to 
unsafe behaviors.

(7) Actions to be taken to minimize

3.c.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
d. Is updated as needed.
3.d.F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Refer to the Glossary for the definition of plan.
The program strives to eliminate the use of 
seclusion and restraint. Depending on the needs 
of the persons served and the type of program 
provided, elimination may not occur, but the 
program is always looking at what could be 
done differently to progress in that direction. If 
a program uses both seclusion and restraint, it is 
expected that the plan addresses elimination of 
both. 
3.a.(3) Workforce culture includes attitudes, skills, 
and practices.
3.a.(5) Details of the debriefing process are 
addressed in Standard 9.
Examples
3.a.(2) Examples may include data from critical 
incident reports and information from debrief-
ings as well as input received from the leadership 
reviews of each event.
2.F. 4. Written procedures for seclusion and 
restraint are implemented that include 
protocols for:
4.F . Nonviolent Practices
a. Children and youth.
4.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. Adults.
4.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. Special populations.
4.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
d. Individual interventions.
4.d.F. Nonviolent Practices
e. Team interventions, including:
4.e.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) Defining team leadership.
4.e.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) Assigning team duties.
4.e.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Written procedures and protocols address all 
methods of restraint used by the program, incl-
uding physical, mechanical, or chemical restraint.
4.a.–b. The program is expected to have pro-
tocols for each of these populations unless it 
excludes children, youth, or adults through its 
written scope, program description, admission 
criteria, or other mechanism. 
Examples
4.c. Special populations might include persons 
who have medically complex needs, older adoles-
cents transitioning to adulthood, LGBTQ+, foster 
children and youth, older adults, or programs 
that specialize in working with persons who have 
autism, persons with a history of sexual offense, 
and victims of trafficking or domestic abuse.
2.F. 5. Written procedures are implemented 
that address:
5.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Risk assessment of each person 
served:
5.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) Including:
5.a.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) Medical history.
5.a.(1)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) Trauma history.
5.a.(1)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices
(c) History of unsafe behaviors 
resulting in seclusion or 
restraint.
5.a.(1)(c)F. Nonviolent Practices
(d) Identification of interven -
tions that have been 
successful in interrupting 
unsafe behaviors, when 
applicable.
5.a.(1)(d)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) That results in identification of:
5.a.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) Risks associated with the 
potential use of seclusion 
or restraint.
5.a.(2)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) Precautions to be taken.
5.a.(2)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 161
b. When applicable, identification of 
actions to be taken by personnel 
to de-escalate unsafe behaviors, 
including: 
5.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) Documentation in the record of 
the person served.
5.b.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) Communication with program 
personnel.
5.b.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
When a program uses seclusion or restraint, the 
assessment process addresses the risks associ-
ated with potentially utilizing these interventions 
with the persons served. While some or all of this 
information may have been gathered as part of 
the assessment process addressed in Section 2.B. 
Screening and Access to Services, the focus of this 
standard is on identification of the specific risks to 
each person served and the precautions to be 
taken should seclusion or restraint become 
necessary. 
The information gathered on the person’s history 
of unsafe behaviors and interventions that have 
been effective in de-escalating those behaviors, 
including the person’s input on preferred inter-
ventions, guides identification of what 
interventions can be used in future situations 
to avoid the use seclusion or restraint and/or 
minimize risk to the person served. It is important 
that personnel are aware of this information in 
advance of circumstances that may require inter-
vention. The risk assessment may be done by the 
program or obtained from an external resource, 
or the required information may be gathered 
through a combination thereof.
Examples
5.a.(1)(a) This aspect of the risk assessment iden-
tifies whether the person served has any medical 
conditions that could interfere with the program’s 
use of seclusion or restraint; e.g., a person who 
has recently broken a limb may be at higher risk 
of additional injury if a traditional four point 
restraint is used, or a child who has asthma that 
is triggered by anxiety may be at higher risk of 
an adverse reaction if seclusion is used.
5.a.(1)(b) This aspect of the risk assessment 
addresses whether the person served has a his-
tory of trauma and how that may impact the 
program’s use of seclusion or restraint. For exam-
ple, if a person served has been the victim of an 
assault by a group of males, consideration is given 
to whether intervention by female personnel or 
not replicating the same number of persons 
involved in the assault could make a difference 
if restraint becomes necessary for the safety of 
the person served or others.
2.F. 6. When seclusion or restraint is used, 
documentation in the record of the 
person served demonstrates:
6.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Less-restrictive interventions were 
attempted prior to the use of seclu-
sion or restraint.
6.a.F . Nonviolent Practices
b. Administration in a safe manner, with 
consideration given to the history of 
the person served.
6.b.F . Nonviolent Practices
c. Personnel communication to the per -
son served that the purpose of the 
seclusion or restraint is to keep the 
individual and others safe.
6.c.F . Nonviolent Practices
d. Monitoring by trained personnel in 
accordance with established proto-
cols, including continual face-to-face 
monitoring when there is simultane-
ous use of seclusion and restraint.
6.d.F . Nonviolent Practices
e. Ongoing reevaluation of the person 
served to determine whether seclu-
sion or restraint is still needed.
6.e.F . Nonviolent Practices
f. Removal of the person served from 
the seclusion or restraint as soon 
as
 the threat of harm is no longer 
present.
6.f.F. Nonviolent Practices
g. Immediate medical attention for any 
injury resulting from seclusion or 
restraint.
6.g.F . Nonviolent Practices
h. Notification as soon as possible of the 
initial use of seclusion or restraint to:
6.h.F . Nonviolent Practices
(1) The family.
6.h.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) The treating practitioner.
6.h.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
Examples
6.d. Monitoring protocols often vary between 
persons in seclusion or in restraint. Some pro-
grams use a 15-minute monitoring schedule for 
persons in restraint and check vital signs, circula-
tion, hydration, and elimination needs; the level 
of distress and agitation of the person served; 

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
162 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
mental status, cognitive functioning, skin integ-
rity, nutritional needs, range of motion, and other 
care needs particular to the individual.
2.F. 7. Written procedures regarding orders are 
implemented that specify:
7.F . Nonviolent Practices
a. Seclusion or restraint is ordered by 
a physician or advanced practice 
professional who has training and 
competence in the prevention and 
management of unsafe behaviors.
7.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. A physician or advanced practice 
professional provides face-to-face 
evaluation of the person served 
within one hour of the order for 
seclusion or restraint being given.
7.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. An order for seclusion or restraint 
does not exceed one hour for a child 
or youth or four hours for an adult.
7.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
d. Orders for renewal may only occur 
following a face-to-face evaluation 
by a physician or advanced practice 
professional.
7.d.F. Nonviolent Practices
e. Orders for seclusion or restraint may 
be renewed for a total of up to 24 
hours.
7.e.F. Nonviolent Practices
f. After 24 hours, a new order is 
required following a face-to-face 
evaluation by a physician or 
advanced practice professional.
7.f.F. Nonviolent Practices
g. All orders are entered into the record 
of the person served as soon as possi-
ble but not more than two hours after 
implementation.
7.g.F. Nonviolent Practices
h. The physician or advanced practice 
professional signs all orders within 
the time period mandated by law.
7.h.F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Orders for seclusion or restraint are written in 
response to current behaviors and the need for 
immediate safety. There should not be “standing 
orders” or “PRN” orders for seclusion or restraint. 
Face-to-face evaluations assess the physical, 
emotional, and psychological well-being of the 
person served and help determine potential 
approaches to avoid the need for future seclusion 
or restraint.
2.F. 8. If there is a room designated for the use 
of seclusion or restraint:
8.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. It provides for:
8.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) The safety of the person served.
8.a.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) Continuous, face-to-face 
observation.
8.a.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(3) Access to bathroom facilities, 
directly or through escort.
8.a.(3)F. Nonviolent Practices
b. It promotes the privacy and dignity 
of the person served.
8.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. There is an identified procedure for 
exit in case of emergency.
8.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
The physical environment safely and humanely 
accommodates the intervention of seclusion and/
or restraint.
8.a.(2) Personnel are available to immediately 
respond to signs of distress.
2.F. 9. Following the use of seclusion or 
restraint, a debriefing process:
9.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Is initiated as soon as possible and 
no more than 24 hours after the 
incident.
9.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. Includes, unless contraindicated:
9.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) The person served.
9.b.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) All involved personnel.
9.b.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(3) Family members.
9.b.(3)F. Nonviolent Practices
(4) Others observing the incident, 
when permitted.
9.b.(4)F. Nonviolent Practices
c. Is documented, including:
9.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) A description of the incident.
9.c.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) From the perspective of the 
person served, what the person 
served experienced.
9.c.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
(3) The antecedents of the incident.
9.c.(3)F. Nonviolent Practices
(4) An assessment of contributing 
factors.
9.c.(4)F. Nonviolent Practices
(5) Actions taken by personnel in 
an attempt to avoid the use of 
seclusion or restraint.
9.c.(5)F. Nonviolent Practices
(6) The reasons for the use of seclu -
sion or restraint.
9.c.(6)F. Nonviolent Practices
(7) The specific intervention used.
9.c.(7)F. Nonviolent Practices

SECTION 2.F. PROMOTING NONVIOLENT PRACTICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 163
(8) The person’s reaction to the 
intervention.
9.c.(8)F. Nonviolent Practices
(9) Actions that could make future 
use of seclusion or restraint 
unnecessary.
9.c.(9)F. Nonviolent Practices
(10) Modifications made to the indi -
vidualized plan to address issues 
or behaviors that impact the 
need to use seclusion or restraint, 
as applicable.
9.c.(10)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
The debriefing process is conducted in a way that 
is least traumatizing to the person served, family 
members, personnel, and others who were 
involved in or witnessed the use of seclusion or 
restraint. While the intent is to learn from the 
debriefing process, it may be determined that 
participation of a particular individual in the 
process is contraindicated. During a survey, the 
program is prepared to explain its rationale if this 
has occurred. 
The format of the debriefing process may vary as 
long as all of the elements of the standard are 
addressed. For example, rather than having 
everyone meet together at the same time, infor-
mation for the debriefing may be gathered from 
the person served through a one-on-one conver-
sation separate from the other parties involved or 
via phone call with family members or personnel 
who are not on site at the time the debriefing 
occurs.
Examples
A person served may be excused from the 
debriefing process if there is concern that it may 
be too traumatizing to relive the details of the 
intervention or the person needs more time to 
deal with feelings toward personnel who admin-
istered the seclusion or restraint.
Specific personnel may be excluded from the 
debriefing process if there is concern about their 
own trauma response to the incident or they 
request to not participate.
2.F. 10. A written procedure is implemented that 
addresses leadership review of all
 uses of 
seclusion or restraint:
10.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. After every occurrence.
10.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. Within a designated timeframe.
10.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
c. To determine:
10.c.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) Adherence to applicable policies 
and procedures.
10.c.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) The need for performance 
improvement.
10.c.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Reviews are conducted in accordance with the 
timeframe specified in the written procedure.
Examples
The organization identifies who will conduct the 
review. Some organizations may have the execu-
tive director or CEO conduct the reviews, while 
others may delegate the responsibility to program 
leadership, a risk manager, or other personnel 
with appropriate knowledge of seclusion and 
restraint.
2.F. 11. A documented analysis of the program’s 
use of seclusion and/or restraint: 
11.F. Nonviolent Practices
a. Is conducted at least annually.
11.a.F. Nonviolent Practices
b. Addresses:
11.b.F. Nonviolent Practices
(1) Trends, including:
11.b.(1)F. Nonviolent Practices
(a) Patterns of use.
11.b.(1)(a)F. Nonviolent Practices
(b) History of use by personnel.
11.b.(1)(b)F. Nonviolent Practices
(c) Environmental contributing 
factors.
11.b.(1)(c)F. Nonviolent Practices
(d) Program design contribut -
ing factors.
11.b.(1)(d)F. Nonviolent Practices
(2) Areas in need of performance 
improvement.
11.b.(2)F. Nonviolent Practices
Intent Statements
Seclusion and restraint are subject to extensive 
review. As noted in Standards 9. and 10., each use 
of seclusion or restraint is reviewed through a 
debriefing process within 24 hours of occurrence 
as well as by leadership. In addition, aggregate 
information is analyzed at least annually for 
trends. Information from these reviews is consid-
ered in identifying opportunities for performance 
improvement, including formulation of the plan 

SECTION 2.G. RECORDS OF THE PERSONS SERVED
164 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
to eliminate the use of seclusion and/or restraint 
addressed in Standard 3.
The minimum frequency for review of aggregate 
information is annually, although the leadership 
or program may determine that a more frequent 
review is indicated.
G. Records of the Persons 
Served
Description
A complete and accurate record is developed 
to ensure that all appropriate individuals have 
access to relevant clinical and other information 
regarding each person served.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable 
Standards on page 112 to determine how these 
standards will be applied to the core program or ser-
vice areas for which you are seeking accreditation.
2.G. 1. The organization implements policies 
and procedures regarding information 
to be transmitted to other individuals or 
agencies that include:
1.G. Records of Persons Served
a. The identification of information 
that can legally be shared without 
an authorization for release of 
information.
1.a.G. Records of Persons Served
b. Forms to authorize release of infor -
mation that:
1.b.G. Records of Persons Served
(1) Comply with applicable laws.

(2) Identify, at a minimum:
1.b.(2)G. Records of Persons Served
(a) The name of the person 
about whom information 
is
 to be released.
1.b.(2)(a)G. Records of Persons Served
(b) The content to be released.
1.b.(2)(b)G. Records of Persons Served
(c) To whom the information is 
to be released.
1.b.(2)(c)G. Records of Persons Served
(d) The purpose for which the 
information is to be released.
1.b.(2)(d)G. Records of Persons Served
(e) The date on which the 
release is signed.
1.b.(2)(e)G. Records of Persons Served

upon which the authoriza-

1.b.(2)(f)G. Records of Persons Served

when the authorization can 
be revoked.
1.b.(2)(g)G. Records of Persons Served

c. A description of how it tracks 
information released.

SECTION 2.G. RECORDS OF THE PERSONS SERVED
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 165
1.c.G. Records of Persons Served
Intent Statements
Organizations in the United States submitting or 
maintaining information in electronic formats 
regarding the persons served need to pay par-
ticular attention to requirements of HIPAA and 
42 CFR Part 2 (PIPEDA/FOIPA in Canada). Auth-
orization to share information is documented 
and specifically refers to the information being 
transmitted. Signed authorization forms that 
are not specific or that are “boilerplates” will not 
meet this standard.
The standards do not address the specific 
instances in which it is necessary to have a signed 
release-of-information form. The intent of this 
standard is that, if the organization is providing 
any information that identifies a person served, 
it has an authorized release-of-information form 
completed, unless exempted by law. However, 
this does not mean that there should be a sepa-
rate release form for every instance (every phone 
call or conversation with the same agency) in 
which information is released; one release per 
agency or person, with a time limitation, is suffi-
cient. There are occasions when signed release-
of-information forms are required by law.
Examples
1.b.(2)(f) Release forms stipulate the expiration 
date by either providing an actual date or by indi-
cating that the release is valid for only a specific 
amount of time from the date it was signed.
Typically, the authorization will not exceed one 
year. However, some laws may require that the 
authorization for release of information be for 
the tenure of a specific relationship; i.e., during 
the length of a person’s time on probation or 
parole.
2.G. 2. The individual record communicates 
information in a manner that is:
2.G. Records of Persons Served
a. Organized.
2.a.G. Records of Persons Served
b. Clear.
2.b.G. Records of Persons Served
c. Complete.
2.c.G. Records of Persons Served
d. Current.
2.d.G. Records of Persons Served
e. Legible.
2.e.G. Records of Persons Served
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is that the records 
be organized in a systematic way to ensure that 
information is readily accessible. CARF does not 
prescribe any particular type of organizing or 
filing system.
Examples
2.c. Complete refers to a central record contain-
ing information regarding all the services the 
person receives. This is considered the main 
record.
2.G. 3. All documents generated by the organi-
zation that require signatures include 
original or electronic signatures.
3.G. Records of Persons Served
Intent Statements
Written signatures are defined as full signatures, 
not initials. Electronic systems that restrict or 
automatically identify the person entering the 
data and the date the information is entered will 
conform to the intent of this standard.
2.G. 4. The individual record includes:
4.G. Records of Persons Served
a. The date of admission.
4.a.G. Records of Persons Served
b. Contact information for the indiv -
idual’s personal representative, 
conservator, guardian, or represen-
tative payee, if any of these have 
been appointed.
4.b.G. Records of Persons Served
c. Contact information for the person to 
notify in the event of an emergency.
4.c.G. Records of Persons Served
d. The name of the person currently 
coordinating the services of the 
person served.
4.d.G. Records of Persons Served
e. The location of any other records, 
if applicable.
4.e.G. Records of Persons Served
f. Information about the individual’s 
primary care physician or similar 
medical practitioner, including, 
when
 available:
4.f.G. Records of Persons Served
(1) Name.
4.f.(1)G. Records of Persons Served
(2) Address.
4.f.(2)G. Records of Persons Served
(3) Telephone number.
4.f.(3)G. Records of Persons Served
g. Financial agreement with the person 
served.
4.g.G. Records of Persons Served
h. Healthcare reimbursement informa -
tion, if applicable.
4.h.G. Records of Persons Served
i. Health history.
4.i.G. Records of Persons Served
j. Current medications.
4.j.G. Records of Persons Served

166 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
k. Preadmission screening, 
when conducted.
4.k.G. Records of Persons Served
l. Documentation of orientation.
4.l.G. Records of Persons Served
m. Assessments.

4.v.G. Records of Persons Served
