The records of the person served should be com-
plete and accurate; however, it is noted that 
individual records will contain only those items 
relevant to the person served.
Examples
4.f. Depending on the primary care an individual 
receives, the program may be coordinating with 
an identified physician, a physician practice or 
clinic, a nurse practitioner, etc.
4.g. This could include situations where the 
person’s fee is paid by Medicaid, Medicare, etc.
4.q. A transition plan would not be relevant if 
the person served left services without notice.
4.s. There should be a discharge summary for all 
persons who have left an organization’s services.
2.G. 5. Entries to the records of the persons 
served follow the organization’s policy 
that specifies timeframes for entries.
5.G. Records of Persons Served
Intent Statements
The program implements practices to ensure that 
entries in the records follow the policy and writ-
ten procedures defined in Standard 1.E.3.f. It is 
expected that the organization demonstrates 
activities that monitor the timeliness of entries 
and provides feedback to personnel.
Examples
Timeframes are needed for treatment planning 
and can be based on federal, state, provincial, or 
funding source requirements.

SECTION 2.H. QUALITY RECORDS MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 167
H. Quality Records 
Management
Description
The organization implements systems and proce-
dures that provide for the ongoing monitoring of 
the quality, appropriateness, and utilization of the 
services provided. This is largely accomplished 
through a systematic review of the records of the 
persons served. The review assists the organiza-
tion in improving the quality of services provided 
to each person served.
NOTE: Please refer to the grid of Applicable 
Standards on page 112 to determine how these 
standards will be applied to the core program or ser-
vice areas for which you are seeking accreditation.
2.H. 1. The program conducts a documented 
review of the services provided:
1.H. Records Mgmt
a. At least quarterly.
1.a.H. Records Mgmt
b. That addresses, as evidenced by the 
record of the person served:
1.b.H. Records Mgmt
(1) The quality of service delivery.
1.b.(1)H. Records Mgmt
(2) Appropriateness of services.
1.b.(2)H. Records Mgmt
(3) Patterns of service utilization.
1.b.(3)H. Records Mgmt
(4) Model fidelity, when an evi -
dence-based practice is 
identified.
1.b.(4)H. Records Mgmt
c. By personnel who are trained and 
qualified.
1.c.H. Records Mgmt
d. On a representative sample of per -
sons served that includes:
1.d.H. Records Mgmt
(1) Current records.
1.d.(1)H. Records Mgmt
(2) Closed records.
1.d.(2)H. Records Mgmt
Intent Statements
The procedures for review of services include:
n Oversight of the review process by 
the management of the program.
n Use of individual reviewers who carry out 
professional functions and who may be 
either internal or external to the program. 
A committee is not required to carry out the 
professional reviews.
n In small programs and small remote sites, the 
accomplishment of reviews through ongoing 
supervision and case review, a system of quar-
terly peer review, or the use of an outside 
reviewer on a quarterly basis.
Examples
1.b.(4) In programs that use evidenced-based 
practices, the quarterly review includes key 
components of the model. This might include 
frequency of services, delivery of specific curric-
ulum, or implementation of specific protocols 
for handling particular behaviors. For example, 
if a program uses cognitive behavioral therapy 
(CBT), there would be evidence of identification 
of core beliefs, dysfunctional assumptions, and 
interventions that counter them. If motivational 
interviewing is used, there would be evidence 
of the stage of change and progress made. The 
documentation would show that the clinician is 
implementing the model. 
The procedures for review of services may 
include:
n Oversight of the review process by the man-
agement of the organization.
n Use of individual reviewers who carry out 
professional functions and who may be either 
internal or external to the organization. A 
committee is not required to carry out the 
professional reviews.
n In small organizations and small remote sites, 
the accomplishment of reviews through ongo-
ing supervision and case review, a system of 
quarterly peer review, or the use of an outside 
reviewer on a quarterly basis.
In a program with short lengths of stay, or in 
situations where the records will not be available 
following discharge, a review of the records of 
the persons served may include a review of the 
records of those persons discharged during the 
current quarter as well as persons currently being 
served.
In programs serving few people or having long 
terms of treatment, it may only be possible to 
review closed records annually.
1.d. See the Glossary for the definition of repre-
sentative sample.
There are tools available through software such as 
Excel® to help determine a representative sample 
to enhance validity in records review.

SECTION 2.H. QUALITY RECORDS MANAGEMENT
168 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
2.H. 2. Of records selected for review, the per-
sons responsible for providing the 
service/treatment are not:
2.H. Records Mgmt
a. Solely responsible for the selection of 
records to be reviewed.
2.a.H. Records Mgmt
b. A reviewer of their own records.
2.b.H. Records Mgmt
Examples
Creative means may be used if there is a limited 
number of qualified personnel available to per-
form a review. For example, if there is only one 
therapist on staff, an organization may invite 
another similarly qualified individual who works 
in the geographic area to review the quality of the 
person-centered plans. The organization should 
consult state/provincial/territorial/tribal and fed-
eral confidentiality regulations with regard to 
who can assist in the review of services and the 
confidentiality assurances necessary for the 
review to be completed.
2.H. 3. The program implements a systematic 
review of records that: 
3.H. Records Mgmt
a. Is based on priority areas identified 
by the program.
3.a.H. Records Mgmt
b. Considers regulatory and/or contrac -
tual requirements.
3.b.H. Records Mgmt
c. Ensures that documentation of 
services is completed in accordance 
with the organization’s policies.
3.c.H. Records Mgmt
Intent Statements
The program determines what it will review 
based on a systematic process that is informed by 
past review results, external audit results, or areas 
of contractual emphasis. The program should be 
able to demonstrate how it manages its planning 
process to determine the elements included in 
the reviews.
This type of review is often referred to as a quality 
assurance or peer review, and it focuses on the 
care of the persons served on a case-by-case 
basis. It provides an opportunity for professional 
staff members (and qualified others) to objec-
tively review and suggest alternative program 
or service strategies to the team responsible for 
establishing and carrying out the person’s indi-
vidual program.
Examples
3.a. Priority areas the program could identify for 
review include:
n Persons served were provided with an appro-
priate orientation and were actively involved 
in making informed choices regarding the 
services they received. 
n Confidential information was released 
according to applicable laws and regulations.
n Assessments of the persons served were thor-
ough, complete, and timely.
n Risk factors were adequately addressed and 
resulted in safety plans when appropriate.
n Goals and service/treatment objectives were 
based on the results of the assessments and 
the input of the person served, and were 
revised when indicated.
n The services provided were related to the 
goals and objectives in the person’s plan and 
reflect appropriate levels of care and reason-
able duration.
n Individual transition plans and discharge 
summaries were completed, when applicable.
3.c. An area the organization may include for 
review in accordance with organizational policies 
is the timeliness of documentation in the records 
of the persons served.
2.H. 4. The organization demonstrates that the 
information collected from its records 
review process is:
4.H. Records Mgmt
a. Reported to personnel.
4.a.H. Records Mgmt
b. Used to:
4.b.H. Records Mgmt
(1) Identify personnel training 
needs.
4.b.(1)H. Records Mgmt
(2) Improve the quality of its services 
through performance improve-
ment activities.
4.b.(2)H. Records Mgmt
(3) Ensure compliance with reg -
ulatory and/or contractual 
requirements.
4.b.(3)H. Records Mgmt
(4) Improve conformance to the 
CARF standards.
4.b.(4)H. Records Mgmt

SECTION 2.I. SERVICE DELIVERY USING INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGIES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 169
Intent Statements
The quality record review should be used to iden-
tify training needs and to improve the quality of 
services.
Examples
Evidence of quality improvement could be 
demonstrated through the provision of training 
to personnel, revision of policies, development 
of new processes or protocols, or other organiza-
tional change.
4.a. The information that is shared with person-
nel can be used in clinical supervision, in quality 
improvement activities that benchmark individ-
ual performance, or in other appropriate 
channels designed to improve quality.
4.b.(1) Information gathered should be assessed 
for potential training needs.
4.b.(2) Quality improvement may be demon-
strated through:
n Revisions to overall programmatic design, 
curriculum, equipment, environment, or 
delivery methods.
n Personnel competencies, staffing ratios, or 
consultation/affiliation resources available 
or needed by the program(s).
n Financial resource planning or allocation to 
program(s).
n Correlation between the summarized results 
of these reviews and results obtained from 
the organization’s performance improvement 
system. This may also include modifying a 
performance improvement system to more 
accurately track the outcomes, progress, or 
effectiveness of a program on those served.
I. Service Delivery Using 
Information and 
Communication 
Technologies
Description
Based on the individual plan for the person 
served, the use of information and communica-
tion technologies (ICT) allows personnel to see, 
hear, and/or interact with persons served, family/
support system members, and other organiza-
tions in or from remote settings (i.e., the person 
served and personnel are not in the same physical 
location). Depending on the type of program, a 
variety of terminology may be used to describe 
the use of information and communication tech-
nologies to deliver services (e.g., telepractice, 
telehealth, telemental health, telerehabilitation, 
virtual meeting, virtual services, remote monitor-
ing, etc.).
The provision of services via information and 
communication technologies may:
n Include services such as assessment, indiv-
idual planning, monitoring, prevention, 
intervention, family services, transition 
planning, follow-up, supervision, education, 
consultation, and counseling.
n Involve a variety of personnel such as case 
managers/service coordinators, social 
workers, psychologists, speech-language 
pathologists, occupational therapists, physi
-
cal therapists, physicians, nurses, dieticians, 
employment specialists, direct support profes
-
sionals, peer support specialists, rehabilitation 
engineers, assistive technologists, teachers, 
and other personnel providing services and/
or supports to persons served.
n Encompass settings such as: 
– Hospitals, clinics, professional offices, and 
other organization-based settings.
– Schools, work sites, libraries, community 
centers, and other community settings.
–C o n g r e g a t e  l i v i n g ,  i n d i v i d u a l  h o m e s ,  a n d  
other residential settings.
n Be provided via fully virtual platforms.

SECTION 2.I. SERVICE DELIVERY USING INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGIES
170 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
The quality and integrity of services delivered via 
ICT should be comparable to services provided 
face to face.
The use of technology for strictly informational 
purposes (such as having a website that provides 
information about the programs and services 
available, meeting reminders, or service delivery 
scheduling) or the use of self-directed apps is 
not considered providing services via the use of 
information and communication technologies. 
Applicable Standards
If the program uses information and communi-
cation technologies (ICT) to deliver services, 
Standards 2.I.1.–7. apply.
 
2.I. 1. The program implements written 
procedures:
1.Service Delivery using ICT
a. That address:
1.a.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Consent of the person served.
1.a.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Audio recording, video recording, 
and photographing the 
person served.
1.a.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) Decision making about the use of 
information and communication 
technologies versus in-person 
services:
1.a.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
(a) At the beginning of services.
1.a.(3)(a)Service Delivery using ICT
(b) Throughout the course of 
services.
1.a.(3)(b)Service Delivery using ICT
b. To confirm that all necessary technol -
ogy and/or equipment is available 
and functions:
1.b.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Prior to the start of service 
delivery.
1.b.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) As needed throughout services.
1.b.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) At the location of the:
1.b.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
(a) Person served.
1.b.(3)(a)Service Delivery using ICT
(b) Provider.
1.b.(3)(b)Service Delivery using ICT
c. To verify at each encounter:
1.c.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) The identity of the person served.
1.c.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) The identity of the provider.
1.c.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) The physical location of the 
person served.
1.c.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
d. To maintain privacy during the deliv -
ery of services.
1.d.Service Delivery using ICT
e. To respond to technology disruption 
that impacts the delivery of services.
1.e.Service Delivery using ICT
Intent Statements
1.a.(1) The program implements written proce-
dures that describe the method(s) used to obtain 
consent of the person served and that satisfy its 
regulatory and payment requirements. Although 
the program may have already obtained consent 
for service delivery, this standard is specifically 
about obtaining consent to participate in service 
delivery using information and communication 
technologies.
1.a.(3) Decision making about whether the use 
of information and communication technologies 
is appropriate for a person served considers the 
person’s preferences, initial and ongoing assess-
ments, including any risk assessments, under 
what circumstances it is safe and effective to 
deliver services at a distance, and reasonable 
expectation of the person benefiting from ser-
vices delivered this way. While it may be 
determined that service delivery via information 
and communication technologies is appropriate 
at the beginning of services, it is important to 
revisit decision making throughout services to 
address changes in the condition or circum-
stances of the person served or the service 
delivery site and the need for in-person (i.e., 
the person served and provider are in the same 
physical location) contact.
1.c.(3) Identifying the physical location of the 
person served is critical should an urgent situa-
tion arise that requires response to that location. 
Based on the identified location of the person 
served, personnel may also assess whether the 
service can be delivered safely where the person 
served is located. The information may also be 
used in verifying the credentials of a provider to 
deliver services in the location of the person 
served and for billing purposes.
1.d. Maintaining privacy during the delivery of 
services includes the person served and the 
provider. If the person served is in a location 
where privacy may be compromised (e.g., a pub-
lic library or community center, walking outside, 
on the job, at home with young children who 
require supervision), the program addresses the 
importance of privacy and makes an effort to 

SECTION 2.I. SERVICE DELIVERY USING INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGIES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 171
help problem solve and identify alternatives with 
the person served. As personnel also could be 
providing services from locations that are suscep-
tible to losses of privacy, they should take steps to 
protect the privacy of the persons served in their 
environment.
1.e. Identifying a back-up method for communi-
cation will reduce the impact of technology 
disruption such as hardware or software failure or 
environmental conditions on service delivery.
Examples
1.a.(1) The procedures may include obtaining 
consent to participate in service delivery via 
information and communication technologies in 
writing, obtaining consent from a parent or other 
legal representative on behalf of a child/adoles-
cent, and obtaining consent via virtual platform 
when applicable.
1.a.(3) The procedures may include regular 
timeframes/intervals to consider decisions 
regarding service delivery via information and 
communication technologies or criteria that 
trigger reassessment under specific conditions.
In addition to risks related to the status of the 
person served, the use of information and 
communication technologies may present risks 
such as access to or the reliability of technology, 
equipment, and the internet; receptivity and 
understanding of the person served regarding the 
use of technology and equipment; availability, 
support, and/or interference of members of the 
family/support system; or safety and/or privacy 
of the location of the person served during ser-
vice delivery.
1.b.(2) There may be a need to confirm avail-
ability and/or function during the course of 
services if new technology or equipment is being 
implemented, there have been problems with 
the technology or equipment during prior 
encounters, or someone new will be responsible 
for operating the equipment or technology. 
1.c.(1) Verification of the identity of the person 
served may be accomplished a variety of ways 
including asking the person’s name, accessing a 
picture in the record of the person served for 
comparison, voice recognition from a prior 
encounter, or asking the person to show a 
picture ID that includes his or her name.
2.I. 2. As appropriate, personnel who deliver 
services via information and communica
-
tion technologies receive documented 
competency-based training on:
2.Service Delivery using ICT
a. How to deliver services effectively 
via information and communication 
technologies, including:
2.a.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Human factors.
2.a.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Crisis response procedures.
2.a.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) Assessment of risk factors in 
the environment of the person 
served.
2.a.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
(4) How to modify treatment tech -
niques/interventions to deliver 
services virtually.
2.a.(4)Service Delivery using ICT
b. Equipment used in service delivery, 
including hardware and software:
2.b.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Features.
2.b.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Setup.
2.b.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) Use.
2.b.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
(4) Maintenance.
2.b.(4)Service Delivery using ICT
(5) Safety considerations.
2.b.(5)Service Delivery using ICT
(6) Infection control.
2.b.(6)Service Delivery using ICT
(7) Troubleshooting.
2.b.(7)Service Delivery using ICT
Intent Statements
For service delivery to be effective, personnel 
are trained how to deliver or adapt services at 
a distance, how to use the equipment and tech-
nology needed to deliver those services, and how 
to guide persons served, members of the family/
support system, and others in the location of the 
person served on their use.
2.a.(2) Responding to a crisis when the person 
served is in a different location requires a dif-
ferent skill set than responding in person. 
Regardless of the activity occurring, a person 
served could experience a medical emergency, 
a fall or other injury, a medication error, suicidal 
ideation, threat from another person at the 
location, or other crisis that requires immediate 
attention. Comprehensive training on crisis 
response procedures helps prepare personnel 
who provide services via information and com-
munication technologies to effectively and 
efficiently respond to the needs of a person 
served who experiences a crisis during an 
encounter.

SECTION 2.I. SERVICE DELIVERY USING INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGIES
172 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
2.a.(4) Most providers have been trained to 
deliver services, treatment, and/or interventions 
in person. With the increasing and, in some cases, 
exclusive provision of services using information 
and communication technologies, providing per-
sonnel with training on how to modify and adapt 
treatment techniques/interventions is essential 
to effective service delivery. Depending upon the 
type of program being provided modifications 
may range from minor to more substantial.
Examples
2.a.(1) Training on human factors may address 
strategies for communication with persons 
served and, if applicable, members of the family/
support system who are in a different location 
than the provider, understanding nonverbal com-
munication, establishing rapport, maintaining 
eye contact, and providing a conducive environ-
ment including lighting, background, and staying 
framed in the field of the camera.
2.a.(2) Training on crisis response procedures 
may include how to identify a crisis situation, 
assessing the situation and the associated level of 
risk, identifying and implementing a response, 
and reporting and following up on the situation 
as appropriate.
2.a.(3) Personnel are trained to observe and 
assess risks in the environment of the person 
served, which will vary depending on the type 
of program and persons served. For example, a 
provider may observe:
n Someone in the environment of the person 
served who speaks or otherwise behaves in 
a way that seems threatening to the person 
served; nonverbal communication on the 
part of the person served signaling a 
threat; new bruising, cuts, or other signs of 
injury; etc.
n Trip hazards such as rugs, electrical cords, 
spills, or clutter for someone with balance, 
sensory, or ambulation concerns or who 
uses equipment such as a walker, cane, or 
wheelchair.
n Guns, knives, or other items that could 
cause harm to the person served.
n Medications that are not properly stored/
secured.
n Lack of supervision for someone with 
cognitive impairment or a child who is 
too
 young to be home alone.
2.b.(6) Infection control addresses equipment 
used at the location of the person served and the 
provider. For example:
n Equipment that touches any part of the body 
or is used to look into someone’s eyes, ears, or 
mouth is properly sanitized between each use.
n The person served and family members in the 
home are instructed in proper handwashing 
technique; shielding coughs and sneezes; and 
the use, if necessary, of gloves or masks to 
minimize risks associated with sharing 
equipment.
n When the person served is using a computer 
at a school or library, the keyboard, mouse, 
and headset are cleaned appropriately before 
they are used.
2.I. 3. As appropriate, instruction and training 
are provided:
3.Service Delivery using ICT
a. To:
3.a.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Persons served.
3.a.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Members of the family/support 
system.
3.a.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) Others.
3.a.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
b. On equipment used in service deliv -
ery, including:
3.b.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) Features.
3.b.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Setup.
3.b.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(3) Use.
3.b.(3)Service Delivery using ICT
(4) Maintenance.
3.b.(4)Service Delivery using ICT
(5) Safety considerations.
3.b.(5)Service Delivery using ICT
(6) Infection control.
3.b.(6)Service Delivery using ICT
(7) Troubleshooting.
3.b.(7)Service Delivery using ICT
c. On creating an appropriate environ -
ment to receive services.
3.c.Service Delivery using ICT

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 173
Examples
3.c. Environmental considerations include:
n Being in a location that is safe, e.g., indoors if 
the weather is bad, parked if sitting in a car, in 
an area that is lit if outside and it is after dark.
n Being in a location that provides privacy, 
whether at home, work, or in a community 
setting such as a library, school, or commu
-
nity center.
n Access to the necessary technology, equip-
ment, and internet service to successfully 
connect.
n Human factors as outlined in the example for 
Standard 2.a.(1).
n Expectations such as being on time and hav-
ing equipment or materials prepared for 
sessions that are scheduled.
2.I. 4. Service delivery via information and 
communication technologies includes:
4.Service Delivery using ICT
a. The provision of information to the 
persons served on:
4.a.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) How to contact the program.
4.a.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) The expected timeframe for 
response.
4.a.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
b. Personnel to provide technical 
assistance with accessing services 
provided by the program.
4.b.Service Delivery using ICT
c. Personnel to address questions 
related to service delivery.
4.c.Service Delivery using ICT
d. Based on identified need:
4.d.Service Delivery using ICT
(1) An appropriate facilitator at the 
location of the person served.
4.d.(1)Service Delivery using ICT
(2) Modification to:
4.d.(2)Service Delivery using ICT
(a) Treatment techniques/
interventions.
4.d.(2)(a)Service Delivery using ICT
(b) Equipment.
4.d.(2)(b)Service Delivery using ICT
(c) Materials.
4.d.(2)(c)Service Delivery using ICT
(d) Environment at the location 
of the person served, 
including:
4.d.(2)(d)Service Delivery using ICT
(i) Accessibility.
4.d.(2)(d)(i)Service Delivery using ICT
(ii) Privacy.
4.d.(2)(d)(ii)Service Delivery using ICT
(iii) Usability of equipment.
4.d.(2)(d)(iii)Service Delivery using ICT
Examples
4.a. The program provides information to the 
persons served on how to contact the program 
and when persons served may expect a response. 
This may be information on program hours of 
operation, methods persons should use to con-
tact the program when they have specific needs, 
the typical timeframe in which the persons 
served can expect a response to inquiries, and 
whether contacts will returned in evenings or 
over weekends.
4.c. Between scheduled sessions persons served 
and others who may be assisting them might 
have questions related to their individual plans, 
e.g., regarding medication use; a home exercise 
program; assisting with transfers; or how to con-
tact other providers, vendors, or support services.
4.d.(1) Depending on the purpose of the session 
and the needs of the person served, professional 
personnel, support personnel, family members, 
or caregivers might function in the role of facili-
tator. Use and selection of a facilitator also 
consider the preferences of the person served 
and who can provide appropriate support or may 
pose challenges to the delivery of services, e.g., a 
family member who tends to speak for the person 
served or is otherwise disruptive during service 
delivery.
4.d.(2) Examples of modifications include the 
use of assistive technology such as alternative 
keyboards and headphones, close-captioning 
software, a sign language interpreter, materials in 
large font or braille, the use of appropriate light-
ing and backgrounds so faces can be clearly seen, 
and the provision of printed materials and audio 
recordings to supplement information provided 
during an encounter.
4.d.(2)(d)(ii) Privacy considerations may include 
whether the environment where the person 
served is located provides for confidentiality 
during interactions between the provider and 
person served.

174 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
2.I. 5. Prior to the start of each session:
5.Service Delivery using ICT
a. Any participants in the session in 
addition to the person served are 
identified.
5.a.Service Delivery using ICT
b. The program provides information 
that is relevant to the session.
5.b.Service Delivery using ICT
Examples
5.a. In addition to the person served and pro-
vider, other participants in the session may 
include members of the family/support system, 
caregivers or others who are assisting the person 
served; other members of the service delivery 
team; a supervisor who is observing the provider; 
and students or other trainees.
5.b. Information may be shared on the creden-
tials of the provider, structure and timing of 
services, recordkeeping, scheduling, contact 
between sessions, privacy and security, potential 
risks, safety considerations, confidentiality, 
billing, rights and responsibilities.
Service delivery using information and commu-
nication technologies may be ongoing rather 
than session-based, e.g., supporting persons 
served in their living environment through the 
use of video, audio, or motion monitoring. In 
addition to sharing relevant information about 
the use of ICT at the initiation of services, the 
program may establish a minimum frequency, 
such as at least annually, to review that informa-
tion; may review it based on need, such as when 
the person served or a member of the family/sup-
port system has questions; or may link it to other 
activities in the service process such as periodic 
review of the person’s individual plan, goals, and 
preferences.
2.I. 6. The organization maintains equipment 
in accordance with manufacturers’ 
recommendations.
6.Service Delivery using ICT
  
2.I. 7. Emergency procedures address the 
unique aspects of service delivery via 
information and communication tech
-
nologies, including:
7.Service Delivery using ICT
a. Identification of an emergency con -
tact for the person served, including 
phone number.
7.a.Service Delivery using ICT
b. Identification of local emergency 
resources, including phone numbers.
7.b.Service Delivery using ICT
c. The provider becoming familiar with 
the emergency procedures at the 
location of the person served, if the 
procedures exist.
7.c.Service Delivery using ICT
Intent Statements
In case of emergency, the provider has immediate 
access to emergency contact information for 
the person served and information on local emer-
gency resources, including their phone numbers.
Examples
7.a.–b. Through the intake process the program 
gathers information from the person served 
on an emergency contact and local emergency 
resources such as emergency response systems 
and law enforcement.
7.c. When the person served is located at an 
organization or a community setting, the pro-
vider becomes familiar with the procedures of 
that setting in the event there is an emergency 
involving the person served. 
If a person served who is at home utilizes a medi-
cal alert system such as Life Alert, the provider 
becomes familiar with how the system is activa-
ted in case of an emergency. 

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 175
SECTION 3
Core Treatment Program Standards
Description
The standards in this section address the unique 
characteristics of each type of core program area. 
Behavioral health programs are organized and 
designed to provide services for persons who 
have or who are at risk of having psychiatric 
disorders, harmful involvement with alcohol 
or other drugs, other addictions, or who have 
other behavioral health needs. Through a team 
approach, and with the active and ongoing par-
ticipation of the persons served, the overall goal 
of each program is to improve the quality of life 
and the functional abilities of the persons served. 
Each program selected for accreditation dem-
onstrates cultural competency and relevance. 
Family members and significant others are 
involved in the programs of the persons served 
as appropriate and to the extent possible.
Applicable Standards
All organizations seeking accreditation for a 
behavioral health core program must apply the 
standards in Sections 1–2, unless otherwise 
indicated in the applicable standards informa-
tion in the program section and/or in the table 
at the beginning of Section 2.
Note the requirements for programs serving 
children and adolescents under the Guidelines 
for Organizations Seeking a Specialty Designa-
tion at the beginning of Section 5.
 
Behavioral Health Field 
Categories
For each behavioral health core program selected 
for accreditation, an organization must identify 
under which behavioral health field category the 
core program operates. Field categories are used 
to characterize the purpose, intent, and overall 
focus of a core program and to distinguish the 
specific fields in behavioral health that the core 
program reflects and serves.
The behavioral health field categories are 
Addictions Pharmacotherapy, Substance Use 
Disorders/Addictions, Mental Health, Psychoso-
cial Rehabilitation, Family Services, Integrated 
SUD/Mental Health, Integrated IDD/Mental 
Health, and Comprehensive Care. The following 
are descriptions of each field category:
n Addictions Pharmacotherapy: Core pro-
grams in this field category are designed to 
provide services to persons who have harmful 
involvement with alcohol or other drugs/
addictions. These are medically managed 
programs that provide medications in addi-
tion to psychosocial interventions designed to 
assist persons served to achieve their highest 
possible recovery. Programs in the United 
States that administer methadone must use 
the Opioid Treatment Program standards 
manual and would not be appropriate for this 
field category. This field category applies only 
to Office-Based Opioid Treatment programs.
n Substance Use Disorders/Addictions: Core 
programs in this field category are designed to 
provide services for persons who have or are 
at risk of having harmful involvement with 
alcohol or other drugs/addictions, including 
process addictions, such as addiction to gam
-
bling, pornography, video gaming, etc. These 
programs use a team approach to minimize 

SECTION 3. CORE TREATMENT PROGRAM STANDARDS
176 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
the effects and risks associated with alcohol, 
other drugs, or other addictions.
n Mental Health: Core programs in this field 
category are designed to provide services for 
people with or who are at risk for psychiatric 
disabilities/disorders or have other mental 
health needs. These programs encompass a 
wide variety of therapeutic settings and inter
-
vention modalities and may provide services 
to those with behavioral health disabilities or 
co-occurring disabilities; intellectual or devel
-
opmental disabilities; victims or perpetrators 
of violence or abuse; persons needing treat
-
ment because of eating or sexual disorders; 
and/or drug, gambling, or internet addictions.
n Psychosocial Rehabilitation: Core programs 
in this field category demonstrate a strong 
collaborative partnership with the persons 
served, the development of opportunities 
for
 personal growth, a commitment to 
community integration, goal-oriented and 
individualized supports, and the promotion of 
satisfaction and success in community living. 
Programs in this category may serve persons 
with a variety of concerns, including persons 
with developmental or physical disabilities.
n Family Services: Core programs in this field 
category are designed to maintain or improve 
the quality of life for children, adolescents, 
or
 other family members individually or in 
their relationships with their families, their 
environments, or other individuals. Core pro-
grams in this field category are directed at the 
reduction of symptoms and/or the improve-
ment of functioning for the person served or 
family unit.
n Integrated SUD/Mental Health: 
Core programs in this field category are 
designed to provide a combination of alcohol 
and other drugs/addictions and mental health 
services. This may include services provided 
in a psychosocial format. Services may be pro
-
vided through a seamless system of care for 
individuals with needs in one or both areas 
or
 for persons with identified co-occurring 
disorders, including any of the concerns listed 
under the Mental Health field category.
n Integrated IDD/Mental Health: Core pro-
grams in this field category are designed to 
provide services to persons whose primary 
diagnoses are intellectual or other develop-
mental disabilities and who are at risk for or 
exhibiting behavioral disorders or have iden-
tified mental health needs. These programs 
encompass many therapeutic settings and 
intervention modalities and a commitment to 
community integration.
n Comprehensive Care: Core programs in this 
field category are designed to provide any 
combination of behavioral health services 
related to mental illness, addictions or intel
-
lectual/developmental disabilities, and 
management of or coordination with the 
healthcare needs of the person served. This 
field category applies only to Health Home or 
Integrated Behavioral Health/Primary Care 
programs. If you choose this category for 
any
 programs other than Health Home or 
Integrated Behavioral Health/Primary Care, 
please call the CARF office to discuss this 
option.

Organizations Seeking a 
Specialty Designation
If an organization is required or chooses to add a 
Specialty Designation to a core program(s) being 
surveyed, the standards for these designations 
will be applied at the time of the survey in addi-
tion to the core program standards. See Section 5 
for details and applicable standards.
The Specialty Designations available are:
n 5.A. Adults with Autism Spectrum Disorder
n 5.B. Children/Adolescents with Autism Spec-
trum Disorder
n 5.C. Children and Adolescents (may be 
required—see Section 5, page 297)
n 5.D. Consumer-Run
n 5.E. Criminal Justice (may be required—see 
Section 5, page 297)
n 5.F . Eating Disorders (includes Eating Disor-
ders for Children/Adolescents (EDCA—see 
Section 5, page 297)
n 5.G. Integrated Primary Care (required for 
Health Home, see Section 5, page 297)
n 5.H. Juvenile Justice (may be required—see 
Section 5, page 297)
n 5.I. Medically Complex (may be required; 
includes Medically Complex for Children/
Adolescents (MCCA)—see Section 5, page 
297)
n 5.J. Older Adults
A. Assertive Community 
Treatment
Description
Assertive Community Treatment (ACT) 
is a multidisciplinary team approach that 
assumes responsibility for directly providing 
acute, active, and ongoing community-based 
psychiatric treatment, assertive outreach, rehabil-
itation, and support. The program team provides 
assistance to individuals to maximize their recov-
ery, ensure consumer-directed goal setting, assist 
the persons served to gain hope and a sense of 
empowerment, and provide assistance in helping 
the persons served become respected and valued 
members of their community. The program pro-
vides psychosocial services directed primarily to 
adults with severe and persistent mental illness 
who often have co-occurring problems, such as 
substance abuse, or are homeless or involved with 
the judicial system.
The team is the single point of clinical responsi-
bility and is accountable for assisting the persons 
served to meet their needs and to achieve their 
goals for recovery. Multiple members of the team 
are familiar with each person served to ensure 
the timely and continuous provision of services. 
Services are provided on a long-term care basis 
with continuity of caregivers over time. The 
majority of services are provided directly by ACT 
team members, with minimal referral to outside 
providers, in the natural environment of the per-
son served and are available 24 hours a day, 7 
days per week. Services are comprehensive and 
highly individualized and are modified as needed 
through an ongoing assessment and treatment 
planning process. Services vary in intensity based 
on the needs of the persons served.
Assertive Community Treatment has been 
identified as an effective model for providing 
community-based services for persons whose 
needs and goals have not been met through tradi-
tional office-based treatment and rehabilitation 
services. Desired outcomes specific to ACT 
services may include positive change in the fol-
lowing areas: community tenure, independent 
living, quality of life, consumer satisfaction of the 
person served, functioning in work and social 

SECTION 3.A. ASSERTIVE COMMUNITY TREATMENT
178 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
domains, community integration, psychological 
condition, subjective well-being, and the ability 
of the persons served to manage their own 
healthcare.
In certain geographic areas, Assertive Com-
munity Treatment programs may be called 
Community Support programs, Intensive Com-
munity Treatment programs, Mobile Community 
Treatment Teams, or Assertive Outreach Teams.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for an 
assertive community treatment program must 
apply the standards in Sections 1 and 2, in addi-
tion to the standards in this subsection.
3.A. 1. Assertive community treatment (ACT) 
services are provided by one or more 
multidisciplinary treatment teams.
1.3.A. ACT
  
3.A. 2. The ACT team:
2.3.A. ACT
a. Has sufficient staff to provide identi -
fied hours of coverage.
2.a.3.A. ACT
b. Includes the variety of disciplines 
necessary to meet the needs of the 
persons served.
2.b.3.A. ACT
c. At least annually reviews its capacity 
to provide comprehensive integrated 
treatment services.
2.c.3.A. ACT
d. Makes recommendations to the 
organization’s administration to 
ensure the team’s ability to meet 
the needs of the persons it serves.
2.d.3.A. ACT
e. Is the central point for delivering 
services, as based on the needs of 
the persons served.
2.e.3.A. ACT
f. Is available at times and locations 
that meet the needs of the persons 
served, including evenings and 
weekends.
2.f.3.A. ACT
g. Delivers the majority of the treat -
ment, rehabilitation, and recovery 
support services needed by the 
persons served, including:
2.g.3.A. ACT
(1) Symptom assessment and 
management.
2.g.(1)3.A. ACT
(2) Individual supportive therapy.
2.g.(2)3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
2.a. Staff sufficiency includes plans for backup in 
case of emergency and planned absences.
3.A. 3. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, the composition of the ACT team 
provides for a staff-to-client ratio of at 
least one full-time equivalent direct care 
staff member for each eight to fifteen 
persons served.
3.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
Caseloads would be expected to be lower (one 
to eight or ten) in a program that serves a greater 
percentage of persons with acute needs, experi-
ences a high turnover of persons served, or is 
provided in a more rural area that requires a 
greater amount of travel time. If the ACT team 
serves a broader population or continues to 
provide supports to persons served who are 
functioning at a higher, more stable level, the 
caseloads may move toward the higher end of 
the range (one to twelve or fifteen). Caseload size 
is indexed to the severity of need and functioning 
level of the persons served.
3.A. 4. The ACT team is coordinated by a team 
leader who:
4.3.A. ACT
a. Is a qualified behavioral health 
practitioner.
4.a.3.A. ACT
b. Has specialized knowledge and 
competencies that meet the needs 
of the persons served.
4.b.3.A. ACT
c. Provides clinical supervision to ACT 
team staff.
4.c.3.A. ACT
d. Provides direct services to persons 
served by the ACT team.
4.d.3.A. ACT

SECTION 3.A. ASSERTIVE COMMUNITY TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 179
Intent Statements
4.a. See the Glossary for the definition of a quali-
fied behavioral health practitioner.
3.A. 5. The majority of the ACT team members 
are qualified behavioral health 
practitioners.
5.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
The program can demonstrate that on a consis-
tent basis the ACT team is composed primarily 
of qualified behavioral health practitioners.
3.A. 6. Each ACT team has one or more nursing 
staff members who:
6.3.A. ACT
a. Participate(s) in treatment planning 
meetings based on the needs of the 
persons served.
6.a.3.A. ACT
b. Provides sufficient nursing coverage 
to meet the needs of the persons 
served.
6.b.3.A. ACT
c. Directly provide(s) services.
6.c.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
The number of nursing staff members may vary 
depending on the responsibilities of the nursing 
staff and the needs of the persons served. Nurses 
provide nursing consultation to the team. It is 
expected that registered nursing coverage would 
relate to the amount of medical or medication 
use responsibilities required. Systems should be 
in place to provide for backup in case of emer-
gency or planned absences.
3.A. 7. Each ACT team has a psychiatrist or 
a physician specialist in addiction 
medicine who:
7.3.A. ACT
a. Is a member of the team.
7.a.3.A. ACT
b. Directly provides services.
7.b.3.A. ACT
c. Is available to participate in treat -
ment planning meetings based on 
the needs of the persons served.
7.c.3.A. ACT
d. Provides clinical consultation and 
supervision to the team.
7.d.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
On a team providing services to persons with 
psychiatric needs, a psychiatrist is a member. 
Addiction medicine specialists are needed when 
there is a high incidence of co-occurring disor-
ders in the population served. A team primarily 
serving persons with addictions may have a 
physician specialist. Other advanced practice 
providers who have training in serving the 
targeted population are acceptable alternatives 
to a physician as allowed by applicable regula-
tions and practice acts.
3.A. 8. The organization demonstrates its 
efforts to recruit staff or volunteers, who 
are peers, to become team members and 
to provide peer support or consultation 
to persons served by the ACT team.
8.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
Peer support staff members or volunteers can 
provide a unique perspective to the rest of the 
team and work to foster positive, effective rela-
tionships with the persons served. This position 
may be shared between two or more individuals.
3.A. 9. The treatment plan is reviewed at least 
quarterly and modified as necessary 
based on the needs of the person served.
9.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
State, federal, or provincial regulations may 
require the program to review treatment plans 
on a more frequent basis.
3.A. 10. The ACT team directly provides the 
following crisis intervention services:
10.3.A. ACT
a. Developing an init ial crisis interven-
tion plan upon contact for each 
person served.
10.a.3.A. ACT
b. Providing teleph one intervention 
services.
10.b.3.A. ACT
c. Providing face-to-face assessment 
services.
10.c.3.A. ACT
d. Providing mobile services.
10.d.3.A. ACT
e. Implementation of written emer -
gency procedures that address:
10.e.3.A. ACT
(1) Screening for medical or emer -
gency psychiatric services.
10.e.(1)3.A. ACT
(2) Making referrals to emergency 
medical or psychiatric services.
10.e.(2)3.A. ACT

12.c.3.A. ACT

12.c.(2)3.A. ACT

12.c.(3)3.A. ACT
Examples
A variety of means, including teaching, planning 
of social and leisure-time activities, side-by-side 
support and coaching, and organizing individual 
and group social and recreational activities can 
be used to assist the persons served in commu-
nity integration. These supports are directed 
toward assisting the persons served in improving 
communication skills; developing assertiveness 
and increased self-esteem; developing social 
skills and meaningful personal relationships; 
effectively relating to landlords, neighbors, and 
others; and familiarizing themselves with and 
increasing their use of available social and recre-
ational opportunities.
12.c.(2) May include volunteer activities.

13.c.3.A. ACT
d. Long-term housing.
13.d.3.A. ACT
e. Housing subsidies.
13.e.3.A. ACT
f. Medical benefits/care.
13.f.3.A. ACT

stability.
19.e.3.A. ACT
f. Access/utilize self-help or support 
groups.

Intent Statements
Although not all person served in an ACT pro-
gram will require these interventions, the 
program should have the expertise to provide 
these services in an integrated manner within 
the program. 
3.A. 20. The ACT team directly provides voca-
tional services by actively assisting 
the person served to find, obtain, and 
maintain employment or volunteer 
opportunities in community-based sites 
that are consistent with their goals and 
choices.
20.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
If the ACT team collaborates with other providers 
of vocational services, the expectation is that they 
are actively involved in ensuring that the services 
are provided and available to the persons served. 
Simply having the services available or referring 
the persons served to other providers would not 
meet the intent of this standard.
3.A. 21. With consent of the persons served, 
the ACT team provides services to the 
families and other major supports of 
the persons served, including:
21.3.A. ACT
a. Education about the illness/disorder 
of the persons served.
21.a.3.A. ACT

abilities of the persons served.
21.b.3.A. ACT

the role of the family in the thera-
peutic process.
21.c.3.A. ACT
d. Intervention to prevent or resolve 
conflict.
21.d.3.A. ACT
e. Ongoing communication and collab -
oration between the team and the 
family.
21.e.3.A. ACT
  
3.A. 22. The ACT team provides:
22.3.A. ACT
a. Assertive outreach and engagement 
to assist the persons served in their 
own environment.
22.a.3.A. ACT
b. At least 75 percent of its service con -
tacts in the community, outside of the 
clinical office setting.
22.b.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
An essential principle of ACT programs is that the 
team members provide support and services to 
the persons served in the community and their 
natural environment.
3.A. 23. The ACT team:
23.3.A. ACT
a. Provides multiple contacts per week 
based on the clinical needs of the 
persons served.
23.a.3.A. ACT
b. Increases service intensity to the 
persons served when their needs 
require additional contacts.
23.b.3.A. ACT

and liaison services for persons who 
are
 hospitalized or in criminal justice 
or other restrictive settings.
24.3.A. ACT
Intent Statements
The length of time during which these supportive 
services occur may be limited by state or other 
regulations.
3.A. 25. The ACT team provides outreach and 
follow-up to persons who have been 
admitted to its program and:
25.3.A. ACT
a. Become isolated in the community.
25.a.3.A. ACT
b. Are admitted to more intensive levels 
of treatment but are likely to return 
to the program.
25.b.3.A. ACT

3.A. 28. Discharge from the program occurs:
28.3.A. ACT
a. When the persons served and pro -
gram staff members mutually agree 
to the termination of services.
28.a.3.A. ACT
b. When the person served moves 
outside the geographic area of the 
team’s responsibility. In such cases, 
the ACT team:
28.b.3.A. ACT
(1) Arranges for transfer of mental 
health service responsibility to a 
provider in the location to which 
the person served is moving.
28.b.(1)3.A. ACT
(2) When feasible, maintains contact 
with the person served until 
service transfer is arranged.
28.b.(2)3.A. ACT
c. When the person served demon -
strates an ability to function in all 
major role areas (i.e., work, social, 
self-care), with only minimal assis-
tance from the program for a period 
of one year or more as agreed to by 
the person served and the ACT team.
28.c.3.A. ACT
d. When the person served is not court 
ordered and requests termination of 
services.
28.d.3.A. ACT
e. When the team, despite repeated 
efforts, cannot locate the person 
served.

SECTION 3.B. CASE MANAGEMENT/SERVICES COORDINATION
184 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
B. Case Management/
Services Coordination
Description
Case management/services coordination pro-
grams provide goal-oriented and individualized 
supports focusing on improved self-sufficiency 
for the persons served through assessment, 
planning, linkage, advocacy, coordination, and 
monitoring activities. Successful service coordi-
nation results in community opportunities and 
increased independence for the persons served. 
Programs may provide occasional supportive 
counseling and crisis intervention services, when 
allowed by regulatory or funding authorities.
Case management/services coordination may be 
provided by an organization as part of its person-
centered planning and delivery, by a department 
or division within the organization that works 
with individuals who are internal and/or external 
to the organization, or by an organization with 
the sole purpose of providing case management/
services coordination. Such programs are typi-
cally provided by qualified case managers/
coordinators or by case management teams.
Organizations performing case management/
services coordination as a routine function of 
other services or programs are not required 
to apply these standards unless they are specifi-
cally seeking accreditation for this program.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for 
a case management/services coordination 
program must apply the standards in Sections 
1 and 2 in addition to the standards in this 
subsection.
3.B. 1. The persons served are linked to services 
and resources to achieve objectives as 
identified in their person-centered plan.
1.3.B. CM
  
3.B. 2. Personnel providing services have a 
working knowledge of the:
2.3.B. CM
a. Services that are appropriate for the 
needs of the persons served.
2.a.3.B. CM
b. Support systems that are relevant to 
the lives of the persons served.
2.b.3.B. CM
Intent Statements
In order to provide the linkages, coordination, 
and support needed by the persons served, the 
case managers are able to demonstrate knowl-
edge of healthcare, social services, employment, 
housing, recreational opportunities, and other 
services and systems available in the community.
3.B. 3. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, case management/services 
coordination includes:
3.3.B. CM
a. Activities carried out in collaboration 
with the persons served.
3.a.3.B. CM
b. Outreach to encourage the participa -
tion of the persons served.
3.b.3.B. CM
c. Coordination of, or assistance with, 
crisis intervention and stabilization 
services, as appropriate.
3.c.3.B. CM
d. Assistance with achieving goals as 
defined by the persons served.
3.d.3.B. CM
e. Optimizing resources and opportuni -
ties through:
3.e.3.B. CM
(1) Community linkages.
3.e.(1)3.B. CM
(2) Enhanced social support 
networks.
3.e.(2)3.B. CM
f. Assistance with:
3.f.3.B. CM
(1) Accessing transportation.
3.f.(1)3.B. CM
(2) Securing safe housing that is 
reflective of the:
3.f.(2)3.B. CM
(a) Needs of the persons served.
3.f.(2)(a)3.B. CM
(b) Abilities of the persons 
served.
3.f.(2)(b)3.B. CM
(c) Preferences of the persons 
served.
3.f.(2)(c)3.B. CM
(3) Exploring employment or other 
meaningful activities.
3.f.(3)3.B. CM

SECTION 3.B. CASE MANAGEMENT/SERVICES COORDINATION
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 185
g. Provision of, or linkage to, skill devel -
opment services needed to enable 
the person served to perform daily 
living activities, including, but not 
limited to:
3.g.3.B. CM
(1) Budgeting.
3.g.(1)3.B. CM
(2) Meal planning.
3.g.(2)3.B. CM
(3) Personal care.
3.g.(3)3.B. CM
(4) Housekeeping and home 
maintenance.
3.g.(4)3.B. CM
(5) Other identified needs.
3.g.(5)3.B. CM
h. Evidence of linkage with necessary 
and appropriate:
3.h.3.B. CM
(1) Financial services.
3.h.(1)3.B. CM
(2) Medical or other healthcare.
3.h.(2)3.B. CM
(3) Other community services.
3.h.(3)3.B. CM
Intent Statements
These case management activities are carried 
out in partnership and collaboration with the 
persons served. Elements provided are depen-
dent on the needs of persons served and/or 
funder/regulatory requirements.
3.c. In some programs, such as Healthy Families 
America, guidelines specify a variety of positive 
outreach methods and are used to build trust, 
engage the person served in services, and main-
tain ongoing involvement.
3.h.(2) Medical or other healthcare includes the 
coordination of the healthcare of the persons 
served. Often individuals are seeing a variety of 
healthcare professionals and using a variety of 
medications that need to be monitored and coor-
dinated. When working with infants or children, 
healthcare includes immunizations.
3.B. 4. The organization provides case manage-
ment activities in locations that meet the 
needs of the persons served.
4.3.B. CM
Intent Statements
Services, such as assessment, planning, coord-
ination, and monitoring, can be provided in 
any setting that provides the best access to the 
persons served and is preferred by the persons 
served.
Examples
Such locations may include residences, correc-
tional settings, shelters, community resource 
sites, hospitals, schools, medical, or other service 
sites.
3.B. 5. The intensity of case management is 
based on the needs of the person as 
identified in the person-centered plan.
5.3.B. CM
Intent Statements
The intensity of case management and the fre-
quency of contact are individualized and clearly 
defined.
Examples
There is wide variability among types of case 
management. Many programs provide intensive 
case management to a small, select group of indi-
viduals, and other programs provide services 
only periodically. However, there is a clear rela-
tionship between how often individuals are 
served and their specific needs.
Some programs, such as Healthy Families Amer-
ica, have clearly defined criteria for increasing/
decreasing the intensity of services.
3.B. 6. When multiple case management pro-
viders exist:
6.3.B. CM
a. A primary case manager is identified.
6.a.3.B. CM
b. There is coordination to:
6.b.3.B. CM
(1) Facilitate continuity of care.
6.b.(1)3.B. CM
(2) Reduce duplication of services.
6.b.(2)3.B. CM
  
3.B. 7. With the permission of the persons 
served, personnel provide advocacy 
by
 sharing feedback regarding the 
services received with the agencies 
and organizations providing the 
services.
7.3.B. CM
  

SECTION 3.C. COMMUNITY INTEGRATION
186 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
C. Community 
Integration
Description
Community integration is designed to help 
persons to optimize their personal, social, and 
vocational competency in order to live success-
fully in the community. Activities are determined 
by the needs of the persons served. The persons 
served are active partners in all aspects of these 
programs. Therefore, the settings can be informal 
in order to reduce barriers between staff mem-
bers and program participants. In addition to 
services provided in the home or community, this 
program may include a psychosocial clubhouse, 
a drop-in center, an activity center, or a day 
program.
Community integration provides opportunities 
for the community participation of the persons 
served. The organization defines the scope of 
these services based on the identified needs 
and desires of the persons served. A person may 
participate in a variety of community life experi-
ences that may include, but are not limited to:
n Leisure or recreational activities.
n Communication activities.
n Spiritual activities.
n Cultural activities.
n Vocational pursuits.
n Development of work attitudes.
n Employment activities.
n Volunteerism.
n Educational and training activities.
n Development of living skills.
n Health and wellness promotion.
n Orientation, mobility, and destination 
training.
n Access and utilization of public 
transportation.
NOTE: The use of the term persons served in 
Community Integration may include members, 
attendees, or participants.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
community integration program must apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition 
to the standards in this subsection.
If it is a consumer-run program, the organiza-
tion would apply Section 1; Section 2.E., 2.F., 
and 2.I., as applicable; and Section 5.D.
3.C. 1. The persons participating in services/
activities move toward:
1.3.C. COI
a. Optimal use of:
1.a.3.C. COI
(1) Natural supports.
1.a.(1)3.C. COI
(2) Self-help.
1.a.(2)3.C. COI
b. Greater self-sufficiency.
1.b.3.C. COI
c. Greater choice.
1.c.3.C. COI
d. Greater control of their lives.
1.d.3.C. COI
e. Increased participation in the 
community.
1.e.3.C. COI
  
3.C. 2. Services/activities are organized around:
2.3.C. COI
a. The stated goals of the persons 
served.
2.a.3.C. COI
b. The identified preferences of the 
persons served.
2.b.3.C. COI
c. The identified needs of the persons 
served.
2.c.3.C. COI
d. Improving the ability of the persons 
served to understand their needs.
2.d.3.C. COI
e. Assisting the persons served to 
achieve their goals of choice in the 
following areas:
2.e.3.C. COI
(1) Community living skill 
development.
2.e.(1)3.C. COI
(2) Interpersonal relations.
2.e.(2)3.C. COI
(3) Recreation or use of leisure time 
opportunities.
2.e.(3)3.C. COI
(4) Vocational development or 
employment.
2.e.(4)3.C. COI
(5) Educational development.
2.e.(5)3.C. COI
(6) Self-advocacy.
2.e.(6)3.C. COI
(7) Access to nondisability related 
social resources.
2.e.(7)3.C. COI

SECTION 3.C. COMMUNITY INTEGRATION
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 187
Intent Statements
The organization demonstrates that a range of 
basic services is provided. These services could be 
arranged within a psychosocial clubhouse, an 
activity center, or a day program, but the com-
mon services consist of providing assistance with 
independent living skills and the other activities 
described in this standard.
2.e.(1) The program assists the person served to 
develop the skills needed to live as independen-
tly as possible in the community.
Examples
2.e.(1) Assistance may be provided to develop or 
enhance skills related to performing household 
activities, cooking, grocery shopping, laundry, or 
money management.
2.e.(3) This may include volunteer activities.
3.C. 3. If work is performed by program partici-
pants, legal wage guidelines 
are observed.
3.3.C. COI
  
3.C. 4. Services are provided at times and loca-
tions that meet the needs of the persons 
served.
4.3.C. COI
Intent Statements
The program’s services and hours of operation, 
including evenings, weekends, and holidays, are 
evaluated periodically to ensure that the services 
are available and accessible to meet the needs 
and interests of the persons served.
3.C. 5. Personnel are available to meet with per-
sons served to discuss matters of mutual 
interest or concern.
5.3.C. COI
Examples
These meetings could be community meetings or 
house meetings for the purpose of collaboratively 
discussing issues such as:
n Program operations and activities.
n Hours of operation.
n Problems.
n Plans.
n The use of program resources.
3.C. 6. The organization provides information 
or referral to assist the persons served in 
securing assistance to meet their basic 
needs.
6.3.C. COI
Examples
This may include any of the following based on 
the needs of the person served:
n Income maintenance.
n Benefits.
n Food, clothing, and household goods.
n Short-term or emergency shelter.
n Housing subsidies, including long-term 
housing.
n Medical and healthcare.
n Information on the impact of employment on 
securing and accessing future benefits.
n Transportation.
n Other community supports.
Other relevant behavioral health services may 
include therapy, testing, medication manage-
ment, crisis intervention, and psychiatric 
assessment.
3.C. 7. The program’s outreach to and follow-up 
procedures for the persons served are 
directed to:
7.3.C. COI
a. Those who drop out of services.
7.a.3.C. COI
b. Those who have been admitted to 
a treatment, institutional, or other 
setting.
7.b.3.C. COI
Intent Statements
Each program is encouraged to work coopera-
tively with other agencies in the community to 
develop a seamless continuum of services and 
to reduce all barriers to access. The intent of this 
standard is to ensure that the program imple-
ments procedures that describe how the program 
will coordinate services and referrals to reduce 
disruption of the persons served.

SECTION 3.D. COURT TREATMENT
188 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
D. Court Treatment
Description
Court Treatment programs provide comprehen-
sive, integrated behavioral health services that 
work in conjunction with the judicial system. 
The purpose of court treatment programs is to 
appropriately respond to the abuse of alcohol 
and/or other drugs, mental illness, post traumatic 
stress disorder, family problems, or other con-
cerns and their related criminal and/or civil 
judicial actions, in order to reduce recidivism 
and further involvement in the criminal justice 
system. Court treatment includes services pro-
vided to persons referred through various types 
of problem-solving courts including substance 
abuse, mental health, veterans, family depen-
dency, re-entry, and others.
The treatment team works in collaboration with 
judges, prosecutors, defense counsel, probation 
authorities, law enforcement, pretrial services, 
treatment programs, evaluators, and an array 
of local service providers. Treatment is usually 
multi-phased and is typically divided into a 
stabilization phase, an intensive phase, and a 
transition phase. During each phase, the treat-
ment team is responsible for assessing the 
behavioral health needs of the person served 
within the parameters of the legal sanctions 
imposed by the court. The treatment team either 
directly provides or arranges for the provision 
of screening and assessment, case management, 
withdrawal support, intensive outpatient 
treatment, outpatient, residential treatment, 
medication use, self-help and advocacy, recovery, 
health and wellness, relapse prevention, and 
education regarding factors contributing to the 
person’s court involvement.
A court treatment program may be a judicial or 
law enforcement organization that provides or 
contracts for the identified services or may be a 
direct treatment provider working as part of the 
court treatment team.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
court treatment program must apply the 
standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition to 
the standards in this subsection.
NOTE: Court Treatment programs serving juveniles 
must also apply the standards in Section 5.C. Chil-
dren and Adolescents or Section 5.H. Juvenile 
Justice.
3.D. 1. The court treatment program works:
1.3.D. CT
a. With the following, as appropriate:
1.a.3.D. CT
(1) Prosecutors. 
1.a.(1)3.D. CT
(2) Defense counsel.
1.a.(2)3.D. CT
(3) Court personnel.
1.a.(3)3.D. CT
(4) Other criminal justice 
representatives.
1.a.(4)3.D. CT
b. To design policies and procedures for:
1.b.3.D. CT
(1) Screening.
1.b.(1)3.D. CT
(2) Eligibility.
1.b.(2)3.D. CT
(3) Case processing.
1.b.(3)3.D. CT
  
3.D. 2. Participation in a court treatment pro-
gram is not denied solely on the basis of 
inability to pay fees, fines, or restitution.
2.3.D. CT
  
3.D. 3. The program communicates the need 
for
 ongoing judicial interaction to each 
court treatment participant.
3.3.D. CT
Intent Statements
The assigned clinician or case manager com-
municates with the participant on a regularly 
scheduled basis to determine progress and 
compliance. Status hearings are used to monitor 
program compliance, participation, and progress 
for each court participant. The court applies 
appropriate incentives or sanctions to match 
treatment progress.

4.c.3.D. CT

 the person served.

describe the methods for arranging for the ser-

3.D. 8. When the person served has a substance 
use disorder, alcohol and other drug test-
ing is used to monitor substance use.
8.3.D. CT
Intent Statements
The person served will be randomly or regularly 
scheduled alcohol or other drug testing based 
on individual needs during participation in the 
program. Test results are communicated to the 
person served and the court.
3.D. 9. All members of the team:
9.3.D. CT

9.a.3.D. CT
