9.b.3.D. CT
c. Have specialized knowledge of the 
various theories of, and approaches 
to, criminal justice behavioral health 
services.
9.c.3.D. CT
Examples
9.a. Access to clinical records can include access 
to information such as:
n Person-centered plans.
n Custody records.
3.D. 10. Training:
10.3.D. CT
a. Is provided to personnel prior to 
the delivery of services.
10.a.3.D. CT
b. Includes regular interdisciplinary 
joint cross-training related to clinical 
and
 criminal justice issues.
10.b.3.D. CT
c. Includes such topics as:
10.c.3.D. CT
(1) The requirements imposed on 
personnel from the criminal 
justice system who participate 
on
 the treatment team.
10.c.(1)3.D. CT
(2) Safeguards that are available 
to personnel.
10.c.(2)3.D. CT
Intent Statements
10.a. Behavioral health professionals who work 
in criminal justice settings encounter a unique 
service delivery system with both opportunities 
and challenges. The intent of this standard is to 
ensure that individuals new to this type of setting 
receive full and complete training prior to the 
delivery of services, and throughout their 
employment, to ensure that they are familiar with 
the unique procedures and characteristics of the 
environment in which they work.
10.b. Interdisciplinary cross-training refers to 
criminal justice staff members providing criminal 
justice training to clinical staff members and 
also to clinical staff members providing clinical 
training to criminal justice staff members. It also 
requires that training be conducted jointly with 
members from both the criminal justice and clini-
cal services participating.
Examples
10.c.(1) May include requirements such as man-
datory reporting.
3.D. 11. The treatment team works in a partner-
ship with the judge to:
11.3.D. CT
a. Review treatment progress on an 
ongoing basis.
11.a.3.D. CT
b. Respond to the progress and/or lack 
of progress of each person served.
11.b.3.D. CT
  
3.D. 12. The person served is provided with a 
description of the relationship between 
the criminal justice entity and the pro-
gram, including:
12.3.D. CT
a. The extent and limitations of confi -
dentiality and sanctions.
12.a.3.D. CT
b. The possible implications of having a 
criminal justice member on the team.
12.b.3.D. CT
Intent Statements
Those individuals who play a significant role in 
the treatment, education, and incarceration of 
the person served work cooperatively and 
collaboratively as a team.
The person served has the option of refusing to 
have the criminal justice system actively involved 

SECTION 3.D. COURT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 191
in the treatment process and be told of the 
consequences.
Examples
The team involves a blend of behavioral health 
providers and criminal justice personnel, such as 
correctional officers, control agents, guards, and 
probation and parole officers.
12.b. The staff members of the program might 
discuss the possible advantages and disadvan-
tages of having a criminal justice member on 
the team, including such issues as:
n Access to confidential records.
n Action the criminal justice member may be 
forced to take based on information provided 
by the team.
n The impact on the therapeutic relationship.
3.D. 13. If the person served is sanctioned to an 
external setting for 30 days or more:
13.3.D. CT
a. An updated transition plan is 
completed.
13.a.3.D. CT
b. The person’s status is tracked/
monitored.
13.b.3.D. CT
  
3.D. 14. When the person served is referred to a 
different level of care in the community, 
the court treatment program establishes 
a process to consistently receive informa-
tion regarding the person’s status.
14.3.D. CT
Intent Statements
Case management activities support regular 
communication and coordination of services 
with the provider of treatment services delivered 
outside the court treatment program. Case man-
agement services provide ongoing assessment 
of the participant’s progress and needs, provide 
structure and support for participants who expe-
rience difficulties with using services, and ensure 
communication with the court.
3.D. 15. Person-centered planning for a person 
receiving education and training services 
in a court treatment program:
15.3.D. CT
a. Addresses issues specific to the 
person’s individual needs.
15.a.3.D. CT
b. Is consistent with the person’s 
cognitive and learning abilities.
15.b.3.D. CT
c. Is consistent with the program’s 
philosophy of treatment.
15.c.3.D. CT
d. Addresses:
15.d.3.D. CT
(1) Relapse prevention.
15.d.(1)3.D. CT
(2) Potential contingency plans.
15.d.(2)3.D. CT
Intent Statements
15.b. The intent of this standard is to ensure 
that the assessment has included cognitive and 
learning abilities and that reading materials, 
assignments, and the requirements for partici-
pation take into consideration the learning 
abilities of the person served. This standard 
includes ensuring that reasonable accommo-
dations are available for persons with special 
educational needs.
15.c. Since many of the criminal justice educa-
tional services are provided as part of or within 
a treatment program, this standard encourages 
the organization to ensure that the educational 
plan for each person served is consistent with the 
philosophy of the treatment program.
3.D. 16. Treatment planning is:
16.3.D. CT
a. Reviewed at least monthly.
16.a.3.D. CT
b. Modified as necessary based on the 
needs of the person served.
16.b.3.D. CT
  

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
192 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
E. Crisis Programs
Description
Crisis programs include a continuum of services 
designed to rapidly respond to the needs of 
persons experiencing acute emotional, mental 
health, and/or substance use crises in order to 
keep them safe, seek to resolve the crisis, and 
maintain community tenure. Crisis response, 
depending on the immediate needs and prefer-
ences of the persons served, may be managed 
through a crisis contact center, a crisis interven-
tion program that might include mobile crisis 
intervention services, or admission to a crisis 
stabilization program.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for crisis 
programs must apply the standards in Sections 
1 and 2 in addition to the standards in this 
subsection, as follows:
n All Crisis Programs meet Standards 1.–6.
n Crisis Contact Center Programs also meet 
Standards 7.–12.
n Crisis Intervention Programs also meet 
Standards 13.–19., and, if mobile crisis 
intervention services are included in the 
program Standards 20.–21.
n Crisis Stabilization Programs also meet 
Standards 22.–39.
n Sobering Centers also meet Standards 40.–
57.
3.E. 1. The program demonstrates the capabil-
ity to make appropriate clinical decisions 
to:
1.3.E. CP
a. Determine an appropriate course of 
action.
1.a.3.E. CP
b. Stabilize the situation as quickly as 
possible.
1.b.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program has personnel with appropriate clin-
ical training, education, and experience to make 
clinical decisions, and records reflect the clinical 
decisions made. Basic components of any crisis 
service are the ability to quickly assess the prob-
lem, decide on the appropriate course of action, 
and bring together the necessary services and 
providers to stabilize the situation as soon as 
possible.
3.E. 2. The program implements written proce-
dures to guide placement of the persons 
served into the least intrusive care that 
safely meets their needs.
2.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program’s procedures assure that the care 
provided is appropriate to the needs and prefer-
ences of the person served and that it does not 
exceed what is needed for safety. In crisis contact 
center and crisis intervention programs, these 
procedures guide whether or not additional ser-
vices beyond the contact are needed. In crisis 
stabilization programs, these procedures may 
complement admission criteria.
3.E. 3. Information on community resources is:
3.3.E. CP
a. Developed.
3.a.3.E. CP
b. Maintained.
3.b.3.E. CP
c. Used for:
3.c.3.E. CP
(1) Coordination of care.
3.c.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Appropriate referral of the 
persons served.
3.c.(2)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Information about community resources that are 
often needed by persons served, such as trans-
portation services, hospital emergency services, 
ambulance services, housing and food assistance, 
information and referral services, and others as 
appropriate, is developed and maintained by the 
program and available for coordination of care 
and referral. In communities where resources are 
well documented and maintained by external 
groups, such as 211 or similar, programs may 
rely upon those groups for some of the ongoing 
maintenance of lists and contacts.
3.E. 4.  If the program is responsible for dis-
patching, there is a process to prioritize 
deployment of personnel based on:
4.3.E. CP
a. Current needs.
4.a.3.E. CP
b. Resources available.
4.b.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 193
c. Safety of the persons served and the 
responding personnel.
4.c.3.E. CP
d. Whether there is a need for involve -
ment of first responders.
4.d.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As resources are frequently limited and triaging of 
acuity is warranted to respond most effectively, 
the program implements processes to evaluate 
current needs and can then allocate resources to 
respond to crises in the community in a way that 
best assures safety and responds to the expecta-
tions of the persons served and community 
stakeholders.
3.E. 5. The program implements written proce-
dures that:
5.3.E. CP
a. Identify thresholds  for timeliness of 
response.
5.a.3.E. CP
b. Provide for monitoring of attainment 
of thresholds.
5.b.3.E. CP
c. Identify a process for implementing 
changes in response to:
5.c.3.E. CP
(1) Results achieved.
5.c.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Fluctuations in demand or 
capacity.
5.c.(2)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program should identify reasonable targets 
for response times and routinely collect data for 
comparison to the targets. Performance targets 
may be established by the program or based on 
the expectations of external stakeholders. For 
ongoing performance improvement, action is 
taken when meeting/exceeding targets or when 
performance is below identified targets.
Examples
5.a. In a crisis stabilization program, the timeli-
ness of response may equate to time of first 
contact to admission or availability of admission 
capacity.
3.E. 6.  The program demonstrates ongoing 
efforts to support the well-being of 
personnel.
6.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As programs in the crisis continuum frequently 
expose personnel to trauma and stress, there 
should be a variety of mechanisms in place to 
assist personnel to maintain their well-being.
Crisis Contact Center
Description
Crisis contact centers interact with persons 
served in the community through a variety of 
mechanisms including voice, chat, text, and 
video, and provide or arrange for the provision of 
services 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Programs 
are designed to rapidly respond to and resolve 
crises in a manner that is sensitive to the needs, 
preferences, and cultural backgrounds of the per-
sons served and that protects their safety and the 
safety of others. The program may work in coor-
dination with other crisis resources such as crisis 
intervention and mobile crisis programs. Crisis 
contact centers may be staffed by a variety of 
individuals including full and part-time employ-
ees, volunteers, and peer support specialists. 
Programs may be referred to as a crisis line, sui-
cide hotline, 988 contact center, or other similar 
description.
3.E. 7. The program implements written proce-
dures for:
7.3.E. CP
a. Determination of eligibility.
7.a.3.E. CP
b. Handling of contacts from persons 
ineligible for services.
7.b.3.E. CP
c. Identification of the person making 
the contact.
7.c.3.E. CP
d. Active rescue.
7.d.3.E. CP
e. Follow-up.
7.e.3.E. CP
f. Third-party contacts.
7.f.3.E. CP
g. Monitoring of contacts.
7.g.3.E. CP
h. Recording of contacts.
7.h.3.E. CP
i. Contact refusal or termination.
7.i.3.E. CP
j. Safety of personnel specific to a 24/7 
setting.
7.j.3.E. CP
k. Soliciting from the persons served 
their:
7.k.3.E. CP
(1) Expectations of the encounter.
7.k.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Preferences.
7.k.(2)3.E. CP
l. Frequent utilization.
7.l.3.E. CP
m. Response to inappropriate use.
7.m.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
194 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n. Determining when face-to-face 
response is indicated.
7.n.3.E. CP
o. Wellness checks.
7.o.3.E. CP
p. Mandatory reporting.
7.p.3.E. CP
Examples
7.a.–b. Eligibility may be limited by scope of con-
tract or geographic limitation.
7.c. The person contacting the program may be 
doing so on behalf of another person and know-
ing who that is and what the connection is to the 
person in crisis may be useful.
7.d. Active rescue refers to the process of contact-
ing emergency services to secure the physical 
safety of a person in crisis who is unable or 
unwilling to do so themselves. This may be nec-
essary in cases when the person is in immediate 
danger of harming themselves or others, or 
where they are at risk of being harmed by some-
one else. Active rescue is a last resort, and it is 
only used when all other options have been 
exhausted. Personnel will first try to de-escalate 
the situation and assess the person’s risk of harm. 
If they believe that the person is in immediate 
danger, they will contact emergency services and 
ask for help.
7.e. These might be caring contacts or welfare 
checks.
7.h. Calls do not have to be recorded; however, if 
they are this is disclosed to the caller.
7.m. Inappropriate use may include prank, 
harassing, abusive, sexually inappropriate, or 
other inappropriate contacts.
3.E. 8. The program implements written proce-
dures to manage contacts that:
8.3.E. CP
a. Identify:
8.a.3.E. CP
(1) The nature of the contact.
8.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) A screening process appropriate 
to the presenting concern.
8.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Suggested responses based on 
needs and preference of the 
persons served.
8.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) The need to document results of 
screening.
8.a.(4)3.E. CP
b. Guide the potential involvement of:
8.b.3.E. CP
(1) Social supports of the person 
served.
8.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Other providers involved with 
the person served, if applicable.
8.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Legal representatives.
8.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Mobile crisis services.
8.b.(4)3.E. CP
(5) First responders.
8.b.(5)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
8.a.(2) The intent of this standard is to document 
the collection of an adequate amount of informa-
tion to provide appropriate and safe services.
8.b. Procedures describe how and when the 
social supports or other providers are engaged 
and circumstances that would warrant dispatch-
ing of mobile crisis services (when available) and 
first responders.
3.E. 9. When the screening process identifies an 
urgent risk for suicide, violence, a medi-
cal emergency, or other risky behaviors:
9.3.E. CP
a. Immediate action is taken.
9.a.3.E. CP
b. There is ongoing monitoring of the 
intervention.
9.b.3.E. CP
c. There is a written statement describ -
ing the resolution.
9.c.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
9.b. Depending on the type of intervention, there 
may be limits to the program’s ability to monitor. 
If the action taken is a call to emergency services, 
once the person has been met there may be 
limited or no ongoing monitoring possible. In 
situations where additional services are provided 
that can be coordinated, the program will 
attempt to monitor the effectiveness of the 
intervention.
3.E. 10. A crisis contact center provides or 
arranges for the provision of services 
24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
10.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to ensure that crisis 
contact services are available at all hours to the 
population served.

3.E. 11. When a crisis contact center uses a sec-

or 24/7 coverage, there is evidence of:

Intent Statements
Rollover provider relationships are closely coor-
dinated arrangements based on written 
agreements that define the expectations of the 
parties involved, the expected training of person-
nel, and routine evaluation of the performance of 
the rollover contacts that ensures timeliness and 
quality are maintained.
Examples
11.d. As is addressed in Standard 1.G.5., service 
evaluation should be documented at least annu-
ally and actions taken to improve performance 
when results are below expectations.

onstrate competencies in:

Programs should establish competencies in each 
area and demonstrate methods for evaluating the 
competencies against the expectations of the 
position for individuals providing direct service. 
This standard is related to Standard 7. in 1.I. Work-
force Development and Management.
Crisis Intervention
Description
Crisis intervention is a face-to-face service 
(which may be delivered using information 
and communication technologies) that rapidly 
assesses the needs of and seeks to stabilize per-
sons in crisis. In accordance with their needs, 
preferences, and cultural backgrounds, the pro-
gram strives to engage and link persons served 
to appropriate services while maintaining their 
community tenure. Services are provided at times 
and in locations that meet the needs of the 
persons served. The program follows up with 
persons served subsequent to the crisis inter-
vention to facilitate adequate coordination of 
care. Crisis intervention services may be deliv-
ered by a mobile crisis intervention team, in an 
urgent care setting, embedded in care settings 
such as an emergency department or psychiatric 
emergency center, or in other settings.
3.E. 13. The program conducts a written, crisis-
focused assessment of each person 
served that includes at
 a minimum:
13.3.E. CP
a. Presenting concerns.
13.a.3.E. CP
b. Preferences of the person served.
13.b.3.E. CP
c. Social determinants of health con -
tributing to the current crisis.
13.c.3.E. CP
d. Current medications, including 
adherence.
13.d.3.E. CP
e. Current/recent use of alcohol and/or 
other substances.
13.e.3.E. CP
f. Risk factors for:
13.f.3.E. CP
(1) Suicide, using a standardized tool 
for persons age 12 and older 
normed for the population 
served.
13.f.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Other self-harm or risk-taking 
behaviors.
13.f.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Violence.
13.f.(3)3.E. CP
g. Trauma.
13.g.3.E. CP
h. Current medical conditions.
13.h.3.E. CP
i. Current treatment providers.
13.i.3.E. CP
j. Current safety plan, when applicable.
13.j.3.E. CP
k. Current living situation.
13.k.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
196 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
l. Availability of supports.
13.l.3.E. CP
m. Advance directives, when applicable.
13.m.3.E. CP
n. Current criminal justice involvement.
13.n.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Persons served in a crisis intervention program 
are frequently involved in the program for per-
haps a matter of hours. As the completion of a 
thorough and comprehensive assessment as 
specified in Section 2.B. is impractical under these 
circumstances, this standard defines the mini-
mum assessment data that should be collected 
for each person served. The emphasis of the 
crisis-focused assessment is more on current 
status and needs and less on history. There may 
be times when completing this is challenging 
for some persons served if they are unable to 
participate in the assessment due to their con-
dition. In those cases, the program will seek to 
gather what information it can in order to ensure 
the best possible referral and care upon comple-
tion of services.
13.c. Although the person’s social determinants 
of health may or may not have a direct correlation 
to the current crisis, the program should deter-
mine how these determinants can be addressed 
to improve the status of the person served upon 
completion of services. 
13.d. Although a person’s medication profile and 
history may be complex, the assessment of cur-
rent medications and adherence focuses on the 
information that is critical to respond to the 
immediate crisis.
13.f. These standards relate to the screening 
process addressed in Section 2.B and the identifi-
cation of suicide risk and other urgent or critical 
needs.
13.f.(1) The intent of this standard is to ensure 
universal screening for all persons served age 12 
and older. Programs may choose to screen per-
sons younger than 12 at their discretion when 
clinically indicated.
13.f.(3) This includes risk of violence toward oth-
ers and from others.
13.n. Information on current criminal justice 
involvement is collected in order to determine 
what services may need to be offered to coordi-
nate care and to prevent further escalation or 
involvement.
Examples
13.c. Examples of social determinants of health 
include lack of adequate housing, unemployment 
or working conditions, lack of access to health-
care, abusive relationships, food insecurity, 
racism, discrimination, violence, and language 
and/or literacy skills. In addition, for children/
youth, social factors may include protective 
services intervention such as removal from home 
or placement changes; parent/caretaker place-
ment complications such as incarceration, illness, 
or death; and other trauma experiences, whether 
victim or perpetrator.
13.d. Depending on the individual, more com-
prehensive information on medication use and 
history, such as efficacy, adverse reactions, aller-
gies, etc., may also be gathered.
13.e. When appropriate, the program might seek 
to develop a complete drug and alcohol use his-
tory in addition to current use that may have 
contributed to the crisis.
13.f. Programs should look holistically at the risk 
factors of the person served for suicide, violence, 
or other risky behaviors. Personnel are encour-
aged to look across life domains in a thorough 
clinical assessment to evaluate current intent and 
plans as well as risk factors that indicate the need 
for development of a safety plan with the person 
served.
Typically, suicide assessments evaluate:
n Risk factors:
– Current psychiatric illness/symptoms
– Alcohol and/or other drug use
– Serious physical or emotional pain
– Previous self-harm or suicide attempts
– Suicide attempts or completion in close 
family/support network
– Age, gender, and social situation
– Relationships that may be supportive/
protective or that may pose a threat 
(abuse or neglect)
– Lack of adequate coping skills/
mechanisms
– Financial difficulties
– Access to lethal methods

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 197
n Current intent and plans:
–W i s h  t o  b e  d e a d
– Feelings of hopelessness
– Regret/remorse over current/previous 
attempt
– Expectation about outcome of self-harm -
ing behavior or suicide attempt/threat
–S p e c i f i c  p l a n s
– Lethality and frequency of plans or 
attempts
–O t h e r  s e l f - h a r m i n g  b e h a v i o r
– Current suicide intent/wishes
– Length of time suicidal feelings have been 
present
– Mental state at time of self-harm or suicide 
attempt or threat (alcohol or drug intake, 
social situation, relationship changes, 
bereavements)
– Plans for others after death, including 
suicide notes, changes to will, and 
consequences
The most common tool for assessing suicide risk 
is the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale 
(CSSRS), and there are several versions of this 
tool for use with different populations and at 
different times. There is a significant amount of 
resource information available at the Columbia 
Lighthouse Project website at http://
cssrs.columbia.edu.
Another tool to consider is the SAFE-T screener. 
Information can be found at www.sprc.org/
resources-programs/suicide-assessment-five-
step-evaluation-and-triage-safe-t-pocket-card.
A screening tool that has been normed for 
children and adolescents age 12 and older is 
the Ask Suicide-Screening Questions (ASQ). 
Information about this tool can be found at 
www.nimh.nih.gov/labs-at-nimh/asq-toolkit-
materials/index.shtml.
13.f.(2) This could include, but is not limited to, 
such things as cutting, needle sharing, unpro-
tected sex, driving at excessive speeds, driving 
under the influence, and extreme sports.
13.f.(3) Programs assess the possibility that the 
person served may harm others in the living 
environment or other environments. Programs 
might consider the use of the Historical Clinical 
Risk Management-20 Version 3 (HCR-20) for 
assistance in evaluating risks. Information about 
this tool can be found at http://hcr-20.com.
13.i. When identifying current treatment provid-
ers, programs may include providers of medical 
care and behavioral health, case managers, or 
other care team members.
13.m. The program gives the person served the 
opportunity to communicate whether or not 
there is an advance directive that may impact the 
course of the particular services provided. See the 
Glossary for the definition of advance directives. 
Applicability will generally occur in those states 
that allow psychiatric advance directives.
3.E. 14. Based on the crisis-focused assessment, a 
crisis intervention plan is developed with 
each person served that includes:
14.3.E. CP
a. Immediate response needs.
14.a.3.E. CP
b. Preferences of the person served.
14.b.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Since crisis intervention programs consist of 
immediate and short-term response to the crises, 
the plan for care may be briefer and more focused 
than the person-centered plan described in 
Section 2.C. The plan might only address the 
immediate services needed to respond to the 
current crisis of the person served and the tran-
sition to other services.
Examples
14.a. Immediate response needs may vary from 
the need for specific clinical interventions, 
modalities of care, and medications prescribed 
or administered to securing housing, food, 
healthcare, or addressing other social determi-
nants that can help stabilize the persons served 
and maintain community tenure.
3.E. 15. When the crisis-focused assessment 
identifies a potential risk for suicide, 
violence, or other risky behaviors, a 
safety plan is developed:
15.3.E. CP
a. In accordance with an established 
protocol.
15.a.3.E. CP
b. With the person served.
15.b.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
198 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
c. As soon as possible.
15.c.3.E. CP
d. That includes:
15.d.3.E. CP
(1) Warning signs.
15.d.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Current coping skills.
15.d.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Actions to be taken to:
15.d.(3)3.E. CP
(a) Respond to periods of 
increased emotional pain.
15.d.(3)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Make the environment safe.
15.d.(3)(b)3.E. CP
(4) Preferred interventions neces -
sary for:
15.d.(4)3.E. CP
(a) Personal safety.
15.d.(4)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Public safety.
15.d.(4)(b)3.E. CP
(5) Advanced directives, when 
available.
15.d.(5)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The assessment should gather sufficient informa-
tion to determine when the person served has 
risk factors consistent with activities or potential 
for self-harm or harm to others, as noted in Stand-
ard 2.B.13.q. The program should establish a 
formal protocol that describes when personal 
safety plans are clinically indicated and should 
assist the provider and support system to provide 
methods for responding to dangerous behaviors 
exhibited by the person served in a safe and 
effective manner.
Examples
A personal safety plan may be referred to as a 
crisis intervention or behavioral management 
treatment plan, a crisis plan, or may be referred 
to in a psychiatric advance directive.
An example of a safety plan template that can be 
used is the Stanley-Brown Safety Plan found at: 
www.mysafetyplan.org/
Other tools that can be considered for use to 
assist persons served can be found at: https://
moodtools.org/
15.d.(3)(b) Acute episodes of strong suicidal ide-
ation are generally brief, and often people choose 
a suicide method that is readily available. Reduc-
ing access to the most lethal means can increase 
survival even when people make attempts.
3.E. 16. For each person served, the program 
documents at the conclusion of the 
encounter:
16.3.E. CP
a. Resolution or disposition.
16.a.3.E. CP
b. Follow up.
16.b.3.E. CP
c. Recommendations for additional 
supports, if applicable.
16.c.3.E. CP
  
3.E. 17. The program implements written 
procedures that address involuntary 
hospitalization.
17.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Every state/province has established laws and 
regulations for the involuntary hospitalization 
of individuals who are typically determined to 
be a threat to themselves or others. The crisis 
intervention personnel demonstrate knowledge 
of the laws and procedures for involuntary 
hospitalization.
3.E. 18. Qualified behavioral health practitioners 
are available during the program’s hours 
of operation.
18.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
See the Glossary for the definition of a qualified 
behavioral health practitioner. Available may 
include being on call.

Programs should establish competencies in each

3.E. 20. The program implements written proce-
dures that address:
20.3.E. CP
a. Safety of the mobile crisis environ -
ment including:
20.a.3.E. CP
(1) Known environmental factors/
considerations from dispatch.
20.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Composition of the mobile 
response team.
20.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) On-site assessment of environ -
mental risk.
20.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Program monitoring of the 
response team deployed.
20.a.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Actions to take in response to 
an escalating situation, when 
applicable.
20.a.(5)3.E. CP
(6) Transportation of persons 
served, when applicable.
20.a.(6)3.E. CP
(7) Reporting on the status of the 
response team at the conclusion 
of the encounter.
20.a.(7)3.E. CP
b. Involvement of first responders in the 
crisis response including:
20.b.3.E. CP
(1) When first responder involve -
ment is indicated.
20.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) How to access first responders.
20.b.(2)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Mobile services include the capacity to respond 
to the site where the individual in crisis is located.
20.b. If the program utilizes a co-responder 
model in which first responders are always part 
of the team it would not be expected to have 
a written procedure for b.(1). Otherwise, the 
program’s procedures should specify when to 
include first responders and how to access them 
expediently in the required situations.
Examples
Mobile services may be provided directly by the 
program, or linkages for the provision of emer-
gency or crisis intervention services may be 
established through community organizations 
such as visiting nurse groups, community mental 
health centers, case management programs, or 
other designs.
20.a.(1) There may be information shared with 
dispatch about the safety of the environment 
based on reporting of the person’s state of mind, 
access to weapons, known history of prior calls/
events, and/or current or historical risk of 
domestic violence.
20.a.(2) Procedures on the composition of the 
team may address known or unknown factors 
that would determine if the team should be 
composed of 1 or 2 persons or whether a co-
responder team might be added.
20.a.(4) The program might utilize a variety of 
mechanisms to monitor and ensure the safety of 
all parties involved in the mobile response. These 
could include radios, phone or text messages, 
timed check-ins, or others.
3.E. 21. All direct service personnel receive 
competency-based training:
21.3.E. CP
a. At:
21.a.3.E. CP
(1) Orientation.
21.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Regular intervals.
21.a.(2)3.E. CP
b. In:
21.b.3.E. CP
(1) First aid.
21.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Cardiopulmonary resuscitation 
(CPR).
21.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) The use of emergency equip -
ment, when present.
21.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Assessing risks in the location of 
the person served.
21.b.(4)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing staff and physicians are exempt 
from this requirement as their professional train-
ing and licensing ensure competency.
21.a.(2) The regular interval for training is 
defined by the program to ensure personnel 
maintain ongoing competencies and/or certi-
fication in the areas addressed. See the Glossary 
for a definition of regular.
Examples
21.b.(4) Risks in the location of the person 
served can vary, and, although not an exhaustive 
list, may include safety risks in the physical envi-
ronment, other persons present who might pose 
a risk, the presence of weapons, presence of ani-
mals, and being a sole responder in this situation.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
200 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Crisis Stabilization
Description
Crisis stabilization programs are short-term 
programs organized to respond to the needs of 
persons experiencing acute emotional, mental 
health, and/or substance use crises that cannot 
be effectively managed in other less intensive 
programs. These programs operate 24 hours a 
day, 7 days a week and can quickly triage the 
needs of persons served to engage them safely 
into care. Utilizing a person-centered approach 
and a collaborative decision-making process, a 
crisis stabilization plan is developed for each 
person served with the goal of stabilizing the 
acute crisis and managing effective transition 
to appropriate programs/services following 
discharge. A variety of treatment services and 
structured therapeutic activities is available to 
meet the individual needs of persons served. 
Through various observation and monitoring 
activities the program ensures the safety of the 
environment for the persons served and person-
nel. Crisis stabilization programs offer a calm, 
welcoming environment that maintains the 
dignity of the persons served.
3.E. 22. The program has the capability to admit 
persons 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
22.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As individuals may experience a crisis at any time, 
crisis stabilization programs must operate in such 
a way that they are able to receive persons in 
their time of crisis and respond quickly to triage 
their needs. It is understood that there may be 
times when the program is at full capacity or a 
person arriving at the program requires a higher 
level of supervision or alternative services based 
on acuity. In these types of circumstances, the 
program implements procedures to meet the 
needs of persons through coordination with 
other organizations/providers in the community.
3.E. 23. The program conducts a written, crisis-
focused assessment of each person 
served:
23.3.E. CP
a. Upon admission to the program.
23.a.3.E. CP
b. That includes, at a minimum:
23.b.3.E. CP
(1) Presenting concerns.
23.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Social determinants of health 
contributing to the current crisis.
23.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Current medications, including 
adherence.
23.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Use of alcohol and/or other 
substances.
23.b.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Risk factors for:
23.b.(5)3.E. CP
(a) Suicide, using a standard -
ized tool for persons age 12 
and older normed for the 
population served.
23.b.(5)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Other self-harm or risk-
taking behaviors.
23.b.(5)(b)3.E. CP
(c) Violence.
23.b.(5)(c)3.E. CP
(6) Trauma.
23.b.(6)3.E. CP
(7) Current medical conditions.
23.b.(7)3.E. CP
(8) Current treatment providers.
23.b.(8)3.E. CP
(9) History of previous crises, includ -
ing response and results, when 
applicable.
23.b.(9)3.E. CP
(10) Personal strengths.
23.b.(10)3.E. CP
(11) Individual needs.
23.b.(11)3.E. CP
(12) Abilities and/or interests.
23.b.(12)3.E. CP
(13) Preferences.
23.b.(13)3.E. CP
(14) Cultural/spiritual background.
23.b.(14)3.E. CP
(15) Advance directives, when 
applicable.
23.b.(15)3.E. CP
(16) Legal status, when applicable.
23.b.(16)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Persons served in a crisis stabilization program 
are frequently transferred or discharged in a mat-
ter of hours or very few days. As the completion 
of a thorough and comprehensive assessment 
as specified in Section 2.B. is impractical in these 
cases, this standard defines the minimum assess-
ment data that should be collected for each 
person served. There may be times when com-
pleting this is challenging for some persons 
served if they are unable to participate in the 

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 201
assessment due to their condition at admission. 
In those cases, the program will seek to gather 
the information prior to program completion in 
order to ensure the best possible referral and care 
post-discharge.
23.b.(3) Although a person’s medication profile 
and history may be complex, the assessment of 
current medications and adherence focuses on 
the information that is critical to respond to the 
immediate crisis.
23.b.(5) These standards relate to the screening 
process addressed in Section 2.B and the identifi-
cation of suicide risk and other urgent or critical 
needs.
23.b.(5)(a) The intent of this standard is to ensure 
universal screening for all persons served age 
12 and older. Programs may choose to screen 
persons younger than 12 at their discretion 
when clinically indicated.
23.b.(5)(c) This includes risk of violence toward 
others and from others.
Examples
23.b.(1) Information gathered on presenting 
concerns is typically from the point of view of the 
person served; however, there may be input from 
family members, first responders, and/or others 
interested in the well-being of the person.
23.b.(2) The person served may have one or 
more social factors that contributed to the need 
for admission. Examples include lack of adequate 
housing, unemployment, lack of access to health-
care, abusive relationships, and food insecurity. 
In addition, for children/youth, social factors 
may include protective services intervention such 
as removal from home or placement changes; 
parent/caretaker placement complications such 
as incarceration, illness, or death; and other 
trauma experiences, whether victim or perpetra-
tor. Including this information in the assessment 
assists the program to determine methods to 
respond to social determinants as a means to 
prevent future needs for crisis services.
23.b.(3) Depending on the individual, more 
comprehensive information on medication use 
and history, such as efficacy, adverse reactions, 
allergies, etc., may also be gathered.
23.b.(4) Programs might seek to develop a com-
plete drug and alcohol use history in addition to 
current use that may have contributed to the 
crisis.
23.b.(5) Programs should look holistically at 
the risk factors of the person served for suicide, 
violence, or other risky behaviors. Personnel 
are encouraged to look across life domains in a 
thorough clinical assessment to evaluate current 
intent and plans as well as risk factors that indi-
cate the need for development of a safety plan 
with the person served.
Typically, suicide assessments evaluate:
n Risk factors:
– Current psychiatric illness/symptoms.
– Alcohol and/or other drug use.
– Serious physical or emotional pain.
– Previous self-harm or suicide attempts.
– Suicide attempts or completion in close 
family/support network.
– Age, gender, and social situation.
– Relationships that may be supportive/
protective or that may pose a threat 
(abuse or neglect).
– Lack of adequate coping skills/
mechanisms.
– Financial difficulties.
– Access to lethal methods.
n Current intent and plans:
–W i s h  t o  b e  d e a d .
– Feelings of hopelessness.
– Regret/remorse over current/previous 
attempt.
– Expectation about outcome of self-harm -
ing behavior or suicide attempt/threat.
–S p e c i f i c  p l a n s .
– Lethality and frequency of plans or 
attempts.
– Other self-harming behavior.
– Current suicide intent/wishes.
– Length of time suicidal feelings have been 
present.
– Mental state at time of self-harm or suicide 
attempt or threat (alcohol or drug intake, 

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
202 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
social situation, relationship changes, 
bereavements).
– Plans for others after death, including 
suicide notes, changes to will, and 
consequences.
The most common tool for assessing suicide 
risk is the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating 
Scale (CSSRS), and there are several versions 
of this tool for use with different populations 
and at different times. There is a significant 
amount of resource information available at 
the Columbia Lighthouse Project website at 
https://cssrs.columbia.edu.
Another tool to consider is the SAFE-T 
screener. Information can be found at 
www.sprc.org/resources-programs/suicide-
assessment-five-step-evaluation-and-triage-
safe-t-pocket-card.
A screening tool that has been normed for 
children and adolescents age 12 and older is 
the Ask Suicide-Screening Questions (ASQ). 
Information about this tool can be found at 
www.nimh.nih.gov/research/research-con-
ducted-at-nimh/asq-toolkit-materials. 
23.b.(5)(b) This could include, but is not limited 
to, such things as cutting, needle sharing, unpro-
tected sex, driving at excessive speeds, driving 
under the influence, and extreme sports.
23.b.(5)(c) Programs assess the possibility that 
the person served may harm others in the living 
environment or other environments. Programs 
might consider the use of the Historical, Clinical, 
Risk Management-20 Version 3 (HCR-20) for 
assistance in evaluating risks. Information about 
this tool can be found at http://hcr-20.com.
23.b.(8) When identifying current treatment 
providers, programs may include providers of 
medical care and behavioral health, case manag-
ers, or other care team members.
23.b.(9) Programs will want to be cautious not to 
look at past crises too narrowly. In addition to 
past crisis admissions, the assessment might 
include such things as unintentional overdose, 
suicide gestures/attempts, loss of relationships, 
other experiences of trauma, etc.
23.b.(10) Personal strengths may include assets, 
resources, and natural positives.
23.b.(11) Individual needs may include liabilities, 
weaknesses, and what the person needs to 
recover.
23.b.(12) Abilities and/or interests may include 
skills, aptitudes, capabilities, talents, and 
competencies.
23.b.(13) Preferences are those things the person 
served perceives will enhance the treatment 
experience.
23.b.(15) The intent of this standard is to provide 
the person served with the specific opportunity 
to communicate whether or not there is an 
advance directive that may impact the course of 
the particular services provided. See the Glossary 
for the definition of advance directives. Applica-
bility will generally occur in those states that 
allow psychiatric advance directives.
23.b.(16) Programs understand the ability of the 
person to consent to treatment, or whether the 
person has a court-appointed conservator, guard-
ian, or isn’t of age to consent to treatment. Many 
crisis stabilization programs operate as receiving 
facilities in locations with civil commitment laws 
(or community treatment orders in Canada), and 
the person’s legal status may be affected by invol-
untary treatment orders. Persons served may also 
be on probation or parole, which might require 
some coordination with the person’s community 
supervision department.
3.E. 24. The assessment process includes the 
preparation of a written interpretive 
summary that:
24.3.E. CP
a. Is based on the assessment data.
24.a.3.E. CP
b. Identifies any co-occurring disabili -
ties, comorbidities, and/or disorders.
24.b.3.E. CP
c. Is used to develop the crisis stabiliza -
tion plan.
24.c.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The interpretive summary is a written clinical 
formulation designed to integrate and interpret 
from a broader perspective all history and assess-
ment information collected. It identifies needs 
and addresses how they are considered when 
developing the crisis stabilization plan.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 203
Examples
The interpretive summary could address:
n The central theme(s) apparent in the presen-
tation of the person served.
n Histories and assessments (medical, psycho-
social, spiritual, or vocational), with special 
emphasis on potential interrelationships 
between sets of findings.
n The perception of the persons served of their 
needs, strengths, limitations, and
 problems.
n Clinical judgments regarding both positive 
and negative factors likely to affect the per-
son’s course of treatment and clinical 
outcomes after discharge (i.e., recovery).
n Recommended treatments, including any 
special assessments or tests, as well as routine 
procedures (e.g., laboratory tests).
n A general discussion of the anticipated level 
of care, length and intensity of treatment, 
and expected focus (goals) with 
recommendations.
3.E. 25. Based on the crisis-focused assessment, 
an initial crisis stabilization plan:
25.3.E. CP
a. Is developed:
25.a.3.E. CP
(1) Upon admission.
25.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) In collaboration with the person 
served.
25.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) With the involvement of the fam -
ily/support system of the person 
served, when applicable and 
permitted.
25.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) With the involvement of other 
service/treatment providers, 
when applicable and permitted.
25.a.(4)3.E. CP
b. Addresses:
25.b.3.E. CP
(1) Immediate needs to stabilize the 
acute crisis.
25.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Co-occurring disabilities, comor -
bidities, and/or disorders.
25.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) The person-centered plan, if one 
is available.
25.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Advance directives from the per -
son served and/or legal guardian, 
when applicable.
25.b.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Transition/discharge.
25.b.(5)3.E. CP
c. Is based on the person’s:
25.c.3.E. CP
(1) Personal strengths.
25.c.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Individual needs.
25.c.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Abilities and/or interests.
25.c.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Preferences.
25.c.(4)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Although CARF does not prescribe any particular 
form or format to be used for the crisis stabiliza-
tion plan, this standard has specific requirements 
for the components of the plan. These plans are 
focused on the acute crisis and its stabilization, 
with additional emphasis on managing the care 
transition to ensure continuing treatment/
services.
25.a.(2) The persons served are involved in the 
development and implementation of the plan 
based on their ability. Although it is understood 
that some persons served may be unable to 
participate fully at admission, there should be 
evidence of continuing collaboration until pro-
gram completion.
25.a.(3) When the person served has been 
receiving behavioral healthcare or other services 
in another program at the time of the crisis, the 
program obtains the current person-centered 
plan in order to facilitate consistency and conti-
nuity of care.
Examples
25.b.(1) Treatment needs may vary from specific 
clinical interventions, modalities of care, medi-
cations prescribed or administered, and may 
additionally include securing housing, food, 
healthcare, or addressing other social determi-
nants that can stabilize the persons served in 
their community of choice.
25.b.(2) Special attention may be paid to coor-
dination of care for chronic illnesses such as 
diabetes, asthma, hypertension, chronic obstruc-
tive pulmonary disease (COPD), etc.
25.b.(4) Programs may make efforts to under-
stand the desires of persons served in individual 
situations and not limit themselves to obtaining 
legally executed advanced directives. For exam-
ple, a wellness recovery action plan (WRAP) 
might also be a document a program would 
obtain to ensure consistency with ongoing 
services.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
204 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
25.c.(1) Personal strengths may include assets, 
resources, and natural positives.
25.c.(2) Individual needs may include liabilities, 
weaknesses, and what the person needs to 
recover.
25.c.(3) Abilities and/or interests may include 
skills, aptitudes, capabilities, talents, 
and competencies.
25.c.(4) Preferences are those things the person 
served perceives will enhance the treatment 
experience.
3.E. 26. Twenty-four hours a day, seven days a 
week, the program provides:
26.3.E. CP
a. On-site awake personnel.
26.a.3.E. CP
b. Availability of supervisory personnel.
26.b.3.E. CP
c. Access to appropriate medical 
personnel.
26.c.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
26.b. The intent of this standard is to ensure that 
there is direct access to supervisory personnel to 
quickly respond to the variety of situations that 
can occur in a 24-hour crisis stabilization pro-
gram. This access may not always be face to face; 
it can be via telephone or other appropriate 
channels.
26.c. The program has access to medical person-
nel to meet the needs of the persons served. 
Medical personnel may be on site, on call, accessi-
ble via telehealth or other technology, etc.
Examples
26.c. Medical personnel may include physicians, 
nurses, pharmacists, phlebotomists, etc.
3.E. 27. The program provides access to nutri-
tious meals and snacks that meet the 
dietary needs of the persons served.
27.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Because persons served may be admitted into 
the program at all hours of the day, the program 
should provide access to food outside of fixed 
meal-service times.
Examples
Dietary needs may include nutritional needs 
of persons served, dietary allowances or restric-
tions, allergies, or dietary customs such as 
vegetarian or kosher foods.
3.E. 28. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, the program provides or 
arranges for the following services:
28.3.E. CP
a. Individual counseling/therapy.
28.a.3.E. CP
b. Group counseling/therapy.
28.b.3.E. CP
c. Family/support system counseling/
therapy.
28.c.3.E. CP
d. Skills building.
28.d.3.E. CP
e. Substance use disorder counseling/
therapy.
28.e.3.E. CP
f. Self-care planning.
28.f.3.E. CP
g. Peer support.
28.g.3.E. CP
h. Care coordination.
28.h.3.E. CP
i. Interpretation services.
28.i.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Not all listed services must be provided to each 
person served, but the program should have the 
capacity to provide each of these to individual 
persons served based on their identified needs.
Examples
28.i. This standard might be met with ready 
access to a language line that can assist with 
interpretation.
3.E. 29. The program provides:
29.3.E. CP
a. A written daily schedule of activities 
that contribute to the wellness of the 
persons served.
29.a.3.E. CP
b. Opportunities to engage in unstruc -
tured activities.
29.b.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Scheduled activities should include some of the 
services listed in Standard 3.E.7. (above). The 
schedule should still allow for persons served to 
seek calming or stress-reducing activities, as well 
as have the opportunity to take care of personal 
business.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 205
Examples
29.a. The written daily schedule may include a 
description of the activities offered in order for 
persons served to determine the appropriateness 
of the activity for themselves.
29.b. Unstructured activities might include 
reading, puzzles, listening to music, crafts, 
schoolwork, paying bills, checking email, or 
other personal activities.
3.E. 30. The program reduces barriers to inter-
action between the persons served and 
personnel.
30.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program should make efforts to reduce both 
attitudinal and physical barriers that create dis-
tance and separation between program 
personnel and the persons served. Personnel 
should be engaged with and accessible to the 
persons served, rather than remaining behind 
glass barriers or in an office or lounge where the 
persons served cannot interact with them. In 
addition, this ensures that personnel are able 
to quickly respond to any instability of persons 
served.
3.E. 31. The program’s physical facilities provide:
31.3.E. CP
a. A calming environment.
31.a.3.E. CP
b. Personal privacy.
31.b.3.E. CP
c. Security of personal items.
31.c.3.E. CP
d. Adequate space for:
31.d.3.E. CP
(1) Group interactions.
31.d.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Quiet activities.
31.d.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Self–de-escalation by persons 
served.
31.d.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Visits with family/support 
system.
31.d.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Therapeutic activities.
31.d.(5)3.E. CP
(6) Cultural and/or spiritual 
activities.
31.d.(6)3.E. CP
(7) Meals.
31.d.(7)3.E. CP
(8) Recreation.
31.d.(8)3.E. CP
(9) Based on gender, age, and needs, 
appropriate areas for:
31.d.(9)3.E. CP
(a) Sleeping.
31.d.(9)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Hygiene.
31.d.(9)(b)3.E. CP
e. Access to an outdoor setting, if 
possible.
31.e.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
31.d. The program is not required to maintain 
exclusive spaces for each of these, but appropri-
ate areas should be provided and available for 
these activities as needed.
Examples
31.a. Consideration might be given to lighting, 
noise, odors, overhead announcements, carpet 
pattern, and comfort of furniture.
31.d.(9) Needs might include illness or conta-
gion, history of violence toward others, trauma, 
or other individual needs.
31.d.(9)(a) Separation based on gender does not 
need to occur when parents and children sleep in 
the same area. When serving children/youth, age 
also considers developmental level.
3.E. 32. The program demonstrates continuous 
vigilance regarding safety of the environ-
ment for the persons served and 
personnel through:
32.3.E. CP
a. Assessment of risks, including:
32.a.3.E. CP
(1) Ligatures.
32.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Sharps.
32.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Furnishings.
32.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Facility maintenance issues.
32.a.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Other safety risks.
32.a.(5)3.E. CP
b. Response to identified risks.
32.b.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program implements surveillance activities 
that ensure the safety of all persons served and 
personnel on an ongoing basis. The program 
likely alters these activities based on the persons 
served and the assessment of risks in the milieu.
32.a.(1) It is not the intent of this standard to 
require that facilities be retrofitted into a ligature-
free environment; however, awareness of ligature 
points is crucial to ensuring vigilant observation 
and protection of persons served.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
206 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
32.a.(5) Other safety risks might include leisure 
items; toys; visitors/family members who are 
upset, angry, or carrying a weapon; and entrances 
and exits in a secured program environment.
3.E. 33. To ensure the safety of persons served 
and personnel, the program implements 
written procedures for searches:
33.3.E. CP
a. Of persons served.
33.a.3.E. CP
b. Of belongings.
33.b.3.E. CP
c. Of the facility.
33.c.3.E. CP
d. That:
33.d.3.E. CP
(1) Protect privacy.
33.d.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Preserve dignity.
33.d.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Are sensitive to potential trauma 
of the persons served.
33.d.(3)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program ensures that persons served and 
personnel remain safe in the program, without 
introduction of weapons, drugs, or alcohol into 
the milieu. Personnel are adequately trained in 
the search process in order to respect the dignity 
and privacy of the persons served. Programs 
may find it necessary to conduct searches of the 
persons served, their belongings, or the physical 
facility based on observed behaviors or reason-
able suspicion of persons served or others.
Examples
33.d. Searches are conducted by personnel with 
whom the person served feels comfortable. This 
may include specific protocols based on a per-
son’s gender identity, as well as consideration 
of religious or cultural factors, prior history of 
trauma, and/or the person’s age. In order to assist 
the persons served to retain some feeling of 
control, the program could seek permission 
to conduct searches of individuals or their 
belongings.
3.E. 34. The program:
34.3.E. CP
a. Identifies an indicator to measure 
successful transitions of the persons 
served into ongoing services post 
discharge.
34.a.3.E. CP
b. At least annually addresses:
34.b.3.E. CP
(1) Performance in relationship to an 
established target.
34.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Trends.
34.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Actions for improvement.
34.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Results of performance improve -
ment plans.
34.b.(4)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
This standard complements Standard 1.L.4. and 
addresses measurement of the effectiveness of 
the crisis stabilization program. As these pro-
grams are rarely, if ever, the final point of care 
for persons served, a significant responsibility 
of crisis stabilization programs is transitioning 
persons served into care post discharge. Through 
this standard, the program measures its effective-
ness at transitioning persons served into ongoing 
treatment services.
3.E. 35. Within 48 hours of discharge/transition 
the program provides or arranges for a 
caring contact to each person served.
35.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Programs implement actions to contact persons 
served after discharge to demonstrate ongoing 
concern about their well-being, and depending 
on the method of communication, may gather 
information about the success of the transition.
Examples
There are a variety of ways the program may 
choose to offer a caring contact. Calls might be 
initiated by peer support specialists or other 
personnel. The program might have arranged to 
send text messages of encouragement or follow-
up. A letter could be mailed to the person’s home.
3.E. 36. The program implements 
written procedures that address:
36.3.E. CP
a. Bed assignment.
36.a.3.E. CP
b. Capacity management.
36.b.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 207
c. Involvement of first responders, if 
applicable.
36.c.3.E. CP
d. Management of personal belongings.
36.d.3.E. CP
e. Levels of observation, including:
36.e.3.E. CP
(1) Close observation.
36.e.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Routine observation and 
monitoring.
36.e.(2)3.E. CP
f. Management of involuntary status, 
including communicating to persons 
served regarding changes in status.
36.f.3.E. CP
g. Persons served leaving the premises.
36.g.3.E. CP
h. Mail.
36.h.3.E. CP
i. Telephonic and electronic 
communication.
36.i.3.E. CP
j. Use of personal electronics.
36.j.3.E. CP
k. Visits with family/support system.
36.k.3.E. CP
l. Involvement of family/support sys -
tem, identified legal representatives, 
or others, with legal right or the con-
sent of the persons served.
36.l.3.E. CP
Examples
36.b. Procedures may describe actions under-
taken when the program approaches capacity, 
when it reaches capacity, and when it has to 
divert persons at full capacity. If the program 
manages capacity based on acuity of the popula-
tion, the program might describe what steps are 
taken to manage admissions.
36.c. Programs that accept persons served 
directly from first responders in the community 
may describe the process for admissions, what 
information is collected, if there are commitment 
processes, how weapons are secured, and how to 
manage the handoff.
36.f. Procedures may describe the responsibilities 
of the program when a person served is admitted 
to the program under an involuntary commit-
ment order, how communication with the person 
served occurs and informed consent is obtained, 
and how the expiration of the order is managed.
36.j. Programs are often reluctant to allow the 
use of personal electronic devices. However, the 
use of personal electronic devices can have a pos-
itive influence on the person served, particularly 
through the use of apps targeting recovery and 
self-regulation. Programs could attempt to strike 
a balance in the use of these devices, rather than 
simply prohibiting their use.

a. At:
37.a.3.E. CP
(1) Orientation.
37.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Regular intervals.
37.a.(2)3.E. CP
b. In:
37.b.3.E. CP
(1) First aid.
37.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Cardiopulmonary resuscitation 
(CPR).
37.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) The use of emergency equip -
ment, when present.
37.b.(3)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing personnel and physicians are 
exempt from this requirement as their profes-
sional training and licensing ensure competency.
Examples
37.b.(1) First aid includes Naloxone 
administration.
37.b.(3) Emergency equipment could include 
automatic external defibrillators, eyewashing 
stations, splints, tourniquets, and EpiPens®.

38.3.E. CP
a. At:
38.a.3.E. CP
(1) Orientation.

(2) Regular intervals.

with lived experience.

Intent Statements
38.b. Persons served often report that the experi-
ences they have with admissions and stays in 
crisis stabilization programs were challenging 
and sometimes traumatic to them. Including 
persons with lived experience in the program’s 
training assists personnel to understand more 
fully how their actions and practices affect the 
persons served. If the program does not directly 
include persons with lived experience in the 
training, it should seek to gather input from 
persons served to inform the training process.
3.E. 39.  If the program allows first responders to 
drop off or pick up persons served from 
the program, the program demonstrates 
efforts to educate first responders on:
39.3.E. CP
a. Its procedures for the involvement of 
first responders with the program.
39.a.3.E. CP
b. How to safely manage persons who 
are experiencing acute emotional, 
mental health, and/or substance use 
crises.
39.b.3.E. CP
Examples
39.a. This may include procedures for drop off 
and pick up, what information about persons 
served can and cannot be shared, securing of 
weapons, and any involuntary commitment 
procedures.
39.b. The program may provide training, develop 
materials, identify resources, etc.
Sobering Center
Description
Sobering centers provide a safe, supportive, and 
welcoming environment for persons with acute 
intoxication from alcohol and other substances. 
By offering low-barrier access, these centers aim 
to divert persons from emergency healthcare and 
criminal justice systems and connect them with 
community resources. Sobering centers operate 
with minimal requirements for entry, including 
accepting persons regardless of their financial 
status or housing situation. The intake process is 
streamlined to allow rapid access to a safe space, 
prioritizing the person’s well-being over adminis-
trative hurdles.
The primary function of a sobering center is to 
offer a secure and nonjudgmental place for per-
sons to rest and stabilize from intoxication to 
safely return to their community. Sobering cen-
ters are staffed by a multidisciplinary team of 
medical and other professionals who provide 
continuous monitoring of the person’s condition 
in an environment that is clean, calm, and 
designed to minimize stress.
In addition to providing for a person's immediate 
health needs related to intoxication, a sobering 
center functions as a diversion program. By 
providing an alternative to arrest, the program 
reduces the burden on law enforcement and the 
criminal justice system. It may also prevent per-
sons from incurring or help to lessen criminal 
records, fines, or other legal consequences that 
can perpetuate a cycle of poverty and instability. 
This approach reframes intoxication from a 
criminal offense to a public health issue. Like-
wise, sobering centers function as an alternative 
to emergency services. The program's aim is to 
reduce the burden on an often-overcrowded 
emergency healthcare system and provide a level 
of care that is appropriate for the needs of per-
sons with uncomplicated intoxication.
Recognizing that dangerous intoxication can 
occur at any time, sobering centers operate 24 
hours a day, 7 days a week. This constant avail-
ability ensures that law enforcement, emergency 
healthcare services, other referring parties, and 
individuals can access services when needed.
Sobering centers also serve as a critical entry 
point to a broader network of treatment and 
social services. Personnel assist the person served 
to determine short and longer-term needs and 
offer referrals to care and resources as part of the 
discharge planning process. These often include 
referrals for substance use disorder treatment, 
mental health services, medical care, housing 
assistance, and other social health needs. The 
goal is not just to manage the immediate crisis, 
but to begin to address the underlying issues that 
impact potential for recovery.

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 209
3.E. 40. The program offers low-barrier accessi-
bility for:
40.3.E. CP
a. Persons served.
40.a.3.E. CP
b. Emergency medical services.
40.b.3.E. CP
c. Hospitals.
40.c.3.E. CP
d. Law enforcement.
40.d.3.E. CP
e. Other stakeholders.
40.e.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
An important principle of sobering centers is to 
provide immediate, accessible care with as few 
administrative, financial, or eligibility barriers as 
possible that might delay or prevent access to 
services. This approach ensures that persons 
experiencing acute intoxication can receive 
appropriate care when needed, while also 
facilitating efficient utilization by emergency 
responders, healthcare facilities, law enforce-
ment, and other stakeholders.
3.E. 41. The program has a qualified medical 
director who:
41.3.E. CP
a. Is a physician or an advanced practice 
provider.
41.a.3.E. CP
b. Has a written ag reement with the 
program that outlines the medical 
director’s responsibilities.
41.b.3.E. CP
c. Leads the medical personnel of the 
program.
41.c.3.E. CP
d. Is responsible for:
41.d.3.E. CP
(1) Developing medical criteria for 
admission to the program.
41.d.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Developing protocols for ongo -
ing medical monitoring based on 
the needs of the person served.
41.d.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Developing medical criteria for 
discharge from the program.
41.d.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Developing criteria for conditions 
that warrant transfer of a person 
served to a higher level of care.
41.d.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Medical consultation regarding 
the needs of the persons served.
41.d.(5)3.E. CP
(6) Actively participating in perfor -
mance improvement activities.
41.d.(6)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
41.a.  The program’s medical director can be a 
physician (M.D. or D.O.) or an advanced practice 
provider (APRN, P .A.) who has the capability to 
satisfy the requirements of the position described 
in the standards. There is not a set requirement 
for the number of hours the medical director 
provides this service; however, they should be 
available for enough time to reasonably perform 
the described activities.
41.d. The medical director is not required to 
perform all of these functions directly but is 
engaged in these activities and has decision-
making authority regarding the medical care 
of the program.
Examples
41.d.(1) Criteria for admission address the fac-
tors that allow admission to the program and 
remain sensitive to the low-barrier concept. 
Examples for inclusion are:
n Persons experiencing acute intoxication from 
alcohol or other substances that require moni-
toring in a supportive environment.
n The person can ambulate with minimal assis-
tance or with mobility aids available to the 
program.
n The person can follow basic safety instruc-
tions, even if cognition is impaired.
n The person is eligible for admission regardless 
of housing status, insurance coverage, or abil
-
ity to pay.
n The persons can be admitted even if they 
have
 been to the program multiple times 
previously.
Programs may also define exclusionary criteria. 
Exclusionary criteria are:
n Based on safety and clinical appropriateness 
rather than administrative convenience.
n As limited as possible to facilitate low-barrier 
access.
n Clearly defined in medical protocols devel-
oped by the medical director.
n Documented and applied consistently.
n Accompanied by referral to appropriate alter-
native resources.

210 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
41.d.(2) Ongoing medical monitoring protocols 
may describe frequency of collection of vital 
signs, standardized screening administration 
(e.g., COWS, CIW A-Ar, SHOT scale, AUDIT, 
DAST or similar), and responses to findings.
41.d.(4) Care that is outside of the sobering 
center's capability to safely support due to 
concurrent medical conditions (e.g., severe 
intoxication, injuries, active seizures, hyper/
hypothermia) that require advanced care and 
management of the transfer of the person served.
41.d.(5) The program may have protocols that 
define when to contact the medical director for 
consultation.
41.d.(6) The medical director participates in per-
formance improvement activities conducted by 
the program. Examples of these activities might 
include examining data related to new drug 
trends, managing infectious disease outbreaks, 
addressing co-occurring health and substance 
use conditions, and monitoring protocols related 
to emergency transfer rates.
3.E. 42. The program has a qualified program 
director who:
42.3.E. CP
a. Has appropriate experience to lead 
the sobering center program.
42.a.3.E. CP
b. Has responsibility for:
42.b.3.E. CP
(1) Establishing program policies 
and procedures.
42.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Educational ac tivities with 
program personnel.
42.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Program development and 
modification.
42.b.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Review of all critical incident 
reports.
42.b.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Performance improvement 
activities.
42.b.(5)3.E. CP
(6) Developing relationships with 
key community partners, includ-
ing, but not limited to:
42.b.(6)3.E. CP
(a) Behavioral health providers.
42.b.(6)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Social services providers.
42.b.(6)(b)3.E. CP
(c) Healthcare providers.
42.b.(6)(c)3.E. CP
(d) Hospitals. 
42.b.(6)(d)3.E. CP
(e) Law enforcement.
42.b.(6)(e)3.E. CP
(f) Emergency services.
42.b.(6)(f)3.E. CP
(g) Criminal justice.
42.b.(6)(g)3.E. CP
(h) Other stakeholders, as 
appropriate.
42.b.(6)(h)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
42.a.  The program director’s experience dem-
onstrates the capability to manage the unique 
demands of sobering center operations, incl-
uding managing safety risks in a fast-paced, 
unpredictable environment while maintaining 
low-barrier access.
42.b.(6)  The program director’s community rela-
tionship-building function is vital, as sobering 
centers depend on strong partnerships with 
referring agencies and receiving treatment pro-
viders to fulfill their role as an effective diversion 
and linkage program. These relationships pro-
mote appropriate referrals to the sobering center, 
facilitate successful connections to ongoing care 
and resources, and build the community infra-
structure necessary to support persons served 
in achieving their recovery goals. Without active 
cultivation of these partnerships, sobering 
centers risk limiting their effectiveness as both 
a diversion strategy and a gateway to compre-
hensive services.
Examples
42.a. Relevant experience may include working 
in sobering care prior to appointment. Similar 
settings with transferable skills could include 
crisis intervention/stabilization, acute medical 
care, other work with high-risk populations, 
and leading multidisciplinary teams under 
challenging conditions.
3.E. 43. The program has the capability to admit 
persons 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
43.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As acute intoxication can happen at any time, 
sobering centers are staffed and capable of 
admitting persons at all times. There may be 
times when the sobering center cannot admit 
persons due to situational circumstances, such 
as operating at full capacity or the supervision 
requirements of the current persons served 
make additional admissions unsafe. The program 
endeavors to be available to persons and referral 
sources 24/7.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 211
3.E. 44. The program provides the following 
services:
44.3.E. CP
a. Continuous medical monitoring of 
the persons served in accordance 
with protocols established by the 
medical director, including:
44.a.3.E. CP
(1) Rounding based on the popula -
tion(s) served.
44.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Administration and documenta -
tion of standardized screens, if 
applicable.
44.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Vital signs.
44.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Determination of the current 
condition of persons served.
44.a.(4)3.E. CP
b. Identification of:
44.b.3.E. CP
(1) Immediate needs.
44.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Longer-term needs.
44.b.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Desired outcomes.
44.b.(3)3.E. CP
c. Discharge planning, including:
44.c.3.E. CP
(1) Provision of relevant education 
and information.
44.c.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Transportation, if needed.
44.c.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Assistance with accessing care/
resources based on the deter-
mination of immediate and 
longer-term needs.
44.c.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Warm handoff, when applicable.
44.c.(4)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
44.a.(1)  Rounding is the ongoing monitoring of 
persons served to maintain safety for each person 
and the environment. The frequency of rounding 
and documentation requirements are defined in 
the medical protocols.
44.a.(2) The frequency of administration of stan-
dardized screening tools (e.g., COWS, CIWA-Ar, 
SHOT scale, AUDIT, DAST, or similar) is determined 
by the medical protocols.
44.b. The program identifies the needs and 
desired outcomes of persons served. These are 
aligned with the person’s readiness, values, and 
comfort. Engaging in suggested services should 
not be communicated as a required step for 
returning to the program, if needed. 
44.c.(2) Upon discharge, some persons served 
may need transportation to another program, 
shelter, or other service for continued care. 
Understanding there are limitations to the avail-
ability or transportation, the program seeks to get 
the person served to the next service or home 
through the resources available. The sobering 
center may directly provide transportation, offer 
transportation vouchers or subsidies, or provide 
the persons served with information on available 
public transportation options. 
44.c.(4) Face-to-face linkage is often referred to 
as a “warm handoff” that includes direct contact 
between the person served and the receiving 
provider.

(3) Engagement.

(4) Response to emergencies.
45.b.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Discharge planning.
45.b.(5)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program has adequate personnel available 
on site to meet the needs of the persons served 
and to maintain the safety of the environment. 
45.a. At least one of the defined personnel 
should be on site at all times. Personnel should 
function within their scopes of practice and be 
able to perform duties in accordance with the 
program’s medical monitoring protocols.
45.a.(1) This can be any level of licensed nurse, 
e.g., R.N, L.P .N., L.V.N. or equivalent.
45.a.(2) The EMT should be certified minimally as 
an EMT Basic or equivalent.
45.a.(3) This can be a certified medical assistant, 
certified nursing assistant, or other equivalent.
45.b. Other personnel on site could include 
additional nurses, EMTs, or CMAs, peer support 
specialists, recovery specialists, or other person-
nel determined by the program. The number 
of additional personnel is determined by the

3.E. 46. The intake process for each person 
served addresses:
46.3.E. CP
a. Risk of:
46.a.3.E. CP
(1) Suicide, using a standardized tool 
or instrument.
46.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Other self-harm or risk-taking 
behaviors.
46.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Violence toward others.
46.a.(3)3.E. CP
b. Acute medical conditions.
46.b.3.E. CP

(2) Respiration rate.
46.c.(2)3.E. CP

46.d.3.E. CP

46.f.3.E. CP
