46.g.3.E. CP
h. History of withdrawal symptoms.
46.h.3.E. CP
i. Decision making regarding admis -
sion to the program.
46.i.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As the persons served in a sobering center are 
unstable and intoxicated, the intake process is 
intentionally brief to maintain a low-barrier 
admission process. This information is sought to 
help determine whether the person can be safely 
managed in the program. Although efforts are 
routinely made to collect complete data on each 
person served, it is understood that the ability 
of persons served to provide the information 
requested is dependent on their condition upon 
admission.
46.a. These standards relate to the screening 
process addressed in Section 2.B. and the iden-
tification of suicide risk and other urgent or 
critical needs.
46.a.(1) The intent of this standard is to ensure 
universal screening for all persons served using 
a standardized screening tool.
46.a.(3) This includes risk of violence toward 
others and from others.
Examples
46.a. Programs should look holistically at the risk 
factors of the person served for suicide, violence, 
or other risky behaviors. Personnel are encour-
aged to look across life domains in a thorough 
clinical assessment to evaluate current intent and 
plans as well as risk factors that indicate the need 
for development of a safety plan with the person 
served.
Typically, suicide assessments evaluate:
n Risk factors:
– Current psychiatric illness/symptoms.
– Alcohol and/or other drug use.
– Serious physical or emotional pain.
– Previous self-harm or suicide attempts.
– Suicide attempts or completion in close 
family/support network.
– Age, gender, and social situation.
– Relationships that may be supportive/
protective or that may pose a threat 
(abuse
 or neglect).
– Lack of adequate coping skills/
mechanisms.
– Financial difficulties.
– Access to lethal methods.
n Current intake plans:
–W i s h  t o  b e  d e a d .
– Feelings of hopelessness.
– Regret/remorse over current/previous 
attempt.
– Expectation about outcome of self-harm -
ing behavior or suicide attempt/threat.
–S p e c i f i c  p l a n s .
– Lethality and frequency of plans or 
attempts.
–O t h e r  s e l f - h a r m i n g  b e h a v i o r .
– Current suicide intent/wishes.
– Length of time suicidal feelings have been 
present.
– Mental state at time of self-harm or suicide 
attempt or threat (alcohol or drug intake, 
social situation, relationship changes, 
bereavements).

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 213
– Plans for others after death, including 
suicide notes, changes to will, and 
consequences.
The most common tool for assessing suicide risk 
is the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale 
(CSSRS), and there are several versions of this 
tool for use with different populations and at 
different times. There is a significant amount of 
resource information available on the Columbia 
Lighthouse Project website at https://
cssrs.columbia.edu.
Another tool to consider is the SAFE-T screener. 
Information can be found at  
https://www.sprc.org/resources-programs/
suicide-assessment-five-step-evaluation-and-
triage-safe-t-pocket-card.
46.a.(2) This could include, but is not limited to, 
cutting, needle sharing, unprotected sex, driving 
at excessive speeds, driving under the influence, 
and extreme sports.
46.a.(3) Sobering centers assess the possibility 
that the persons served may harm others in the 
program environment or other environments. 
Programs might consider the use of the Histori-
cal Clinical Risk Management-20 Version 3 
(HCR-20) for assistance in evaluating risks. 
Information about this tool can be found at http:/
/hcr-20.com.
3.E. 47. The program implements a written 
procedure regarding persons who 
have engaged in excluding behaviors, 
including:
47.3.E. CP
a. Criteria for exclusion.
47.a.3.E. CP
b. Timeframes for exclusion.
47.b.3.E. CP
c. What happens when someone who 
is currently excluded presents for 
admission to the program.
47.c.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Excluding behaviors refers to a person who 
engaged in unacceptable behavior that put 
themselves, other persons served, or personnel in 
jeopardy of serious harm. As a result, the sobering 
center may bar (exclude) the person from future 
admission for a period of time. It is not intended 
to be permanent.
Examples
47.b. Development of a 30-60-90 day progressive 
plan to address one-time incidents versus 
patterns of exclusionary behaviors.
3.E. 48. The program implements a written pro-
cedure that addresses diversion of new 
admissions.
48.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Diversion of admissions refers to when a sobering 
center is unable to admit new persons for a 
limited period of time, which could happen for 
various reasons. These may include when the 
program is at full capacity, the acuity of current 
persons served requires supervision at levels that 
preclude additional admissions, or other 
circumstances. The procedure includes the iden-
tification of alternative resources and the referral 
process for persons who present for admission 
during this time.
3.E. 49. The program reduces barriers to inter-
action between the persons served and 
personnel.
49.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The sobering center demonstrates efforts to 
reduce both attitudinal and physical barriers that 
create distance and separation between program 
personnel and the persons served. Personnel 
should be engaged with, and accessible to, the 
persons served, rather than behind glass barriers, 
in an office, or in a lounge where the persons 
served cannot interact with them. In addition, this 
allows personnel to more quickly respond to any 
instability of persons served.
3.E. 50. The program’s physical facilities provide:
50.3.E. CP
a. A calming environment:
50.a.3.E. CP
b. Individual space.
50.b.3.E. CP
c. Security of personal belongings.
50.c.3.E. CP
d. Space for:
50.d.3.E. CP
(1) Privacy.
50.d.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Quiet activities.
50.d.(2)3.E. CP
e. Based on gender, age, and needs, 
separate areas for hygiene.
50.e.3.E. CP

SECTION 3.E. CRISIS PROGRAMS
214 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Intent Statements
The program operates in designated space that 
allows for an appropriate physical and social 
setting that is respectful of persons served. The 
program is not required to maintain exclusive 
spaces for each of these, but appropriate areas 
should be provided and available for these activi-
ties as needed.
Examples
50.a. Calming environment considerations can 
include lighting, noise, colors, amount of stimu-
lation, and furnishings.
50.b. An individual space can include an 
assigned cot/bed/recliner in an open dorm.
3.E. 51. The program demonstrates continuous 
vigilance regarding the safety of the 
environment for persons served and 
personnel through:
51.3.E. CP
a. Assessment of risks, including:
51.a.3.E. CP
(1) Ligatures.
51.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Sharps.
51.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Furnishings.
51.a.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Facility maintenance issues.
51.a.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Other safety risks.
51.a.(5)3.E. CP
b. Response to identified risks.
51.b.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
The program implements surveillance activities 
that maintain the safety of all persons served 
and personnel on an ongoing basis. The program 
likely alters these activities based on the persons 
served and the assessment of risks in the 
environment.
51.a.(1) It is not the intent of this standard to 
require that facilities be retrofitted into a ligature-
free environment; however, awareness of ligature 
points is crucial to ensuring vigilant observation 
and protection of persons served.
3.E. 52. The program always has food available 
for persons served.
52.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
As sobering centers operate 24 hours a day, 7 
days a week, and persons served can be admitted 
at all hours and could be hungry, the program has 
food available at all times. It is not the intent that 
full meals be available at all times, but that, at a 
minimum, nutritious snacks are readily available 
to persons served.
3.E. 53. The program implements written pro-
cedures regarding service delivery 
documentation that:
53.3.E. CP
a. Specify documentation 
requirements.
53.a.3.E. CP
b. Meet the needs of the program.
53.b.3.E. CP
c. Meet the needs of the stakeholders.
53.c.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
Written procedures for documentation require-
ments related to service delivery could include, 
but are not limited to, the content of records, 
timelines for documentation, signatures needed, 
and other requirements established by payers/
regulators.
3.E. 54. The program demonstrates efforts to 
engage with community stakeholders to:
54.3.E. CP
a. Increase awareness of sobering care.
54.a.3.E. CP
b. Optimize resources and opportuni -
ties for persons served through 
community connections.
54.b.3.E. CP
c. Reduce stigma.
54.c.3.E. CP
d. Enhance safety for persons served.
54.d.3.E. CP
Intent Statements
54.a. To facilitate referrals and funding, the sober-
ing center may engage with a variety of internal 
and external stakeholders to educate about the 
purpose of the program, what sobering care is, 
what the program offers, and the benefits of this 
type of care. 
Stakeholder tours of sobering centers help illus-
trate the concept and purpose and provide an 
avenue for education on substance use recovery 
and service needs.
3.E. 55. All direct service personnel receive 
competency-based training in:
55.3.E. CP
a. First aid.
55.a.3.E. CP
b. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).
55.b.3.E. CP
c. The use of emergency equipment, 
when present.
55.c.3.E. CP

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 215
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing personnel and physicians are 
exempt from this requirement as their profes-
sional training and licensing ensure competency.
Examples
55.a. First aid includes Naloxone administration.
55.c. Emergency equipment could include 
automatic external defibrillators, eye washing 
stations, splints, tourniquets, and EpiPens®.
3.E. 56. All direct service personnel receive 
competency-based training:
56.3.E. CP
a. At orientation.
56.a.3.E. CP
b. At least annually.
56.b.3.E. CP
c. That addresses:
56.c.3.E. CP
(1) Administration of naloxone.
56.c.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Signs and symptoms of acute 
intoxication and withdrawal of 
alcohol and other drugs.
56.c.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Cognitive impairment.
56.c.(3)3.E. CP
(4) Unique characteristics of the 
population served.
56.c.(4)3.E. CP
(5) Skills for engaging the persons 
served.
56.c.(5)3.E. CP
(6) Creating a welcoming 
environment.
56.c.(6)3.E. CP
(7) Trauma-informed practices.
56.c.(7)3.E. CP
Examples
56.c.(4) In addition to information regarding 
alcohol and other substance use, training may 
include information regarding homelessness, 
human trafficking, physical/emotional abuse, 
food insecurity, justice involvement, and mental 
health issues/conditions.
56.c.(6) Training on creating a welcoming envi-
ronment may include topics such as cultural 
humility; how to establish trust; identifying per-
sonal biases; respecting personal circumstances 
and decisions of persons served; active listening; 
using respectful, inclusive language when speak-
ing and on documents; addressing people by 
their preferred names and pronouns; creating a 
physical environment that is comfortable and 
calm; and basic customer service.
3.E. 57. The program:
57.3.E. CP
a. Collects information on:
57.a.3.E. CP
(1) Redirect rates.
57.a.(1)3.E. CP
(2) Emergency transfers.
57.a.(2)3.E. CP
(3) Leaving the program against 
advice.
57.a.(3)3.E. CP
b. Conducts a written analysis of the 
information collected:
57.b.3.E. CP
(1) At least annually.
57.b.(1)3.E. CP
(2) That addresses:
57.b.(2)3.E. CP
(a) Causes.
57.b.(2)(a)3.E. CP
(b) Trends.
57.b.(2)(b)3.E. CP
(c) Actions for improvement.
57.b.(2)(c)3.E. CP
(d) Necessary education and 
training of:
57.b.(2)(d)3.E. CP
(i) Personnel.
57.b.(2)(d)(i)3.E. CP
(ii) Key community 
partners.
57.b.(2)(d)(ii)3.E. CP
Intent Statements
57.a.(1) Redirect rate refers to persons who pre-
sented to the program, yet were not admitted 
due to being ineligible. This could be because 
of not meeting admission criteria for medical or 
behavioral reasons, or other types of ineligibility.
57.a.(2) Emergency transfers refers to persons 
admitted to the program and were subsequently 
transferred to a different level of care due to an 
emergency or medical instability. Most often 
would be to a hospital for care outside of the 
program’s capability to manage, but could also be 
to other locations for behavioral emergencies.
Examples
57.b.(2)(d)(i) Data can be used to help train 
personnel in better assessing persons upon 
admission to improve operations and quality 
of care.
57.b.(2)(d)(ii) Data analyzed on persons having 
to be redirected could be used to assist referral 
sources in refining their assessment of persons 
brought to the program to improve the likelihood 
of admission and reduce perceived barriers.

SECTION 3.F. DAY TREATMENT
216 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
F. Day Treatment
Description
Day treatment programs offer person-centered, 
culturally and linguistically appropriate, compre-
hensive, coordinated, and structured treatment 
services and activities. A day treatment program 
consists of a scheduled series of structured, face-
to-face therapeutic sessions organized at various 
levels of intensity and frequency in order to assist 
the persons served in achieving the goals iden-
tified in their person-centered plans. Day 
treatment programs are offered four or more days 
per week, typically with support available in the 
evenings and on weekends. A day treatment pro-
gram may prevent or minimize the need for a 
more intensive level of treatment. It may also 
function as a step-down from inpatient care or 
partial hospitalization or as transitional care fol-
lowing an inpatient or partial hospitalization stay 
to facilitate return to the community.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a day 
treatment program must apply the standards in 
Sections 1 and 2 in addition to the standards in 
this section.
3.F. 1. The program is available for each person 
served:
1.3.F. DT
a. At least four days per week.
1.a.3.F. DT
b. At least three hours per day.
1.b.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
While the program is available to the persons 
served three hours per day, four days per week, 
individualized plans and variable lengths of stay 
will determine the degree to which each person 
actually participates in a day treatment program.
3.F. 2. The majority of program hours consist of 
scheduled treatment services that 
include at least three of the following:
2.3.F. DT
a. Individual counseling/therapy.
2.a.3.F. DT
b. Family counseling/therapy.
2.b.3.F. DT
c. Group counseling/therapy.
2.c.3.F. DT
d. Education, including at least one of 
the following topic areas:
2.d.3.F. DT
(1) Alcohol, nicotine, or other drugs.
2.d.(1)3.F. DT
(2) Medication.
2.d.(2)3.F. DT
(3) Psychoeducation.
2.d.(3)3.F. DT
e. Occupational therapy.
2.e.3.F. DT
f. Other therapy services as 
appropriate.
2.f.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
The program ensures that the majority of services 
delivered are therapeutic activities designed to 
assist the persons served to achieve the goals 
outlined in their person-centered plans and may 
include services provided through technology. 
There may be some activities that are social or 
otherwise supportive in nature, but those ser-
vices are secondary to the intention of providing 
therapeutic activities.
Examples
2.f. Could include activities such as art therapy, 
dance therapy, and animal-assisted therapy.
3.F. 3. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, the program offers additional 
activities that include, but are not limited 
to, the following areas:
3.3.F. DT
a. Emotional.
3.a.3.F. DT
b. Environmental.
3.b.3.F. DT
c. Financial.
3.c.3.F. DT
d. Intellectual.
3.d.3.F. DT
e. Occupational.
3.e.3.F. DT
f. Physical.
3.f.3.F. DT
g. Social.
3.g.3.F. DT
h. Spiritual.
3.h.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
These other activities provided by the program 
are designed to increase functioning of the per-
sons served and serve as examples of additional 
nontherapeutic activities performed by the pro-
gram. Additionally, these activities are focused 
on improving dimensions of wellness of persons 
served.

SECTION 3.G. WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 217
3.F. 4. The program has consistently:
4.3.F. DT
a. Assigned personnel.
4.a.3.F. DT
b. Scheduled activities.
4.b.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
4.a. The program establishes a stable staffing 
pattern by assigning the same personnel to the 
program. Should the need arise, the organization 
may add personnel from a consistent pool to 
provide the needed intensity of interventions.
3.F. 5. The program’s services are provided by 
an interdisciplinary team.
5.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
Please see the Glossary for the definition of 
interdisciplinary.
3.F. 6. The program provides or arranges for 
psychiatric services to meet the needs 
of
 the persons served.
6.3.F. DT
Intent Statements
Psychiatric services are provided to persons 
served who need them by the program through 
its own psychiatrist, a contract psychiatrist, or 
other appropriate arrangement. Other appropri-
ately trained and supervised psychiatric providers 
such as Advanced Practice Registered Nurses, 
Physician Assistants, or Prescribing Psychologists 
may be used.
G. Withdrawal 
Management
Description
A withdrawal management program is a time-
limited program designed to assist the persons 
served with the physiological and psychological 
effects of acute withdrawal from alcohol and 
other drugs. Based on current best practices in 
the field, the program’s purpose is to provide a 
medically safe, professional and supportive 
withdrawal experience for the persons served 
while preparing and motivating them to continue 
treatment after discharge from the program and 
progress toward a full and complete recovery. 
The program is staffed to ensure adequate 
biomedical and psychosocial assessment, 
observation and care, and referrals to meet the 
individual needs of the persons served. Addition-
ally, the program develops and maintains a rich 
network of treatment providers for referrals after 
completion of the program to ensure the best 
possible match for the persons served to ongoing 
treatment services. A withdrawal management 
program may be provided in the following 
settings:
n Inpatient: This setting is distinguished by 
services provided in a safe, secure facility-
based setting with 24-hour nursing coverage 
and ready access to medical care. This is for 
persons served who need round-the-clock 
supervision in order to successfully manage 
withdrawal symptoms or when there are 
additional complications or risk factors that 
warrant medical supervision, such as co-
occurring psychiatric or other medical 
conditions. 
n Residential: This setting is distinguished by 
services provided in a safe facility with 24-
hour coverage by qualified personnel. Persons 
served need the supervision and structure 
provided by a 24-hour program but do not 
have risk factors present that warrant an inpa
-
tient setting. It may also be appropriate for 
persons who lack motivation or whose living 
situation is not conducive to remaining sober. 

SECTION 3.G. WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT
218 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n Ambulatory: This setting is distinguished by 
services provided in an outpatient environ-
ment with the persons served residing in their 
own homes, a sober living environment or 
other supportive community settings. Persons 
served in ambulatory settings typically have 
adequate social supports to remain sober, 
family involvement in care planning, the 
ability to maintain regular appointments for 
ongoing assessment and observation, and 
the
 ability to successfully self-manage pre-
scription medications. Persons served in 
ambulatory settings are concurrently enrolled 
in or actively linked to a treatment program.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
withdrawal management program must apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition to 
the standards in this subsection, as follows:
n Inpatient withdrawal management 
programs must meet Standards 1.–26.
n Residential withdrawal management 
programs must meet Standards 1.–25.
n Ambulatory withdrawal management 
programs must meet Standards 1.–19. 
and Standards 27.–29.
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
withdrawal management program must 
include all levels of withdrawal management 
that it provides in the accreditation survey. 
3.G. 1. The program’s documented admission 
criteria address, at a minimum:
1.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Identified risks.
1.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Mental health needs.
1.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Physical needs.
1.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Support environment.
1.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
e. Relapse risk.
1.e.3.G. BH Withdrawal
f. Withdrawal risk.
1.f.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
The program describes in its admission criteria 
its capacity to serve and manage behaviors and 
risks associated with each of the dimensions 
identified.
1.a. The program identifies the risks that persons 
served may present and describes the limitations 
of the program and how it will handle persons 
whose risks fall outside of the program’s 
expertise.
1.b. The program describes types of co-occurring 
psychiatric illnesses and/or symptoms it can man-
age based on the competencies of personnel.
1.c. The program describes its abilities and limita-
tions related to the physical health needs and 
medical stability of persons served.
1.d. Based on the withdrawal management set-
ting, the program describes the requirements for 
the support environment of the persons served.
1.e. Based on the withdrawal management set-
ting, the program’s admission criteria ensure that 
the setting matches the needs of the persons 
served based on their risk of relapse.
1.f. Based on the withdrawal management set-
ting, the program establishes criteria that ensure 
that the level of supervision available in the pro-
gram is appropriate to the withdrawal risk of the 
persons served.
Examples
1.a. Examples of risks include suicide, violence, 
date/time of last use, prior withdrawal manage-
ment concerns, etc.
1.d. When the setting is ambulatory, the admis-
sion criteria may address what types of supports 
must be present to manage medications that are 
prescribed (if applicable), the availability of 
transportation to appointments, and the use of 
drugs/alcohol by others where the persons served 
reside.
3.G. 2. Appropriate placement of each person 
served is addressed through:
2.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. The admission criteria.
2.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. The discharge/transition criteria.
2.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. The resources available.
2.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Treatment history.
2.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
e. Ongoing reassessment.
2.e.3.G. BH Withdrawal
f. The person’s potential to benefit.
2.f.3.G. BH Withdrawal
g. The person’s preferences.
2.g.3.G. BH Withdrawal

SECTION 3.G. WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 219
Intent Statements
The program accepts into its care persons who, 
based on an assessment of their needs and 
preferences, are most likely to benefit from the 
program and setting offered.
3.G. 3. For each person served, a medical 
evaluation:
3.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Is obtained within 24  hours of 
admission to the program.
3.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Is documented.
3.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Includes:
3.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) A physical examination.
3.c.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Orders for appropriate services.
3.c.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Face-to-face consultation.
3.c.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
3.c.(1) Readmission within 30 days would not 
require a new physical examination unless speci-
fied otherwise by regulation. When allowed by 
medical practice boards or other regulation, a 
physician’s assistant or nurse practitioner may 
conduct the physical examination.
3.c.(3) When admission occurs on a weekend or 
holiday, face-to-face consultation may be delayed 
until the first working day following admission, 
unless earlier consultation is medically necessary.
Face-to-face consultation could be done through 
telehealth services that allow the physician to see 
the person served from a separate location.
Examples
3.c.(2) Orders may include medications, labora-
tory tests, physical activity or restrictions, 
notification requirements, or others.
3.G. 4. A risk assessment for each person served 
is conducted at the time of admission.
4.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
At the time of admission is considered to be when 
the admission order is written.
This standard is related to Standard 2.B.12.d. Refer 
to Standard 2.C.4. if risks are identified.
3.G. 5. Initial and ongoing assessments of each 
person served include:
5.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. An assessment of the person’s readi-
ness to change.
5.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. The use of appropriate symptom 
scales and/or standardized tools to 
support admission and continued 
stay decisions.
5.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. An interpretive summary addresses 
the six dimensions of the American 
Society of Addiction Medicine 
(ASAM) or similar criteria.
5.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
5.c. This standard relates to Standard 2.B.13., with 
an expectation that the program provides infor-
mation in its interpretive summary reflective of 
the six dimensions for assessment as defined by 
ASAM. There may be additional information in 
the summary; however, assessment of each 
dimension with results should be included
3.G. 6. The program has a qualified program 
director who:
6.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Has appropriate training and experi -
ence in withdrawal management.
6.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Has responsibility and authority to 
direct:
6.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal

policies and procedures.
6.b.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Educational activities with the 
program personnel.
6.b.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Program development and 
modification.
6.b.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal

reports.
6.b.(4)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(5) Performance improvement 
activities.
6.b.(5)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
The person fulfilling these responsibilities does 
not have to come from any particular discipline. 
The organization determines who has responsi-
bility for the areas listed and the job title.
Examples
The medical director or another qualified person 
may fill this role.

7.3.G. BH Withdrawal

3.G. 8. The program implements the appropri-
ate withdrawal management protocol 
for
 each person served based on the 
individual’s assessed needs.
8.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
In accordance with Standard 7.  in this section, 
the program has written treatment protocols for 
withdrawal management for alcohol and other 
drugs, special populations, and co-occurring 
conditions. The program demonstrates adher-
ence to the protocols by personnel and that the 
appropriate protocol is utilized for each person 
served.
Examples
The organization might utilize forms for concur-
rent review, post-discharge audit, or peer review 
to monitor its implementation of withdrawal 
management protocols.

3.G. 10. A physician available to the program 
24
 hours a day, 7 days a week.
10.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
In addition to in-person availability, the physician 
may be available through a variety of mecha-
nisms, including, but not limited to, telephone, 
telehealth services, FaceTime, or other use of 
technology. The purpose of this standard is to 
provide real-time direction to personnel regard-
ing care and crisis intervention for the persons 
served. When the program’s medical director is an 
advanced practice provider in lieu of a physician, 
the program maintains access to a physician for 
complex cases.
3.G. 11. The persons served are provided with 
services designed to motivate them 
to continue treatment following with-
drawal management.
11.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
It is important to begin the treatment process as 
soon as possible and to begin intervening at a 
point when the persons served may be most 
open to counseling. When the persons served are 
being treated for physical withdrawal, they are 
also engaged in counseling that encourages the 
continuation of services.
3.G. 12. The program provides or arranges for:
12.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Medical consultative services.
12.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Ancillary medical services.

c. Pharmacy services.
12.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Emergency medical services.
12.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
e. Other services, as appropriate.
12.e.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Examples
12.a. Medical consultative services may include 
medical services to address the needs of persons 
served that are outside the expertise of the pro-
gram’s medical director.
12.b. Ancillary medical services may include 
services such as laboratory and occupational, 
physical, or speech therapy.
3.G. 13. The program:
13.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Links with resources in each of the 
following areas:
13.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Substance use disorder treat -
ment programs.
13.a.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Medication assisted treatment 
programs.
13.a.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Specialized treatment programs.
13.a.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(4) Self-help programs.
13.a.(4)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(5) Other recovery and social 
support services.
13.a.(5)3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Identifies key communication 
contacts at the programs/services 
with which it links.
13.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Provides or refers the persons served 
to the programs/services that meet 
their needs.
13.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
As withdrawal management programs are 
frequently an entry point to the addictions treat-
ment system, the program cultivates a robust 
network of providers for referral of the persons 
served based upon their specific needs.
Examples
13.a.(3) Treatment programs may be gender 
specific, age specific, or specialize in services 
for persons with co-occurring disorders, persons 
in the criminal justice system, and the LGBTQ+ 
community.
13.a.(5) Other recovery and social support ser-
vices could include community social services, 
entitlement programs, housing services, employ-
ment programs, and other similar supports.

3.G. 14. All direct service personnel receive

14.3.G. BH Withdrawal

14.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal

14.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal

when present.

Intent Statements
Licensed nursing staff and physicians are exempt 
from this requirement as their professional train-

Examples
14.c. Emergency equipment could include 
automatic external defibrillators, eye washing

3.G. 15. The program implements written proce-
dures for transfer to emergency medical

15.3.G. BH Withdrawal

15.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal

received during absence from the 
program, including medications 
prescribed.
15.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Documentation of actions taken 
when the person served returns from 
the emergency service provided.
15.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
These procedures address acute psychiatric and 
other medical illnesses/conditions that fall out-
side the scope of the program to manage. The 
procedures are designed to ensure that there is 
not a break in care and that the emergency treat-
ment is coordinated by the program, including 
following through on orders from the treating 
entity.

competency-based training to direct

16.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. At:
16.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Orientation.
16.a.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Regular intervals.
16.a.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. That includes, at a minimum, the 
following topics:
16.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Risk assessment.
16.b.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Withdrawal management 
protocols.
16.b.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Withdrawal syndromes.

17.3.G. BH Withdrawal

17.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal

ple of the persons served.

17.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal

review process.
17.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal

17.e.3.G. BH Withdrawal

17.e.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal

17.e.(1)(c)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(d) Timeliness of physician 
response.
17.e.(1)(d)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Negative outcomes.
17.e.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
This standard is intended to complement Stand-
ard 2.H.3. to ensure that the unique aspects of 
service delivery in a withdrawal management 
program are reviewed at least annually.

3.G. 18. The program demonstrates that the 
information collected from its review 
of
 medical services is:
18.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Reported to personnel.
18.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Used to improve the quality of its

improvement activities.
18.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal

needs.
18.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal

The program establishes a mechanism to share 
the results of the reviews of medical services (as

sonnel and address areas needing improvement 
through training or other quality improvement 
activities.
3.G. 19. The program:
19.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Identifies an indicator to measure 
successful transitions of the persons 
served into ongoing services post 
discharge.
19.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. At least annually addresses:
19.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Performance in relationship to an 
established target.
19.b.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Trends.
19.b.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Actions for improvement.
19.b.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(4) Results of performance improve -
ment plans.
19.b.(4)3.G. BH Withdrawal

of:
19.b.(5)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(a) Persons served.
19.b.(5)(a)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(b) Families/support systems.
19.b.(5)(b)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(c) Personnel.
19.b.(5)(c)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(d) Other stakeholders, as 
appropriate.
19.b.(5)(d)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
This standard complements Standard 1.L.4. and 
addresses measurement of the effectiveness of 
the withdrawal management program. As these 
programs are frequently an entry point to the 
addictions treatment system, a significant 
responsibility of these programs is placement 
into care post discharge. Through this standard, 
the program measures its effectiveness at transi-
tioning persons served into ongoing treatment 
services.
Inpatient and Residential Withdrawal 
Management Programs
Applicable Standards
Inpatient and residential withdrawal manage-
ment programs must also meet Standards 20.–
25.
3.G. 20. A nursing assessment is conducted upon 
admission.
20.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
The nursing assessment is conducted as a part of 
the admission process by a nurse who is able to 
perform this function through training and scope 
of practice.
Examples
The following components of an addictions-
focused nursing assessment may be included:
n Patient History
– Substance Use History: Types of sub -
stances used, duration, frequency, amount, 
and last use.
– Pattern of Use: Situations, triggers, and rit -
uals associated with use.
– Previous Treatment: History of past treat -
ments for substance use and outcomes.
– Medical History: Chronic illnesses, hospi -
talizations, surgeries, and medications.
– Psychiatric History: Mental health disor -
ders, treatments, hospitalizations, and 
medications.
– Family History: Family history of sub -
stance use or mental health disorders.
– Social History: Marital status, children, 
living arrangements, employment, educa-
tion, and legal issues.
n Physical Assessment
– Vital Signs: Blood pressure, heart rate, 
respiratory rate, and temperature.
– General Appearance: Physical signs of sub -
stance use (e.g., needle marks, tremors).

SECTION 3.G. WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT
224 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
– Neurological Examination: Cognitive 
function, orientation, memory, and 
coordination.
– Respiratory Assessment: Lung sounds, 
respiratory effort, and signs of respiratory 
distress.
– Cardiovascular Assessment: Heart sounds, 
rhythm, and signs of cardiovascular 
complications.
– Gastrointestinal Assessment: Abdominal 
examination and signs of liver damage.
– Skin Assessment: Signs of infections, 
abscesses, and skin conditions.
n Psychological Assessment
– Mental Status Examination: Appearance, 
behavior, mood, affect, thought processes, 
and cognition.
– Psychiatric Symptoms: Depression, 
anxiety, hallucinations, delusions, and 
suicidality.
– Coping Mechanisms: Strategies used to 
deal with stress and substance use triggers.
n Social Assessment
– Support System: Family, friends, and 
community support.
–L i v i n g  E n v i r o n m e n t :  S a f e t y ,  s t a b i l i t y ,  a n d  
influence on substance use.
– Occupational Functioning: Employment 
status, job performance, and work 
environment.
– Legal Issues: Current or past legal prob -
lems related to substance use.
n Readiness to Change
– Motivation: Patient’s willingness to change 
and perceived need for treatment.
– Stages of Change: Determining which 
stage of change the patient is in (pre-
contemplation, contemplation, 
preparation, action, or maintenance).
–G o a l s  f o r  R e c o v e r y :  S h o r t - t e r m  a n d  l o n g -
term goals related to substance use.
n Risk Assessment
– Overdose Risk: History of overdoses and 
risk factors for future overdoses.
– Withdrawal Symptoms: Assessment of 
current or past withdrawal symptoms 
and potential for severe withdrawal.
– Risky Behaviors: High-risk behaviors 
related to substance use (e.g., sharing 
needles, unsafe sex).
n Laboratory T ests and Screening
– Toxicology Screening: Urine or blood tests 
to detect substances.
– Liver Function Tests: Assess liver damage 
from substance use.
– Infectious Disease Screening: HIV , Hepati -
tis B and C, and other infections related to 
substance use.
n Nutritional Assessment
– Dietary Habits: Nutritional intake, weight 
changes, and signs of malnutrition.
– Eating Patterns: Impact of substance use 
on eating habits.
n Education and Counseling Needs
– Knowledge of substance use: Patient’s 
understanding of the effects of substances.
– Health Education: Information on sub -
stance use and its impact on health.
– Treatment Options: Education on available 
treatment options and resources.
Including these components ensures a thorough 
assessment, allowing for a comprehensive care 
plan tailored to the needs of the person served.
3.G. 21. Withdrawal management services are 
provided by qualified personnel 24 hours 
a day, 7 days a week.
21.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
The withdrawal management program is staffed 
by skilled and trained professionals who are avail-
able to persons served at all times.
Examples
Qualified withdrawal management personnel can 
include medical personnel, qualified behavioral

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 225
healthcare practitioners, and healthcare 
technicians.
3.G. 22. Licensed nursing services are provided 
on site 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
22.3.G. BH Withdrawal
  
3.G. 23. The program’s physical facilities provide:
23.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. An environment that is conducive to 
a withdrawal management program.
23.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Personal privacy.
23.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Security of personal belongings.
23.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Space for:
23.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Quiet activities.
23.d.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Family or other guests.
23.d.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Therapeutic activities.
23.d.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(4) Cultural and/or spiritual 
activities.
23.d.(4)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(5) Meals.
23.d.(5)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(6) Based on gender, age, and needs, 
separate areas for:
23.d.(6)3.G. BH Withdrawal

(b) Hygiene.

Intent Statements
The program operates in designated space that 
allows for an appropriate treatment environment 
and is respectful of personal privacy to the 
degree possible.
23.d. The program is not required to maintain 
exclusive spaces for each of these, but appropri-
ate areas are available and provided as needed.
Examples
23.d.(1) Quiet activities may include de-escala-
tion and meditation.
3.G. 24. To ensure the safety of persons served 
and personnel, the program implements 
written procedures for searches:
24.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Of persons served.
24.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Of belongings.
24.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Of the physical facility.
24.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. That:
24.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Preserve privacy.
24.d.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Preserve dignity.
24.d.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Are sensitive to potential trauma 
of the persons served.
24.d.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
Withdrawal management programs ensure that 
personnel and persons served remain safe in 
the program, without introduction of weapons, 
drugs, or alcohol into the milieu. Personnel are 
adequately trained in the search process in order 
to respect the dignity and privacy of the persons 
served. Programs may find it necessary to 
conduct searches of the persons served, their 
belongings, or the physical facility based on 
observed behaviors of persons served or others.
Examples
24.d.(3) Searches are conducted by personnel 
with whom the person served feels comfortable. 
This may include specific protocols based on a 
person’s gender identity, as well as consideration 
of religious or cultural factors, prior history of 
trauma, and/or the person’s age. In order to assist 
the persons served to retain some feeling of con-
trol, the program could seek permission to 
conduct searches of individuals or their 
belongings.
3.G. 25. The program implements written 
procedures that address:
25.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. Visitation.
25.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Mail.
25.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. Telephone use.
25.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Use of personal electronics.
25.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Examples
25.d. The use of personal electronic devices can 
have a positive influence on the person served, 
particularly through the use of apps targeting 
recovery and self-regulation. Programs could 
attempt to strike a balance in the use of these 
devices, rather than simply prohibiting their use.

Inpatient Withdrawal Management 
Programs
Applicable Standards
Inpatient withdrawal management programs 
must also meet Standard 26. 
3.G. 26. Each day, a physician or advanced prac-
tice provider determines the medical 
necessity of the person served to remain 
in the inpatient withdrawal management 
program.
26.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
The person served is transitioned from the inpa-
tient setting to a less intensive level of care as 
soon as clinically appropriate. When allowed by 
medical practice boards or other regulation, or 
contract, a physician’s assistant or nurse practi-
tioner may make this determination.
Examples
This may be accomplished through options such 
as telephone consultation with team members or 
the use of telehealth services if there is not a phy-
sician/physician extender on site on a daily basis.
Ambulatory Withdrawal Management 
Programs
Applicable Standards
Ambulatory withdrawal management pro-
grams must also meet Standards 27.–29.
3.G. 27. Persons served who are not concurrently 
enrolled in a treatment program are 
actively linked to an appropriate 
program.
27.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
Persons served in an ambulatory program are 
engaged with active treatment in addition to 
the withdrawal management program.
3.G. 28. Admission to an ambulatory setting is 
based on assessments that address:
28.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. The ability of the person’s support 
system to be supportive and 
observant.
28.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. The risk of diversion of medications 
prescribed as part of the program.
28.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. The person’s ability to self-manage 
medications.
28.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
Intent Statements
If the support system of the person served does 
not seem consistent with the level of support 
required by the person, another setting should 
be utilized.
Examples
28.a. The support system of the person served 
could include family members, significant others, 
and/or members of a social support system.
3.G. 29. The program implements written pro-
cedures that address drug-screening 
practices, including:
29.3.G. BH Withdrawal
a. The frequency of drug screening.
29.a.3.G. BH Withdrawal
b. Provisions for the individualization 
of drug screening.
29.b.3.G. BH Withdrawal
c. An interpretation of the results of 
drug screening.
29.c.3.G. BH Withdrawal
d. Actions to be taken based on the 
results of drug screening.
29.d.3.G. BH Withdrawal
e. Education for:
29.e.3.G. BH Withdrawal
(1) Persons served.
29.e.(1)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(2) Families/support systems.
29.e.(2)3.G. BH Withdrawal
(3) Personnel.
29.e.(3)3.G. BH Withdrawal
Examples
Written procedures related to drug-screening 
practices might address the collection and pro-
cessing of urine samples, continuing education 
for personnel on urinalysis practices, and emer-
gency services procedures and define who has 
access to and responsibility for maintaining con-
fidentiality of all drug-screen results information. 
The procedures for urine sample collection might 
define how respect for persons served is main-
tained while minimizing the potential for 
falsification during the collection process.

SECTION 3.H. HEALTH HOME
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 227
29.a. The procedures may require drug screening 
at the time of admission, when there appears to 
be a change in behavior, or at the request of 
the team. It may also specify only a one-time 
screening.
H. Health Home
Description
A health home is a healthcare delivery approach 
that focuses on the whole person and integrates 
and coordinates primary care, behavioral health, 
other healthcare, and community and social 
support services. A health home allows for indi-
vidual choice and assesses the physical and 
behavioral health needs of persons served. The 
program demonstrates the capacity to address, 
either directly or through linkage with or referral 
to external resources, behavioral health condi-
tions and physical health conditions. Programs 
may also serve persons who have intellectual or 
other developmental disabilities and physical 
health needs or those who are at risk for or exhib-
iting behavioral disorders. Care is coordinated 
over time across providers, functions, activities, 
and sites to maximize the value and effectiveness 
of services delivered to persons served.
A health home provides comprehensive care 
management, care coordination, health promo-
tion, comprehensive transitional care, individual 
and family/support services, and linkage and 
referral to community and social support ser-
vices. Services are designed to support overall 
health and wellness and:
n Embody a recovery-focused model of care 
that respects and promotes independence 
and
 responsibility.
n Promote healthy lifestyles and provide pre-
vention and education services that focus 
on
 wellness and self-care.
n Ensure access to and coordination of care 
across prevention, primary care (including 
ensuring that persons served have a primary 
care physician), and specialty healthcare 
services.
n Monitor critical health indicators.
n Support individuals in the self-management 
of chronic health conditions.
n Coordinate/monitor emergency room visits 
and hospitalizations, including participation 
in transition/discharge planning and follow 
up.

SECTION 3.H. HEALTH HOME
228 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
A health home provides health promotion, care 
coordination, care management, individual and 
family support services, and referral to commu-
nity and/or social support services. If the health 
home is not the actual provider of a particular 
healthcare service, it remains responsible for 
supporting and facilitating improved outcomes 
by providing disease management supports and 
care coordination with other providers.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation as a 
health home must apply the standards in 
Sections 1 and 2 in addition to the standards 
in this subsection and the specialty designation 
standards for integrated primary care and use 
the Comprehensive Care field category.
3.H. 1. The health home enhances access to the 
program through:
1.3.H. HH
a. Personnel response to contacts 
within 24 hours.
1.a.3.H. HH
b. Capacity for same-day or next-day 
services.
1.b.3.H. HH
c. Flexible scheduling.
1.c.3.H. HH
Intent Statements
1.a. To meet the needs of the persons served, 
personnel response to contacts may be made 
face-to-face or via telehealth, telephone call, 
messaging, or other appropriate means and by 
an appropriate provider for the service or infor-
mation needed.
1.b. Same-day or next-day services are based on 
the specific needs of the person served. The num-
ber of these appointments available is based on 
the typical demands of the population served. 
Capacity is usually based on urgent rather than 
routine needs.
1.c. The intent of this standard is to offer avail-
ability of services at times that meet the needs of 
the persons served and that do not interfere with 
work, school, or other personal commitments.
3.H. 2. The program has a qualified medical 
director who:
2.3.H. HH
a. Is a licensed physician (M.D. or D.O.).
2.a.3.H. HH
b. Demonstrates expertise in the core 
medical knowledge to treat behav-
ioral and physical health conditions.
2.b.3.H. HH
c. Is responsible for:
2.c.3.H. HH
(1) Leading the medical staff of the 
program.
2.c.(1)3.H. HH
(2) Ensuring the quality of medical 
care.
2.c.(2)3.H. HH
(3) Establishing protocols for health 
screenings.
2.c.(3)3.H. HH
(4) Providing guidance to foster 
integration and coordination of 
behavioral health and primary 
care.
2.c.(4)3.H. HH
(5) Leading programmatic perfor -
mance improvement activities.
2.c.(5)3.H. HH
Intent Statements
A qualified physician with expertise in both 
behavioral and physical health guides the pro-
gram’s medical services. The medical director 
serves a critical role in promoting optimal, inte-
grated health outcomes and the continuous 
improvement of services for persons served by 
providing medical leadership, assuring the qual-
ity of clinical care, and driving integration.
Examples
2.c.(2) Examples of activities that would support 
the quality of medical care could include:
n Developing and approving medical policies 
and procedures that align with evidence-
based guidelines and regulatory requirements.
n Reviewing medical adverse events, critical 
incidents, and peer reviews related to medical 
care.
n Participating in chart audits and medical 
records reviews to assess the quality of docu-
mentation and care delivery.
n Implementing and monitoring adherence to 
clinical protocols to ensure consistent, high-
quality medical services.

SECTION 3.H. HEALTH HOME
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 229
2.c.(3) Examples might include:
n Developing and approving standardized pro-
tocols for comprehensive health screenings 
(e.g., vital signs, BMI, blood pressure, lipid 
panels, HbA1c) upon admission and at regu
-
lar intervals.
n Establishing evidence-based guidelines for 
age-appropriate screening for specific physical 
health conditions common in the population 
served (e.g., cardiovascular disease, cancer, 
diabetes, metabolic syndrome, vaccine 
requirements).
n Approving protocols for screening for social 
determinants of health that impact physical 
well-being.
n Ensuring protocols include clear criteria for 
follow-up, referral, and linkage to appropriate 
care based on screening results.
2.c.(4) Examples of activities that would foster 
integration include:
n Actively participating in interdisciplinary 
leadership meetings focused on ongoing 
integrated care model development and 
implementation.
n Developing clinical pathways or referral 
guidelines that promote seamless coordina-
tion between behavioral health and primary 
care providers.
n Championing shared electronic health record 
utilization and joint care planning across 
disciplines.
n Leading educational initiatives for both medi-
cal and behavioral health team members on 
integrated care principles and practices.
n Facilitating structural integration efforts in 
the program.
2.c.(5) Based on costs and availability of the 
medical director, there may be limitations on the 
degree of participation in performance improve-
ment activities;, however, the medical director’s 
leadership can include, at a minimum, establish-
ing performance targets and measures relevant to 
the medical activities of the program.
3.H. 3. A documented health assessment is 
completed for each person served:
3.3.H. HH
a. Annually.
3.a.3.H. HH
b. That includes:
3.b.3.H. HH
(1) A review of:
3.b.(1)3.H. HH
(a) Behavioral health factors 
that may affect physical 
health.
3.b.(1)(a)3.H. HH
(b) Physical health factors that 
may affect behavioral health.
3.b.(1)(b)3.H. HH
(c) Social health factors that 
may affect physical and 
behavioral health.
3.b.(1)(c)3.H. HH
(2) Chronic disease status as relevant 
to the person served, including, 
but not limited to:
3.b.(2)3.H. HH
(a) Asthma.
3.b.(2)(a)3.H. HH
(b) Cardiovascular disease.
3.b.(2)(b)3.H. HH
(c) Pulmonary disease.
3.b.(2)(c)3.H. HH
(d) Diabetes.
3.b.(2)(d)3.H. HH
(e) Hypertension.
3.b.(2)(e)3.H. HH
(f) Obesity.
3.b.(2)(f)3.H. HH
(g) Neurological conditions.
3.b.(2)(g)3.H. HH
(h) Chronic pain.
3.b.(2)(h)3.H. HH
(i) Other chronic health condi -
tions, as appropriate to the 
person served.
3.b.(2)(i)3.H. HH
(3) Preventive health screening, 
including, but not limited to:
3.b.(3)3.H. HH
(a) Metabolic syndrome screen.
3.b.(3)(a)3.H. HH
(b) HIV.
3.b.(3)(b)3.H. HH

3.b.(3)(c)3.H. HH

230 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
(5) Perception of needs from the per -
spective of the person served.
3.b.(5)3.H. HH
c. The results of which are incorporated 
into the person-centered plan.
3.c.3.H. HH
Intent Statements
The annual health assessment is important for 
promoting the holistic well-being of individuals, 
particularly those with behavioral health disor-
ders and co-occurring physical health conditions. 
It goes beyond a typical behavioral health assess-
ment to include physical health considerations. 
The findings of this integrated assessment are 
documented in the record of the person served 
and identified needs are addressed in the person-
centered plan.
3.H. 4. The health home provides services, 
either directly or through linkage with or 
referral to external resources, to address 
the needs of the persons served, 
including:
4.3.H. HH
a. Physical health needs.
4.a.3.H. HH
b. Behavioral health needs.
4.b.3.H. HH
c. Social health needs.
4.c.3.H. HH
Intent Statements
The health home consistently meets the indiv-
idualized needs of persons served by actively 
facilitating access to a robust spectrum of inte-
grated services. By either delivering services 
directly or establishing effective linkages and 
referral pathways to external resources, the 
program demonstrates its commitment to holis-
tically addressing the interconnected physical, 
behavioral, and social health needs of each per-
son served.
3.H. 5. The health home holds routine, struc-
tured team meetings about the persons 
served that address:
5.3.H. HH
a. Assessments and screening evalua -
tions needed.
5.a.3.H. HH
b. Follow-up on referrals provided and 
evaluation of referrals needed.
5.b.3.H. HH
c. Health maintenance.
5.c.3.H. HH
d. Care plan goals.
5.d.3.H. HH
e. Emergency department visits/
hospitalizations.
5.e.3.H. HH
f. Medical reconciliation/concerns.
5.f.3.H. HH
g. Person-centered considerations.
5.g.3.H. HH
h. Safety.
5.h.3.H. HH
Intent Statements
5.b. Follow-up addresses all referrals, including 
those made to address behavioral, physical, and 
social health needs and the results of those refer-
rals (such as appointments made and no-shows).
5.c. Health maintenance discussions include 
activities to manage chronic diseases and pre-
ventive health screenings that may be indicated 
for the person served.
Examples
5.h. Safety discussions may include an array of 
potential needs such as in-home safety in pro-
gram locations or with partners, interactions 
with law enforcement, suicide risks, response to 
crises, and other risky behaviors.

6.a.3.H. HH
(1) Persons served.
6.a.(1)3.H. HH

6.a.(2)3.H. HH

6.b.3.H. HH
(1) Active involvement in person-

6.b.(1)3.H. HH

6.b.(5)3.H. HH

SECTION 3.H. HEALTH HOME
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 231
Intent Statements
The program provides structured, needs-based 
education that empowers both persons served 
and their families/support systems with compre-
hensive, age, and developmentally appropriate 
knowledge and skills. By addressing critical areas 
such as active involvement in care planning; the 
interplay of behavioral, physical, and social 
health; harm reduction; prevention strategies 
across various health domains; resilience; 
recovery; and self-management of diverse life 
concerns, the program demonstrates its com-
mitment to fostering informed participation, 
promoting well-being, and optimizing long-term 
positive outcomes for the persons served.
3.H. 7. The health home:
7.3.H. HH
a. Assesses its level of primary and 
behavioral healthcare integration 
using an industry-recognized tool:
7.a.3.H. HH
b. At least annually addresses:
7.b.3.H. HH
(1) Performance in relationship to 
established targets.
7.b.(1)3.H. HH
(2) Trends.
7.b.(2)3.H. HH
(3) Actions for improvement.
7.b.(3)3.H. HH
(4) Results of performance improve -
ment plans.
7.b.(4)3.H. HH
(5) Necessary education and training 
of:
7.b.(5)3.H. HH
(a) Persons served.
7.b.(5)(a)3.H. HH
(b) Families/support systems.
7.b.(5)(b)3.H. HH
(c) Personnel.
7.b.(5)(c)3.H. HH
(d) Other stakeholders.
7.b.(5)(d)3.H. HH
Intent Statements
The health home systematically evaluates the 
maturity and effectiveness of its primary and 
behavioral healthcare integration efforts through 
the application of a credible, industry-recognized 
assessment tool. By objectively measuring its 
current level of integration, the health home 
demonstrates its commitment to identifying 
strengths, pinpointing areas for improvement, 
and continually advancing its capacity to deliver 
coordinated, whole-person care.
Examples
Integrated Practice Assessment Tool (IPAT):
n Purpose: Developed by SAMHSA, IPAT helps 
practices self-assess their level of integration 
along a six-level continuum, from “Minimal 
Collaboration” to “Full Collaboration in a 
Transformed/Merged Integrated Practice.”
n Focus: It uses a decision-tree model with yes/
no questions to place practices on this contin-
uum across various functional areas (e.g., 
physical location, information sharing, pro-
vider involvement, leadership support).
Practice Integration Profile (PIP):
n Purpose: A 30-item, practice-level measure 
derived from the AHRQ Lexicon, designed to 
assess the degree of integration across multi
-
ple domains.
n Focus: It measures aspects such as work flow 
integration, clinical integration, patient-
centered care, and the use of evidence-based 
practices. It aims to allow for comparisons 
between practices and identify impactful 
integration activities.
Comprehensive Health Integration (CHI) 
Framework:
n Purpose: Developed by the National Council 
for Mental Wellbeing, CHI is an evidence-
based, bidirectional framework that guides 
organizations through sustainable physical 
health/behavioral health integration (PH/
BHI).
n Focus: It includes a “Framework Self-assess-
ment Tool” that uses 8 domains and 15 sub-
domains to assess “integratedness” and plan 
for improvement. It considers different inte
-
gration stages and emphasizes sustainable 
financing.
Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care 
(PACIC)/Patient Perceptions of Integrated Care 
(PPIC):
n Purpose: These are patient-reported outcome 
measures (PROMs) that assess the patient’s 
experience with chronic care delivery and 
integrated care.

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
232 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n Focus: They capture the patient's perspective 
on elements such as care coordination, proac-
tive care, shared decision-making, and self-
management support, providing valuable 
insights into the felt experience of 
integration.
Development Model for Integrated Care (DMIC):
n Purpose: This model helps integrated care 
services evaluate their level of development 
and identify areas for improvement.
n Focus: It describes nine clusters of elements 
that contribute to care integration, including 
client-centeredness, delivery system, perfor-
mance management, interprofessional 
teamwork, and roles and tasks
I. Inpatient Treatment
Description
Inpatient treatment programs provide inter-
disciplinary, coordinated, integrated, medically 
supervised services in freestanding or hospital 
settings. Inpatient treatment programs include 
a comprehensive, biopsychosocial approach to 
service delivery in a managed milieu that is 
recovery focused and trauma informed. There 
are daily therapeutic and other activities in which 
the persons served participate. Inpatient treat-
ment is provided 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. 
The goal of inpatient treatment is to provide a 
protective environment that includes medical 
stabilization, support, treatment for psychiatric 
and/or addictive disorders, supervision, wellness, 
and transition to ongoing services. Such pro-
grams operate in designated space that allows for 
appropriate medical treatment and engagement.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation as a 
health home must apply the standards in 
Sections 1 and 2, the specialty designation 
standards for integrated primary care, and 
use the Comprehensive Care field category in 
addition to the standards in this subsection.
3.I. 1. For each person served, a medical 
evaluation:
1.3.I. IT
a. Is obtained within 24 hours of 
admission.
1.a.3.I. IT
b. Includes:
1.b.3.I. IT
(1) A physical examination.
1.b.(1)3.I. IT
(2) Orders for appropriate tests.
1.b.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Face-to-face consultation.
1.b.(3)3.I. IT
Intent Statements
When allowed by law or funding authorities, a 
physician’s assistant or nurse practitioner may 
be used.
When an admission occurs on a weekend or 
holiday, face-to-face consultation may not occur 
until the first working day after admission unless 
medically required.

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 233
Examples
1.a. The medical evaluation may be obtained 
from an appropriately credentialed source before 
or after admission.
3.I. 2. A risk assessment for each person served 
is conducted at the time of admission.
2.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
This standard is related to Standard 2.B.12.d.; 
however, the expectation for an inpatient pro-
gram is that this assessment is conducted at the 
time of admission and not delayed until a later 
time when a full assessment may be conducted. 
Refer to Standard 2.C.4. if risks are identified.
3.I. 3. The assessment of each person served:
3.3.I. IT
a. Is completed within 24 hours of 
admission to the program.
3.a.3.I. IT
b. Results in a preliminary treatment 
plan.
3.b.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
Refer to Section 2.B. standards on assessments for 
additional information and requirements.
3.I. 4. The program has a qualified medical 
director who:
4.3.I. IT
a. Is a physician.
4.a.3.I. IT
b. Demonstrates appropriate training 
and experience in inpatient 
treatment. 
4.b.3.I. IT
c. Has a written ag reement with the 
organization that outlines the medi-
cal director’s responsibilities.
4.c.3.I. IT
d. Leads the medical staff.
4.d.3.I. IT
e. Actively participates in:
4.e.3.I. IT
(1) Ensuring the adequacy of indi -
vidual treatment prescriptions 
and programs, including nota
-
tions of contraindications and 
precautions, developed with the 
participation of professional 
personnel.
4.e.(1)3.I. IT
(2) The development of ongoing 
relationships with the medical 
community.
4.e.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Educational activities with the 
program personnel.
4.e.(3)3.I. IT
(4) Performance improvement 
activities.
4.e.(4)3.I. IT
(5) Program development and 
modification.
4.e.(5)3.I. IT
(6) Establishing the program’s 
policies and procedures.
4.e.(6)3.I. IT
Intent Statements
The medical director provides significant 
programmatic leadership and participates in 
management and performance improvement 
activities.
Examples
4.b. If the organization hires a medical director 
who lacks sufficient training and experience in 
inpatient treatment, the organization can develop 
a plan to ensure that the requisite experience is 
obtained with appropriate consultation and/or 
supervision.
4.c. The responsibilities may be defined in a 
written job description or contract.
3.I. 5. Licensed nursing services are provided 
on site 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
5.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing personnel can be registered 
nurses, licensed practical nurses, or other equiva-
lent personnel. On site can mean in the facility or 
building, not necessarily directly on the unit at all 
times.
3.I. 6. A nursing assessment is conducted upon 
admission.
6.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
The nursing assessment is conducted as a part of 
the admission process by a nurse who is able to 
perform this function through training and scope 
of practice.

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
234 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
The following components of an addictions-
focused nursing assessment may be included:
n Patient History
– Substance Use History: Types of sub -
stances used, duration, frequency, amount, 
and last use.
– Pattern of Use: Situations, triggers, and 
rituals associated with use.
– Previous Treatment: History of past treat -
ments for substance use and outcomes.
–M e d i c a l  H i s t o r y :  C h r o n i c  i l l n e s s e s ,  h o s p i-
talizations, surgeries, and medications.
– Psychiatric History: Mental health dis -
orders, treatments, hospitalizations, and 
medications.
– Family History: Family history of sub -
stance use or mental health disorders.
– Social History: Marital status, children, 
living arrangements, employment, educa-
tion, and legal issues.
n Physical Assessment
– Vital Signs: Blood pressure, heart rate, 
respiratory rate, and temperature.
– General Appearance: Physical signs of sub -
stance use (e.g., needle marks, tremors).
– Neurological Examination: Cognitive 
function, orientation, memory, and 
coordination.
– Respiratory Assessment: Lung sounds, 
respiratory effort, and signs of respiratory 
distress.
– Cardiovascular Assessment: Heart sounds, 
rhythm, and signs of cardiovascular 
complications.
– Gastrointestinal Assessment: Abdominal 
examination and signs of liver damage.
– Skin Assessment: Signs of infections, 
abscesses, and skin conditions.
n Psychological Assessment
– Mental Status Examination: Appearance, 
behavior, mood, affect, thought processes, 
and cognition.
– Psychiatric Symptoms: Depression, 
anxiety, hallucinations, delusions, and 
suicidality.
– Coping Mechanisms: Strategies used to 
deal with stress and substance use triggers.
n Social Assessment
– Support System: Family, friends, and 
community support.
– Living Environment: Safety, stability, and 
influence on substance use.
– Occupational Functioning: Employment 
status, job performance, and work 
environment.
– Legal Issues: Current or past legal prob -
lems related to substance use.
n Laboratory T ests and Screening
– Toxicology Screening: Urine or blood tests 
to detect substances.
– Liver Function Tests: Assess liver damage 
from substance use.
– Infectious Disease Screening: HIV , Hepati -
tis B and C, and other infections related to 
substance use.
n Nutritional Assessment
– Dietary Habits: Nutritional intake, weight 
changes, and signs of malnutrition.
– Eating Patterns: Impact of substance use 
on eating habits.
n Education and Counseling Needs
– Knowledge of substance use: Patient’s 
understanding of the effects of substances.
– Health Education: Information on sub -
stance use and its impact on health.
– Treatment Options: Education on available 
treatment options and resources.
Including these components ensures a thorough 
assessment, allowing for a comprehensive care 
plan tailored to the needs of the person served.

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 235
3.I. 7. There is a written daily schedule of activi-
ties that contribute to the recovery and 
wellness of the persons served.
7.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
A therapeutic environment promotes the ability 
of each person served to meet the goals and 
objectives jointly agreed upon in the develop-
ment of the person’s plan. It is free of unnecessary 
interruptions and distractions.
3.I. 8. Services are provided by a coordinated 
team that includes, at a minimum:
8.3.I. IT
a. A qualified behavioral health practi -
tioner who coordinates the plan of 
the person served.
8.a.3.I. IT
b. Providers of appropriate medical 
services.
8.b.3.I. IT
c. Assigned inpatient personnel.
8.c.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
It should be evident that various team members 
interact with persons served and communicate 
with each other about their interactions and 
plans for providing individual services to persons 
served.
8.a. See the Glossary for the definition of qualified 
behavioral health practitioner.
3.I. 9. The program provides or makes formal 
written arrangements for:
9.3.I. IT
a. Medical consultative services.
9.a.3.I. IT
b. Ancillary medical services.
9.b.3.I. IT
c. Pharmacy services.
9.c.3.I. IT
d. Emergency medical services.
9.d.3.I. IT
e. Other services, as appropriate.
9.e.3.I. IT
Examples
Formal written arrangements may include con-
tracts, letters of agreement, or memorandum of 
understanding (MOU).
9.a. Medical consultative services may include 
medical services needed to address needs of 
persons served that are outside the scope of 
the program’s medical director.
9.b. Ancillary medical services may include 
services such as laboratory , occupational or 
physical therapy, or speech therapy.
3.I. 10. To facilitate seamless service delivery, 
the program:
10.3.I. IT
a. Identifies resources for ongoing care 
of the person served.
10.a.3.I. IT
b. Engages and integrates referral 
resources into the program.
10.b.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
To ensure that the program successfully places 
persons served in the next level of care after 
inpatient treatment, the program develops a 
comprehensive post-discharge network of ser-
vices. The program should integrate this network 
of services as fully as possible into the program 
to strengthen the likelihood of a successful transi-
tion into the next level of care.
Examples
10.b. The program meaningfully engages the 
person served with the intended referral program 
during the inpatient treatment episode. This 
can be done face to face; using communications 
technologies such as telemedicine, Zoom, or 
FaceTime; or through telephone conference 
calling. The purpose is to begin to develop a rap-
port and a relationship with the next treatment 
program to more successfully engage the person 
served in ongoing care post discharge.
3.I. 11. At the time of discharge from the pro-
gram, the person served has: 
11.3.I. IT
a. An established appointment(s) for 
ongoing services.
11.a.3.I. IT
b. Sufficient medication, if applicable.
11.b.3.I. IT

12.3.I. IT
a. Personal privacy.
12.a.3.I. IT
b. Security of personal belongings.
12.b.3.I. IT
c. Space for:
12.c.3.I. IT
(1) Group interactions.
12.c.(1)3.I. IT
(2) Quiet activities.
12.c.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Family or other guests.
12.c.(3)3.I. IT
(4) Therapeutic activities.
12.c.(4)3.I. IT
(5) Cultural and/or spiritual 
activities.
12.c.(5)3.I. IT
(6) Meals.
12.c.(6)3.I. IT

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
236 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
(7) Recreation.
12.c.(7)3.I. IT
(8) Based on gender, age, and needs, 
separate areas for:
12.c.(8)3.I. IT
(a) Sleeping.
12.c.(8)(a)3.I. IT
(b) Hygiene.
12.c.(8)(b)3.I. IT
d. Access to an outdoor setting, if

12.d.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
The program is operated in designated space that 
allows for an appropriate medical treatment envi-
ronment that is respectful of individual privacy to 
the degree possible. 
12.c. The program is not required to maintain 
exclusive spaces for each of these, but appropri-
ate areas should be provided and available as

Examples
12.c.(2) Quiet activities may include de-
escalation.
3.I. 13. Each day, in programs where persons 
served have been committed to inpa-
tient treatment through legal processes, 
a physician or advanced practice pro-
vider determines the medical necessity 
of the person served to remain in the 
inpatient treatment program.
13.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
Persons served in inpatient settings should be 
transferred to lower levels or care as soon as it 
is safe to do so.
Examples
This may be accomplished through options such 
as telephone consultation with team members or 
the use of telemedicine if there is not a physician/
advanced practice provider on site on a daily 
basis.
3.I. 14. To facilitate integrated service delivery, 
the program demonstrates timely com
-
munication with all service providers 
involved with the person served.
14.3.I. IT
  
3.I. 15. To ensure the safety of persons served 
and personnel, the program implements 
written procedures for searches:
15.3.I. IT
a. Of persons served.
15.a.3.I. IT
b. Of belongings.
15.b.3.I. IT
c. Of the physical facility.
15.c.3.I. IT
d. That:
15.d.3.I. IT
(1) Preserve privacy.
15.d.(1)3.I. IT
(2) Preserve dignity.
15.d.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Are sensitive to potential trauma 
of the persons served.
15.d.(3)3.I. IT
Intent Statements
Inpatient treatment programs ensure that per-
sonnel and persons served remain safe in the 
program, without introduction of weapons, 
drugs, or alcohol into the milieu. Personnel are 
adequately trained in the search process in order 
to respect the dignity and privacy of the persons 
served. Programs may find it necessary to con-
duct searches of the persons served, their 
belongings, or the physical facility based on 
observed behaviors of persons served or others.
Examples
15.d. Searches are conducted by personnel with 
whom the person served feels comfortable. This 
may include specific protocols based on a per-
son’s gender identity, as well as consideration 
of religious or cultural factors, prior history of 
trauma, and/or the person’s age. In order to assist 
the persons served to retain some feeling of con-
trol, the program could seek permission to 
conduct searches of individuals or their 
belongings. 
3.I. 16. The program implements written 
procedures that address:
16.3.I. IT
a. Visitation.
16.a.3.I. IT
b. Mail.
16.b.3.I. IT
c. Telephone use.
16.c.3.I. IT
d. Use of personal electronics.
16.d.3.I. IT
Examples
16.d. Programs are often reluctant to allow the 
use of personal electronic devices. However, the 
use of these devices can have a positive influence 
on the person served, particularly the use of apps 

SECTION 3.I. INPATIENT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 237
targeting recovery and self-regulation. Programs 
could attempt to strike a balance, rather than 
simply prohibiting the use of electronic devices.
3.I. 17. All direct service personnel receive 
competency-based training in:
17.3.I. IT
a. First aid.
17.a.3.I. IT
b. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).
17.b.3.I. IT
c. The use of emergency equipment.
17.c.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing staff and physicians are exempt 
from this requirement as their professional train-
ing and licensing ensure competency.
Examples
Direct service personnel are those personnel who 
provide services or supports that require direct 
interaction with the persons served; e.g., thera-
pists, clinicians, aides, case managers, etc.
3.I. 18. The program reduces barriers to inter-
action between the persons served and 
personnel.
18.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
The program should make efforts to reduce 
both attitudinal and physical barriers that create 
distance and separation between program per-
sonnel and the persons served. Personnel should 
be engaged with and accessible to the persons 
served, rather than remaining behind glass barri-
ers or in an office or lounge where the persons 
served cannot interact with them.
3.I. 19. The program demonstrates continuous 
vigilance regarding safety of the envir-
onment for the persons served and 
personnel through:
19.3.I. IT
a. Assessment of risks, including:
19.a.3.I. IT
(1) Ligatures.
19.a.(1)3.I. IT
(2) Sharps.
19.a.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Furnishings.
19.a.(3)3.I. IT
(4) Facility maintenance issues.
19.a.(4)3.I. IT
(5) Other safety risks.
19.a.(5)3.I. IT
b. Response to identified risks.
19.b.3.I. IT
Intent Statements
The program implements surveillance activities 
that ensure the safety of all persons served and 
personnel on an ongoing basis. The program 
likely alters these activities based on the persons 
served and the assessment of risks in the milieu.
19.a.(1) It is not the intent of this standard to 
require that facilities be retrofitted into a ligature-
free environment; however, awareness of ligature 
points is crucial to ensuring vigilant observation 
and protection of persons served.
Examples
19.a.(5) Other safety risks might include leisure 
items; toys; visitors/family members who are 
upset, angry, or carrying a weapon; and entrances 
and exits in a secured program environment.
3.I. 20. The program provides documented, 
competency-based training to direct 
service personnel:
20.3.I. IT
a. At:
20.a.3.I. IT
(1) Orientation.
20.a.(1)3.I. IT

20.a.(2)3.I. IT
b. That includes, at a minimum, the 
following topics:
20.b.3.I. IT
(1) De-escalation techniques.
20.b.(1)3.I. IT
(2) Risk assessment.
20.b.(2)3.I. IT
(3) Trauma-informed approaches.
20.b.(3)3.I. IT
