21.3.I. IT
a. Identifies an indicator to measure

in
 ongoing services post discharge.

21.b.3.I. IT

21.b.(5)3.I. IT

(c) Personnel.

Intent Statements
This standard is related to the standards in

J. Intensive Family-Based 
Services
Description
Intensive family-based services are provided in a 
supportive and interactive manner and directed 
toward maintaining or restoring a healthy family 
relationship by building and strengthening the 
capacity of families to care for their children. 
The services are time limited and are initially 
intensive, based on the needs of the family. The 
services demonstrate a multisystemic approach 
and have a goal of keeping families together or 
supporting reunification when a child has been 
in an out-of-home placement. The services may 
include wraparound and family preservation type 
programs.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for 
an intensive family-based services program 
must apply the standards in Section 1. In addi-
tion, standards in Section 2 must be applied 
according to the table at the beginning of 
Section 2, along with the standards in this 
subsection.
3.J. 1. Services are designed to maintain intact 
families, prevent out-of-home placement 
and promote reunification including:
1.3.J. IFBS
a. Individual counseling services.
1.a.3.J. IFBS
b. Skills development services 
including:
1.b.3.J. IFBS
(1) Behavior management skills.
1.b.(1)3.J. IFBS
(2) Life skills.
1.b.(2)3.J. IFBS
(3) Conflict resolution skills.
1.b.(3)3.J. IFBS
(4) Problem-solving skills.
1.b.(4)3.J. IFBS
(5) Anger management skills.
1.b.(5)3.J. IFBS
(6) Decision-making skills.
1.b.(6)3.J. IFBS
(7) Crisis management skills.
1.b.(7)3.J. IFBS
(8) Parenting skills.
1.b.(8)3.J. IFBS
c. Family therapy.
1.c.3.J. IFBS
d. School-based services.
1.d.3.J. IFBS

SECTION 3.J. INTENSIVE FAMILY-BASED SERVICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 239
e. Crisis management/stabilization 
services.
1.e.3.J. IFBS
f. Positive youth development services.
1.f.3.J. IFBS
g. Substance abuse services.
1.g.3.J. IFBS
h. Nutritional services.
1.h.3.J. IFBS

Intent Statements
The program provides services designed to meet 
needs in any area of a family’s functioning that 
may impact family stability. Not all services need 
to be provided to every family, however, the pro-
gram should have the capability to provide all 
services listed as needed. Services may be pro-
vided directly by the program or they may be 
provided under contract or through referrals.
Examples
1.q. Domestic violence may include interper-
sonal, family, and intimate partner violence and 
is not limited to physical or emotional abuse.
3.J. 2. The program implements a process for 
identifying, locating, and engaging 
family members, as appropriate, in 
services.
2.3.J. IFBS
Examples
The program demonstrates efforts to engage 
family, including extended family, in services. 
This may include the use of internet-based 
search services to locate family members.
3.J. 3. The program facilitates access to profes-
sionals trained in child/youth and family 
care, including:
3.3.J. IFBS
a. A psychologist.
3.a.3.J. IFBS
b. A counselor.
3.b.3.J. IFBS
c. A family therapist.
3.c.3.J. IFBS
d. A social worker.
3.d.3.J. IFBS
e. A youth worker.
3.e.3.J. IFBS
f. A psychiatrist.
3.f.3.J. IFBS
g. Medical personnel.
3.g.3.J. IFBS
h. Other providers, as appropriate.
3.h.3.J. IFBS
Intent Statements
It is understood that in some geographic loca-
tions, not all of the listed professionals may be 
available locally. It is important for the program to 
assist the persons served to connect with appro-
priate professionals for identified needed services 
that are beyond the scope of the program.
Examples
3.g. Medical personnel could include a nurse, a 
physical therapist, or a speech therapist, based 
on the needs of the child/youth and family 
served.
3.J. 4. The program collaborates with other 
programs in planning service delivery.
4.3.J. IFBS
  
3.J. 5. Based on need, the program provides the 
person served and/or family:
5.3.J. IFBS
a. Information on community resources.
5.a.3.J. IFBS
b. Referrals to community resources.
5.b.3.J. IFBS
c. Concrete services and supports.
5.c.3.J. IFBS
Intent Statements
The program provides or facilitates access to 
available resources to meet the family’s needs. 
Examples
5.c. Concrete services and supports might 
include contingency funds or petty cash that can 
be used for essentials for the family, such as fixing 
a car to allow family to have transportation to 
work or other necessary appointments, purchas-
ing needed clothing, or paying utility bills. 
3.J. 6. The program implements a procedure 
that addresses assigning consistent team 
members for each person served.
6.3.J. IFBS

Intent Statements
The program’s commitment to assigning a pri-

builds strong relationships which promotes

and maintain family stability, the pro-

including:
7.3.J. IFBS

The program’s procedures include normative 
responses and actions as well as the most restric-
tive responses and actions that may be necessary 
to ensure the safety of the child/youth and main-
tain family stability.
3.J. 8. The program has on-call availability of 
supervisory personnel to respond to 
urgent situations 24 hours a day, 7 days 
a week.

K. Intensive Outpatient 
Treatment
Description
Intensive outpatient treatment programs are 
clearly identified as separate and distinct pro-
grams that provide culturally and linguistically 
appropriate services. The intensive outpatient 
program consists of a scheduled series of sessions 
appropriate to the person-centered plans of the 
persons served. These may include services pro-
vided during evenings and on weekends and/or 
interventions delivered by a variety of service 
providers in the community. The program may 
function as a step-down program from partial 
hospitalization, withdrawal management, or resi-
dential services; may be used to prevent or 
minimize the need for a more intensive level of 
treatment; and is considered to be more intensive 
than traditional outpatient services. The program 
is required to offer the number of hours identi-
fied in the standards and provided based on the 
individual needs of the persons served.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for an 
intensive outpatient treatment program must 
apply the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addi-
tion to the standards in this subsection.
3.K. 1. An intensive outpatient treatment pro-
gram offers:
1.3.K. IOP
a. At least nine direct contact hours per 
week to adult persons served.
1.a.3.K. IOP
b. At least six direct contact hours per 
week to children/adolescents served.
1.b.3.K. IOP
Intent Statements
Based on the needs of the persons served, the 
intensive outpatient program offers a variety of 
service modalities that are designed to assist 
individuals to achieve their goals related to psy-
chological or social functioning, self-esteem, and 
coping abilities or to external opportunities such 
as vocational, educational, or social.

SECTION 3.K. INTENSIVE OUTPATIENT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 241
3.K. 2. Intensive outpatient treatment programs 
provide two or more of the following 
services:
2.3.K. IOP
a. Individual counseling/therapy.
2.a.3.K. IOP
b. Family counseling/therapy.
2.b.3.K. IOP
c. Group counseling/therapy.
2.c.3.K. IOP
  
3.K. 3. The program offers education on:
3.3.K. IOP
a. Wellness.
3.a.3.K. IOP
b. Recovery.
3.b.3.K. IOP
c. Resiliency.
3.c.3.K. IOP
Intent Statements
These educational activities may be provided in 
individual, group, or other settings.
Examples
3.a. Wellness education is designed to assist the 
person served to achieve balance in physical and 
emotional health and wellbeing. For additional 
examples and ideas, see the SAMHSA website at 
https://store.samhsa.gov/sites/default/files/d7/
priv/sma16-4958.pdf.
Other examples include:
n Diagnosis Education: Information about spe-
cific mental health disorders, symptoms, and 
diagnostic criteria.
n Etiology: Understanding the causes and con-
tributing factors of mental health disorders 
(genetic, biological, environmental, etc.).
n Prognosis and Course: Information about the 
typical course of the disorder, potential out
-
comes, and long-term expectations.
n Nutrition and Exercise: The impact of diet 
and physical activity on mental health and 
overall well-being.
n Sleep Hygiene: Tips for improving sleep qual-
ity and establishing healthy sleep patterns.
n Self-Care Practices: Importance of self-care 
and practical ways to incorporate it into daily 
life.
n Mindfulness Practices: Techniques such as 
mindfulness meditation, mindful breathing, 
and mindful eating.
n Relaxation Exercises: Progressive muscle 
relaxation, guided imagery, and other relax-
ation techniques.
3.b. Recovery education includes activities 
designed to provide information about the per-
son’s disability/disorder with a focus on achieving 
the highest possible personal functioning and 
improvements in the person’s social and occu-
pational interactions.
Examples of things that might be included:
n Stress Management: Techniques for managing 
stress, such as relaxation exercises, mindful-
ness, and breathing techniques.
n Emotion Regulation: Strategies for under-
standing and managing emotions, such as 
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) tech
-
niques and Dialectical Behavior Therapy 
(DBT) skills.
n Problem Solving: Steps and methods for effec-
tive problem-solving in everyday situations
n Relapse Prevention
– Triggers and Warning Signs: Identifying 
personal triggers and early warning signs 
of relapse.
– Relapse Prevention Plan: Developing and 
implementing a personalized relapse pre-
vention plan.
– Crisis Management: Steps to take in a cri -
sis and resources for immediate help.
3.c. Resiliency education is focused on improving 
awareness of the person’s strengths and building 
on those strengths.
Examples might include:
n Resource Information: Information about 
local resources, support groups, and commu-
nity services.
n Navigating the System: Guidance on how to 
access and navigate mental health and social 
services.

SECTION 3.K. INTENSIVE OUTPATIENT TREATMENT
242 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
n Mindfulness Practices: Techniques such as 
mindfulness meditation, mindful breathing, 
and mindful eating.
n Relaxation Exercises: Progressive muscle 
relaxation, guided imagery, and other relax-
ation techniques.
3.K. 4. To maximize the opportunity of the 
persons served to participate in the 
program, services are provided:
4.3.K. IOP
a. In locations that meet the needs of 
the persons served.
4.a.3.K. IOP
b. At times that meet the needs of the 
persons served.
4.b.3.K. IOP
c. On days that meet the needs of the 
persons served.
4.c.3.K. IOP
Intent Statements
Services can be provided in traditional office set-
tings, community settings, online or in virtual 
settings, or in personal homes. Programs seek to 
minimize interruptions of activities such as work 
and school as well as other daily responsibilities 
of persons served.
3.K. 5. When appropriate, and with the consent 
of the person served, the program inte-
grates treatment with other services.
5.3.K. IOP
Intent Statements
Often persons receiving outpatient treatment are 
also involved with healthcare and/or social ser-
vices. The intent of this standard is to ensure that 
the program actively seeks information from and 
communicates with other healthcare providers, 
social service entities, schools, legal entities, child 
welfare agencies, and other services that are likely 
to improve the quality of its services to persons 
served and the outcomes achieved.
3.K. 6. The program addresses the emerging 
needs of the persons served through 
linkage to appropriate resources and 
supports.
6.3.K. IOP
Examples
When a person served has emerging needs that 
are outside of the person-centered plan, such as 
being unable to pay utility bills, having a medical 
emergency in the family, or being unable to get to 
work due to a car breaking down, the program 
helps find support and assistance to address these 
needs through linkages to other services or 
providers.
3.K. 7. The program:
7.3.K. IOP
a. In collaboration with the person 
served, identifies the person’s natural 
supports.
7.a.3.K. IOP
b. Assists the person to develop and 
utilize natural supports.
7.b.3.K. IOP
Examples
The program demonstrates its understanding of 
the need for persons served to develop and main-
tain a healthy support system. There is evidence 
that the program assists the person served to cre-
ate long-term natural supports to reduce reliance 
on providers in their transition post discharge.
3.K. 8. A review of the person-centered plan for 
each person served in an intensive out-
patient treatment program occurs at 
least once per month.
8.3.K. IOP
Examples
The review may be documented with updates or 
changes to the plan, with a plan update docu-
ment, or through progress notes. The program 
demonstrates that it is adjusting to ongoing 
assessments and emerging issues of the person 
served.

SECTION 3.L. OFFICE-BASED OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAM
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 243
L. Office-Based Opioid 
Treatment Program
Description
Office-based opioid treatment (OBOT) programs 
are medically managed programs that provide 
treatment services to persons with opioid use 
disorders. Central to treatment are medications, 
typically buprenorphine or naltrexone, which are 
provided in concert with other medical and psy-
chosocial interventions designed to realize a 
person’s highest achievable recovery. Based on 
the needs of the persons served, these programs 
provide or arrange for a comprehensive array 
of treatment services that includes counseling/
therapy, medication supports, social supports, 
education and training, care coordination, and 
other recovery-enhancing services. 
OBOT programs provide services under the 
supervision of a physician and are guided by 
written treatment procedures and protocols that 
address the routine needs of persons with opioid 
use disorders, including the needs of special pop-
ulations. From induction to stabilization and into 
maintenance, OBOT programs provide ongoing 
care to persons served to support their recovery.
NOTE: These services may also be known as medica-
tion-assisted treatment (MAT).
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for an 
office-based opioid treatment program must 
apply the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in 
addition to the standards in this subsection 
and use the Addictions Pharmacotherapy field 
category.
NOTE: Programs that serve adolescents must also 
meet the Specialty Designation standards in Section 
5.C. Children and Adolescents.
3.L. 1. Based on the needs of each person 
served, the program provides or 
arranges for the following services:
1.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Individual counseling/therapy.
1.a.3.L. BH OBOT
b. Group counseling/therapy.
1.b.3.L. BH OBOT
c. Family counseling/therapy.
1.c.3.L. BH OBOT
d. Recovery support services.
1.d.3.L. BH OBOT
e. Case management/services 
coordination.
1.e.3.L. BH OBOT
f. Medical/nursing services, including:
1.f.3.L. BH OBOT
(1) Nursing assessment.
1.f.(1)3.L. BH OBOT
(2) History and physical 
examination.
1.f.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
(3) Medication administration, 
including injections.
1.f.(3)3.L. BH OBOT
(4) Specimen collection for drug 
testing.
1.f.(4)3.L. BH OBOT
(5) Laboratory studies.
1.f.(5)3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
The program demonstrates the capability to 
provide all of these services through program 
personnel or through close coordination of refer-
rals. When the service is provided by an outside 
service provider, the program demonstrates that 
it has regular communication with the provider, 
information is shared with appropriate consents, 
and progress is documented in the person’s 
record. It is not expected that each person served 
receives all of these services, but rather that these 
services are available to persons based on their 
assessed needs.
3.L. 2. The medical director for the program is a 
physician who:
2.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Is certified in a specialty area by a 
nationally recognized board.
2.a.3.L. BH OBOT
b. Demonstrates appropriate experi -
ence in addiction treatment through 
one or more of the following:
2.b.3.L. BH OBOT
(1) A fellowship in addiction medi -
cine or addiction psychiatry for 
a minimum of one year.
2.b.(1)3.L. BH OBOT
(2) A minimum of two years’ exp -
erience providing addiction 
treatment services.
2.b.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
(3) A plan to develop competency in 
addiction medicine or addiction 
psychiatry, including continuing 
medical education in addiction 
medicine or addiction psychiatry, 
within two years.
2.b.(3)3.L. BH OBOT

SECTION 3.L. OFFICE-BASED OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAM
244 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
c. Is responsible for:
2.c.3.L. BH OBOT

2.c.(1)3.L. BH OBOT

2.c.(1)(a)3.L. BH OBOT

2.g.3.L. BH OBOT

2.g.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
Although there may be other prescribers who 
work in the program and prescribe medications 
within their individual scopes of practice and 
supervision, accredited programs have a board 
certified, trained, and experienced medical 
director who is a physician. 
2.e.(4) Programs that serve adolescents must 
also meet the Specialty Designation standards 
in Section 5.C. Children and Adolescents.
2.g. The medical director for these programs is 
not an appointment in name only. The medical 
director demonstrates adequate availability to 
provide program supervision, directs care 
through staff meetings or other interactions, 
and is available to persons served and program 
personnel for consultation as needed.
Examples
When the medical director is not an employee, 
the responsibilities could be articulated in a writ-
ten agreement or contract, and reviewed at least 
annually as described in Standard 1.G.5.
2.a. In the U.S., the medical director is board 
certified typically by the American Board of 
Addiction Medicine or American Board of 
Preventive Medicine, or in another specialty 
recognized by the American Board of Medical 
Specialties.
2.e.(3) Protocols take into consideration com-
mon co-occurring conditions such as depression, 
anxiety, trauma, PTSD, and others.
2.e.(6) Other populations might include veterans, 
persons with physical health conditions, or 
persons with intellectual or developmental 
disabilities.
2.f. Protocols may address routine laboratory 
studies such as a complete blood count, or tests 
for diseases or illnesses often seen in the popula-
tion served such as HIV and hepatitis.
3.L. 3. The assessment process includes the 
preparation of a written interpretive 
summary that addresses the six dim
-
ensions of the American Society of 
Addiction Medicine (ASAM) or similar 
criteria.
3.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
This standard relates to Standard 2.B.14., with an 
expectation that the program provides informa-
tion in its interpretive summary reflective of the 
six dimensions for assessment as defined by 
ASAM. There may be additional information in 
the summary; however, assessment of each 
dimension with results should be included.

4.c.(3)3.L. BH OBOT
(4) Response to relapse.
4.c.(4)3.L. BH OBOT
(5) Strategies for maintaining 
engagement in treatment.
4.c.(5)3.L. BH OBOT
(6) Overdose prevention and 
response.
4.c.(6)3.L. BH OBOT
(7) Dose reduction/weaning when 
initiated by:
4.c.(7)3.L. BH OBOT
(a) The person served.
4.c.(7)(a)3.L. BH OBOT
(b) The program.
4.c.(7)(b)3.L. BH OBOT
Examples
4.a.(2) It is crucial that the physician supervising 
the induction process evaluate the appropriate 
time to begin a medication regimen. The Clinical 
Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) is the typical 
scale used for this purpose.
4.a.(3)(a) Precipitated withdrawal can occur 
when an antagonist is administered to a person 
served dependent on full agonist opioids. The 
program’s procedures describe how to avoid pre-
cipitated withdrawal, how to manage a case when 
precipitated withdrawal occurs, and timeframes 
from last use of opioids to administration of 
buprenorphine or naltrexone.
4.a.(3)(b) The program considers withdrawal 
from opioids and other drugs/alcohol in plan-
ning the delivery of care. 
4.a.(4) Programs often must make the determi-
nation of office-based induction or home-based 
induction. The effectiveness of home-based 
induction can be influenced by a number of 
factors. When determining the potential for 
home-based induction, the program considers 
the motivation of the person served, the effec-
tiveness of the person’s support system, risks of 
diversion, and other relevant factors.
4.a.(6), 4.b.(4), 4.c.(4) The program recognizes 
that relapse is not an unusual occurrence with 
this population and has strategies to skillfully 
manage relapses in a caring manner. There may 
be times when the program determines that more 
intensive services are required, and the program 
has relationships with other treatment programs 
to facilitate effective referrals/transfers.
4.a.(7), 4.b.(5), 4.c.(5) The program implements 
strategies to maintain persons served in the pro-
gram and measures performance on this element 
as described in Standard 9. of this section. 
4.a.(8), 4.b.(6), 4.c.(6) The program trains 
personnel on their role in recognizing and 
responding to overdose. The program likely 
maintains naloxone on-hand to respond immedi-
ately to overdoses. Additionally, these procedures 
outline strategies the program employs to prevent 
overdoses.
4.c.(7)(a) The program’s procedures describe 
how a person served can request the initiation of 
weaning that can lead to discontinuation of treat-
ment. The program will likely want to discuss 
with the person the motivation for discontinua-
tion and, should this begin, offer additional 

SECTION 3.L. OFFICE-BASED OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAM
246 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
supports to the person to prevent a relapse/
overdose.
4.c.(7)(b) The program may discontinue treat-
ment due to continued violation of program 
rules, a change in funding or other resources 
for the person served, or other valid reason. 
The procedures describe how the weaning/
discontinuation process will be managed.
3.L. 5. The program implements written pro-
cedures for medication monitoring that 
address:
5.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Drug testing, including:
5.a.3.L. BH OBOT
(1) Parent compound.
5.a.(1)3.L. BH OBOT
(2) Metabolites.
5.a.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
(3) Interpretation of results.
5.a.(3)3.L. BH OBOT
b. Diversion control.
5.b.3.L. BH OBOT
c. Lost or stolen medication.
5.c.3.L. BH OBOT
d. Medication adherence.
5.d.3.L. BH OBOT
e. Assessment of medication efficacy.
5.e.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
The program should take steps to reduce the risks 
to persons served and others by monitoring that 
the person served is taking the medication as pre-
scribed and implementing procedures to reduce 
the chances of lost or diverted medication.
Examples
5.a. Programs may describe in procedures when 
to utilize simple tests like dip sticks to test for 
parent compounds and when to utilize confir-
mation tests for the presence of metabolites.
5.b. Programs can implement a variety of act-
ivities to ensure that persons served are taking 
medications as prescribed and that medications 
are not being diverted to others. These strategies 
may include, but are not limited to, random call 
backs, witnessing of urine samples, verification 
testing of medication metabolites, wrapper 
counts, verifying lot numbers, witnessing medi-
cation administration through apps like Scene 
Health, etc.
3.L. 6. The program implements written pro-
cedures for pharmacy services that 
address:
6.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Communication that supports clinical 
care.
6.a.3.L. BH OBOT
b. After-hours dispensing.
6.b.3.L. BH OBOT
c. Medication delivery.
6.c.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
The program should seek out a pharmacy(ies) 
that assists the program to meet the needs of 
the persons served with communication that 
supports care coordination, flexible hours for 
dispensing of medications, and delivery of 
medications to the program as allowed.
Examples
6.a. A pharmacy can be helpful in communica-
tion if the person served is not picking up 
medications, if there are concerns based on other 
medications prescribed for the person served, if 
there is behavior observed that is associated with 
diversion, etc.
6.b. The program addresses the potential needs 
of persons served for pharmacy services in the 
evenings and on weekends and holidays through 
its own pharmacy services and/or the availability 
of services in the community.
3.L. 7. Based on the needs of each person 
served, the program provides 
documented training and education on 
the following topics:
7.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Disease of addiction.
7.a.3.L. BH OBOT
b. Secure storage of medication.
7.b.3.L. BH OBOT
c. Overdose prevention and response.
7.c.3.L. BH OBOT
d. Recovery with a medication for 
addiction treatment.
7.d.3.L. BH OBOT
e. Withdrawal symptoms.
7.e.3.L. BH OBOT
f. Neonatal abstinence syndrome.
7.f.3.L. BH OBOT
g. Managing prescribed medications for 
opioid use disorder in the presence of 
pain.
7.g.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
This supplements the training and education for 
persons served addressed in Standard 2.E.2.

SECTION 3.L. OFFICE-BASED OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAM
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 247
3.L. 8. The program provides documented 
training and education to direct service 
personnel:
8.3.L. BH OBOT
a. At:
8.a.3.L. BH OBOT
(1) Orientation.
8.a.(1)3.L. BH OBOT
(2) Regular intervals.
8.a.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
b. That includes, at a minimum, the 
following topics:
8.b.3.L. BH OBOT
(1) Chronic disease model of 
addiction.
8.b.(1)3.L. BH OBOT
(2) Behaviors associated with 
addiction.
8.b.(2)3.L. BH OBOT
(3) Concepts of medications for 
addiction treatment.
8.b.(3)3.L. BH OBOT
(4) Overdose prevention and 
response.
8.b.(4)3.L. BH OBOT
(5) Specimen collection for drug 
testing.
8.b.(5)3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
This relates to Standard 2.E.2. on training and 
education for direct service personnel in 
programs that provide medication control, 
administering, and/or prescribing.
3.L. 9. The performance measurement and 
management system includes an indi-
cator to measure retention of persons 
served in the OBOT program.
9.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
This standard is related to Standard 1.L.4. as a 
measure of effectiveness of treatment. Keeping 
the persons served engaged in an OBOT program 
is crucial to their recovery and not returning to 
opioid use and risking an overdose.
3.L. 10. The program demonstrates efforts to 
educate stakeholders in the community 
about opioid use, including:
10.3.L. BH OBOT
a. Addiction.
10.a.3.L. BH OBOT
b. Treatment.
10.b.3.L. BH OBOT
c. Stigma-reducing language.
10.c.3.L. BH OBOT
Intent Statements
This relates to Standard 1.A.6.d. on organizational 
advocacy efforts for the persons served. 
Examples
Education might be provided through one-on-
one contacts or to groups and include written 
information; lectures; videos; audio recordings; 
or the provision of information via informational 
mailings, emails, or the program’s website.

SECTION 3.M. OUTPATIENT TREATMENT
248 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
M. Outpatient Treatment
Description
Outpatient treatment programs provide cultur-
ally and linguistically appropriate services. These 
include individual, group, and family counseling/
therapy as determined by the needs of the person 
served. These programs offer comprehensive, 
coordinated, and defined services that may vary 
in level of intensity. Outpatient programs may 
address a variety of needs, including, but not 
limited to, situational stressors, family relations, 
interpersonal relationships, mental health issues, 
life span issues, psychiatric illnesses, and sub-
stance use disorders and other addictive 
behaviors.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for an 
outpatient treatment program must apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition 
the standards in this subsection.
3.M. 1. Outpatient treatment programs provide 
one or more of the following services:
1.3.M. OT
a. Individual counseling/therapy.
1.a.3.M. OT
b. Family counseling/therapy.
1.b.3.M. OT
c. Group counseling/therapy.
1.c.3.M. OT
Intent Statements
Based on the needs of the persons served, the 
outpatient program offers or refers to a variety of 
service modalities that are designed to assist the 
persons served to achieve their goals related to 
psychological or social functioning, self-esteem, 
and coping abilities or to external opportunities 
such as vocational, educational, or social.
3.M. 2. The program offers education on:
2.3.M. OT
a. Wellness.
2.a.3.M. OT
b. Recovery.
2.b.3.M. OT
c. Resiliency.
2.c.3.M. OT
Intent Statements
These educational activities may be provided in 
individual, group, or other settings.
Examples
2.a. Wellness education is designed to assist the 
person served to achieve balance in physical and 
emotional health and wellbeing. For additional 
examples and ideas, see the SAMHSA website at 
https://store.samhsa.gov/sites/default/files/d7/
priv/sma16-4958.pdf.
Other examples include:
n Diagnosis Education: Information about 
specific mental health disorders, symptoms, 
and diagnostic criteria.
n Etiology: Understanding the causes and con-
tributing factors of mental health disorders 
(genetic, biological, environmental, etc.).
n Prognosis and Course: Information about 
the
 typical course of the disorder, potential 
outcomes, and long-term expectations.
n Nutrition and Exercise: The impact of diet 
and physical activity on mental health and 
overall well-being.
n Sleep Hygiene: Tips for improving sleep qual-
ity and establishing healthy sleep patterns.
n Self-Care Practices: Importance of self-care 
and practical ways to incorporate it into daily 
life.
n Mindfulness Practices: Techniques such as 
mindfulness meditation, mindful breathing, 
and mindful eating.
n Relaxation Exercises: Progressive muscle 
relaxation, guided imagery, and other relax-
ation techniques.
2.b. Recovery education includes activities 
designed to provide information about the per-
son’s disability/disorder with a focus on achieving 
the highest possible personal functioning and 
improvements in the person’s social and occu-
pational interactions.
Examples of things that might be included:
n Stress Management: Techniques for managing 
stress, such as relaxation exercises, mindful-
ness, and breathing techniques.
n Emotion Regulation: Strategies for under-
standing and managing emotions, such as 
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) tech-
niques and Dialectical Behavior Therapy 
(DBT) skills.

SECTION 3.M. OUTPATIENT TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 249
n Problem Solving: Steps and methods for effec-
tive problem-solving in everyday situations
n Relapse Prevention
– Triggers and Warning Signs: Identifying 
personal triggers and early warning signs 
of relapse.
– Relapse Prevention Plan: Developing and 
implementing a personalized relapse pre-
vention plan.
– Crisis Management: Steps to take in a cri -
sis and resources for immediate help.
2.c. Resiliency education is focused on improving 
awareness of the person’s strengths and building 
on those strengths.
Examples might include:
n Resource Information: Information about 
local resources, support groups, and commu-
nity services.
n Navigating the System: Guidance on how to 
access and navigate mental health and social 
services.
n Mindfulness Practices: Techniques such as 
mindfulness meditation, mindful breathing, 
and mindful eating.
n Relaxation Exercises: Progressive muscle 
relaxation, guided imagery, and other relax-
ation techniques.
3.M. 3. To maximize the opportunity of the 
persons served to participate in the 
program, services are provided:
3.3.M. OT
a. In locations that meet the needs of 
the persons served.
3.a.3.M. OT
b. At times that meet the needs of the 
persons served.
3.b.3.M. OT
c. On days that meet the needs of the 
persons served.
3.c.3.M. OT
Intent Statements
Services can be provided in traditional office set-
tings, community settings, online or in virtual 
settings, or in personal homes. Programs seek to 
minimize interruptions of activities such as work 
and school as well as other daily responsibilities 
of persons served.
3.M. 4. When appropriate, and with the consent 
of the person served, the program inte-
grates treatment with other services.
4.3.M. OT
Intent Statements
Often persons receiving outpatient treatment are 
also involved with healthcare and/or social ser-
vices. The intent of this standard is to ensure that 
the program actively seeks information from and 
communicates with other healthcare providers, 
social service entities, schools, legal entities, child 
welfare agencies, and other services that are likely 
to improve the quality of its services to persons 
served and the outcomes achieved.
3.M. 5. The program addresses the emerging 
needs of the persons served through 
linkage to appropriate resources and 
supports.
5.3.M. OT
Examples
When a person served has emerging needs that 
are outside of the person-centered plan, such as 
being unable to pay utility bills, having a medical 
emergency in the family, or being unable to get to 
work due to a car breaking down, the program 
helps find support and assistance to address these 
needs through linkages to other services or 
providers.
3.M. 6. The program:
6.3.M. OT
a. In collaboration with the person 
served, identifies the person’s natural 
supports.
6.a.3.M. OT
b. Assists the person to develop and 
utilize natural supports.
6.b.3.M. OT
Examples
The program demonstrates its understanding of 
the need for persons served to develop and main-
tain a healthy support system. There is evidence 
that the program assists the person served to cre-
ate long-term natural supports to reduce reliance 
on providers in their transition post-discharge.

SECTION 3.N. PARTIAL HOSPITALIZATION
250 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
N. Partial Hospitalization
Description
Partial hospitalization programs are time limited, 
medically supervised programs that offer 
comprehensive, therapeutically intensive, coord-
inated, and structured clinical services. Partial 
hospitalization programs are available at least 
five days per week but may also offer half-day, 
weekend, or evening hours. Partial hospitaliza-
tion programs may be freestanding or part of a 
broader system but should be identifiable as a 
distinct program or service line.
A partial hospitalization program consists of a 
series of structured, face-to-face therapeutic ses-
sions organized at various levels of intensity and 
frequency. Partial hospitalization programs are 
typically designed for persons who are experienc-
ing increased symptomatology, disturbances in 
behavior, or other conditions that negatively 
impact the mental or behavioral health of the 
person served that cannot be managed effectively 
in less intensive programs. The program must 
be able to address the presenting problems in a 
setting that is not residential or inpatient. Given 
this, the persons served that cannot be managed 
effectively in less intensive programs. Services are 
provided for the purpose of diagnostic evalua-
tion; active treatment of a person’s condition; or 
to prevent relapse, hospitalization, or incarcera-
tion. Such a program functions as an alternative 
to inpatient care, as transitional care following an 
inpatient stay in lieu of continued hospitalization, 
as a step-down service, or when the severity of 
symptoms is such that success in a less acute level 
of care is tenuous.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
partial hospitalization program must apply the 
standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition to the 
standards in this subsection.
3.N. 1. The program is provided under the 
direction of a clinical director who is 
a
 physician or an advanced practice 
provider.
1.3.N. PH
  
3.N. 2. The program:
2.3.N. PH
a. Is available to the persons served at 
least five days per week.
2.a.3.N. PH
b. Includes a minimum of three hours of 
therapeutic services per day.
2.b.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
Although the program is capable of providing a 
multidisciplinary program of medical and thera-
peutic services to the persons served three hours 
per day, five days per week, individualized plans 
and variable lengths of stay will determine the 
degree to which each person actually participates 
in a given week.
3.N. 3. The therapeutic environment includes:
3.3.N. PH
a. Consistently assigned personnel.
3.a.3.N. PH
b. Scheduled activities.
3.b.3.N. PH
c. Sufficient professional staff to:
3.c.3.N. PH
(1) Conduct clinical assessments.
3.c.(1)3.N. PH
(2) Develop appropriate person-
centered plans.
3.c.(2)3.N. PH
(3) Provide therapeutic 
interventions.
3.c.(3)3.N. PH
(4) Review goals/objectives on a 
biweekly basis.
3.c.(4)3.N. PH
Intent Statements
The environment achieves a stable staffing pat-
tern either by assigning the same personnel to 
the program or by rotating personnel from a con-
sistent pool to provide the needed intensity of 
interventions on a consistent basis.
3.N. 4. The program’s services are provided by a 
multidisciplinary team.
4.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
This standard reinforces a team approach to ser-
vices. There is a team made up of persons from a 
variety of disciplines who work cooperatively in 
delivering partial hospitalization services. The 
various disciplines included are based on the 
needs of the person served.

SECTION 3.N. PARTIAL HOSPITALIZATION
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 251
3.N. 5. Qualified behavioral health practitioners 
are on site during program hours to:
5.3.N. PH
a. Supervise personnel.
5.a.3.N. PH
b. Direct services.
5.b.3.N. PH
c. Provide direct clinical treatment 
services, as appropriate.
5.c.3.N. PH
d. Provide interventions, as necessary.
5.d.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
See the Glossary for the definition of a qualified 
behavioral health practitioner.
5.b. Some services, such as education and train-
ing and therapeutic activities, may be provided 
by personnel who are not qualified behavioral 
health practitioners, but are qualified to deliver 
the services, specifically trained to work with the 
population being served, and supervised by qual-
ified behavioral health practitioners.
5.c. Clinical treatment services are provided by 
qualified behavioral health practitioners within 
the scope of their licenses and clinical privileges.
3.N. 6. A registered nurse, trained and compe-
tent in the delivery of behavioral health 
services, is available on site during pro-
gram hours to provide necessary:
6.3.N. PH
a. Nursing care.
6.a.3.N. PH
b. Psychiatric nursing care.
6.b.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
Nursing care may also include supervision of 
ancillary staff members (such as nursing aides or 
technicians) who provide supportive medically 
related services, as allowed by law or regulation.
In a partial hospitalization program where either 
the specific needs of the persons served or the 
program’s regulatory authorities and funding 
sources do not require continuous on-site nurs-
ing coverage, this standard may be met through 
an on-call system or clearly identified process for 
accessing nursing care when needed.
The nursing care and supervision could also be 
done by a nurse practitioner.
3.N. 7. As deemed clinically necessary and 
based on the needs of the persons 
served:
7.3.N. PH
a. Psychiatric services are provided to 
the persons served.
7.a.3.N. PH
b. A psychiatrist is available 24 hours a 
day, 7 days a week.
7.b.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
A psychiatrist can be available either on site or on 
call.
3.N. 8. An initial medical and/or psychological 
necessity determination, establishing 
the need for partial hospitalization, is 
received upon admission and certifies:
8.3.N. PH
a. Inpatient care woul d be necessary if 
partial hospitalization was not 
provided.
8.a.3.N. PH
b. Services will be provided under the 
care of a physician or advanced 
practice provider.
8.b.3.N. PH
c. Services are provided under a written 
plan of care.
8.c.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
In some situations, it is required that a physician 
or advanced practice provider certify that the 
person served would require inpatient care if 
the partial hospitalizations services were not 
provided.
8.b. Face-to-face assessment and services can be 
provided when necessary.
Examples
Medical and/or psychological necessity may 
include the following:
n Identified need for crisis stabilization or treat-
ment of partially stabilized mental health 
disorders.
n Evidence of psychiatric symptoms that cause 
significant impairment in day-to-day social, 
vocational, and/or educational functioning.
n Indication of the person’s physical and intel-
lectual capacity to actively participate in all 
aspects of the therapeutic program.
n Inability to achieve sufficient clinical gains 
within an outpatient setting. Severity of pre-

SECTION 3.N. PARTIAL HOSPITALIZATION
252 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
senting symptoms is such that success in 
outpatient treatment is doubtful.
n Readiness for discharge from an inpatient set-
ting, but needing daily monitoring, support, 
and ongoing therapeutic interventions.
3.N. 9. An initial assessment of the person 
served:
9.3.N. PH
a. Includes:
9.a.3.N. PH
(1) A physical examination:
9.a.(1)3.N. PH
(a) Completed within 24 hours 
of admission.
9.a.(1)(a)3.N. PH
(b) Completed by a qualified 
licensed healthcare 
practitioner.
9.a.(1)(b)3.N. PH

9.a.(2)3.N. PH
(3) A nursing assessment.
9.a.(3)3.N. PH
(4) A skills assessment conducted 
by an activity, occupational, or 
rehabilitation therapist, when 
appropriate.
9.a.(4)3.N. PH
b. Is conducted within 24 hours of 
admission.
9.b.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
This standard is related to Standards 2.B.12. and 
2.B.13., with the clarification of ensuring that the 
physical examination is included and identifying 
the timeframe for completion.
9.a.(4) When the person served presents with 
specific needs pertaining skill deficits, the pro-
gram ensures that the assessment is conducted 
by the appropriate specialist. 
3.N. 10. The person-centered plan is:
10.3.N. PH
a. Completed within seven days of 
admission.
10.a.3.N. PH
b. Reviewed:
10.b.3.N. PH
(1) When major changes occur in 
treatment.
10.b.(1)3.N. PH
(2) At least every two weeks.
10.b.(2)3.N. PH
(3) Periodically by a physician or 
advanced practice provider.
10.b.(3)3.N. PH
  
3.N. 11. Access to primary medical care is avail-
able as needed.
11.3.N. PH
  
3.N. 12. The majority of scheduled program 
hours consist of therapeutic services.
12.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
Therapeutic services may vary according to the 
age, needs, or individual development or com-
prehension level of each person served.
3.N. 13. Therapeutic services include at least 
three of the following:
13.3.N. PH
a. Individual psychotherapy.
13.a.3.N. PH

13.b.3.N. PH

13.c.3.N. PH

h. Activity therapy.
13.h.3.N. PH

are meetings at least weekly with the

case manager.
15.3.N. PH
Intent Statements
These meetings may be in person, via conference

Such therapeutic activities could include act-

served to move to a greater level of personal inde-
pendence. The provision of therapeutic activities 
may be limited by specific funding sources to 
exclude those activities that are primarily recre-
ational and diversionary or provide only a level 
of functional support that does not treat the seri-
ous presenting psychiatric symptoms that are 
currently placing the person served at risk.

19.3.N. PH
a. Medically indicated.
19.a.3.N. PH

O. Residential Treatment 
Description
Residential treatment programs are organized 
and staffed to provide both general and special-
ized nonhospital-based interdisciplinary services 
24 hours a day, 7 days a week for persons with 
behavioral health or co-occurring needs, includ-
ing intellectual or developmental disabilities. 
Residential treatment programs provide environ-
ments in which the persons served reside and 
receive services from personnel who are trained 
in the delivery of services for persons with behav-
ioral health disorders or related problems. These 
services are provided in a safe, trauma-informed, 
recovery-focused milieu designed to integrate the 
person served back into the community and 
living independently whenever possible. The 
program involves the family or other supports 
in services whenever possible.
Residential treatment programs may include 
domestic violence treatment homes, nonhospital 
addiction treatment centers, intermediate care 
facilities, psychiatric treatment centers, or other 
nonmedical settings.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
residential treatment program must apply the 
standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition to the 
standards in this subsection.
3.O. 1. The program provides active treatment 
seven days a week that, based on the 
needs of the persons served, consists of 
services in each of the following areas:
1.3.O. Residential tx
a. Treatment services, including:
1.a.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Individual counseling/therapy.
1.a.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Group counseling/therapy.
1.a.(2)3.O. Residential tx
(3) Family/support system 
counseling/therapy.
1.a.(3)3.O. Residential tx
b. Adjunct therapies.
1.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Psychoeducation.
1.c.3.O. Residential tx
d. Skill-building activities.
1.d.3.O. Residential tx
e. Community integration.
1.e.3.O. Residential tx

SECTION 3.O. RESIDENTIAL TREATMENT
254 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
f. Social activities.
1.f.3.O. Residential tx
g. Recreational activities.
1.g.3.O. Residential tx
h. Spiritual activities.
1.h.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
Not all listed services must be provided to each 
person served, but the program should have the 
capacity to provide each of these as needed, and 
each day should include activities designed to 
meet the needs of the persons served as defined 
in their individual person-centered plans. Some 
services may be provided off-site.
Examples
1.b. Examples of adjunct therapies may be art 
therapy, occupational therapy, equine therapy, 
music and other expressive arts therapies, exp-
eriential therapy, animal-assisted therapy, 
drumming, and adventure-based therapy.
1.c. Psychoeducation may include areas such as 
wellness, recovery, resiliency, and employment.
1.d. Skill-building activities could be in the areas 
of employment, nutrition, self-advocacy, and 
activities of daily living.
3.O. 2. Services are provided by a coordinated 
treatment team that includes, at a 
minimum: 
2.3.O. Residential tx
a. A qualified behavioral health practi -
tioner who coordinates the plan of 
the person served.
2.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Providers of appropriate medical 
support services.
2.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Assigned residential personnel.
2.c.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
It should be evident that various team members 
interact with persons served and communicate 
with each other about their interactions and 
plans for providing individual services to persons 
served.
2.a. See the Glossary for the definition of qualified 
behavioral health practitioner.
Examples
2.b. Medical support services may be provided by 
consulting or contracted professionals.
2.c. Because residential treatment programs serve 
persons with varying needs, the specific qualifi-
cations and credentials of personnel are 
determined based on the specific needs of the 
persons served and the structure of the residen-
tial treatment program.
3.O. 3. The program provides on-site personnel 
support 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
3.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
Personnel should be on the same campus and 
have the ability to quickly respond to emergen-
cies. Some services or support may be provided 
by organizations outside of the program, such as 
in a correctional setting. 
If the program serves children or adolescents, 
personnel are on site 24 hours a day, 7 days a 
week.
Whether on-site personnel remain awake at all 
times will be dependent on the unique needs of 
the persons being served.
3.O. 4. A risk assessment for each person served 
is conducted at the time of admission.
4.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
This standard is related to Standard 2.B.12.d.; 
however, the expectation for a residential 
treatment program is that this assessment is 
conducted at the time of admission and not 
delayed until a later time when a full assessment 
may be conducted. Refer to Standard 2.C.4. if risks 
are identified.
3.O. 5. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, the program provides or 
arranges for:
5.3.O. Residential tx
a. Healthcare services.
5.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Pharmaceutical services.
5.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Social services.
5.c.3.O. Residential tx
d. Educational services.
5.d.3.O. Residential tx
e. Other services, as appropriate.
5.e.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
The frequency and intensity of these services are 
directly related to the length of stay in the pro-
gram. In long-term residential programs, this 
would include regular health monitoring and 
response to findings. The program should also 
ensure access to medications needed by persons 

SECTION 3.O. RESIDENTIAL TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 255
served in addition to any prescribed in the 
program.
Examples
5.a. Laboratory services for drug screening or 
routine blood work could be examples of health-
care services.
5.c. Social services may be related to areas such as 
benefits, child welfare, and domestic violence.
5.d. School-age children must receive educa-
tional services when treatment impacts their 
school attendance.
3.O. 6. The program provides the following 
community living components:
6.3.O. Residential tx
a. A written daily schedule of activities 
that contribute to the wellness of the 
persons served.
6.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. A homelike and comfortable setting.
6.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Access to nutritious meals and 
snacks.
6.c.3.O. Residential tx
d. Personal privacy.
6.d.3.O. Residential tx
e. Opportunities to participate in activi -
ties that would be found in a home.
6.e.3.O. Residential tx
f. Individual possessions and 
decorations.
6.f.3.O. Residential tx
g. Regular meetings between the per -
sons served and program personnel.
6.g.3.O. Residential tx
Examples
6.a. The written daily schedule includes descrip-
tions of the activities offered.
6.b. In a correctional setting, this may not be pos-
sible and this standard would not be applicable. 
6.c. The persons served have access to at least 
three meals and one snack per day.
6.e. The program encourages all persons served 
to take increasing responsibility for cooperative 
operation of the household.
6.f. These items are consistent with the personal 
choices and needs of the persons served, except 
for items that are contraindicated by their per-
son-centered plans.
6.e. Activities may include typical household 
duties such as food preparation, making the bed, 
and routine cleaning.
6.g. These could be community meetings or 
house meetings for collaboratively discussing 
issues such as program operations, problems, 
plans, or the use of program resources.
3.O. 7. To facilitate effective community inte-
gration, the program demonstrates how, 
with the consent of the persons served, 
it engages members of the family and/or 
support system in program services.
7.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
The program should seek to reduce barriers to 
participation in the program for family members 
and/or members of the support system of the 
person served.
Examples
The program might consider the use of commu-
nication technologies such as Zoom, Microsoft 
Teams, or FaceTime as ways to facilitate partici-
pation. The program might also use online 
training resources to extend the knowledge of 
behavioral health conditions with family mem-
bers/support systems.
3.O. 8. The program consults with a dietitian 
regarding its food services to meet the 
nutritional and dietary needs of the 
persons served.
8.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
This standard does not require that the program 
employ a dietitian; rather, at a minimum, the pro-
gram should seek consultation to ensure that 
typical meals meet nutritional requirements for 
the population served and that there are meals 
designed for persons with special dietary needs 
or restrictions.
3.O. 9. The program’s physical facilities provide:
9.3.O. Residential tx
a. Personal privacy.
9.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Security of:
9.b.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Persons served.
9.b.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Personnel.
9.b.(2)3.O. Residential tx
(3) Personal belongings.
9.b.(3)3.O. Residential tx
c. Space for:
9.c.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Group interactions.
9.c.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Quiet activities.
9.c.(2)3.O. Residential tx

(3) Family or other guests.
9.c.(3)3.O. Residential tx
(4) Therapeutic activities.
9.c.(4)3.O. Residential tx
(5) Cultural and/or spiritual 
activities.
9.c.(5)3.O. Residential tx
(6) Meals.
9.c.(6)3.O. Residential tx
(7) Recreation.
9.c.(7)3.O. Residential tx
(8) Based on gender, age, and needs, 
separate areas for:
9.c.(8)3.O. Residential tx
(a) Sleeping.
9.c.(8)(a)3.O. Residential tx
(b) Hygiene.
9.c.(8)(b)3.O. Residential tx
d. Access to an outdoor setting, if 
possible.
9.d.3.O. Residential tx
Examples
9.c. The program is not required to maintain 
exclusive spaces for each of these, but appropriate 
areas should be provided and available as needed.
9.c.(2) Quiet activities may include de-escalation.
9.c.(8)(a) Separation based on gender does not 
need to occur when parents and children sleep 
in the same area. When serving children or ado-
lescents, age also includes developmental level.
3.O. 10. To ensure the safety of persons served 
and personnel, the program implements 
written procedures for searches:
10.3.O. Residential tx
a. Of persons served.
10.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Of belongings.
10.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Of the physical facility.
10.c.3.O. Residential tx
d. That:
10.d.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Preserve privacy.
10.d.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Preserve dignity.
10.d.(2)3.O. Residential tx
(3) Are sensitive to potential trauma 
of the persons served.
10.d.(3)3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
Residential treatment programs ensure that 
personnel and persons served remain safe in 
the program, without introduction of weapons, 
drugs, or alcohol into the milieu. Personnel are 
adequately trained in the search process in order 
to respect the dignity and privacy of the persons 
served. Programs may find it necessary to 
conduct searches of the persons served, their 
belongings, or the physical facility based on 
observed behaviors of persons served or others.
Examples
10.d. Searches are conducted by personnel with 
whom the person served feels comfortable. This 
may include specific protocols based on a per-
son’s gender identity, as well as consideration 
of religious or cultural factors, prior history of 
trauma, and/or the person’s age. In order to assist 
the persons served to retain some feeling of 
control, the program could seek permission 
to conduct searches of individuals or their 
belongings. 
3.O. 11. The program implements written 
procedures that address:
11.3.O. Residential tx
a. Visitation.
11.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Mail.
11.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Telephone use.
11.c.3.O. Residential tx
d. Use of personal electronics.
11.d.3.O. Residential tx
Examples
11.d. Programs are often reluctant to allow the 
use of personal electronic devices. However, the 
use of these devices can have a positive influence 
on the person served, particularly the use of apps 
targeting recovery and self-regulation. Programs 
could attempt to strike a balance, rather than 
simply prohibiting the use of electronic devices.
3.O. 12. All direct service personnel receive 
competency-based training in:
12.3.O. Residential tx
a. First aid.
12.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).
12.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. The use of emergency equipment.
12.c.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
Licensed nursing staff and physicians are exempt 
from this requirement as their professional train-
ing and licensing ensure competency.
Examples
12.c. Emergency equipment could include auto-
matic external defibrillators, eyewashing stations, 
splints, tourniquets, and EpiPens®.
3.O. 13. The program reduces barriers to inter-
action between the persons served and 
personnel.
13.3.O. Residential tx

SECTION 3.O. RESIDENTIAL TREATMENT
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 257
Intent Statements
The program should make efforts to reduce 
both attitudinal and physical barriers that create 
distance and separation between program per-
sonnel and the persons served. Personnel should 
be engaged with and accessible to the persons 
served, rather than remaining behind glass barri-
ers or in an office or lounge where the persons 
served cannot interact with them.
3.O. 14. The program provides documented, 
competency-based training to direct 
service personnel:
14.3.O. Residential tx
a. At:
14.a.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Orientation.
14.a.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Regular intervals.
14.a.(2)3.O. Residential tx
b. That includes, at a minimum, the 
following topics:
14.b.3.O. Residential tx
(1) De-escalation techniques.
14.b.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Risk assessment.
14.b.(2)3.O. Residential tx
(3) Trauma-informed approaches.
14.b.(3)3.O. Residential tx
Examples
Direct service personnel are those personnel 
who provide services or supports that require 
direct interaction with the persons served; e.g., 
therapists, clinicians, aides, case managers, etc.
3.O. 15. To facilitate seamless service delivery, 
the program:
15.3.O. Residential tx
a. Identifies resources for ongoing care 
of the person served.
15.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Engages and integrates referral 
resources into the program.
15.b.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
To ensure that the program successfully places 
persons served in the next level of care after 
residential treatment, the program develops a 
comprehensive post-discharge network of ser-
vices. The program should integrate this network 
of services as fully as possible into the program to 
strengthen the likelihood of a successful transi-
tion into the next level of care.
3.O. 16. The program demonstrates efforts to 
integrate with the surrounding commu-
nity to:
16.3.O. Residential tx
a. Reduce stigma.
16.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Enhance safety for the persons 
served.
16.b.3.O. Residential tx
c. Facilitate community integration for 
the persons served.
16.c.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
The residential program strives to be a good 
neighbor and to develop and maintain positive, 
cooperative relationships with neighbors and the 
surrounding community.
Examples
16.b. The program might establish relationships 
or a calling tree with neighbors who can be con-
tacted if a person served leaves the residential 
facility without permission.
3.O. 17. A review of the person-centered plan for 
each person served in a residential treat
-
ment program:
17.3.O. Residential tx
a. Occurs at least once a month.
17.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. Is documented.
17.b.3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
The intent of this standard is to ensure that the 
person-centered plan is reviewed frequently 
enough to track progress made toward identified 
goals and to note any new needs or interests.
Examples
The review may be documented with updates or 
changes to the plan, with a plan update docu-
ment, or through progress notes. The program 
demonstrates that it is adjusting to ongoing 
assessments and emerging issues of the person 
served.
3.O. 18. The program establishes collaborative 
relationships with resources in the 
community to facilitate community 
integration for the persons served.
18.3.O. Residential tx
Examples
Relationships are established with community 
partners to provide opportunities for persons 
served to participate in such things as volunteer 

SECTION 3.P. SPECIALIZED OR TREATMENT FOSTER CARE

activities, fitness and sports activities, support 
groups, and recreational activities.
3.O. 19. The program:
19.3.O. Residential tx
a. Identifies an indicator to measure 
engagement of the persons served 
in ongoing services post discharge.
19.a.3.O. Residential tx
b. At least annually addresses:
19.b.3.O. Residential tx
(1) Performance in relationship to 
an established target.
19.b.(1)3.O. Residential tx
(2) Trends.
19.b.(2)3.O. Residential tx
(3) Actions for improvement.
19.b.(3)3.O. Residential tx
(4) Results of performance improve -
ment plans.
19.b.(4)3.O. Residential tx
(5) Necessary education and training 
of:
19.b.(5)3.O. Residential tx
(a) Persons served.
19.b.(5)(a)3.O. Residential tx
(b) Families/support systems.
19.b.(5)(b)3.O. Residential tx
(c) Personnel.
19.b.(5)(c)3.O. Residential tx
(d) Other stakeholders, as 
appropriate.
19.b.(5)(d)3.O. Residential tx
Intent Statements
This standard is related to the standards in 
Sections 1.L. Performance Measurement 
and Management and 1.M. Performance 
Improvement.
P. Specialized or 
Treatment Foster Care
Description
Specialized or treatment foster care programs 
use a community-based treatment approach for 
children/youth with emotional and/or behavioral 
issues. Children/youth who participate in the 
program may also have documented reports of 
maltreatment, involvement with juvenile justice, 
and/or co-occurring disorders. Intensive, clini-
cally based treatment that is child/youth centered 
and family focused is delivered through an inte-
grated team approach that individualizes services 
for each child/youth. Treatment foster parents are 
trained, supervised, and supported by program 
personnel and they fulfill a primary role in thera-
peutic interventions. Program personnel monitor 
the child’s/youth’s progress in treatment and 
provide adjunctive services in accordance with 
the individualized plan and program design. 
The program’s goal is to provide clinically effec-
tive treatment to children/youth so they may 
return to their family or alternative community 
placement and avoid being removed from a 
community setting or placed in an inpatient 
or residential treatment setting. 
The program may also be called intensive foster 
care, therapeutic family services, or therapeutic 
foster care.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
specialized or treatment foster care program 
must apply the standards in Sections 1 and 2 
in addition to the standards in this subsection.
3.P. 1. The program implements a process for 
identifying, locating, and engaging fam
-
ily members, as appropriate, in services.
1.3.P . STFC
Examples
The program demonstrates efforts to engage 
family, including extended family, in services. 
This may include the use of internet-based search 
services to locate family members.

3.P. 2. The program provides or arranges for 
documented, competency-based train-
ing to meet the identified needs of the 
children/youth served: 
2.3.P. STFC
a. To:
2.a.3.P. STFC
(1) Personnel.
2.a.(1)3.P. STFC
(2) Specialized or treatment foster 
care providers.
2.a.(2)3.P. STFC
b. At:
2.b.3.P. STFC
(1) Orientation.
2.b.(1)3.P. STFC
(2) Regular intervals.
2.b.(2)3.P. STFC
c. That covers:
2.c.3.P. STFC
(1) Attachment theory. 
2.c.(1)3.P. STFC
(2) Trauma. 
2.c.(2)3.P. STFC
(3) Child/youth growth and 
development.
2.c.(3)3.P. STFC
(4) Behavior management skills.
2.c.(4)3.P. STFC
(5) Learning theory.
2.c.(5)3.P. STFC
(6) Cultural competency and 
diversity.
2.c.(6)3.P. STFC
(7) The effects of placement on 
children/youth.
2.c.(7)3.P. STFC
(8) Applicable legal issues.
2.c.(8)3.P. STFC
(9) Communication skills.
2.c.(9)3.P. STFC
(10) Required medications and/or 
medical services.
2.c.(10)3.P . STFC
(11) Other specialized training as 
needed.
2.c.(11)3.P. STFC
Intent Statements
The content of each subject area should consider 
the specific training needs of personnel and 
specialized/treatment foster care providers. The 
training provided to personnel and foster care 
providers may not be identical.
Examples
2.c.(3) Training may include brain development 
and social, emotional, and cognitive 
development.
2.c.(4) Training may include conflict resolution 
and management of violent, aggressive, or sexu-
alized behaviors. 
2.c.(6) Training about cultural competency may 
be specific and relevant to the diversity of the 
children and families served in the program and/
or introduce or strengthen the concept of cultural 
humility, which includes both knowledge about 
various cultures and self-reflection to discover 
how one’s own biases and perceptions may affect 
interpersonal relationships.
2.c.(8) Legal issues may include court processes if 
the program serves a child welfare/dependency 
population or children involved in the juvenile 
justice system. 
2.c.(11) Specialized training needs may include 
health and nutrition, the need for and use of 
assistive technology, substance abuse or mental 
health issues, or delinquency.

3.b.3.P. STFC

provided.
3.c.3.P . STFC
Examples
One approach to training would be to address 
relevant topics pre-service and in-service.
3.P. 4. The program provides access to profes-
sionals trained in child/youth and family 
care, based on the needs of each person 
served, including:
4.3.P . STFC

4.a.3.P. STFC

4.e.3.P. STFC

4.f.3.P. STFC
g. Medical personnel.
4.g.3.P. STFC
h. Other specialists, as appropriate.
4.h.3.P. STFC
Examples
4.g. May include a nurse, physical therapist, or 
speech-language pathologist, based on the needs 
of the child/youth served.

3.P. 5. A referral network is established for the 
following:
5.3.P. STFC
a. Crisis intervention services.
5.a.3.P. STFC
b. Respite care.
5.b.3.P. STFC
c. Medical care.
5.c.3.P. STFC
d. Other services to meet the needs of 
the children/youth served.
5.d.3.P. STFC
Intent Statements
The program maintains up-to-date contact 
information for other service providers currently 
working with the children/youth served and for 
providers of services that may potentially be 
needed. 
3.P. 6. The children/youth served have opportu-
nities to participate, as appropriate, in:
6.3.P. STFC
a. Community activities.
6.a.3.P. STFC
b. Cultural activities.
6.b.3.P. STFC
c. Recreational activities.
6.c.3.P. STFC
d. Spiritual activities.
6.d.3.P. STFC
Intent Statements
Specialized or treatment foster care programs are 
designed to provide treatment within a commu-
nity setting to foster the development of and/or 
strengthen the child’s/youth’s skills to allow the 
child/youth to safely remain in the community 
and avoid a placement outside of the community. 
The program supports the foster treatment family 
to provide opportunities for the child/youth to 
access various community activities and to 
ensure that the child’s/youth’s cultural heritage 
is honored and preserved. 
3.P. 7. If the program selects specialized or 
treatment foster care providers, it 
implements a comprehensive plan 
for recruitment, selection, and main-
tenance that:
7.3.P. STFC
a. Is reflective of the larger community 
that the program serves.
7.a.3.P. STFC
b. Includes a broad selection of families 
to ensure that the needs of the 
children/youth served are met.
7.b.3.P. STFC
c. Meets the expressed criteria set in all 
applicable jurisdictional guidelines.
7.c.3.P. STFC
d. Includes procedures for the monitor -
ing of each home.
7.d.3.P. STFC
  
3.P. 8. If the program is engaged in child place-
ment activities, it has a comprehensive 
process for matching children/youth 
with available foster care providers that:
8.3.P . STFC
a. Considers the child’s/youth’s:
8.a.3.P. STFC
(1) Needs.
8.a.(1)3.P. STFC
(2) Strengths.
8.a.(2)3.P. STFC
(3) Preferences.
8.a.(3)3.P. STFC
b. Considers the foster care providers’ 
assessed:
8.b.3.P. STFC
(1) Skills.
8.b.(1)3.P. STFC
(2) Competencies.
8.b.(2)3.P. STFC
c. Includes an assessment of the appro -
priateness of the match, including:
8.c.3.P. STFC

Q. Student Counseling
Description
Student counseling programs serve as the 
primary behavioral health resource for higher 
education campus communities and their stu-
dents. Services are designed to provide students 
with an opportunity to develop personal insight, 
identify and solve problems, and implement 
positive strategies to better manage their lives 
both academically and personally. Services 
include individual, family, and/or group coun-
seling, prevention, education, and outreach. 
In addition to working directly with students, 
program goals are realized through outreach, 
partnerships, and consultation initiatives with 
faculty, staff, parents, students’ internships sites, 
or other educational entities or community 
partners.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
student counseling program must apply the 
standards in Section 1. In addition, Section 2 
(please consult grid on page 112 for applicabil-
ity of standards in this section) must be applied 
along with standards from this subsection. 
3.Q. 1. For each person served, a written person-
centered plan:
1.3.Q. Student Counseling
a. Is developed with the active partici -
pation of the person served.
1.a.3.Q. Student Counseling
b. Is prepared using the following infor -
mation relative to the person served:
1.b.3.Q. Student Counseling
(1) Relevant medical history.
1.b.(1)3.Q. Student Counseling
(2) Relevant behavioral health 
information.
1.b.(2)3.Q. Student Counseling
(3) Relevant social information.
1.b.(3)3.Q. Student Counseling
(4) Information on current and previ -
ous direct services and supports.
1.b.(4)3.Q. Student Counseling
(5) Other applicable information.
1.b.(5)3.Q. Student Counseling
c. Is based on the person’s:
1.c.3.Q. Student Counseling
(1) Strengths.
1.c.(1)3.Q. Student Counseling
(2) Needs.
1.c.(2)3.Q. Student Counseling

SECTION 3.Q. STUDENT COUNSELING
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 263
(3) Abilities.
1.c.(3)3.Q. Student Counseling
(4) Preferences.
1.c.(4)3.Q. Student Counseling
d. Is reflective of the person’s:
1.d.3.Q. Student Counseling
(1) Desired outcomes.
1.d.(1)3.Q. Student Counseling
(2) Cultural background and 
diversity.
1.d.(2)3.Q. Student Counseling

1.g.3.Q. Student Counseling

person served.

the person served.

address only the immediate needs of the person 
served.

whether the threat is directed toward them, an 
individual, or the campus.

SECTION 3.R. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNITIES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 265
R. Therapeutic 
Communities
Description
Therapeutic communities are highly structured 
residential environments or continuums of care 
in which the primary goals are the treatment of 
substance abuse or other behavioral health needs 
and the fostering of personal growth leading to 
personal accountability. The program addresses 
the broad range of needs identified by the person 
served. The therapeutic community employs 
community-imposed consequences and earned 
privileges as part of the recovery and growth 
process. In addition to daily seminars, group 
counseling, and individual activities, the persons 
served are assigned responsibilities within the 
therapeutic community setting. Participants and 
staff members act as facilitators, emphasizing 
personal responsibility for one’s own life and  
self-improvement. The therapeutic community 
emphasizes the integration of an individual 
within the person’s community, and progress is 
measured within the context of that therapeutic 
community’s expectation.
Applicable Standards
An organization seeking accreditation for a 
therapeutic community program must apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2 in addition 
to the standards in this subsection.
3.R. 1. The therapeutic community model views 
the community as the modality for indi-
vidual change—i.e., the community as 
the healer. The program’s written plan 
identifies the therapeutic community 
model through:
1.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
a. Use of the mutual-help principle.
1.a.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
b. Program structure.
1.b.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
c. Rules.
1.c.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
d. Schedules.
1.d.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
e. Responsibilities.
1.e.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
f. Behavioral expectations.
1.f.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
g. Role modeling.
1.g.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
h. Feedback mechanisms.
1.h.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
i. Therapeutic learning interventions.
1.i.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
j. Therapeutic work assignments.
1.j.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
Intent Statements
The treatment community itself is viewed as the 
modality for individual change. All members of 
the community, including staff members and 
peers, role model appropriate behaviors for other 
community members in program functions and 
activities.
1.a. Mutual help, also known as self-help or peer 
support, requires the persons served to actively 
participate in their own treatment and the treat-
ment of others using the community as the 
method.
3.R. 2. The program demonstrates use of the 
mutual-help principle through evidence 
of the following:
2.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
a. Adherence to program rules.
2.a.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
b. Adherence to existing schedules.
2.b.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
c. Adherence to behavioral expecta -
tions of the community.
2.c.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
d. Acceptance of responsibility for:
2.d.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(1) Self.
2.d.(1)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(2) Applicable others.
2.d.(2)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(3) The health of the community 
itself.
2.d.(3)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
e. Positively influencing other mem -
bers of the community by teaching 
and by role modeling appropriate 
behaviors in:
2.e.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(1) Program functions.
2.e.(1)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(2) Activities.
2.e.(2)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
(3) The community itself.
2.e.(3)3.R. Therapeutic Communities
f. Providing honest feedback and 
guidance to other members of the 
community that leads to interven-
tions occurring in a community 
forum.
2.f.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
g. Demonstration of empathy and 
genuine concern for other members 
of the community.
2.g.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
Intent Statements
This standard relates to the active involvement of 
the persons served in their treatment process and 

SECTION 3.R. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNITIES
266 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
in that of their peers. An individual’s growth and 
change are the product of that individual’s own 
motivation and commitment, with help from 
others engaged in the same process.
3.R. 3. The program’s hours of operation meet 
the needs of the persons served.
3.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
Intent Statements
In a correction setting, the hours of operation 
may be determined and limited by institutional 
rules.
3.R. 4. The treatment environment is conducive 
to and supportive of recovery.
4.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
  
3.R. 5. When a program is provided in a residen-
tial setting, there is qualified personnel 
available to respond 24
 hours a day, 
7 days a week.
5.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
Intent Statements
Personnel should be on the same campus and 
have the ability to respond to emergencies 
quickly. If the program serves children or adoles-
cents, personnel are on site 24 hours a day, 7 days 
a week. In a correctional setting, services or sup-
port outside of program hours may be provided 
by personnel outside of the organization or pro-
gram seeking accreditation or may be provided 
by institutional staff.
Whether on-site personnel remain awake at all 
times will be dependent on the unique needs of 
the persons being served.
3.R. 6. Whenever possible, peer mentors are 
used as credible role models in the 
program.
6.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
Examples
In a correctional setting, this may include 
inmates who have completed the correctional 
therapeutic community and are used as peer 
mentors to teach, facilitate, and role model for 
newer participants in the therapeutic community.
3.R. 7. Based on the needs of the person served, 
the program provides, either directly or 
through referral, services that seek to 
assist the person served with:
7.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
a. Substance abuse issues.
7.a.3.R. Therapeutic Communities

7.d.3.R. Therapeutic Communities

7.e.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
f. Cognitive functioning.
7.f.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
g. Emotional functioning.
7.g.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
h. Building of self-esteem and self-

7.h.3.R. Therapeutic Communities

7.t.3.R. Therapeutic Communities
