Applicable Standards
If an organization has a criminal justice edu-
cational component in its program, Standards 
13.–14. also apply.
5.E. 13. The curriculum-based program compo-
nent for each person served:
13.5.E.CJ
a. Addresses issues specific to the 
person’s individual needs.
13.a.5.E.CJ
b. Is consistent with the person’s 
cognitive and learning abilities.
13.b.5.E.CJ
c. Is consistent with the program’s 
philosophy of treatment.
13.c.5.E.CJ
d. Includes provisions for:
13.d.5.E.CJ
(1) Evaluation.
13.d.(1)5.E.CJ
(2) Group instruction.
13.d.(2)5.E.CJ
(3) Individual instruction.
13.d.(3)5.E.CJ
e. Meets applicable federal, provincial, 
and state requirements.
13.e.5.E.CJ
Intent Statements
13.b. The intent of this standard is to ensure 
that the assessment has included cognitive, 
behavioral, and learning abilities and that reading 
materials, assignments, and the requirements 
for participation take into consideration the 
learning abilities and styles of the person served. 
This standard includes ensuring that reasonable 
accommodations are available for persons with 
special educational needs.

SECTION 5.F. EATING DISORDERS
326 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
13.c. Because many of the criminal justice educa-
tional services are provided as part of or within a 
treatment program, this standard encourages the 
organization to ensure that the educational plan 
for each person served is consistent with the 
philosophy of the treatment program.
Examples
Based on the needs of the persons served and 
the resources available, the organization is 
encouraged to include the following topics in 
its educational programs:
n Substance abuse treatment, relapse preven-
tion, and recovery.
n Physical health issues or consequences and 
communicable diseases.
n Community resources and community 
integration.
n The possible relationship between substance 
abuse and/or mental illness and criminal 
behavior.
n Violence prevention.
n Family reunification.
n Parenting skills, when applicable.
n Culture-specific issues.
n Interpersonal and relationship skills.
n Communication skills.
n Life-skills training.
n Job readiness.
n Problem solving.
n Conflict resolution.
n Anger management.
5.E. 14. Based on the needs of the person served, 
the educational program addresses the 
development of:
14.5.E.CJ
a. Community living skills.
14.a.5.E.CJ
b. Social skills.
14.b.5.E.CJ
c. Social supports.
14.c.5.E.CJ
d. Vocational skills.
14.d.5.E.CJ
  
F. Eating Disorders
Description
Standards for eating disorder programs apply to 
residential, inpatient, and partial hospitalization 
programs that offer treatment to patients under 
the supervision of a licensed healthcare profes-
sional who has access to a licensed physician. 
Patients served in these programs have been 
diagnosed with eating disorders according to 
the current DSM, ICD-9 or ICD-10, including 
Anorexia Nervosa, Bulimia Nervosa, and Eating 
Disorders Not Otherwise Specified. Symptom 
management and interruption requires an 
intensity of service delivery that is beyond an 
outpatient a level of care.
The standards consider the individual’s biopsy-
chosocial needs and strengths as well as the needs 
and strengths of family members. Services maxi-
mize the person’s ability to function effectively 
within the family, school, and community envi-
ronment and to achieve and maintain an optimal 
state of health to enhance quality of life. Services 
provided also consider any culturally specific 
issues relevant to the individual and family/
caregivers as appropriate. Services to persons 
with eating disorders can be provided in a variety 
of settings and are not necessarily exclusive pro-
grams that serve only this particular population. 
However, programs serving persons with eating 
disorders within larger general medical or psy-
chiatric units, similar to exclusive programs, 
must demonstrate programming that is specialty- 
and evidence-based and demonstrate that staff 
are specialty-trained and competent to provide 
eating disorder treatment. Exclusive programs 
and programs within larger general psychiatric 
or medical units must also demonstrate that 
services are designed based on the needs and 
expectations of the persons served and their legal 
guardians/caregivers. For example, they can be 
informed by the World Wide Charter on Action 
for Eating Disorders (www.aedweb.org/get-
involved/advocacy). The charter describes the 

SECTION 5.F. EATING DISORDERS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 327
following rights of persons with eating disorders 
and carers:
n Right to communication and partnership with 
healthcare professionals
n Right to comprehensive assessment and treat-
ment planning
n Right to accessible, high-quality, fully funded 
specialized care
n Right to respectful, fully informed, age-
appropriate, safe levels of care
n Right of carer(s) to be informed, valued, and 
respected as a treatment resource
n Right of carer(s) to accessible, appropriate 
support and education resources
Some examples of the quality results desired by 
different stakeholders of these services include:
n Replacing the person’s connection with the 
eating disorder with satisfying, supportive 
and meaningful relationships and the use of 
healthy coping strategies.
n Effective transitions between levels of care or 
transition to community living.
n Development of an effective and efficient 
network of community support services 
including access to therapies, medical 
supports, and other school, work, and 
community-based resources.
n Achievement of goals in health, education, 
work, and activities of daily living.
n Personal and family development.
n Maintenance of recovery and improved 
functioning.
Applicable Standards
Organizations seeking accreditation as an 
eating disorders program must apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2, the core 
program standards in Inpatient Treatment (3.I.), 
Partial Hospitalization (3.N.), or Residential 
Treatment (3.O.), and the standards in this 
section.
NOTE: An organization seeking accreditation for an 
Eating Disorders program for Children/Adolescents 
(EDCA) must also apply the standards in Section 5.C.

SECTION 5.F. EATING DISORDERS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 329
Intent Statements
6.a. Care is delivered by persons knowledgeable 
or experienced in the area of working with per-
sons with eating disorders.
Examples
Current evidence in eating disorder research 
supports the use of cognitive-behavioral treat-
ment (CBT) and interpersonal therapy (IPT) for 
adults with bulimia nervosa, and family-based 
treatment for adolescents (under 19 years of age 
with less than three years duration of illness) with 
anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa, as well as 
the use of the principles of CBT and supportive 
clinical management (SCM) for adults.
6.b.(3) If the person served chooses to not have 
family involved in the treatment process, this 
standard would not be applicable.
6.b.(6) Milieu therapy includes day-to-day milieu 
management and support.
5.F. 7. The program implements nutritional

7.5.F. ED
a. Promote growth and development 
in the applicable age group(s) of the 
person served.
7.a.5.F. ED
b. Support regular and consistent 
weight gain (or loss, when 
applicable).
7.b.5.F. ED
c. Measure improvement in symptom -
atic eating behavior and/or urges.
7.c.5.F. ED
d. Include:
7.d.5.F. ED
(1) A physician to prescribe the diet.
7.d.(1)5.F. ED
(2) A registered dietician to:
7.d.(2)5.F. ED
(a) Provide the following:
7.d.(2)(a)5.F. ED
(i) Assessment.
7.d.(2)(a)(i)5.F. ED
(ii) Education.
7.d.(2)(a)(ii)5.F. ED
(iii) Counseling.
7.d.(2)(a)(iii)5.F. ED
(b) Design, implement and 
manage safe and effective 
nutrition-related strategies 
to:
7.d.(2)(b)5.F. ED
(i) Enhance growth and 
development.
7.d.(2)(b)(i)5.F. ED
(ii) Promote recovery from 
disordered eating.
7.d.(2)(b)(ii)5.F. ED
(iii) Reduce disturbances in 
body image.
7.d.(2)(b)(iii)5.F. ED
(iv) Promote lifelong health.
7.d.(2)(b)(iv)5.F. ED
Examples
7.b. Weight loss may be applicable when the 
person served has concurrent obesity.
7.c. May include restricting, binge eating, 
purging, etc.

served, the program’s outcomes

8.5.F . ED
a. Regular and consistent weight gain 
(or loss, when applicable).
8.a.5.F. ED

tomatic eating behavior and/or 
urges.
8.b.5.F. ED
c. Eating disorder diagnostic symptoms 
regarding preoccupation with:
8.c.5.F. ED
(1) Weight.
8.c.(1)5.F. ED
(2) Shape.
8.c.(2)5.F. ED
(3) Body image.
8.c.(3)5.F. ED
d. Improvement as measured by the 
standardized eating disorder assess-
ments chosen to record admission 
and discharge status.
8.d.5.F. ED
e. When possible, measurement 
of outcomes at twelve months 
post discharge.
8.e.5.F. ED
Examples
8.b. Restricting, binging, purging.
8.d. Eating Disorder Examination Questionnaire 
(EDEQ)-6 and Eating Disorder Quality of Life. 
Programs are encouraged to routinely include 
additional measures of mood and other comor-
bid symptoms, such as the Beck Depression 
Inventory, at admission and discharge.

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
330 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
G. Integrated Primary 
Care
Description
Many behavioral healthcare programs strive to 
provide meaningful physical healthcare to holis-
tically address the needs of the persons served. 
Effectively integrating primary care into a behav-
ioral health program enhances the level of care 
provided and improves outcomes for the persons 
served.
A needs assessment of the population served, 
including behavioral health, physical health, and 
social health needs, guides the development of 
services, including screening practices and proto-
cols to optimize health outcomes for the persons 
served. In addition to the person served, the 
interdisciplinary team includes providers who 
can address needs in each of these areas through 
an integrated approach. The program provides 
(either directly or through collaboration and 
linkages) health promotion, care coordination, 
care management, individual and family support 
services, and referral to community and/or social 
support services.
In addition to monitoring and measuring the 
progress of individual persons served, the pro-
gram collects and analyzes data to manage and 
improve behavioral health, physical health, social 
health, and functional outcomes for the popula-
tion served.
Integrated Primary Care programs educate the 
persons served on strategies for wellness and  
self-management. The program provides cross-
training for personnel that addresses common 
conditions in the population served; how behav-
ioral health, physical health, and social health 
needs may affect each other; models of integrated 
care; and collaborative person-centered planning 
practices.
Leadership at both the program and organiza-
tional levels engage in efforts to continually 
improve collaborative care and champion 
integrated care.
Applicable Standards
Accreditation for Integrated Primary Care 
specialty designation is required for organiza-
tions seeking accreditation for a Health Home 
program. 
If an organization chooses to seek accreditation 
for Integrated Primary Care in conjunction with 
another core program in Section 3 or 4, the 
standards in this subsection must be applied 
in addition to the standards in Sections 1 and 
2 (please consult the grid on page 112 for 
applicability) and the core program standards 
in Section 3 or 4.
The Integrated Primary Care specialty designa-
tion cannot be sought in conjunction with the 
following programs: Assessment and Referral, 
Information Call Center, Comprehensive 
Suicide Prevention, Diversion/Intervention, 
Employee Assistance, and Prevention.
5.G. 1. Organizational leadership demonstrates 
decision-making that supports and pro-
motes primary care integration.
1.5.G. IPC
Examples
Organizational leadership can implement sup-
port for integration of primary care through a 
variety of mechanisms that may include strategic, 
operational, and organizational culture activities. 
This support is critical for creating a successful 
integrated care model that improves outcomes 
for persons served, reduces costs, and enhances 
satisfaction of personnel.
Examples of each might include:
Strategy
n Establish a Shared Vision: Leaders clearly 
articulate a compelling vision of whole-per-
son care. This involves developing a shared 
mission and values that unite physical and 
behavioral health providers around a com
-
mon purpose of integrated care. This vision 
is
 communicated consistently across the 
organization.
n Champion Integration: Effective leaders 
serve as champions of integration. This 
includes advocating for it internally with 
personnel and externally with community 

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 331
partners, payers, and policymakers. Leaders 
are continuously visible and vocal about their 
commitment to the initiative.
n Strategic Planning and Goal Setting: 
Care integration is a core component of 
the organization’s strategic plan. Leaders set 
goals related to integration, such as increasing 
co-located services or improving outcomes 
for persons served with co-occurring 
conditions.
Operational and Structural Support
n Allocate Resources: Leadership dedicates 
financial, technological, and human resources 
to care integration efforts. This includes 
funding for appropriate roles, technology 
upgrades, and training programs.
n Develop Aligned Metrics and Incentives: 
Leaders can demonstrate support by creating 
a system of shared accountability. This 
involves development of performance mea-
surement and management systems that have 
performance targets aligned with integrated 
care expectations for effectiveness, efficiency, 
experience/satisfaction, and access.
Cultural and Workforce Development
n Build a Culture of Trust and Respect: 
Leadership works to create an environment 
where providers from different disciplines feel 
valued and respected. Open communication 
and mutual understanding of each other’s 
roles and perspectives are encouraged.
n Invest in Training and Education: Leaders 
support training for personnel at all levels 
on integrated care models. This includes:
– Cross-training: Providing physical health 
providers with training on basic behavioral 
health screening and interventions.
– Professional Development: Offering 
opportunities for personnel to learn about 
different care models (e.g., Collaborative 
Care Model) and best practices in inte
-
grated care.
n Empower Frontline Staff: Leaders might 
decentralize some decision-making and 
empower clinical teams to innovate and 
problem solve. By giving personnel a voice 
in
 how integration is implemented, leadership 
can increase buy-in and create more effective, 
tailored solutions.
n Recognize and Reward Success: Publicly 
acknowledge and celebrate the achievements 
of teams and individuals who exemplify the 
principles of integrated care. This reinforces 
the organization’s values and motivates others 
to embrace the new model.
5.G. 2. A needs assessment of the population(s) 
served:
2.5.G. IPC
a. Documents the prevalence of:
2.a.5.G. IPC
(1) Mental health and substance use 
conditions and related needs.
2.a.(1)5.G. IPC
(2) Chronic health conditions and 
related needs.
2.a.(2)5.G. IPC
(3) Social health needs.
2.a.(3)5.G. IPC
b. Includes input from:
2.b.5.G. IPC
(1) Persons served.
2.b.(1)5.G. IPC
(2) Families/support systems.
2.b.(2)5.G. IPC
(3) Other relevant stakeholders.
2.b.(3)5.G. IPC
c. Is reviewed at least every three years.
2.c.5.G. IPC
d. Is updated as needed.
2.d.5.G. IPC
e. Is used as the basis for design of the 
integrated model of care.
2.e.5.G. IPC
Intent Statements
When there are existing documents from key 
community groups/stakeholders regarding 
assessment of community needs, the organi-
zation can utilize them in demonstrating 
conformance to this standard. These might be 
needs assessments conducted by local govern-
ments, hospital authorities, or payer groups. 
When these do not exist, or are unavailable to the 
organization, a needs assessment is conducted in 
a systematic manner, including seeking input 
from the identified stakeholders.
2.c. As it takes significant effort to gather data for 
a needs assessment, it might be multiple years 
between assessments. Material changes in the 
community such as population changes, signifi-
cant social impacts, loss or increase in community 
resources, or other factors might prompt review 
of the needs assessment sooner than every three 
years.

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
332 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
When community needs assessments are not 
readily available, an organization may consider 
all or some of the following in its information 
gathering:
n Define Y our “Community”:
– Geographic: A specific town, neighbor -
hood, or county.
– Demographic: A particular age group, 
ethnic group, or socioeconomic status.
– Shared Characteristic: Individuals with a 
specific condition, caregivers, or veterans.
n Assess Internal Capacity: Be realistic about 
the time, personnel, and financial resources 
the organization can dedicate. This will help 
determine the methods the organization can 
realistically employ.
n Asset Mapping: Instead of just focusing 
on needs, identify the existing strengths, 
resources, and positive aspects of the com-
munity that can be leveraged. This includes 
community organizations, informal support 
networks, green spaces, cultural institutions, 
etc.
Leverage Existing Data (Secondary Data 
Collection)
n Public Health Data:
– Local and State Health Departments: look 
for statistics on mental health prevalence, 
substance use rates, suicide data, overdose 
deaths, and chronic health conditions with 
behavioral health components. The health 
departments often publish comprehensive 
community health needs assessments 
(CHNAs) that would be available to 
review.
– Census Bureau (see the https://
data.census.gov website): Provides 
detailed demographic information (age, 
income, education, race/ethnicity, house-
hold structure) essential for understanding 
the target population.
– School Districts: Look for data on student 
mental health, disciplinary issues related 
to behavior, truancy, and counselor 
reports.
– Law Enforcement/Emergency Services: 
Data on mental health-related calls, crisis 
interventions, and substance-related inci-
dents can highlight areas of high need.
– Hospital Discharge Data: While often 
requiring formal requests, this data can 
show behavioral health-related emergency 
department visits or hospitalizations.
n Internal Data Review:
– Records of Persons Served: Analyze the 
organization’s data on presenting prob-
lems, diagnoses, service utilization, 
referral sources, wait times, and discharge 
outcomes. This shows whom the program 
is currently serving and what their needs 
are.
– Staff Observations: Direct service person -
nel have invaluable insights into common 
challenges and unmet needs they observe 
in their daily work.
Direct Community Engagement (Primary Data 
Collection)
n Key Informant Interviews:
– Who: Identify individuals who have deep 
knowledge of the community and its 
needs. This includes:
- Other behavioral health providers 
(private practice, larger agencies)
- Primary care physicians and nurses
- School administrators and counselors
- Law enforcement and first responders 
(police, EMTs)
- Social workers and case managers from 
other agencies
-F a i t h  l e a d e r s
- Community advocates or leaders
- Local government officials
- Business owners (understanding 
workforce issues, economic stress)
– How: Conduct structured or semi-
structured interviews. Prepare a list of 
open-ended questions about perceived 
needs, existing resources, service gaps, 
and
 barriers to care.

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 333
n Focus Groups:
– Who: Gather small groups (6–10 people) 
of specific populations relevant to the 
assessment. This could include:
- Individuals with lived experience of 
mental health or substance use 
challenges
-F a m i l y  m e m b e r s  o r  c a r e g i v e r s
- Parents of adolescents
- Specific cultural or ethnic groups
-S e n i o r s
– How: Facilitate a discussion around spe-
cific topics related to behavioral health 
needs, access, and potential solutions. 
This
 allows for rich, nuanced insights.
n Targeted Surveys (Online or Paper):
– Leverage Partnerships: Distribute surveys 
through community partners (e.g., 
churches, schools, community centers, 
food banks) or at local events.
– Online Tools: Use free or low-cost online 
survey tools (e.g., Google Forms, Survey-
Monkey basic) for wider reach.
– Keep it Brief: Small organizations benefit 
from shorter, focused surveys to maximize 
response rates.
– Ask Relevant Questions: Focus on 
perceived needs, barriers to care (e.g., 
transportation, cost, and stigma), aware
-
ness of services, and preferred types of 
support.
Data Analysis and Interpretation
n Triangulate Data: Compare findings from 
secondary data with insights from interviews 
and focus groups. Do they align? Where 
are
 the discrepancies? This helps validate 
findings.
n Identify Key Themes and Gaps: Look for 
recurring patterns in the data. What are the 
most pressing behavioral health needs? What 
services are clearly lacking or inaccessible? 
What are the common barriers?
n Prioritize Needs: Based on the data, decide 
which needs are most critical, prevalent, and 
align with the organization's mission and 
capacity to address. Consider urgency, 
impact, and feasibility.
Reporting and Action Planning
n Create a Concise Report: Summarize meth-
odology, key findings (both quantitative and 
qualitative), and prioritized needs. Make it 
accessible and easy to understand for various 
audiences.
n Develop an Action Plan: Translate the assess-
ment findings into concrete steps for the 
organization.
– Are there new programs or services to 
develop?
– Are modifications to existing services 
indicated?
– How will identified barriers to care be 
addressed?
n Share Findings: Disseminate the report to the 
board, personnel, community partners, and 
potential funders.
5.G. 3. A written program description includes:
3.5.G. IPC
a. Scope of care coordination.
3.a.5.G. IPC
b. Scope of disease management 
services.
3.b.5.G. IPC
c. Population health management 
strategies.
3.c.5.G. IPC
d. How each of the following are pro -
vided, accessed, and/or coordinated:
3.d.5.G. IPC
(1) Primary care.
3.d.(1)5.G. IPC
(2) Behavioral healthcare.
3.d.(2)5.G. IPC
(3) Other healthcare.
3.d.(3)5.G. IPC
(4) Community and social support 
services.
3.d.(4)5.G. IPC
Intent Statements
This standard expands on Standards 2.A.1. (scope 
of services) and 2.A.11. (program description) to 
describe the specialized services provided by an 
integrated primary care specialty designated 
program.
3.a. The scope of care coordination is determined 
by the program and may range from close/high 
intensity to moderate intensity and/or low inten-
sity. The scope describes the activities performed.

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
334 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
3.b. This describes the program’s capabilities and 
diseases managed in the population served.
Examples
3.c. Population health management strategies 
might include some or all of the following:
Risk Stratification and Targeted Interventions:
n Data Analysis: Leveraging Electronic Health 
Records (EHRs) and other data sources (e.g., 
claims data) to identify and stratify the popu-
lation by risk level. This includes identifying 
persons with:
– Multiple chronic physical health condi -
tions (e.g., diabetes, hypertension, heart 
disease) alongside behavioral health 
diagnoses.
– High utilization of emergency depart -
ments or inpatient services (both physical 
and behavioral health).
– Poor adherence to medication or treat -
ment plans.
– Significant social health needs (e.g., 
homelessness, food insecurity, lack of 
transportation).
n Targeted Outreach: Proactive outreach to 
high-risk persons for intensive case man-
agement, appointment scheduling, and 
connection to services. For example, a pro
-
gram might proactively contact all persons 
with SMI who also have uncontrolled diabetes 
to ensure they are receiving appropriate pri-
mary care and diabetes education.
Proactive Screening and Early Intervention:
– Universal Screening: Implementing 
universal screening for common physical 
health conditions (e.g., blood pressure, 
blood glucose, cholesterol, BMI) for all 
persons served.
– Early Identification of Co-occurring 
Conditions: Training personnel to recog-
nize and address early signs of physical 
health problems in individuals with 
behavioral health conditions.
Care Coordination and Integration:
n Integrated Care Teams: Establishing multi-
disciplinary teams (psychiatrists, nurses, 
social workers, primary care providers, 
community health workers) who collabora
-
tively develop and manage comprehensive 
care plans that address both behavioral and 
physical health needs.
n Shared Care Plans and Communication 
Protocols: Utilizing integrated EHRs or other 
shared platforms to ensure all providers 
involved in a person’s care have access to 
a
 unified care plan and can communicate 
seamlessly. This reduces fragmentation and 
improves continuity of care.
n Transitional Care Management: Imple-
menting robust protocols for persons served 
transitioning between care settings to reduce 
readmissions and ensure continuity of care. 
This often involves medication reconcilia
-
tion, follow-up appointment scheduling, 
and direct outreach.
Addressing Social Determinants of Health 
(SDOH):
n Comprehensive SDOH Screening: Systemat-
ically screening all persons served for social 
needs such as housing instability, food insecu-
rity, transportation barriers, and employment 
challenges.
n Community Resource Navigation: Actively 
connect persons served with relevant commu-
nity resources (e.g., food pantries, housing 
assistance, job training programs, legal aid) 
and provide hands-on support in accessing 
these services.
n Advocacy for Systemic Change: Engaging in 
broader community partnerships and advo-
cacy efforts to address systemic issues that 
contribute to poor health outcomes in the 
population served (e.g., advocating for 
affordable housing, improved public 
transportation).

SECTION 5.G. INTEGRATED PRIMARY CARE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 335
Health Promotion and Wellness Programs:
n Education and Self-Management Support: 
Offering group or individual education 
sessions on topics such as healthy eating, 
physical activity, smoking cessation, stress 
management, and chronic disease self-
management.
n Peer Support Activities: Integrating peer 
specialists to provide support, guidance, 
and encouragement for adopting healthier 
lifestyles.
n Wellness Activities: Organizing or promoting 
access to wellness activities like walking 
groups, yoga, mindfulness sessions, or 
nutrition classes within the program or in 
partnership with community organizations.
Performance Measurement and Management 
(Section 1.L.), and Performance Improvement 
(Section 1.M.):
n Include in measurement systems population-
level metrics that track and analyze outcomes 
related to both physical and behavioral health. 
Examples include:
– Rates of preventive screenings (e.g., cancer 
screenings, vaccinations).
– Control rates for chronic conditions 
(e.g., HbA1c for diabetes, blood pressure 
control).
– Reduction in emergency department visits 
and hospitalizations.
– Improvements in mental health symptom 
scores (e.g., PHQ-9, GAD-7 scores across 
the population).
– Satisfaction of persons served with inte -
grated care.
n Quality Improvement: Utilizing the results 
of the data collected and analyzed to identify 
areas for improvement, implement new strat-
egies, and continuously monitor effectiveness 
to optimize health outcomes for the entire 
population served.

These are the core functions of an integrated pri-

Examples
4.a. Health promotion goes beyond treating 
symptoms. It focuses on empowering persons 
served to build resilience, improve overall well-
being, and prevent future mental or physical 
health challenges. These strategies aim to 
enhance protective factors and equip persons 
served with skills for long-term health.
4.b.(4) Programs will usually establish protocols

quency of monitoring for the management of 
chronic diseases.

may occur when a person relocates or there is a 
change in insurance coverage that requires tran-
sition to a different provider.

This standard aligns with Standard 2.A.23., ensur-
ing that the person served is an active member of 
the team and relevant healthcare providers and 
community and/or social support services are 
included in team communications and their input 
is documented.
Examples
5.b.(2) The healthcare providers are determined 
by the specific needs of the person served and 
may include external medical providers, dental 
providers, health educators, public health per-
sonnel, nutritionists, or other similar providers.
5.b.(3) Community and/or social support ser-
vices may include social workers, child welfare 
workers, probation officers, community housing 
staff, or other similar providers.

Intent Statements
6.b. When the integrated primary care program 
is attached to a health home, which is a primary 
resource for physical and behavioral healthcare 
for a complex population, it is imperative that the 
program be accessible to the persons served at 
times that most effectively meet their needs.
Examples
A variety of methods may be used to extend 
access to needed services, e.g., telehealth services, 
home-based services, street medicine/street 
psychiatry, mobile units, and services through 
medical hubs.
5.G. 7. The team demonstrates an integrated 
approach to person-centered planning 
that addresses the physical, behavioral, 
and social health needs of the person 
served.
7.5.G. IPC
Intent Statements
Person-centered planning is conducted with the 
active involvement of the person served as well 
as the various disciplines needed to effectively 
implement the plan. The planning process 
addresses and integrates, in a holistic manner, 
the physical, behavioral and social health needs 
of the person served.

Intent Statements
Consent for treatment allows the person served 
to decline any or all services offered by the pro-
gram. When integrated care services are declined, 
the program continues to provide services the 
person served wishes to engage in without 
reprisal.

Education empowers persons served with the

and promoting self-management across behav-

338 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
ioral, physical, and social health domains. By 
providing comprehensive education, the pro-
gram demonstrates its commitment to equipping 
individuals with the tools necessary to navigate 
their health journeys effectively, prevent chronic 
conditions, and enhance their overall quality of 
life.
Examples
10.a. Examples of education to address wellness 
include, but are not limited to:
n Stress reduction techniques (e.g., mindful-
ness, progressive muscle relaxation).
n The benefits of balanced nutrition and healthy 
eating habits.
n Information on the importance of regular 
physical activity and accessible ways to 
incorporate it into daily routines.
n Effective sleep hygiene practices.
n Discussions and resources for identifying 
and developing personal leisure interests 
and hobbies.
10.e.(3) Education addressing self-management 
of social health needs might include:
n Workshops on communication skills, conflict 
resolution, and setting healthy boundaries in 
relationships.
n Identifying and accessing community 
resources for housing, food security, 
transportation, or employment support.
n Guidance on developing and maintaining 
supportive social networks.
n Information on navigating legal or financial 
systems that affect social well-being.
n Skills training for self-advocacy in social and 
community settings.
n Strategies to address loneliness and isolation.

11.5.G. IPC

Intent Statements
The program leadership provides strategic 
direction, consistent oversight, and proactive 
management to cultivate and sustain a high-
quality, integrated service delivery system. 
By actively guiding all critical operational and 
clinical components, leadership demonstrates 
its commitment to optimizing timely access to 
comprehensive care, driving continuous perfor-
mance improvement, and ultimately enhancing 
outcomes for all persons served through imple-
mentation of a cohesive and effective integrated 
model of care.
Examples
11.g. Examples of tools to measure the effective-
ness of behavioral and physical healthcare 
integration include:
n SAMHSA-HRSA Standard Framework for 
Levels of Integrated Healthcare: Describes 
six
 levels of integration.
n AHRQ Integration Scorecard or Assessment 
Tool: Measures integration using multiple 
domains (clinical, operational, financial).
n Integrated Practice Assessment Tool (IPAT): 
Assesses the level of integration in practice 
operations.
n Collaborative Care Model Fidelity Measures: 
Evaluates adherence to evidence-based mod
-
els like the AIMS Center’s Collaborative Care 
Model.

Cross-training in this standard is conceptualized 
as the systematic and documented process of 
educating direct service personnel from one 
health domain (e.g., behavioral health) with 
foundational knowledge, skills, and perspectives 
typically associated with another health domain 
(e.g., primary physical health) and vice versa, as 
well as an understanding of social health needs.

By collecting and analyzing data, the program

managing the health and well-being of its entire 
population served, enabling responsive care 
adjustments, optimized resource allocation, and 
continuous improvement in outcomes for the 
persons served.
Examples

n Emergency room visits.

n Health indicators.
n Health promotion activities.
n Hospitalizations.
n Risk status.

care professional.

illness.

up plan.

other drug dependence treatment.
n Controlling high blood pressure.
14.b.(1)(e) Penetration rate is commonly viewed 
as the percentage of the target population that is 
engaged in services. Understanding penetration 
rate has the potential to impact/improve care by 
focused efforts regarding access, resource alloca-
tion, and health equity.

SECTION 5.H. JUVENILE JUSTICE
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 341
H. Juvenile Justice
Description
Juvenile justice programs serve special pop-
ulations comprised of accused or adjudicated 
juveniles referred from within the juvenile justice 
system who are experiencing behavioral health 
needs including alcohol or other substance abuse 
or addiction or psychiatric disabilities or disor-
ders. Services can be provided through courts, 
through probation and parole agencies, or in 
community-based or institutional settings. 
Institutional settings may include juvenile 
detention centers, jails, prisons, or other 
delinquency-focused settings. The services 
are designed to maximize the person’s ability 
to function effectively in the community. The 
juvenile justice mandates include community 
safety needs in all judicial decisions and require 
that behavioral health programs are aware of the 
safety requirements of not only the individual, 
program staff members, and peers, but also the 
community at large.
Juvenile justice educational programs may 
include either community-based or institution-
based educational and training services. Such 
programs may include personal and interper-
sonal skills training, conflict resolution, anger 
management, DUI/OWI education, mental 
health education, education about alcohol and 
other drugs, information on criminal thinking 
patterns, or traditional academic education.
Applicable Standards
If a behavioral health core program for which 
the organization is seeking accreditation is 
primarily provided in a correctional facility, the 
Juvenile Justice standards must be applied.
Organizations seeking accreditation in juvenile 
justice must apply the standards in Sections 1 
and 2 and one or more of the service-specific 
core programs in Section 3 or 4 as well as these 
standards. For example, a juvenile justice 
program providing treatment through a 
therapeutic community model would apply 
the standards in Sections 1 and 2 as well as 3.R. 
Therapeutic Communities and 5.H. Juvenile 
Justice.
5.H. 1. Services are delivered by a team with 
specialized knowledge of the various 
theories of, and approaches to, juvenile 
justice behavioral health services.
1.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
In addition to Standard 2.A.23. related to the 
team providing services, this standard provides 
more specific guidance as to the competencies 
of team members providing services in a juvenile 
justice setting who are directly involved in the 
participatory process of defining, refining, and 
assisting the persons served in meeting their 
goals.
5.H. 2. All members of the team have access 
to the confidential information that 
is required for the team members to 
perform their function.
2.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
Access to clinical records can include access to 
information such as:
n Person-centered plans.
n Custody records.
5.H. 3. The person served is provided with a 
description of the relationship between 
the juvenile justice entity and the pro-
gram, including:
3.5.H. JJ
a. The extent and limitations of confi -
dentiality and sanctions.
3.a.5.H. JJ
b. The possible implications of having a 
juvenile justice member on the team.
3.b.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
The team involves a blend of behavioral health 
providers and juvenile justice personnel, such as 
detention officers, control agents, guards, and 
probation and parole officers. Those individuals 
who play a significant role in the treatment, edu-
cation, and incarceration of the person served 
work cooperatively and collaboratively as a team.
The person served has the option of refusing to 
have the juvenile justice system actively involved 
in the treatment process.

SECTION 5.H. JUVENILE JUSTICE
342 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Examples
3.b. The staff members of the program might 
discuss such issues as:
n Access to confidential records.
n Action the juvenile justice member may be 
forced to take based on information provided 
by the team.
n The impact on the therapeutic relationship.
5.H. 4. Training:
4.5.H. JJ
a. Is provided to personnel prior to the 
delivery of services.
4.a.5.H. JJ
b. Includes regular interdisciplinary 
cross-training related to clinical and 
juvenile justice issues.
4.b.5.H. JJ
c. Includes such topics as:
4.c.5.H. JJ
(1) The requirements imposed on 
personnel from the juvenile 
justice system who participate 
on the treatment team.
4.c.(1)5.H. JJ
(2) Safeguards that are available to 
workers.
4.c.(2)5.H. JJ
(3) Safety practices specific to the 
setting.
4.c.(3)5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
4.a. Behavioral health professionals who work 
in juvenile justice settings encounter a unique 
service delivery system with both opportunities 
and challenges. The intent of this standard is to 
ensure that individuals new to this type of setting 
receive full and complete training prior to the 
delivery of services, and throughout their 
employment, to ensure that they are familiar with 
the unique procedures and characteristics of the 
environment in which they work.
4.b. Interdisciplinary cross-training refers to juve-
nile justice staff members providing juvenile 
justice training to clinical staff members and 
also to clinical staff members providing clinical 
training to juvenile justice staff members.
5.H. 5. The juvenile justice program conducts 
a timely assessment for each person 
served that includes:
5.5.H. JJ
a. A detailed history of the person’s 
criminal behavior, including:
5.a.5.H. JJ
(1) Arrests.
5.a.(1)5.H. JJ
(2) Convictions.
5.a.(2)5.H. JJ
(3) Violations of parole and/or 
probation.
5.a.(3)5.H. JJ
(4) Prior incarcerations.
5.a.(4)5.H. JJ
(5) Pending cases.
5.a.(5)5.H. JJ
b. Information on the person’s partici -
pation in organizations or groups 
that encourage criminal behavior.
5.b.5.H. JJ
c. The relationship between the 
person’s behavioral health and 
criminal activity.
5.c.5.H. JJ
d. Risk to self, other persons served, 
personnel, and/or community.
5.d.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
In conducting an assessment in a juvenile justice 
setting, a program emphasizes the collection of 
information related to delinquent or criminal 
behavior.

contributing to impact on

6.f.(2)5.H. JJ

6.g.5.H. JJ

i. Learning ability.

Intent Statements
In short-term programs (such as Assessment and 
Referral, Crisis Intervention, Crisis Stabilization, 
or Withdrawal Management), the amount of 
information collected may be limited by time or 
the condition of the person served. The intent of 
the standard is to collect an adequate amount 
of information to provide appropriate and safe 
services.
6.h. The assessment includes a determination 
of the status of the juvenile’s immunization. A 
copy of the immunization record is not required. 
Organizations can note when juveniles are 
enrolled in school settings where verification 
of immunization is legally required.
Examples

The intent of this standard is to ensure access to

The services could include:
n Screening and assessment.

n Outpatient treatment.
n Medication management.

n Aftercare.

The educational specialist can be available when

functions of the team.
5.H. 13. When the services disrupt the juvenile’s

13.5.H. JJ
Examples
Arrangements could include:
n Use of a facility-based school.
n Use of a private school at the organization.
n Use of on-site educators from a local school

n Coordination with home school services.
n Coordination and monitoring of assignments.
n Coordination with the community school to 
facilitate reintegration.
5.H. 14. The curriculum-based program 
component for each person served:
14.5.H. JJ
a. Addresses issues specific to the 
person’s individual needs.
14.a.5.H. JJ
b. Is consistent with the person’s 
cognitive and learning abilities.
14.b.5.H. JJ
c. Is consistent with the program’s 
philosophy of treatment.
14.c.5.H. JJ
d. Includes provisions for:
14.d.5.H. JJ
(1) Evaluation.
14.d.(1)5.H. JJ
(2) Group instruction.
14.d.(2)5.H. JJ
(3) Individual instruction.
14.d.(3)5.H. JJ
e. Meets applicable federal, provincial, 
and state requirements.
14.e.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
14.b. The intent of this standard is to ensure 
that the assessment has included cognitive 
and learning abilities and that reading materials, 
assignments, and the requirements for participa-
tion take into consideration the learning abilities 
of the person served. This standard includes 
ensuring that reasonable accommodations are 
available for persons with special educational 
needs.
14.c. Because many of the juvenile justice educa-
tional services are provided as part of or within a 
treatment program, this standard encourages the 
organization to ensure that the educational plan 
for each person served is consistent with the 
philosophy of the treatment program.
Examples
The organization is encouraged to include the 
following topics in its educational programs:
n Substance abuse treatment, relapse preven-
tion, and recovery.
n Physical health issues or consequences and 
communicable diseases.
n Community resources and community 
integration.
n Violence prevention.
n Culture-specific issues.
n Interpersonal and relationship skills.
n Life-skills training.
n Problem solving.
n Conflict or anger management.
5.H. 15. Based on the needs of the person served, 
the educational program addresses the 
development of:
15.5.H. JJ
a. Community living skills.
15.a.5.H. JJ
b. Social skills.
15.b.5.H. JJ

c. Social supports.
15.c.5.H. JJ

5.H. 16. For residential services provided in con-
gregate facilities or sites that are owned, 
rented, or leased by the organization, 
staff support is available on site 24 hours 
a day, 7 days a week.
16.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
Staff members are in the facility around the clock 
and are able to respond to emergencies quickly. 
In detention or correctional settings, staff support 
may be provided by an organization other than 
the one providing the behavioral health services.

program provides opportunities for vis-

with:

18.5.H. JJ

18.a.5.H. JJ
b. The furniture.
18.b.5.H. JJ

18.c.5.H. JJ
Examples

n Appropriately sized furniture.
n Recreational equipment.
n Age-appropriate reading materials and video 
equipment.
5.H. 19. The organization implements a pol-
icy(ies) and procedures for:
19.5.H. JJ
a. Obtaining criminal background 
checks on all persons providing 
direct services to juveniles.
19.a.5.H. JJ
b. Acting on the results of the 
background checks.
19.b.5.H. JJ
Intent Statements
Background checks may include fingerprinting 
and FBI criminal history checks. Persons providing 
direct services include personnel, students, 
interns, volunteers, or contracted providers of 
direct service. The provision of direct services 
includes transportation.
Examples
Background checks may be conducted prior to 
employment for new personnel, at the time of job 
change when beginning to work with children or 
adolescents, or prior to an accreditation survey 
for existing personnel.

SECTION 5.I. MEDICALLY COMPLEX
346 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
I. Medically Complex
Description
Medically complex standards are applied to pro-
grams that serve a specific population of persons 
who have a serious ongoing illness or a chronic 
condition that meets at least one of the following 
criteria:
n Has lasted or is anticipated to last at least 
twelve months.
n Has required at least one month of 
hospitalization.
n Requires daily ongoing medical treatments 
and monitoring by appropriately trained 
personnel, which may include parents or 
other family members.
n Requires the routine use of a medical device 
or the use of assistive technology to com-
pensate for the loss of usefulness of a body 
function needed to participate in activities 
of daily living.
n The medically complex condition of the per-
son served presents an ongoing threat to the 
person’s health status.
These standards consider the individual’s overall 
medical condition, including acuity, stability, 
impairments, activity limitations, participation 
restrictions, psychological status, behavioral 
status, placement, and long-term outcomes 
expectations. Appropriate medical consultation 
occurs specific to each person served in addition 
to medical consultation related to policies and 
procedures.
Services to persons with medically complex con-
ditions can be provided in a variety of settings 
and are not necessarily exclusive programs that 
serve only this particular population. The ser-
vices within the program are designed based 
on the needs, desires, and expectations of the 
persons served and their legal guardian/care-
givers to maximize the ability to function 
effectively within their family (or placement), 
school, and/or community environments and to 
achieve and maintain an optimal state of health 
to enhance their quality of life. The services pro-
vided also consider any culturally specific issues 
relevant to the individual and family/caregivers 
as appropriate. The service plan supports all 
transitions in the person’s life and is changed as 
necessary to meet the person’s identified needs 
as well as the needs of the family/caregivers.
Some examples of the quality results desired 
by the different stakeholders of these services 
include:
n Development of an effective and efficient 
network of community support services 
including access to therapies, medical 
supports, and guidance.
n Satisfying and meaningful relationships.
n Achievement of goals in health, education, 
and activities of daily living.
n Being able to choose and pursue meaningful 
activities in the least restrictive environment 
possible to achieve personal satisfaction in life 
activities.
n Maintenance of health and well-being.
n Restored or improved functioning.
n Enhanced quality of life.
n Personal and family development.
n Transitions between levels of care or transi-
tion to independence.
n End-of-life services and supports for the per-
son, family/caregiver, legal guardian, and/or 
other significant persons in the individual’s 
life to assist with meaningful closures.
Applicable Standards
If a core program for which the organization 
is seeking accreditation is designed primarily 
to serve persons who meet the definition of 
medically complex, or the program serves only 
this target population, the medically complex 
standards must be applied in addition to the 
standards in Sections 1 and 2 and one or more 
service-specific core program standards in 
Section 3 or 4.
Organizations that serve persons who meet the 
definition of medically complex in a program 
that has not been specifically designed for this 
population may opt to apply these standards in 
addition to the standards in Sections 1 and 2 
and the core program standards in Section 3 
or 4.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 347
NOTE: An organization seeking accreditation for a 
core program for children/adolescents who meet 
the definition of medically complex (MCCA) must 
apply the standards in Sections
 5.C. and 5.I.
5.I. 1. The program description of services 
available for this population includes 
the following, as applicable:
1.5.I. MC
a. Medical acuity issues.
1.a.5.I. MC
b. Medical stability issues.
1.b.5.I. MC
c. Psychological issues.
1.c.5.I. MC
d. Behavioral issues.
1.d.5.I. MC
e. Activity limitations.
1.e.5.I. MC
f. Participation restrictions.
1.f.5.I. MC
g. Long-term planning criteria.
1.g.5.I. MC
h. Intended discharge environments.
1.h.5.I. MC
i. Environmental modifications.
1.i.5.I. MC
j. Adaptive equipment.
1.j.5.I. MC
k. Respite.
1.k.5.I. MC
Intent Statements
1.a. Medical acuity issues refers to the services 
that are considered urgent and require immedi-
ate attention. 
1.b. Medical stability issues refers to the overall 
medical condition of the person served at a given 
point in time.
5.I. 2. The program collaborates with: 
2.5.I. MC
a. Healthcare providers who provide 
specialized medical, psychological/
behavioral, and other therapeutic 
care to the person served.
2.a.5.I. MC
b. Other providers who provide special -
ized care to the person served.
2.b.5.I. MC
Examples
2.b. Other providers may include child care, 
recreation, and education.
5.I. 3. Services are managed by an individual 
who has:
3.5.I. MC
a. The education, training, and experi -
ence needed to meet the needs of 
persons with medically complex 
needs.
3.a.5.I. MC
b. The competencies needed to manage 
the services.
3.b.5.I. MC
Intent Statements
The program identifies the background and com-
petencies required based on the scope of services 
provided.
Examples
Based on the services provided, the individual’s 
education, training, and experience may be in 
areas such as healthcare or nursing, health 
advocacy, health aspects of disabilities, health 
problems commonly co-occurring with develop-
mental or medical disabilities, palliative care, or 
medication management.
Job descriptions identify qualifications needed 
and ensure compliance with applicable guidelines 
and legal requirements. Applicable laws and 
national/professional organizations may be excel-
lent resources for establishing qualifications.
5.I. 4. The program informs the primary care 
physician(s) of the progress of each 
person served toward the person’s 
individual goals regarding:
4.5.I. MC
a. Assessments.
4.a.5.I. MC
b. Significant changes.
4.b.5.I. MC
c. Discharge/transition.
4.c.5.I. MC
Intent Statements
Communication with primary care physicians is 
critical when providing services to persons with 
medically complex needs, especially when the 
primary care physician is not directly involved 
with the services provided.
The physician(s) to be notified are identified by 
the person served and/or by a residential facility.
Examples
4.b. Examples of significant changes in the status 
of the person served include an acute illness that 

SECTION 5.I. MEDICALLY COMPLEX
348 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
precipitates transfer to another level of care, a fall 
that results in significant injury, or death.
5.I. 5. The service delivery team includes 
specialists, as appropriate.
5.5.I. MC
Intent Statements
In addition to the primary care physician, 
there may be an array of other professionals or 
specialists that would be included on the service 
delivery team.
Examples
The survey team will look for conformance to 
this standard through review of records (doc-
umentation of input into team decisions, 
attendance at team meetings, and phone con-
versations) and interviews with persons served, 
families, personnel, and payers.
Team member involvement can be accomplished 
by a variety of methods such as conference calls; 
sharing information via fax, messenger, or mail; 
and ongoing conversations between team 
members.
Additional individuals on the service delivery 
team could include:
n An audiologist.
n A behavior analyst. (A behavior analyst is a 
psychologist in the distinct specialty of 
applied behavior analysis. A behavior analyst 
conducts functional assessments and analyses 
of behavior and its environmental influences. 
This individual designs and implements pro
-
grams using the principles of learning and 
motivation to effect the acquisition of desired 
instrumental and social behaviors.)
n A case manager/care coordinator, internal or 
external.
n A spiritual advisor.
n A child life specialist. (A child life specialist 
has competence in the areas of growth and 
development, family dynamics, play and 
activities, interpersonal communication, 
developmental observation and assessment, 
the learning process, the group process, 
behavior management, the reactions of 
children to hospitalization and to illness, 
interventions to prevent emotional trauma, 
collaboration with other healthcare profes-
sionals, a basic understanding of child/youth 
illnesses and medical terminology, and super-
visory skills.)
n A child psychologist.
n A creative arts therapist. (Includes music 
therapists, art therapists, dance therapists, 
drama therapists, and poetry therapists.)
n A developmental specialist. (An individual 
who is competent in child/youth development 
and could include, but is not limited to, a 
pediatrician, child psychologist, social worker, 
special educator, or child life specialist.)
n A driving instructor.
n An educational specialist. (A special or 
regular education teacher.)
n A neuropsychologist.
n An occupational therapist.
n An orthotist.
n A pediatric nurse practitioner.
n A pediatric physiatrist.
n A pharmacist.
n A physical therapist.
n A physiotherapist/physical therapist.
n A primary care physician.
n A physician extender (assistant).
n A prosthetist.
n A qualified alcoholism and other substance 
use disorder counselor. (An individual with 
experience and training in the treatment of 
alcoholism and other substance abuse.)
n A registered dietitian.
n A registered nurse. (May include a registered 
nurse with rehabilitation experience.)
n A rehabilitation engineer.
n A rehabilitation nurse.
n A rehabilitation physician.
n A respiratory therapist.
n A school guidance counselor.
n A social worker.
n A speech-language pathologist.
n A therapeutic recreation specialist.
n A vocational specialist.

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 349
5.I. 6. Personnel demonstrate competencies in 
the following areas:
6.5.I. MC
a. Developmental stages.
6.a.5.I. MC
b. Physical impairments.
6.b.5.I. MC
c. Behavioral needs.
6.c.5.I. MC
d. Day-to-day needs.
6.d.5.I. MC
e. Grief and end-of-life support 
concerns.
6.e.5.I. MC
Examples
6.d. May include nutritional needs or medication 
administration.
5.I. 7. The program promotes a positive, 
therapeutic approach to behavior man-
agement, as applicable, that addresses:
7.5.I. MC
a. Instruction and guidance to the per -
son regarding desired behaviors that: 
7.a.5.I. MC
(1) Build on current strengths.
7.a.(1)5.I. MC
(2) Promote resiliency.
7.a.(2)5.I. MC
b. Environmental factors to enhance the 
desired behaviors of the person.
7.b.5.I. MC
c. Environmental modifications.
7.c.5.I. MC
d. Use of medications.
7.d.5.I. MC
Examples
7.c. Environmental modifications might include 
the use of noise-reduction materials to provide a 
quiet environment; the installation of flooring or 
carpeting in neutral solid colors; adjusting the 
volume of phone ringers and doorbells; limiting 
or controlling where and when people may visit 
persons served; reducing noxious stimuli such as 
bright sunlight or odors; and limiting exposure to 
equipment, appliances, substances, etc. that may 
pose risk to persons served.
5.I. 8. As appropriate to the scope of the pro-
gram, end-of-life planning:
8.5.I. MC
a. Is directed by the wishes/desires of 
the person served and/or legal 
guardian.
8.a.5.I. MC
b. Includes advocacy of hospice, pallia -
tive care, or other end-of-life choices 
as needed.
8.b.5.I. MC
c. Includes spiritual or religious ele -
ments, if desired by the person 
served and/or legal guardian.
8.c.5.I. MC
d. Includes the guidance of a medical 
professional, if desired by the person 
served and/or legal guardian.
8.d.5.I. MC
e. Is communicated to applicable ser -
vice providers in the required format, 
if applicable.
8.e.5.I. MC
Intent Statements
Persons served, families/support systems, and 
personnel have opportunities to talk about end-
of-life issues and participate in planning the 
memorial service and creating end-of-life 
protocols.
Examples
Families/support systems should be involved in 
the development of advance directives and in 
identifying the extent to which medical inter-
vention is to be administered.
Whenever possible, no one dies alone. Support 
and presence are planned for each individual 
served so that the person does not die alone.
The person served and the family/support system 
are interviewed about preferences for the dying 
process (e.g., five wishes, music, individuals pres-
ent, preparation and notification, comfort items, 
and spiritual needs); care planning includes these 
preferences.
Memorial gardens may be developed outside on 
organization property in remembrance of those 
lost.
Memorials that reflect the person may be evident 
throughout the organization.
Do-not-resuscitate (DNR) orders are known and 
strictly adhered to. Efforts are made to clarify 
issues related to an individual’s end-of-life wishes 
to avoid any misunderstanding on the part of 
personnel and/or family/support systems.
Some organizations do not choose to have a 
memorial service, but they may provide oppor-
tunities for personnel to express their grief by 
supporting them so they may attend the funeral 
of a person served.

SECTION 5.I. MEDICALLY COMPLEX
350 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
5.I. 9. The program has a written philosophy 
of health and wellness for the persons 
served that:
9.5.I. MC
a. Is designed to:
9.a.5.I. MC
(1) Meet their interests.
9.a.(1)5.I. MC
(2) Align with their cognitive 
capabilities.
9.a.(2)5.I. MC
(3) Reflect their choices.
9.a.(3)5.I. MC
(4) Promote their personal growth.
9.a.(4)5.I. MC
(5) Enhance their self-image.
9.a.(5)5.I. MC
(6) Improve or maintain their func -
tional levels whenever possible.
9.a.(6)5.I. MC
b. Is implemented to:
9.b.5.I. MC
(1) Address:
9.b.(1)5.I. MC
(a) Function.
9.b.(1)(a)5.I. MC
(b) Quality of life.
9.b.(1)(b)5.I. MC
(2) Promote healthy aging and well-
being.
9.b.(2)5.I. MC
c. Addresses aging in place.
9.c.5.I. MC
d. Is shared with:
9.d.5.I. MC
(1) The persons served.
9.d.(1)5.I. MC
(2) Families/support systems.
9.d.(2)5.I. MC
(3) Personnel.
9.d.(3)5.I. MC
Examples
A program’s philosophy may be documented 
separately or included in other documents such 
as a program plan or marketing materials. An 
emphasis might be placed on:
n Maximizing or maintaining independence of 
persons served.
n How changing acuity needs will be addressed.
9.b.(1)(a)–(b) A program’s philosophy addresses 
how it intends to provide services that promote 
and optimize the activities, function, perfor-
mance, productivity, participation, and/or quality 
of life of the persons served.

J. Older Adults
Description
Programs for older adults consist of an array of 
services designed specifically to address the 
behavioral health needs of this population. Such 
programs tailor their services to the particular 
needs and preferences of older adults and their 
families/support systems. Services are provided 
in environments appropriate to their needs. 
Personnel are trained to effectively address the 
complex needs of older adults.
Applicable Standards
If an organization serves older adults and 
chooses to add the Specialty Designation for 
Older Adults to a core program in Section 3 or 
4, the standards in this subsection must be 
applied in addition to the standards in Section 
1 and 2 (please consult grid on page 112 for 
applicability) and the core program standards 
in Section 3 or 4, except for Call Centers, 
Diversion/Intervention, Prevention, and 
Comprehensive Suicide Prevention programs.
Organizations that serve older adults are not 
required to apply these standards to the core 
programs for which they are seeking 
accreditation.
5.J. 1. The assessment process gathers and 
records sufficient information to develop 
a comprehensive person-centered plan 
for each person served that:
1.5.J. OA
a. Includes information obtained from:
1.a.5.J. OA
(1) The person served.
1.a.(1)5.J. OA
(2) Family members/legal represen -
tatives, when applicable and 
permitted.
1.a.(2)5.J. OA
(3) Other collateral sources, when 
applicable and permitted.
1.a.(3)5.J. OA
(4) External sources, when the need 
for specified assessment not able 
to be provided by the program is 
identified.
1.a.(4)5.J. OA

Intent Statements
In short-term programs (such as Assessment and 
Referral, Crisis Intervention, Crisis Stabilization, 
or Withdrawal Management) the amount of 
information collected may be limited by time 
or the condition of the person served. The intent 
of this standard is to collect an adequate amount 
of information to provide appropriate and safe 
services that meet the unique needs of this 
population.
Examples
1.b.(2) This may include substance misuse, 
process addictions, gambling, and spending/
shopping.
1.b.(3) The assessment process addresses prob-
lematic or excessive behaviors impacting the 
person’s functioning.
1.b.(4) This includes a description of the person’s 
memory and thinking abilities.
1.b.(6) Comorbidities in this context could 
include mental health, physical health, and 
substance misuse/abuse/addiction.
1.b.(7) This may include whether the person is 
functioning in a family role that is satisfying and 
that the person views as appropriate and if the 
person has financial responsibilities for other 
family members either in or out of the home.
1.b.(8) This would describe the person’s ability 
to perform functions of daily living.
1.b.(10) Older adults often experience significant 
losses of life partners, children, and close friends, 
and grief may be unexpressed.
1.b.(13) There could be a general description of 
overall physical condition, but there are a num-
ber of typical conditions that impact older adults 
and should be considered in planning for care. 
These include vision and hearing difficulties, 
ambulation restrictions or need for assistive 
devices, fall risks, and incontinence.
1.b.(14) There are a number of risks to this pop-
ulation that should be considered, such as abuse, 
falls, driving, violence in the home, financial or 
other exploitation, and suicide.
1.b.(16) The program considers the person’s 
current sexual behaviors/status and how these 
may impact current functioning.
1.b.(17) There are a number of significant life 
events that can create grief and loss issues as well 
as trauma. These can include retirement, change 
of residence, loss of independence, loss of loved 
ones and pets, recent diagnosis of serious illness, 
and experiences of war or disasters.
1.b.(18) The program considers how the person 
interacts with friends and social networks, types 
of recreation and leisure activities engaged in, 
and volunteer or other activities.

354 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual

Intent Statements
Involvement of immediate or extended family 
members or their proxies often assists programs 
to effectively keep persons served living the most 
independent lives possible. Programs seek to 
assess the quality and safety of the person’s sup-
port network as well as the ability to function as 
appropriate support.
Examples
2.a.(7) The program determines how the mem-
bers of the family/support system interact with 
each other and how the program can interact in 
a manner to be most effective in supporting the 
family/support system.

and authorizations.
3.c.5.J. OA
Intent Statements
Older adults often have a complex constellation 
of care providers, including healthcare and social 
service organizations, which may not communi-
cate effectively with each other. To the degree 
allowed by applicable laws, the program assists 
the person served by bridging communication 
gaps between providers and educating providers 
about needed services.

5.J. 7. As appropriate to the scope of the 
program, planning end-of-life care:
7.5.J. OA
a. Is directed by the wishes/desires 
of the person served and/or legal 
representative.
7.a.5.J. OA
b. Includes advocacy of hospice, pallia -
tive care, or other end-of-life choices 
as needed.
7.b.5.J. OA
c. Includes cultural, ethnic, religious, 
or spiritual elements, if desired by 
the person served and/or legal 
representative.
7.c.5.J. OA
d. Includes the guidance of a medical 
professional, if desired by the person 
served and/or legal representative.
7.d.5.J. OA
e. Is communicated to applicable ser -
vice providers in the required format, 
if applicable.
7.e.5.J. OA
Intent Statements
Persons served, families/support systems, and 
personnel have opportunities to talk about end-
of-life issues and participate in planning of 
memorial services and creating end-of-life 
protocols.
Examples
Families/support systems are involved in the 
development of advance directives and in identi-
fying the extent to which medical intervention is 
to be administered.
Whenever possible, no one dies alone. Support 
and presence are planned for each individual 
served so that the person does not die alone.
The person served and the family/support system 
are interviewed about preferences for the dying 
process (e.g., five wishes, music, individuals pres-
ent, preparation and notification, comfort items, 
and spiritual needs), and care planning includes 
these preferences.
Memorial gardens may be developed on the 
organization’s property in remembrance 
of those lost.
Memorials that reflect persons served who 
have died may be evident throughout the 
organization.
Do-not-resuscitate (DNR) orders are known and 
strictly adhered to. Efforts are made to clarify any 

SECTION 5.J. OLDER ADULTS
356 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
issues related to an individual’s end-of-life wishes 
to avoid any misunderstanding on the part of 
personnel and/or families/support systems.
5.J. 8. The environment is configured appropri-
ately to meet the needs of older adults, 
including the: 
8.5.J. OA
a. Design/layout.
8.a.5.J. OA
b. Furniture.
8.b.5.J. OA
c. Equipment and supplies.
8.c.5.J. OA
Intent Statements
The program considers issues of lighting, carpets, 
handrails, toilets, placement and types of furnish-
ings, as well as equipment needed to ensure the 
safety of persons served such as walkers, wheel-
chairs, raised toilet seats, incontinence supplies, 
pocket talkers, etc.
5.J. 9. The organization implements a policy 
and procedure for obtaining criminal 
background checks on all personnel 
providing direct services to older adults.
9.5.J. OA
Intent Statements
Direct support workers are appropriately 
screened before being allowed to provide 
services.
Examples
Background checks may include fingerprinting, 
FBI criminal history checks, child abuse and 
neglect registry, sex offender registries, or other 
appropriate methods available.
5.J. 10. The program provides documented 
training to direct service personnel on 
topics unique to working with older 
adults:
10.5.J. OA
a. At:
10.a.5.J. OA
(1) Orientation.
10.a.(1)5.J. OA
(2) Regular intervals.
10.a.(2)5.J. OA
b. That includes, at a minimum, the 
following topics:
10.b.5.J. OA
(1) Addiction.
10.b.(1)5.J. OA
(2) Aging.
10.b.(2)5.J. OA
(3) Bereavement.
10.b.(3)5.J. OA
(4) Cognitive decline.
10.b.(4)5.J. OA
(5) Interaction between behavioral 
health issues and comorbidities.
10.b.(5)5.J. OA
(6) Mental illness.
10.b.(6)5.J. OA
(7) Respecting autonomy.
10.b.(7)5.J. OA
(8) Substance use.
10.b.(8)5.J. OA
(9) Other, as appropriate.
10.b.(9)5.J. OA
Intent Statements
Direct service personnel are those who interact 
with persons served for the purpose of providing 
or supporting a service in a program.
Examples
10.b.(2) There are many typical age-related 
changes that may occur in older adults, such as 
cognitive decline, dementia, degenerative dis-
eases, decreases in sexual activity and intimacy, 
organ failures, and incontinence. Personnel are 
knowledgeable in typical and atypical symptoms.
10.b.(3) Personnel are able to distinguish 
between loss, grief, and depression. They are also 
skilled at recognizing and caring for their own 
issues of loss that can be associated with the 
death of persons served.
5.J. 11. When a person served dies, opportuni-
ties are provided to other persons in the 
program, families/support systems, and 
personnel to:
11.5.J. OA
a. Express grief and remembrance.
11.a.5.J. OA
b. Develop and participate in:
11.b.5.J. OA
(1) Memorial services.
11.b.(1)5.J. OA
(2) Memorial rituals.
11.b.(2)5.J. OA
(3) Other forms of grief expression, 
as desired.
11.b.(3)5.J. OA
Examples
Some organizations choose not to have memorial 
services, but may provide opportunities for per-
sonnel to express their grief by supporting them 
so they may attend the funeral of a person served.

SECTION 5.J. OLDER ADULTS
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 357
5.J. 12. The program:
12.5.J. OA
a. Identifies an indicator to measure 
engagement of the persons served 
in
 its services.
12.a.5.J. OA
b. At least annually addresses:
12.b.5.J. OA
(1) Performance in relationship to an 
established target.
12.b.(1)5.J. OA
(2) Trends.
12.b.(2)5.J. OA
(3) Actions for improvement.
12.b.(3)5.J. OA
(4) Results of performance improve -
ment plans.
12.b.(4)5.J. OA
(5) Necessary education and training 
of:
12.b.(5)5.J. OA
(a) Persons served.
12.b.(5)(a)5.J. OA
(b) Families/support systems.
12.b.(5)(b)5.J. OA
(c) Personnel.
12.b.(5)(c)5.J. OA
Intent Statements
Older adults often have difficulty engaging in 
services for a variety of reasons, which may 
include transportation challenges, stigma issues, 
and inability to establish rapport with program 
personnel. Based on the type and intensity of 
services offered, the program identifies a method 
to define meaningful engagement in service and 
collects data for the purpose of performance 
improvement.



2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 359
SECTION 6
Certified Community Behavioral  
Health Clinic Program Standards
Description
A Certified Community Behavioral Health Clinic 
(CCBHC) is a form of provider within the 
U.S., established by the Excellence in Mental 
Health Act. CCBHCs are designed to provide 
whole-person care by integrating physical health 
with a comprehensive range of mental health and 
substance use disorder services to vulnerable 
individuals. Whole-person care means consider-
ing all aspects of a person’s health, including their 
physical, mental, and behavioral health, as well as 
their socioeconomic status, housing situation, 
and other social determinants of health, which 
can exacerbate health issues.
CCBHCs offer nine types of services, including:
n Crisis mental health services
n Screening, assessment, and diagnosis
n Person-centered treatment planning
n Outpatient mental health and substance use 
services
n Outpatient clinic primary care screening and 
monitoring
n Targeted case management
n Psychiatric rehabilitation services
n Peer support, counselor services, and family 
supports
n Intensive, community-based mental health 
care to persons served (includes members of 
the armed forces and Veterans)
Services must be person-centered and family-
centered, trauma-informed, and recovery-
oriented. In addition, CCBHCs are required to 
expand service hours, provide timely access for 
routine needs, provide daily 24-hour access to 
crisis management services, provide services 
beyond the walls of the clinic (for example, in 
clients’ homes and elsewhere in the community), 
and maintain partnerships with a range of health 
and social service providers to facilitate referrals 
and care coordination. CCBHCs can partner 
with Designated Collaborating Organizations 
(DCOs) to provide some of the required services. 
DCOs are entities that are not directly supervised 
by a CCBHC but have a formal relationship with 
a CCBHC to provide specified services. CCBHCs 
that engage DCOs maintain clinical responsibil-
ity for services the DCO provides to CCBHC 
clients.
Applicable Standards
The standards in this section are based on 
SAMHSA’s 2023 certification criteria for a 
Certified Community Behavioral Health Clinic. 
An organization seeking accreditation in this 
manual as a Certified Community Behavioral 
Health Clinic must apply all standards in this 
section as well as all standards in Sections 1 
and 2 referenced in this section. (The standards 
referenced in Sections 1 and 2 are noted by 
Apply Standard to indicate the concept in the 
CCBHC standard is addressed by the standard 
identified.) It must also seek accreditation in at 
least one program from Sections 3 or 4.
 
Field Category
An organization seeking accreditation in this 
manual as a Certified Community Behavioral 
Health Clinic in this manual must select the 
Integrated SUD/Mental Health field category 
(see Section 3 for description) when applying 
for accreditation of the CCBHC.

SECTION 6.A. STAFFING
360 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
A. Staffing
General Staffing Requirements
6.A. 1. A community needs assessment:
1.A. Staffing
a. Addresses the following elements:
1.a.A. Staffing
(1) Description of the physical 
boundaries and size of the 
service area, including identifi-
cation of sites where services 
are
 delivered by the CCBHC, 
including through DCOs.
1.a.(1)A. Staffing
(2) Prevalence of mental health and 
substance use conditions and 
related needs in the service 
area
 (such as rate of suicide 
and overdose).
1.a.(2)A. Staffing
(3) Economic factors and social 
determinants of health affecting 
the population’s access to health 
services (such as percentage of 
the population with incomes 
below poverty level, access to 
transportation, and stable 
housing). 
1.a.(3)A. Staffing
(4) Cultures and languages of the 
populations residing in the 
service area.
1.a.(4)A. Staffing
(5) Identification of underserved 
population(s) within the service 
area.
1.a.(5)A. Staffing
(6) Requirements of certifying 
states, if applicable.
1.a.(6)A. Staffing
(7) Input regarding:
1.a.(7)A. Staffing
(a) Cultural, linguistic, physical 
health, and behavioral 
health treatment needs.
1.a.(7)(a)A. Staffing
(b) Evidence-based practices.
1.a.(7)(b)A. Staffing
(c) Behavioral health crisis 
services.
1.a.(7)(c)A. Staffing
(d) Access and availability of 
CCBHC services including 
days, times, locations, and 
telehealth options.
1.a.(7)(d)A. Staffing
(e) Potential barriers to care 
(such as geographic barriers, 
transportation challenges, 
economic hardship, lack 
of culturally responsive 
services, and workforce 
shortages).
1.a.(7)(e)A. Staffing
(8) Input from the following entities, 
if they are in the CCBHC service 
area:
1.a.(8)A. Staffing
(a) People with lived experience 
of mental health and 
substance use conditions 
and individuals who have 
received/are receiving 
services from the clinic 
conducting the needs 
assessment.
1.a.(8)(a)A. Staffing
(b) Health centers, including 
Federally Qualified Health 
Centers (FQHCs) in the 
service area.
1.a.(8)(b)A. Staffing
(c) Local health departments.
1.a.(8)(c)A. Staffing
(d) Inpatient psychiatric facili -
ties, inpatient acute care 
hospitals, and hospital 
outpatient clinics.
1.a.(8)(d)A. Staffing
(e) One or more Department of 
Veterans Affairs facilities.
1.a.(8)(e)A. Staffing
(f) Representatives from local 
K–12 school systems.
1.a.(8)(f)A. Staffing
(g) Crisis response partners 
(such as hospital emergency 
departments, emergency 
responders, crisis stabiliza
-
tion settings, crisis call 
centers and warmlines).
1.a.(8)(g)A. Staffing
b. Is documented.
1.b.A. Staffing
c. Is updated every three years.
1.c.A. Staffing
Intent Statements
The needs assessment should be comprehensive 
in nature, utilizing a variety of stakeholder input. 
Each element should be included in the final 
assessment.
