6.A. 2. Based on the community needs assess-
ment, the CCBHC:
2.A. Staffing
a. Develops and documents a staffing 
plan that meets the needs of the 
community.
2.a.A. Staffing
b. Updates the staffing plan at least 
every three years.
2.b.A. Staffing
Intent Statements
The staffing plan for the CCBHC should be 
documented for all organizations. For a CCBHC 
SAMHSA expansion grantee, the staffing plan 
should show how it reflects additions based on 
the needs of the community.

ment, the CCBHC demonstrates that

3.A. Staffing
a. Meet the needs of the population 
served.
3.a.A. Staffing
b. Provide the services required of a 
CCBHC.

the management team of the CCBHC

4.A. Staffing
a. A CEO/project director.
4.a.A. Staffing

4.b.A. Staffing
(1) A psychiatrist.
4.b.(1)A. Staffing
(2) Responsible for:
4.b.(2)A. Staffing
(a) Providing guidance regard -
ing behavioral health clinical 
service delivery.
4.b.(2)(a)A. Staffing
(b) Ensuring the quality of the 
medical component of care.
4.b.(2)(b)A. Staffing
(c) Providing guidance to foster 
the integration and coord-
ination of behavioral health 
and primary care.
4.b.(2)(c)A. Staffing
c. Others, as needed.
4.c.A. Staffing

not a psychiatrist:
5.A. Staffing
a. The medical director:
5.a.A. Staffing
(1) Is a medically-trained behavioral 
health care professional with 
prescriptive authority.
5.a.(1)A. Staffing
(2) Demonstrates appropriate edu -
cation, licensure, and experience 
in psychopharmacology.
5.a.(2)A. Staffing
(3) Can prescribe and manage medi -
cations independently, pursuant 
to state law.

b. Psychiatric consultation is obtained 
regarding:
5.b.A. Staffing
(1) Behavioral health clinical service 
delivery.
5.b.(1)A. Staffing
(2) Quality of the medical compon -
ent of care.
5.b.(2)A. Staffing
(3) Integration and coordination of 
behavioral health and primary 
care.
5.b.(3)A. Staffing
Intent Statements
If the medical director is not a psychiatrist, the 
person filling the role should have, through train-
ing/experience, the ability to lead a behavioral 
health medical team.
5.b. Psychiatric consultation can be obtained 
from any licensed clinician for whom their 
experience and scope of practice is appropriate.

Intent Statements

8.b.(2)A. Staffing

Intent Statements
The staffing plan document can take many forms, 
however, it should identify how each element is 
covered in the plan.
6.A. 9. If the CCBHC does not have a provider 
who can prescribe methadone for the 
treatment of opioid use disorder directly, 
it:
9.A. Staffing
a. Has a written agreement with an 
opioid treatment program that estab-
lishes care coordination expectations.
9.a.A. Staffing
b. Coordinates care to ensure access to 
methadone for appropriate persons 
served. 
9.b.A. Staffing
Intent Statements
When the CCBHC refers persons served to an OTP , 
there must be a written agreement that identifies 
the care coordination expectations for each party.
6.A. 10. The medical director of the CCBHC 
demonstrates experience with the treat-
ment of SUD, or the CCBHC has addiction 
medicine physicians or specialists on 
staff or by arrangement to consult on 
addiction medicine for the medical 
director and clinical staff. 
10.A. Staffing
Intent Statements
Experience in this standard refers to direct profes-
sional work in SUD treatment. When the medical 
director does not possess that direct experience, 
then the CCBHC arranges for consultation with an 
addiction medicine specialist physician.
6.A. 11. The CCBHC arranges for, or refers to, 
other providers as needed to meet:
11.A. Staffing
a. The needs of persons served.
11.a.A. Staffing
b. Program requirements for care 
coordination and scope of services. 
11.b.A. Staffing
Examples
Based on the needs of the persons served, there 
may be a variety of specialists needed. Examples 
could include cardiologists, endocrinologists, 
primary care specialists, pulmonary specialists, 
etc. The CCBHC anticipates these needs and has 
arrangements for referral and information 
sharing.

Examples

(AAC) devices: These devices help people with 
speech or language impairments communicate 
effectively. Examples of AAC devices include 
speech-generating devices, communication 
boards, and picture symbols.
Sign language interpreters: Sign language inter-
preters translate spoken language into sign

General Requirements of Access and

6.B. 1. Apply Standard 1.H.1.
1.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 2. Based on the community needs assess-
ment, the CCBHC provides services:
2.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services

population served, including some

2.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services

accessibility needs, and homes.
2.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 3. To facilitate access to services in align-
ment with the person-centered and 
family-centered treatment plan, the 
CCBHC provides, consistent with funding 
sources, transportation or transportation 
vouchers for persons served.
3.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Examples
The organization may have vehicles and trans-
portation directly available to person served. 
When that is not the case, bus passes, or ride-
sharing app vouchers can be considered. There 
may even be coordination with family members 
or other persons receiving services. When the 
CCBHC does not otherwise have any access to 
fund transportation, other avenues for assistance 
may be sought through community resources.
6.B. 4. Apply Standard 2.A.21.
4.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services

b. Initial evaluation.

Intent Statements
The CCBHC’s process should include specifics of 
what is included as well as the expected timelines

6.B. 9. Preliminary triage of each person new to

9.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Is conducted at first contact.
9.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Includes a risk assessment to 
determine:
9.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(1) Acuity of needs.
9.b.(1)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(2) Next steps, including:
9.b.(2)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(a) If an emergency crisis is 
identified:
9.b.(2)(a)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(i)  Immediate actions to 
reduce or remove risk 
of harm.
9.b.(2)(a)(i)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(ii) Facilitation of sub -
sequent outpatient 
follow up.
9.b.(2)(a)(ii)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(b) If an urgent need is 
identified:
9.b.(2)(b)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(i) Provision of clinical 
services.
9.b.(2)(b)(i)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(ii) Completion of an initial 
evaluation within one 
business day of the 
request for services. 
9.b.(2)(b)(ii)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(c) If routine needs are 
identified:
9.b.(2)(c)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(i) Provision of clinical 
services.
9.b.(2)(c)(i)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(ii) Completion of an initial 
evaluation within ten 
business days of the 
request for services. 
9.b.(2)(c)(ii)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Intent Statements
The CCBHC ensures there is a consistent process 
for triage and determination of acute needs.
6.B. 10. If emergency or urgent needs are 
identified:
10.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. An initial evaluation may be con -
ducted by phone or via technology. 
10.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Once the emergency is resolved, 
the individual is seen in person at 
the next encounter and the initial 
evaluation is reviewed.

6.B. 11. A comprehensive evaluation for each 
person new to  services:
11.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Is completed within 60 calendar days 
of the first request for services. 
11.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Apply Standard 2.B.12.h. 
11.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Intent Statements
The comprehensive evaluation is the sum of all 
assessments conducted by the CCBHC in order 
to formulate the individualized, person-centered 
plan.
6.B. 12. Apply Standard 2.C.3.
12.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 13. For persons already receiving services 
from the CCBHC, the CCBHC:
13.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Provides an appointment within ten 
business days for persons seeking 
routine outpatient clinical services.
13.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Takes immediate action for persons 
who present with an emergency/
crisis need, including immediate 
crisis
 response if necessary.
13.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Provides clinical services within one 
business day for persons who present 
with an urgent, non-emergency need, 
unless the person prefers a later time.
13.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Intent Statements
The CCBHC retains capacity to provide routine 
and crisis services to persons based on individual 
needs.
24/7 Access to Crisis Management 
Services
6.B. 14.  Crisis management services are 
available 24/7.
14.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 15. The CCBHC:
15.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Implements policies and written 
procedures that address the pro-
vision of crisis services, including:
15.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(1) Prevention.
15.a.(1)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(2) Response.
15.a.(2)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(3) Postvention.
15.a.(3)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Makes information on crisis services 
available to the public. 
15.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Examples
15.b. CCBHCs can share information on crisis 
services available through a variety of mecha-
nisms, such as on its website, postings in the 
organization’s waiting areas, and in handouts/
client handbooks.
6.B. 16. The CCBHC provides documented edu-
cation to persons served about:
16.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Crisis planning.
16.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Psychiatric advance directives.
16.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. How to access crisis services (such as 
the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline and 
other area hotlines and warmlines).
16.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
d. Overdose prevention, if preliminary 
triage indicates the person is at risk.
16.d.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Intent Statements
Although the CCBHC can provide this education 
through a variety of methods, this crisis edu-
cation should be documented for all persons 
served.
16.d. When the CCBHC does not educate all per-
sons with a substance use disorder on overdose 
prevention, the organization identifies a clear 
protocol that ensures consistent education to 
those that present any reasonable risk.
6.B. 17. The CCBHC:
17.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Has a relationship with local hospital 
emergency departments to meet 
the needs of people who are in 
psychiatric crisis.
17.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Implements protocols to meet 
the needs of people who go to 
the emergency department.
17.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services

SECTION 6.B. AVAILABILITY AND ACCESSIBILITY OF SERVICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 367
Intent Statements
The nature of the relationship with local emer-
gency departments should be documented 
formally whenever possible. The protocols for 
meeting the needs of persons served can be 
formalized in the agreements that articulate 
what the emergency department can expect 
from the CCBHC.
6.B. 18. The CCBHC implements protocols for 
crisis response that:
18.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Maximize the delivery of recovery-
oriented treatment and services.
18.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Address the involvement of law 
enforcement.
18.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Minimize contact with law enforce -
ment and the criminal justice system 
while promoting individual and 
public safety.
18.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 19. Following a psychiatric emergency or 
crisis, the CCBHC ensures a crisis plan is: 
19.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Updated in conjunction with the 
person served.
19.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Maintained.
19.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Followed to prevent/de-escalate 
future crisis situations.
19.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
No Refusal of Services Due to Inability 
to Pay
6.B. 20. The CCBHC:
20.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Provides behavioral health services 
regardless of an individual’s ability 
to pay.
20.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Reduces or waives fees/payment for 
services as needed.
20.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Makes its sliding fee discount sched -
ule for services readily accessible to 
persons served and families:
20.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(1) On its website.
20.c.(1)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(2) In the CCBHC waiting room.
20.c.(2)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(3) In languages/formats appropri -
ate for individuals with limited 
English proficiency, literacy 
barriers, or disabilities.
20.c.(3)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 21. The CCBHC’s fee schedule(s):
21.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Conforms to applicable statutory 
and administrative requirements.
21.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Absent statutory or administrative 
requirements:
21.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(1) Is based on locally prevailing 
rates or charges.
21.b.(1)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
(2) Includes reasonable costs of 
operation.
21.b.(2)B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Intent Statements
21.b. When the CCBHC establishes its own fee 
structure absent of statutory or administrative 
requirements, it can demonstrate its process for 
fee determination after analysis of prevailing 
rates and assessment of costs.
6.B. 22. Policies and written procedures address 
the CCBHC’s sliding fee discount sched-
ule including:
22.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Eligibility. 
22.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Implementation.
22.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Equal application for all individuals 
seeking services.
22.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  

SECTION 6.C. CARE COORDINATION
368 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Provision of Services Regardless of 
Residences
6.B. 23.  The CCBHC provides behavioral health 
services regardless of an individual’s 
place of residence, homelessness, or 
lack
 of permanent address.
23.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
  
6.B. 24. The CCBHC provides the following 
services, regardless of an individual’s 
place of residence:
24.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Crisis response.
24.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Crisis evaluation.
24.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
c. Crisis stabilization.
24.c.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Examples
The CCBHC is prepared to respond to crisis 
needs of persons served in a variety of settings 
and locations. The CCBHC’s personnel are 
trained to ensure their safety and the safety of 
persons served.
6.B. 25. The CCBHC implements protocols to 
address the ongoing treatment needs 
of persons served:
25.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
a. Directly or through referral to pro -
viders where individuals reside.
25.a.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
b. Including the use of technology in 
accordance with the preferences of 
the person served.
25.b.B. Availability and Accessibility of Services
Examples
Individuals may present for services at a CCBHC 
in locations where they do not have permanent 
residences and may return to their home loca-
tions, or may leave the local area where it is not 
convenient to continue in-person services. The 
CCBHC may work with these individuals to refer 
to local providers or may continue to provide 
services remotely utilizing information and 
communication technologies.
C. Care Coordination
General Requirements of Care 
Coordination
6.C. 1. Based on the person-centered and 
family-centered treatment plan, the 
CCBHC coordinates care for persons 
served, including, as necessary, 
coordination with:
1.C. Care Coordination
a. Physical health services (both 
acute and chronic).
1.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Behavioral health services.
1.b.C. Care Coordination
c. Social services.
1.c.C. Care Coordination
d. Housing.
1.d.C. Care Coordination
e. Educational systems.
1.e.C. Care Coordination
f. Employment opportunities.
1.f.C. Care Coordination
g. Criminal/juvenile justice.
1.g.C. Care Coordination
h. Child welfare.
1.h.C. Care Coordination
Examples
CCBHCs develop a vast array of relationships 
with health and human service organizations 
in their local area to meet the needs of persons 
served. The CCBHC obtains releases of infor-
mation in order to communicate with other 
providers to effectively coordinate care.
6.C. 2. Apply Standards 1.E.1. and 2.G.1.
2.C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 3. Apply Standard 2.G.1.
3.C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 4. The CCBHC:
4.C. Care Coordination
a. Assists persons served and families 
of children/youth who are referred 
to external providers or resources 
to obtain an appointment.
4.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Tracks participation in services to 
ensure coordination and receipt of 
supports.
4.b.C. Care Coordination
Intent Statements
The CCBHC is actively involved in processes that 
ensures external appointments are obtained and 
when there are challenges or barriers to access, 

SECTION 6.C. CARE COORDINATION
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 369
seeks to remove those barriers. The CCBHC man-
ages the referral and care coordination processes 
and does not merely offer referrals for needed 
services.
6.C. 5. In accordance with the preferences of the 
person served and their care needs, the 
CCBHC coordinates care in collaboration 
with:
5.C. Care Coordination
a. Family members/caregivers.
5.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Other supports identified by the 
person served.
5.b.C. Care Coordination
Examples
The CCBHC engages families and/or other 
support persons in care whenever possible in 
order to maximize community recovery.
6.C. 6. To identify the preferences of the person 
served in the event of psychiatric or sub
-
stance use crisis, the CCBHC:
6.C. Care Coordination
a. Develops a crisis plan with each 
person served.
6.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Shares the crisis plan with the person 
served.
6.b.C. Care Coordination
c. Educates the person served about the 
use of crisis resources (such as the 
National Suicide and Crisis Lifeline, 
local hotlines, warmlines, mobile 
crisis, and stabilization services) in 
the event the provider is not available 
at
 the time of crisis.
6.c.C. Care Coordination
d. If desired by th e person served:
6.d.C. Care Coordination
(1) Develops a psychiatric advanced 
directive.
6.d.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Enters a copy of the psychiatric 
advanced directive in the elec-
tronic health record so that the 
information is available to pro-
viders in emergency care settings 
where those electronic health 
records are accessible.
6.d.(2)C. Care Coordination
Intent Statements
A crisis plan is documented in the record of the 
person served, and is shared with the person and 
families/supports as appropriate.
6.C. 7. The CCBHC:
7.C. Care Coordination
a. Documents medications prescribed 
for the person served by other 
providers.
7.a.C. Care Coordination
b. If unable to obtain necessary 
consents, documents the attempts 
that have been made.
7.b.C. Care Coordination
c. With appropriate consent to release 
information, provides, to the extent 
necessary for safe and quality care, 
medication information to other pro
-
viders not affiliated with the CCBHC.
7.c.C. Care Coordination
Examples
If the CCBHC is utilizing an electronic prescrib-
ing method that is connected to a PDMP in 
which information is available and shared, this 
can be used to satisfy this standard.
6.C. 8. The CCBHC ensures persons served may 
exercise freedom of choice regarding 
their CCBHC, DCO, and other providers.
8.C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 9. The CCBHC assists persons served and 
families to:
9.C. Care Coordination
a. Access benefits, including Medicaid.
9.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Enroll in programs or supports that 
may benefit them.
9.b.C. Care Coordination
  
Care Coordination and Other Health 
Information Systems
6.C. 10. The CCBHC’s Health Information Tech-
nology System (HITS) includes electronic 
health records.
10.C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 11. Using its HITS and related technology 
tools, the CCBHC:
11.C. Care Coordination
a. Conducts activities such as:
11.a.C. Care Coordination
(1) Population health management.
11.a.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Quality improvement.
11.a.(2)C. Care Coordination
(3) Quality measurement and 
reporting.
11.a.(3)C. Care Coordination

370 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
(4) Reducing disparities.
11.a.(4)C. Care Coordination
(5) Outreach.
11.a.(5)C. Care Coordination
(6) Research.
11.a.(6)C. Care Coordination
b. Ensures appropriate protections are 
in place.
11.b.C. Care Coordination
c. If it uses federal funding to acquire, 
upgrade, or implement technology 
to support these activities, utilizes 
nationally recognized, HHS-adopted 
standards to enable health informa-
tion exchange.
11.c.C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 12. The CCBHC uses technology:
12.C. Care Coordination
a. That is certified to current criteria 
under the ONC Health IT Certification 
Program.
12.a.C. Care Coordination
b. For the following clinical practice 
and care delivery activities:
12.b.C. Care Coordination
(1) Capturing health information

mation (e.g., race, ethnicity, 
preferred language, sexual and

status).
12.b.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Supporting care coordination 
by sending and receiving sum-
maries of care records.
12.b.(2)C. Care Coordination
(3) Providing persons served with

download, or transmit their 
health information.
12.b.(3)C. Care Coordination
(4) Providing evidence-based 
clinical decision support.
12.b.(4)C. Care Coordination
(5) Conducting electronic 
prescribing.
12.b.(5)C. Care Coordination

compliance with:
13.C. Care Coordination
a. HIPAA
13.a.C. Care Coordination
b. 42 CFR Part 2.
13.b.C. Care Coordination
c. Other applicable federal and state 
privacy laws, including privacy 
requirements specific to the care 
of
 minors.
13.c.C. Care Coordination  
6.C. 14. The CCBHC implements a plan within 
two years from CCBHC certification or 
submission of attestation that:
14.C. Care Coordination
a. Focuses on ways to improve care 
coordination between the CCBHC 
and all DCOs.
14.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Includes information on how the 
CCBHC can support electronic health 
information exchange to improve 
care transition to and from the 
CCBHC.
14.b.C. Care Coordination
Intent Statements
This is a formal written plan that articulates the 
activities it engages in to improve care coord-
ination activities and the improvement of care 
transitions.
6.C. 15. To support integrated evaluation plan-
ning, treatment, and care coordination, 
the CCBHC:
15.C. Care Coordination
a. Works with DCOs to integrate 
clinically-relevant treatment 
records generated by the DCO for 
the person served and incorporate 
them into the CCBHC health record.
15.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Makes available to DCOs all clinically 
relevant treatment records main-
tained by the CCBHC in compliance 
with federal and/or state laws gov-
erning the sharing of health records.
15.b.C. Care Coordination
  
Care Coordination Partnerships

tocols regarding care coordination 
for persons served including written 
agreements with:
16.C. Care Coordination
a. Federally Qualified Health Centers 
(FQHCs) and, as applicable, Rural 
Health Clinics (RHCs) to provide 
health care services that are not 
provided directly through the CCBHC.
16.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Other primary care providers, 
including, but not limited to, FQHC 
Look-Alikes and Community Health 
Centers.
16.b.C. Care Coordination

Intent Statements
The CCBHC will have written agreements, such 
as Memorandum of Understanding (MOU,) with 
appropriate primary care providers that articulate 
responsibilities of the partners.
6.C. 17. The CCBHC has written agreements that 
establish care coordination expectations 
with programs that:
17.C. Care Coordination
a. Can provide:
17.a.C. Care Coordination
(1) Inpatient psychiatric treatment.
17.a.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Medical withdrawal manage -
ment services for SUDs.

programs.

17.b.C. Care Coordination
(1) Admitted to programs providing 
the services listed above.
17.b.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Discharged from the programs 
providing the services listed 
above, unless there is a formal 
transfer of care to a non-CCBHC 
entity.
17.b.(2)C. Care Coordination
  
6.C. 18. The CCBHC implements protocols and 
written procedures:
18.C. Care Coordination
a. For transitioning individuals from 
the following programs to a safe 
community setting:
18.a.C. Care Coordination
(1) Emergency departments.
18.a.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Inpatient psychiatric programs.
18.a.(2)C. Care Coordination
(3) Medically monitored withdrawal 
management services.
18.a.(3)C. Care Coordination
(4) Residential or inpatient facilities 
that serve children and youth.
18.a.(4)C. Care Coordination
b. That include:
18.b.C. Care Coordination
(1) Transfer of health records 
of services received.
18.b.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Active follow-up after discharge.
18.b.(2)C. Care Coordination
(3) As appropriate, a plan for suicide 
prevention and safety, overdose 
prevention, and provision for 
peer services.
18.b.(3)C. Care Coordination
Intent Statements
The CCBHC has protocols and written procedures 
that describe how it manages care transitions 
from each setting that ensures safety of the per-
son served, how information is shared, and how 
follow-up is conducted. When crisis plans need 
to be updated, they are updated in response to 
new needs identified.
6.C. 19. The CCBHC has established relationships 
with community or regional services, 
supports, and providers that:
19.C. Care Coordination
a. Support joint planning for care and 
services.
19.a.C. Care Coordination
b. Provide opportunities to identify 
individuals in need of services.

c. Enable the CCBHC to provide services 
in community settings.
19.c.C. Care Coordination
d. Enable the CCBHC to provide support 
and consultation with a community 
partner.
19.d.C. Care Coordination
e. Support CCBHC outreach and 
engagement efforts. 
19.e.C. Care Coordination
f. By statute include:
19.f.C. Care Coordination
(1) Schools.
19.f.(1)C. Care Coordination
(2) Child welfare agencies.
19.f.(2)C. Care Coordination

agencies and facilities (including 
drug, mental health, Veterans, 
and other specialty courts).
19.f.(3)C. Care Coordination

(5) State licensed and nationally 
accredited child placement 
agencies for therapeutic foster 
care services.
19.f.(5)C. Care Coordination
(6) Other social and human services.
19.f.(6)C. Care Coordination
g. Include the 988 Suicide and Crisis 
Lifeline call center serving the area 
in which the CCBHC is located.
19.g.C. Care Coordination

23.C. Care Coordination
  
D. Scope of Services
General Service Provisions
6.D. 1. The CCBHC:
1.D. Scope of Services
a. Is responsible for the provision, either 
directly or through DCO agreements, 
of all care specified in Protecting 
Access to Medicare Act (PAMA), 
including:
1.a.D. Scope of Services
(1) Crisis services.
1.a.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Screening, assessment, and 
diagnosis.
1.a.(2)D. Scope of Services
(3) Person-centered and family-
centered treatment planning.
1.a.(3)D. Scope of Services
(4) Outpatient behavioral health 
services.
1.a.(4)D. Scope of Services
(5) Outpatient primary care screen -
ing and monitoring.
1.a.(5)D. Scope of Services
(6) Targeted case management.
1.a.(6)D. Scope of Services
(7) Psychiatric rehabilitation.
1.a.(7)D. Scope of Services
(8) Peer and family supports.
1.a.(8)D. Scope of Services
(9) Intensive community-based 
outpatient behavioral health 
care
 for members of the United 
States Armed Forces and 
Veterans.
1.a.(9)D. Scope of Services
b. Delivers directly the majority (51% 
or more) of encounters across the 
required services, with the exception 
of crisis services, rather than through 
DCO agreements.
1.b.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
There should be evidence that all of the required 
services are available to persons served by the 
CCBHC, and the majority of services is provided 
by the CCBHC directly with the exception of crisis 
services, if provided through DCO arrangement.

SECTION 6.D. SCOPE OF SERVICES
374 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
6.D. 2. The CCBHC:
2.D. Scope of Services
a. Has in place DCO agreements to 
deliver services not directly available 
through the CCBHC.
2.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Based on the needs of the persons 
served, refers to outside specialty 
services not available through the 
CCBHC or DCOs.
2.b.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
2.a. DCO arrangements are not merely referral 
sources, but formal documented agreements 
defining specific services to be provided with 
accountability measures in place that allows the 
CCBHC to provide oversight and direction.
6.D. 3. The CCBHC’s grievance procedures:
3.D. Scope of Services
a. Include services directly provided by 
the CCBHC and those provided via 
DCO agreement.
3.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Are made available to the persons 
served.
3.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Satisfy the requirements of:
3.c.D. Scope of Services
(1) Medicaid.
3.c.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Other applicable grievance 
requirements, such as those 
mandated by relevant accredit-
ing entities or state authorities.
3.c.(2)D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 4. The CCBHC demonstrates a process to 
ensure that the DCOs with which it has 
agreements meet the mandatory aspects 
of these criteria.
4.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
The CCBHC engages in activities designed to 
ensure that providers in DCO arrangements meet 
all required aspects of care including, but not lim-
ited to, regulatory requirements, person-centered 
and family-centered care, trauma-informed care, 
grievance procedures, information sharing, and 
coordination of care.
Requirements of Person-Centered and 
Family-Centered Care
6.D. 5. The CCBHC’s services, including those 
supplied by its DCOs, demonstrate:
5.D. Scope of Services
a. Person-centered and family-centered, 
recovery-oriented care that:
5.a.D. Scope of Services
(1) Is responsive to the race, ethnic -
ity, sexual orientation, and 
gender identity of the person 
served.
5.a.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Recognizes the cultural/ethnic 
needs of the person served, 
including American Indian or 
Alaska Native (AI/AN) or other 
cultural or ethnic groups for 
whom access to traditional 
approaches or medicines may 
be
 part of CCBHC services.
5.a.(2)D. Scope of Services
b. Respect for the needs, preferences, 
and values of the person served.
5.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Involvement of the person served 
in and self-direction of services 
received.
5.c.D. Scope of Services
d. For children and youth, family-
centered, youth-guided, and 
developmentally appropriate care.
5.d.D. Scope of Services
e. A shared decision-making model 
for engagement.
5.e.D. Scope of Services
  
Crisis Behavioral Health Services
6.D. 6. The CCBHC provides directly or through 
DCO agreement with a state-sanctioned, 
certified, or licensed system or network 
crisis behavioral health services that 
include:
6.D. Scope of Services
a. Emergency crisis intervention 
services (crisis call/contact centers).
6.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Mobile crisis services.
6.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Crisis receiving/stabilization services.
6.c.D. Scope of Services
  

SECTION 6.D. SCOPE OF SERVICES
2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 375
6.D. 7. The CCBHC:
7.D. Scope of Services
a. Provides or coordinates with crisis 
call centers that meet 988 suicide 
and
 crisis lifeline standards for risk 
assessment and engagement of indi-
viduals at imminent risk of suicide.
7.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Participates in:
7.b.D. Scope of Services
(1) State, regional, or local air traffic 
control (ATC) systems that pro-
vide coordination of crisis care.
7.b.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Service capacity registries, as 
appropriate.
7.b.(2)D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 8. Mobile crisis teams:
8.D. Scope of Services
a. Are available 24/7 to respond to 
adults, children, youth, and families 
in the CCBHC’s service area.
8.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Arrive in-person within one hour 
(one hours in rural and frontier 
settings) from the time that they 
are dispatched.
8.b.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
When the CCBHC has a DCO arrangement with 
a state sanctioned crisis provider that delivers 
mobile crisis services, the CCBHC coordinates 
with that provider and is not required to directly 
provide these services. However, the CCBHC 
should ensure these services are provided by 
the crisis provider.
6.D. 9. The CCBHC may utilize technology to:
9.D. Scope of Services
a. Connect individuals in crisis to quali -
fied mental health providers while 
they are waiting for a mobile crisis 
team to arrive.
9.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Provide crisis care to individuals 
when remote travel distances 
make
 the two hour response time 
unachievable.
9.b.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 10. For voluntary individuals crisis receiving/
stabilization services include urgent 
care/walk-in mental health and SUD 
services that:
10.D. Scope of Services
a. Identify the individual’s immediate 
needs.
10.a.D. Scope of Services
b. De-escalate the crisis.
10.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Connect the individual to a safe and 
least-restrictive setting for ongoing 
care.
10.c.D. Scope of Services
d. Operate during hours that align with 
the community needs assessment, 
including evening hours.
10.d.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 11. Crisis receiving/stabilization services 
include:
11.D. Scope of Services
a. Suicide prevention.
11.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Suicide intervention.
11.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Services to address crises related to 
substance use including:
11.c.D. Scope of Services
(1) Risk of drug and alcohol-related 
overdose.
11.c.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Access to naloxone for indiv -
iduals who are at risk of opioid 
overdose and, as appropriate, 
to
 their family members.
11.c.(2)D. Scope of Services
(3) Support following non-fatal 
overdose once the individual 
is medically stable.
11.c.(3)D. Scope of Services
d. A protocol specifying the role of law 
enforcement during the provision of 
crisis services.
11.d.D. Scope of Services

376 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
Screening, Assessment, and Diagnosis
6.D. 12. For behavioral health conditions the 
CCBHC:
12.D. Scope of Services
a. Provides the following services 
directly or through DCO agreement:
12.a.D. Scope of Services
(1) Apply Standard 2.B.3.
12.a.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Apply Standard 2.B.12.
12.a.(2)D. Scope of Services
(3) Apply Standard 2.B.12.d.
12.a.(3)D. Scope of Services
(4) Apply Standard 2.B.10.
12.a.(4)D. Scope of Services
b. Refers persons served for specialized 
screening, assessment, and/or diag-
nostic services outside the scope of 
its expertise.
12.b.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
The screening, assessment, risk assessment and 
diagnosis of behavioral health conditions should 
be in the scope of the expertise of the CCBHC. 
Health conditions that are complex or non-
routine may be out of the scope of the CCBHC 
and performed through DCO agreement.
6.D. 13. Apply Standards 2.B.3. and 2.B.4.
13.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 14. Apply Standard 2.B.14.
14.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 15. Apply Standard 2.B.13.
15.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 16. The CCBHC uses:
16.D. Scope of Services
a. Screening and assessment tools that 
are: 
16.a.D. Scope of Services
(1) Apply Standard 2.B.4.e.
16.a.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Developmentally appropriate.
16.a.(2)D. Scope of Services
b. When appropriate, motivational 
interviewing techniques to facilitate 
engagement.
16.b.D. Scope of Services
c. Approaches that are culturally and 
linguistically appropriate for the 
person served.
16.c.D. Scope of Services
d. Tools that accommodate literacy 
levels and disabilities (e.g., hearing 
disability, cognitive limitations).
16.d.D. Scope of Services  
6.D. 17. Apply Standard 2.B.6.
17.D. Scope of Services
  
Person-Centered and Family-Centered 
Treatment Planning
6.D. 18. Apply Standards 2.B.12.d. and 2.C.4.
18.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 19. Apply Standard 2.C.1.
19.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 20. The CCBHC demonstrates a process to 
ensure that the DCOs with which it has 
agreements meet the mandatory aspects 
of person-centered and family-centered 
treatment planning.
20.D. Scope of Services
Examples
DCO agreements can define how each criteria is 
satisfied as part of the person-centered planning 
process, and how oversight is conducted.
6.D. 21. Apply Standards 2.C.1. and 2.C.2.
21.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 22. Apply Standard 2.C.7. 
22.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 23. As appropriate, consultation is sought 
during treatment planning to address 
specialty needs of the person served.
23.D. Scope of Services
Intent Statements
As CCBHC’s are required to provide as integrated 
care as is possible, the CCBHC should have access 
to a variety of specialists that can offer consulta-
tion in both behavioral healthcare and physical 
healthcare needs.

documents the person’s:
24.D. Scope of Services
a. Advance directives related to treat -
ment and crisis planning.
24.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Decision to not share their prefer -
ences, when applicable.

6.D. 25. Person- and family-centered treatment 
planning includes any other components

Outpatient Mental Health and Substance 
Use Services
6.D. 26. The CCBHC provides directly or through 
DCO agreement outpatient behavioral 
health care that includes:
26.D. Scope of Services
a. Psychopharmacological treatment.
26.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Evidence-based treatment for mental 
health and SUDs across the lifespan, 
including tailored approaches for 
adults, children, and families.
26.b.D. Scope of Services
c. SUD treatment and services based on 
the American Society for Addiction 
Medicine Levels 1 and 2.1., and 
includes treatment for tobacco use 
disorders.
26.c.D. Scope of Services
d. Referral to specialty mental health 
and SUD services as needed by 
persons served.
26.d.D. Scope of Services
e. Motivational techniques and harm 
reduction strategies for persons 
served with potentially unsafe sub
-
stance use.
26.e.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 27. The CCBHC demonstrates use of evi-
dence-based treatment practices that 
are appropriate for the phase of life 
and
 development of the person served, 
including:
27.D. Scope of Services
a. When treating children and adoles -
cents, services that are 
developmentally appropriate, youth-
guided, and family/caregiver-driven.
27.a.D. Scope of Services
b. When treating older adults, services 
that consider the person’s desires and 
functioning.
27.b.D. Scope of Services
c. When treating individuals with 
developmental or other cognitive 
disabilities, services that reflect the 
individual’s level of functioning.
27.c.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 28. Staff are trained in evidence-based treat-
ment practices that are appropriate to 
the population(s) served.
28.D. Scope of Services

Intensive, Community-Based Mental Health 
Care for Members of the Armed Forces and 
Veterans
6.D. 36. The CCBHC provides directly or through 
DCO agreement intensive, community-
based behavioral health care for:
36.D. Scope of Services
a. Armed Forces members located 50 
miles or more (or one hour’s drive 
time) from a Military Treatment 
Facility (MTF).
36.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Veterans:
36.b.D. Scope of Services
(1) Living 40 miles or more (driving 
distance) from a VA medical 
facility or as otherwise required 
by federal law.
36.b.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Care that is consistent with 
minimum clinical mental health 
guidelines promulgated by 
the
 Veterans Health Administra-
tion (VHA), including clinical 
guidelines contained in the 
Uniform Mental Health Services 
Handbook of such Administration.
36.b.(2)D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 37. The CCBHC implements written 
procedures:
37.D. Scope of Services
a. To identify whether individuals 
inquiring about services have 
served
 in the United States Military.
37.a.D. Scope of Services
b. To assist current military personnel 
as follows:
37.b.D. Scope of Services
(1) For Active Duty Service Members 
to contact their primary care 
manager regarding referrals 
outside the military treatment 
facility.
37.b.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) For Active Duty Service and 
Active Guard/Reserve Members 
who reside more than 50 miles 
(or one hour’s drive time) from 
a
 military hospital or military 
clinic enroll in TRICARE PRIME 
Remote and use the network 
primary care manager, or select 
any other authorized TRICARE 
provider as
 the primary care 
manager.
37.b.(2)D. Scope of Services
(3) For members of the Selected 
Reserves eligible for TRICARE 
Reserve Select and can schedule 
an appointment with any 
TRICARE-authorized provider, 
network or non-network.
37.b.(3)D. Scope of Services
c. To assist Veterans as follows:
37.c.D. Scope of Services
(1) To enroll in VHA for the delivery 
of health and behavioral health 
services.
37.c.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) If they decline or are ineligible 
for VHA services, to provide ser-
vices consistent with minimum 
clinical mental health guidelines 
promulgated by the VHA.
37.c.(2)D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 38. The CCBHC demonstrates how it coordi-
nates care for Veterans including care for:
38.D. Scope of Services
a. Behavioral health conditions and 
other components of health care.
38.a.D. Scope of Services
b. SUDs and other mental health con -
ditions for Veterans who experience 
both.
38.b.D. Scope of Services
  
6.D. 39. A principal behavioral health provider:
39.D. Scope of Services
a. Is assigned to each Veteran served.
39.a.D. Scope of Services
b. Is identified:
39.b.D. Scope of Services
(1) To the Veteran.
39.b.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) In the Veteran’s medical record.
39.b.(2)D. Scope of Services
(3) On a tracking database for 
those Veterans who need case 
management.
39.b.(3)D. Scope of Services
c. Is responsible for the following:
39.c.D. Scope of Services
(1) Maintaining regular contact 
with the Veteran, as clinically 
indicated.
39.c.(1)D. Scope of Services
(2) Ensuring that a psychiatrist or 
other prescriber who satisfies the 
requirements of the VHA Uniform 
Mental Health Services Hand
-
book reconciles the Veteran’s 
psychiatric medications on a 
regular basis.
39.c.(2)D. Scope of Services

(3) Incorporating input from the

rized surrogate decision

E. Quality and Other 
Reporting
Data Collection, Reporting and Tracking
6.E. 1. The CCBHC collects, reports, and tracks 
data including, but not limited to:
1.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. Characteristics of persons served.
1.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. Staffing.
1.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
c. Access to services.
1.c.E. Quality and Other Reporting
d. Use of services.
1.d.E. Quality and Other Reporting
e. Screening, prevention, and 
treatment.
1.e.E. Quality and Other Reporting
f. Care coordination.
1.f.E. Quality and Other Reporting
g. Other processes of care.
1.g.E. Quality and Other Reporting
h. Costs.
1.h.E. Quality and Other Reporting
i. Outcomes of persons served.
1.i.E. Quality and Other Reporting
Intent Statements
Reporting of these data are both internal and 
external to appropriate stakeholders of the 
CCBHC.
6.E. 2. Section 223 Demonstration CCBHCs and 
CCBHC-Es awarded SAMHSA discretion-
ary CCBHC expansion grants beginning 
in 2022 report data:
2.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. For clinic-collected measures on all 
persons served, including those 
receiving services via DCO 
agreement.
2.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. For state-collected measures on all 
Medicaid enrollees.
2.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
c. On optional clinic-collected or state-
collected measures required by the 
state.
2.c.E. Quality and Other Reporting
d. Annually, within nine months after 
the end of the measurement year.
2.d.E. Quality and Other Reporting
e. To their states and/or SAMHSA.
2.e.E. Quality and Other Reporting
  
6.E. 3. Section 223 Demonstration programs: 
3.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. Submit to CMS:
3.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
(1) All required data through T-MSIS.
3.a.(1)E. Quality and Other Reporting
(2) Annual cost reports.
3.a.(2)E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. Participate in discussions with the 
national evaluations team.
3.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
  
Continuous Quality Improvement (CQI) 
Plan
6.E. 4. The CCBHC develops, implements, and 
maintains a continuous quality improve-
ment (CQI) plan that includes: 
4.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. A process to review outcomes.
4.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. Indicators related to behavioral 
and physical health outcomes.
4.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
c. Implementation of changes to 
improve the quality and timeliness 
of
 services.
4.c.E. Quality and Other Reporting
d. A focus on improving patterns of care 
delivery (e.g., reductions in ED use, 
rehospitalization, and repeated crisis 
episodes).
4.d.E. Quality and Other Reporting
e. Involvement of the medical director 
in aspects of the CQI plan that apply 
to the quality of the medical compo-
nents of care, including coordination 
and integration with primary care.
4.e.E. Quality and Other Reporting
  
6.E. 5. The CCBHC’s CQI plan addresses:
5.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. Deaths by suicide or suicide attempts 
of persons served.
5.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. Fatal and non-fatal overdoses.
5.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
c. All-cause mortality among people 
receiving CCBHC services.
5.c.E. Quality and Other Reporting
d. 30-day hospital readmissions for psy -
chiatric or substance use reasons.
5.d.E. Quality and Other Reporting
e. Other events the state or applicable 
accreditation bodies deem appropri-
ate for examination and remediation.
5.e.E. Quality and Other Reporting
  

SECTION 6.F. ORGANIZATIONAL AUTHORITY, GOVERNANCE, AND ACCREDITATION
382 2026 Behavioral Heal th Standards Manual
6.E. 6. The CQI plan: 
6.E. Quality and Other Reporting
a. Addresses, at a minimum:
6.a.E. Quality and Other Reporting
(1) Data collected by the CCBHC.
6.a.(1)E. Quality and Other Reporting
(2) As applicable for the Section 223 
Demonstration, state-collected 
quality measures required as part 
of the Demonstration.
6.a.(2)E. Quality and Other Reporting
b. Focuses on populations experiencing 
health disparities (e.g., racial and 
ethnic groups and sexual and gender 
minorities).
6.b.E. Quality and Other Reporting
c. Addresses how the CCBHC will use 
disaggregated data from the quality 
measures and, as available, other 
data to track and improve outcomes 
for populations facing health 
disparities.

F. Organizational 
Authority, 
Governance, and 
Accreditation
General Requirements of Organizational 
Authority and Finances
6.F. 1. The CCBHC provides documented 
evidence that it is a non-profit organi
-
zation exempt from tax under Section 
501(c)(3) of the United States Internal 
Revenue Code; part of a local govern-
ment behavioral health authority; 
operates under the authority of the 
Indian Health Service (IHS), an Indian 
tribe, or tribal organization pursuant 
to
 a contract, grant, or cooperative 
agreement, or compact with the IHS; 
or is an urban Indian organization 
pursuant to a grant or contract with 
the IHS under Title V of the Indian 
Health Care Improvement Act.
1.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
  
6.F. 2. A CCBHC that does not operate under 
the authority of the IHS, an Indian tribe, 
or tribal or urban Indian organization 
establishes arrangements with those 
entities to inform and assist in the pro
-
vision of services to tribal members.
2.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
Intent Statements
When the CCBHC has a tribal organization located 
in a reasonable distance from its service locations, 
this arrangement is made in accordance with the 
wants and needs of the tribal organization.
6.F. 3. Apply Standard 1.F.8.
3.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
  
6.F. 4. Apply Standard 1.F.8.b.(2)

Governance
6.F. 5. The CCBHC’s governance:
5.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
a. Is representative of the demo -
graphics of persons served by 
the CCBHC (e.g., geographic area, 
race, ethnicity, sex, gender identity, 
disability, age, sexual orientation, 
and health and behavioral health 
needs). 
5.a.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
b. Includes individuals with lived exp -
erience of mental health and/or SUDs 
and their families, including youth.
5.b.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
  
6.F. 6. The CCBHC governing board dem-
onstrates meaningful participation 
by
 individuals with lived experience 
of mental health and/or SUDs and 
family members, including:
6.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
a. Development of initiatives.
6.a.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
b. Identification of community needs, 
goals, and objectives.
6.b.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
c. Provision of input on service devel -
opment and CQI processes.
6.c.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
d. Budget development and fiscal 
decision-making.
6.d.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
  
6.F. 7. The CCBHC demonstrates substantial 
participation by having at least fifty-one 
percent of the CCBHC governing board 
comprised of individuals with lived exp
-
erience of mental health and/or SUDs 
and family members [Option 1 under 
SAMHSA Criteria 6.B.] or by other means 
established to demonstrate meaningful 
participation in board governance 
involving people with lived experience 
of
 mental health and/or SUDs [Option 2 
under SAMHSA Criteria 6.B.].
7.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
Intent Statements
If the organization is utilizing Option 1, the org-
anization has documented which governing 
board members occupy seats as individuals 
with lived experience and/or family members. 
When an organization is utilizing Option 2, the 
advisory committee or other method meets 
at intervals frequently enough to provide 
meaningful input to the governing board.
6.F. 8. If the CCBHC demonstrates substantial 
participation by SAMHSA Option 1, the 
CCBHC describes how it meets, or will 
meet, the requirement that at least fifty-
one percent of the
 CCBHC governing 
board is comprised of individuals with 
lived experience of mental health and/or 
SUDs and family members.
8.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
  
6.F. 9. If the CCBHC demonstrates substantial 
participation by SAMHSA Option 2:
9.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
a. The CCBHC provides staff support 
to the individuals involved that is 
equivalent to the support given to 
the governing board.
9.a.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
b. The governing board implements 
protocols for incorporating input 
from individuals with lived experi-
ence and family members into: 
9.b.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
(1) Identification of community 
needs and goals and objectives 
of the CCBHC.
9.b.(1)F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
(2) Service development, quality 
improvement, and the activities 
of the CCBHC. 
9.b.(2)F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
(3) Fiscal and budgetary decisions.
9.b.(3)F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
(4) Governance (e.g., human 
resource planning, leadership 
recruitment and selection, etc.).
9.b.(4)F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
c. Board meeting summaries are shared 
with those participating in the alter-
nate arrangement.
9.c.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
d. Recommendations from representa -
tives of the alternate arrangement 
are entered into the formal board 
record.
9.d.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation
e. A member(s) of the alternate arrange -
ment is invited to board meetings.
9.e.F. Organizational Authority, Governance, and Accreditation

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 385
APPENDIX A
Required Written Documentation
When used in the CARF standards, the following terms always indicate the need 
for written evidence to demonstrate conformance: policy, plan, documented, 
documentation, and written. The following tables list only those standards that 
explicitly require some form of written evidence to demonstrate conformance. 
This appendix is intended to provide a quick reference to such standards and 
provide a tool that organizations may find helpful in preparing for their surveys.
What is not included in this Appendix:
During a site survey, an organization may provide a variety of documents and 
written materials related to standards that are not listed here, including:
n  Documents and written materials that demonstrate conformance to standards 
that do not explicitly require written evidence; e.g., procedures that do not 
specify written or education or training that does not specify documented.
n  Documents and written materials that support conformance to standards 
but are not specifically required by the standards; e.g., minutes of meetings 
or orientation packets for persons served.
NOTE: This appendix is available in an editable electronic format in the Resources section 
of Customer Connect (https://customerconnect.carf.org).

Standard(s) Requirements Location of Documents

or augmented intelligence (AI), if applicable

dures to deal with allegations of violations of ethical 
codes
1.A.7.a. For U.S. organizations receiving federal funds, policy 
on corporate compliance adopted by organization 
leadership
1.A.7.b. For U.S. organizations receiving federal funds, written 
procedures that address exclusion of individuals and 
entities from federally funded healthcare programs
1.A.7.c.(1) For U.S. organizations receiving federal funds, docu-
mentation of staff member designated to serve as the

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 407
APPENDIX B
Operational Timelines
The following tables list standards that require activities be conducted at specific 
time intervals. The evidence assembled as part of survey preparation should 
support that these activities occur at the specified intervals. 
During an original survey, or a survey subsequent to issuance of Inaugural One-Y ear 
Accreditation, an organization is expected to demonstrate that the activity has 
occurred at least once within the specified time period prior to the survey. 
The timelines for the standards listed in the last table, Activities to be Conducted 
at a Frequency Determined by the Organization (e.g., verification of backgrounds, 
credentials, and fitness for duty of members of the workforce, or data collected about 
persons served at appropriate intervals during services) may be influenced by various 
factors such as regulatory requirements or the needs of the organization and the 
persons served. For these standards, the organization should identify the frequency 
at which the activities are scheduled to be conducted. The survey team will look 
for evidence that the organization adheres to the timelines it has identified. 
What is not included in this Appendix:
n Standards that require an organization’s policy or written procedure to include 
a timeframe, such as timeframes for addressing a formal complaint or entering 
information into the records of the persons served, are not listed in this appendix. 
n Standards that require activities be conducted on an ongoing or as needed basis 
are not included in this Appendix. 
Please contact your resource specialist with any questions.
NOTE: This appendix is available in an editable electronic format in the Resources section 
in Customer Connect (https://customerconnect.carf.org).

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 419
APPENDIX C
Required Training
The following tables list the standards that explicitly require an organization to provide 
some form of education or training to personnel, persons served, and/or other 
stakeholders. 
Some standards require competency-based training or education. As defined in the 
CARF Glossary, competency-based training is an approach to education that focuses 
on the ability to demonstrate adequate skills, knowledge, and capacity to perform a 
specific set of job functions. 
What is not included in this Appendix:
n  Standards that require specifically qualified or trained personnel to provide 
certain services or require an organization to verify or ensure that personnel 
have appropriate qualifications, education, and/or training but do not require 
the organization to directly provide the requisite education or training are not 
listed in this Appendix. 
n  Many standards require sharing or exchange of information with persons served, 
personnel, and/or other stakeholders. Although this may be accomplished through 
educational efforts, such standards are not listed in this Appendix. 
NOTE: This appendix is available in an editable electronic format in the Resources section 
in Customer Connect (https://customerconnect.carf.org).

2026 Behavioral Health Standards Manual 429
APPENDIX D
Resources
Following are some resources that may be helpful to your organization in 
implementing or conforming to the CARF standards. Resources may be relevant 
to multiple standards.
CARF does not endorse or expect the use of any specific products, companies, 
or resources. Resources listed are not inclusive of all resources available on a 
topic and are provided as guidance only.
Resources include references to websites, organizations, and publications. When 
available, links are provided. Some websites contain multiple helpful resources.
If you discover a broken link, or would like to submit a resource you feel may be 
helpful, please submit your feedback for consideration to documents@carf.org.
NOTE: This appendix is available in an editable electronic format in the Resources section 
of Customer Connect (https://customerconnect.carf.org).
Section 1. ASPIRE to Excellence®
1.A. Leadership

n U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office of Inspector General:
– https://oig.hhs.gov
– https://oig.hhs.gov/documents/compliance-guidance/1135/HHS-OIG-
GCPG-2023.pdf
– https://oig.hhs.gov/compliance/101/index.asp
– https://exclusions.oig.hhs.gov
n System for Award Management Exclusions: https://sam.gov/content/exclusions
n CMS Compliance Program Policy and Guidance:  
https://www.cms.gov/medicare/audits-compliance/part-c-d/compliance-
program-policy-and-guidance
n CMS Provider Compliance:  
https://www.cms.gov/Outreach-and-Education/Medicare-Learning-Network-
MLN/MLNProducts/ProviderCompliance
n Federal Register (final rulings for regulations) Modernization of Compliance 
Program Guidance Documents:  
https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/04/25/2023-08326/
modernization-of-compliance-program-guidance-documents

1.H. Health and Safety
Resources related to emergency preparedness, planning, and procedures:
Local Red Cross associations, state/provincial or other jurisdictional regulations, 
regional disaster preparedness groups, and many websites offer current and useful 
information in the development of emergency plans.
The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) is a national resource for 
education, training, and emergency information in the U.S. FEMA has established 
an emergency planning guide for business and industry that provides advice for 
creating and maintaining an overall emergency management plan specific to each 
organization’s corporate culture. Other free emergency procedures that may be 
incorporated into your plans are also available online. Resources include:
n https://www.fema.gov/
n https://www.ada.gov/emergencyprepguide.htm
Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA): 
Disaster Planning Handbook for Behavioral Health Service Programs: https://
store.samhsa.gov/product/tap-34-disaster-planning-handbook-for-behavioral-
health-service-programs/pep21-02-01-001
Other websites that offer resources for developing emergency procedures include:
n Emergency Preparedness Requirements for Medicare and Medicaid Participating 
Providers and Suppliers:
– https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2016/09/16/2016-21404/medi-
care-and-medicaid-programs-emergency-preparedness-requirements-for-
medicare-and-medicaid
– https://www.cms.gov/medicare/health-safety-standards/quality-safety-
oversight-emergency-preparedness/emergency-preparedness-rule
– https://www.cms.gov/medicare/health-safety-standards/quality-safety-
oversight-emergency-preparedness
– https://asprtracie.hhs.gov/cmsrule
– https://ltcombudsman.org/uploads/files/issues/cms-ep-rule-resources-at-
your-fingertips.pdf
n U.S. Department of Homeland Security Active Shooter Preparedness: 
https://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/active_shooter_booklet.pdf
n Ready: https://www.ready.gov
n National Library of Medicine Emergency Preparedness for Vulnerable 
Populations:  
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2646456/
n U.S. Department of Labor Occupational Safety and Health Administration 
Emergency Preparedness and Response:  
https://www.osha.gov/emergency-preparedness
n Disaster Resources for People with Disabilities and Others:  
https://www.jik.com/disaster.html
